This diagram translates the iconic **TPS House** into a functional architecture showing all interacting subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Roof: Quality Lowest Cost Shortest Lead Time Safety High Morale Environment"]
    B1["Pillar Right: Just In Time JIT"]
    B2["Pillar Left: Jidoka Autonomation Build In Quality"]
    C1["Foundation: Heijunka Level Production"]
    C2["Foundation: Standardization Stable Processes"]
    C3["Foundation: Kaizen Continuous Improvement"]
    D1["Base: Respect For People"]
    D2["Base: Continuous Improvement Culture"]

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> C3
```

### 4.2 Lean Revolution Timeline — Historical Progression

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["1945: Post War Japan Resource Scarcity"]
    H2["1950: Ohno Visits Ford River Rouge Plant Sees Mass Production Limits"]
    H3["1956: Ohno Studies US Supermarkets Kanban Inspiration"]
    H4["1973: Oil Crisis Western Auto Collapse Toyota Thrives"]
    H5["1978: Taiichi Ohno Publishes Toyota Production System Book"]
    H6["1990: Womack Jones Roos The Machine That Changed the World Term Lean Coined"]
    H7["1996: Womack and Jones Lean Thinking Book Global Adoption"]
    H8["2003: Jeffrey Liker The Toyota Way 14 Principles"]
    H9["2020s: Lean 4.0 Lean Digital Industry 4.0 Integration"]

    H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4 --> H5 --> H6 --> H7 --> H8 --> H9
```

### 4.3 JIT Pull System Flow — Kanban Movement Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K0["Customer Demand Signal"]
    K1["Final Assembly Line"]
    K2["Sub Assembly Process"]
    K3["Parts Machining Process"]
    K4["Supplier Raw Materials"]

    K0 --> K1
    K1 -- Withdraws Parts Via Withdrawal Kanban --> K2
    K2 -- Withdraws Parts Via Withdrawal Kanban --> K3
    K3 -- Withdraws Materials Via Withdrawal Kanban --> K4
    K4 -- Production Ordering Kanban --> K3
    K3 -- Production Ordering Kanban --> K2
    K2 -- Production Ordering Kanban --> K1
    K1 -- Delivers Product --> K0
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows (downstream)**: *Withdrawal Kanban* — the downstream process pulls only what it needs.
- **Dashed-style logic (upstream)**: *Production-Ordering Kanban* — the upstream process produces only to replace what was withdrawn.
- This **dual Kanban loop** ensures zero overproduction and zero stockouts when sized correctly.

### 4.4 Continuous Improvement Cycle (PDCA + Kaizen)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Plan: Identify Problem Set Target Analyze Root Cause 5 Whys"]
    P2["Do: Pilot Small Scale Test Implement Countermeasure"]
    P3["Check: Measure Results Compare To Standard Validate Effect"]
    P4["Act: Standardize If Successful Or Iterate If Not"]
    P5["Next PDCA Cycle Higher Standard"]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    P5 -.Loop Back.-> P1
```

### 4.5 TPS Cause-Effect Chain — The Hidden Waste Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Overproduction Root Waste Hides All Other Problems"]
    R2["Excess Inventory"]
    R3["Waiting Queue Time"]
    R4["Defects Hidden In Inventory"]
    R5["Excess Motion To Handle Stock"]
    R6["Excess Transportation"]
    R7["Over Processing To Fix Hidden Defects"]
    R8["Low Morale Wasted Human Potential"]

    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R2 --> R4
    R4 --> R5
    R4 --> R7
    R2 --> R6
    R3 --> R8
    R5 --> R8
    R7 --> R8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for the KTU exam:** Taiichi Ohno explicitly stated that **overproduction is the worst waste** because it **generates or hides all the other wastes**. When you produce too much, you create inventory (waste 2), which causes waiting (waste 4), hides defects (waste 6), and forces excess motion and transport to manage. **Eliminate overproduction first** — and the rest of the waste mountain collapses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*"Define the Toyota Production System. List its two pillars and briefly state the purpose of each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is an integrated socio-technical management philosophy developed by Taiichi Ohno at Toyota Motor Corporation, designed to eliminate waste (*Muda*), unevenness (*Mura*), and overburden (*Muri*) while delivering the highest quality products at the lowest cost and shortest lead time.
>
> **The two pillars of TPS are:**
>
> 1. **Just-in-Time (JIT):** A pull-based production approach in which each process produces *only what is needed, in the exact quantity, at the exact time* demanded by the next downstream process. **Purpose:** to eliminate overproduction and excess inventory.
>
> 2. **Jidoka (Autonomation):** The principle of building quality into the process by automatically stopping the line when an abnormality is detected, and empowering workers to fix the root cause. **Purpose:** to prevent defective products from flowing downstream.
>
> *(The two pillars rest on the foundation of Standardization, Heijunka, and Kaizen, and are supported by the base of respect for people and continuous improvement.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*"Differentiate between Muda, Mura, and Muri with one industrial example of each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Meaning | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Muda** | Waste — any activity that consumes resources but creates no value | Storing excess WIP in a warehouse *(inventory waste)* |
| **Mura** | Unevenness — irregularity in demand, workload, or flow | Producing 500 units in the first half of the month and 50 in the second half |
| **Muri** | Overburden — unreasonable demand on workers or machines | Asking one CNC operator to manage 4 machines simultaneously, leading to errors and breakdowns |

> **Key insight:** TPS attacks all three simultaneously. JIT tackles *Mura* (levels it), Jidoka tackles *Muda*, and standardized work tackles *Muri*.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*(a)* Explain the **TPS House** model in detail. Discuss the roof, the two pillars, the foundation, and the base. Why is *Respect for People* considered the bedrock of the system? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* With suitable industrial examples, explain **Jidoka** and **Poka-Yoke** as mechanisms to build in quality. Why is Jidoka called the "automation with a human touch"? *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) The TPS House Model [7 marks]**

The **TPS House** is the canonical visual model that captures the structure and interdependencies of the Toyota Production System. The house metaphor is deliberate: if any structural element is missing, the entire system collapses.

**1. Roof (Goal — what TPS delivers):**
- Highest quality
- Lowest cost
- Shortest lead time
- Safety
- High morale
- Environmental responsibility

This represents the **customer value** outcomes that TPS is designed to produce.

**2. The Two Pillars (How TPS delivers the roof):**
- **Right Pillar — Just-in-Time (JIT):** Produces and delivers "the right items, in the right quantity, at the right time." JIT enables short lead times and reduces inventory cost.
- **Left Pillar — Jidoka:** Builds quality into the process by detecting abnormalities and stopping the line. Jidoka prevents defects, eliminates rework, and ensures the right pillar (JIT) is not compromised by defective parts flowing into the line.

**3. Foundation (The supporting mechanisms):**
- **Heijunka (Level Production):** Levels the production mix and volume to avoid Mura. Supports JIT by making pull demand predictable.
- **Standardization:** Establishes best-known methods as standards so variability is controlled and improvement can be measured.
- **Kaizen:** Continuous incremental improvement of standards, processes, and skills.

**4. The Base (The bedrock — the cultural foundation):**
- **Respect for People:** Treat workers, suppliers, and customers with respect. Empower them to use their intelligence, stop the line when needed, and contribute to improvement. Without this, the pillars cannot be erected.
- **Continuous Improvement Culture:** A never-ending pursuit of perfection (*Kaizen*) embedded in the organizational DNA.

**Why is Respect for People the bedrock?**
Toyota's leadership realized that the Jidoka pillar **cannot function** if workers are not **empowered and trusted** to stop the line. JIT cannot function if workers are not **respected enough** to follow standards and suggest improvements. Suppliers will not share process data if not respected. Therefore, **respect for people is the philosophical prerequisite** for all technical pillars to work. *(3 marks for this reasoning)*

---

**(b) Jidoka and Poka-Yoke [7 marks]**

**Jidoka (自働化 — "Autonomation"):**
The term was coined by **Sakichi Toyoda** in 1896 when he invented the automatic loom that would *automatically stop* when a thread broke. Taiichi Ohno later expanded this to the entire production system.

**Industrial Example — Toyota Assembly Line:**
A worker on the chassis line notices a missing bolt. He pulls the **Andon cord**. The line stops within seconds. A team leader arrives, the issue is diagnosed, the root cause is fixed, and production resumes — *with all subsequent cars guaranteed to have the bolt correctly installed.* If the worker had NOT stopped the line, the defect would have propagated to 50 more cars.

**Why "automation with a human touch"?**
Traditional automation replaces human **muscle** (e.g., a robotic arm). Jidoka replaces human **muscle but retains human judgment** — the machine stops automatically, but a *human decides what to do next*. This is the key innovation.

**Poka-Yoke (ポカヨケ — "Mistake-Proofing"):**
A Poka-Yoke is a **device, jig, or procedural mechanism** that makes it physically impossible for an operator to make a mistake, or that immediately signals when a mistake is made.

**Industrial Example — USB Connector:**
The USB Type-A connector can only be inserted in one orientation. The asymmetrical design **prevents** the user from inserting it upside down. *(Example in manufacturing: a fixture that only allows a part to be loaded in the correct orientation.)*

**Another Example — Car Door Assembly:**
Toyota uses a sensor jig that detects whether all 4 door bolts are tightened. If even one is missed, the jig refuses to release, and the operator cannot move the car to the next station. **The defect is caught at the source.**

**Relationship between Jidoka and Poka-Yoke:**
- *Jidoka* is the **philosophical principle** (stop on abnormality).
- *Poka-Yoke* is one of the **practical tools** that implements Jidoka at the workstation level.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*(a)* What is **Lean Production**? Compare **Mass Production** and **Lean Production** across any **five** parameters. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A food processing unit in Palakkad operates **8 hours per day, 250 days per year**. The customer annual demand is **100,000 units**. Breaks (lunch + tea) total **1 hour per day**. The line is currently experiencing **30 days of unplanned downtime per year**. Calculate the **Takt Time in seconds**, the **OEE components**, and recommend **two TPS tools** to improve performance. Use the following additional data: ideal cycle time $= 90$ seconds, parts produced in available running time $= 105000$, good parts $= 92000$. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Lean vs Mass Production [7 marks]**

**Lean Production** is a systematic method for waste minimization without sacrificing productivity. It originated from TPS and was named by Womack, Jones, and Roos in 1990.

| Parameter | Mass Production | Lean Production |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Henry Ford, early 20th century | Taiichi Ohno, Toyota, mid-20th century |
| **Primary Goal** | High volume, low unit cost at scale | High quality, low cost, short lead time, high variety |
| **Inventory Philosophy** | Large buffer stock to absorb variability | Minimal inventory, expose problems |
| **Production Trigger** | Forecast (push system) | Actual demand (pull system) |
| **Workforce** | Specialized, low decision authority | Multi-skilled, empowered to stop the line |
| **Lead Time** | Long (high WIP) | Short (continuous flow) |
| **Defect Handling** | Inspect at end, rework in batches | Build quality in, stop at source (Jidoka) |
| **Supplier Relationship** | Adversarial, many suppliers | Long-term partnership, few suppliers |
| **Best Suited For** | Stable, high-volume, low-variety demand | Low-to-medium volume, high variety |

*(Any five parameters with valid comparisons get full marks.)*

---

**(b) Quantitative Analysis [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Calculate daily available production time.**

Operating hours per day: $8$ hours $= 480$ minutes.
Breaks per day: $1$ hour $= 60$ minutes.

$$
T_{a,\text{day}} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ minutes/day} = 25200 \text{ seconds/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate annual available production time.**

Operating days per year: $250$.
Unplanned downtime: $30$ days.

$$
T_{a,\text{year}} = (250 - 30) \times 25200 = 220 \times 25200 = 5544000 \text{ seconds/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Takt Time.**

Annual demand $D = 100000$ units.

$$
T_{t} = \frac{T_{a}}{D} = \frac{5544000}{100000} = 55.44 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

> **Interpretation:** To meet annual demand, the line must complete **one unit every 55.44 seconds**.

**Step 4 — Calculate OEE components.**

Given:
- Planned production time per year: $250 \text{ days} \times 8 \text{ hr} \times 3600 = 7200000$ sec
- Ideal cycle time: $T_{ideal} = 90$ sec
- Actual parts produced: $N_{total} = 105000$
- Good parts: $N_{good} = 92000$

Availability:

$$
A = \frac{T_{run}}{T_{planned}} = \frac{5544000}{7200000} = 0.7700 = 77.00\%
$$

Performance:

$$
P = \frac{T_{ideal} \times N_{total}}{T_{run}} = \frac{90 \times 105000}{5544000} = \frac{9450000}{5544000} = 1.7045
$$

> **Note:** Performance is capped at 1.00 (or 100%) in real-world calculation, since the line is exceeding ideal cycle expectations — a sign of measurement error or unrealistic ideal. In exam valuation, state the raw value and explain the cap: $P = 100\%$ (capped), but the calculation shows the line is theoretically faster than ideal — data anomaly.

Quality:

$$
Q = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} = \frac{92000}{105000} = 0.8762 = 87.62\%
$$

OEE:

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.7700 \times 1.00 \times 0.8762 = 0.6747 = 67.47\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** OEE of $67.47\%$ is below the world-class benchmark of $85\%$. Major losses are in **Availability ($77\%$)** and **Quality ($87.62\%$)**.

**Step 5 — Two TPS Tools Recommended:**

1. **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** to reduce the $30$ days of unplanned downtime → improve Availability toward 90%+. Implement preventive maintenance, autonomous maintenance by operators, and root-cause analysis of breakdowns.

2. **Poka-Yoke + Jidoka** to eliminate the $13000$ defect units ($105000 - 92000$) → improve Quality toward 99%+. Install mistake-proofing fixtures at critical stations and empower line workers to stop the process on detecting an abnormality.

3. *(Bonus)* **Heijunka** to level the production mix throughout the year, reducing the strain that may be causing both downtime and defects.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in TPS Questions (as observed in KTU valuation keys):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "TPS" with just "tools"** — Students list 5S, Kanban, SMED as the *whole* of TPS. *TPS is a philosophy + system; the tools are mere instruments.* Always anchor to the **two pillars** and the **cultural base**.
>
> 2. **Mistranslating Jidoka as "automation"** — The KTU board deducts marks for equating Jidoka with simple mechanization. **Always emphasize "human intelligence to stop the line."**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert time units in Takt Time** — Students frequently divide minutes by units and report "minutes/unit" without converting. *Show the unit conversion steps explicitly.*
>
> 4. **OEE: forgetting to cap Performance at 100%** — When ideal cycle time data is inconsistent, raw $P$ may exceed 1.0. *State the cap and explain the anomaly.*
>
> 5. **Mixing Muda, Mura, Muri** — Many students use these terms interchangeably. *Muda = waste, Mura = unevenness, Muri = overburden.* They are distinct.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the "Respect for People" base** — In TPS House questions, students often describe the roof and pillars beautifully but forget the *base*. *Always end with respect for people as the philosophical bedrock.*
>
> 7. **Writing generic answers without industrial examples** — KTU examiners award higher marks for answers with **specific industrial examples** (Toyota assembly line, CNC shop, hospital ER, etc.). Use at least one example per 7-mark sub-part.
>
> 8. **Performance metric: ideal cycle time must be the *fastest sustainable* time, not the *target* time.** Misinterpreting this is a common error in OEE problems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **TPS High-Yield Revision Bullets — Read Before Every Exam**

- ✅ TPS = **Integrated socio-technical system** with **2 pillars** (JIT + Jidoka) on a **foundation** (Heijunka + Standardization + Kaizen) above a **base** (Respect for People + Continuous Improvement Culture).
- ✅ **JIT (Just-in-Time)** = Pull system. Right item, right quantity, right time. **Eliminates overproduction.**
- ✅ **Jidoka (Autonomation)** = Build quality in. Stop on abnormality. **Prevents defect propagation.**
- ✅ **Lean Revolution timeline:** 1950 (Ohno visits Ford) → 1956 (Supermarket inspiration) → 1973 (Oil crisis) → 1990 (Womack, Jones, Roos coin "Lean") → 2003 (Liker, 14 Principles) → Present (Lean 4.0).
- ✅ **Three Ms:** **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden). All three must be attacked.
- ✅ **Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD):** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, **Overproduction (worst)**, Over-processing, Defects. **Plus 8th:** Unused human talent.
- ✅ **Takt Time formula:** $T_t = T_a / D$ (sec/unit). Set the **heartbeat** of the line.
- ✅ **OEE formula:** $A \times P \times Q$. **World-class = 85%+.**
- ✅ **Value-Added Ratio:** $V_a\% = T_{va} / L \times 100$. In many processes, this is **< 5%** — Lean aims to push it up by eliminating non-value-added time.
- ✅ **14 Principles of Toyota Way** — Liker (2004): 4 categories (Philosophy, Process, People & Partners, Problem Solving). Phrases to remember: Genchi Genbutsu (go & see), Nemawashi (consensus decision-making), Hansei (reflection).
- ✅ **Kanban** = visual signal card for pull. Two types: **Withdrawal Kanban** (downstream pulls) and **Production-Ordering Kanban** (upstream replenishes).
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** = mistake-proofing device. **Andon** = visual/auditory signal to call for help. **Heijunka** = level production. **Kaizen** = continuous improvement. **5S** = workplace organization.
- ✅ TPS is **not industry-specific** — applies to manufacturing, services, healthcare, software, construction.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, **always include a labeled diagram** (TPS House, JIT flow, etc.) for higher marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systems Thinking in Lean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Systems Thinking in Lean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Systems Thinking** is a holistic analytical framework that focuses on understanding how the constituent parts of a system interrelate and how systems evolve over time, emphasizing the optimization of the whole rather than the individual components. In the context of Lean Manufacturing, it is the foundational discipline that enables practitioners to view the **Value Stream** as an integrated, dynamic network of processes, people, and information flows, rather than as isolated, independent operational silos.

The term was popularized in management theory by **Peter Senge** in his seminal work *"The Fifth Discipline"* (1990), where it was identified as the fifth and most critical discipline of the learning organization. In Lean literature, it forms the cognitive bedrock upon which tools like **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)**, **Just-In-Time (JIT)**, and the **Theory of Constraints (TOC)** are constructed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine the human body. A doctor trained in "reductionist thinking" might treat a headache with a painkiller and a cough with syrup — treating each symptom (part) independently. A doctor trained in **Systems Thinking**, however, sees the body as an interconnected system. He understands that the headache, the cough, and the fever might all stem from a single infection (a root cause), and that treating symptoms in isolation can mask the real disease and create dangerous side effects elsewhere in the body.

Applied to a **manufacturing plant**:
- **Reductionist thinking** → The stamping department speeds up its press to "improve output" (local optimization), but it creates a massive pile of Work-In-Progress (WIP) in front of the next station, increasing lead time for the **entire** value stream.
- **Systems thinking** → The plant manager realizes that locally optimizing one press **increased** the overall WIP, exposed a constraint, and degraded the system's overall **flow**.

### 1.3 Core Tenets of Systems Thinking (Syllabus Highlights)

| Tenet | Core Idea | Lean Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Holism** | The whole is greater than the sum of its parts. | Optimize the **value stream**, not individual workstations. |
| **Interconnectedness** | Every element affects and is affected by others. | A change in takt time ripples through every process. |
| **Emergent Properties** | Properties of the system arise from interactions, not from components. | **Lead Time** is an emergent property of the entire system. |
| **Feedback Loops** | Outputs of a system become inputs. | **Kanban** signals are feedback mechanisms. |
| **Non-Linear Causality** | Cause and effect are not always proportional in time/space. | A small change in setup time can dramatically reduce WIP (Little's Law). |
| **Dynamic Complexity** | Behavior over time matters more than static snapshots. | **PDCA** cycles track the system's evolution. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Systems thinking is the cognitive prerequisite for understanding *why* a Lean transformation is not a collection of tools (5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke) but a fundamental shift in **how an organization perceives its operations**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Disciplines (Peter Senge's Framework)

For KTU 2024 evaluation, you must know the **5 Disciplines of a Learning Organization** and their precise roles in Lean. Memorize the table below — it is a guaranteed high-yield question.

| # | Discipline | Function | Lean Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Personal Mastery** | Individual learning and growth. | Kaizen at the operator level; skill matrices. |
| 2 | **Mental Models** | Deeply held assumptions and generalizations. | Challenging "we have always done it this way." |
| 3 | **Shared Vision** | A common picture of the future. | True North metrics; Hoshin Kanri. |
| 4 | **Team Learning** | Dialogue and group thinking. | Quality Circles; A3 problem solving. |
| 5 | **Systems Thinking** | The "fifth discipline" that integrates the other four. | Value Stream thinking; TOC. |

### 2.2 The Iceberg Model of Systems Thinking

A KTU-favorite concept. The Iceberg Model explains why problems persist: we only address the visible **events**, ignoring the deeper **structures** and **mental models** that cause them.

| Layer (Top → Bottom) | Description | Lean Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Events** | Day-to-day occurrences. "What happened?" | Machine breakdown on Tuesday. |
| **2. Patterns / Trends** | Behavior over time. "What is happening?" | The same machine breaks down every 14 days. |
| **3. Systemic Structures** | The underlying causes. "Why is it happening?" | Preventive maintenance schedule is missing; skill gap in TPM. |
| **4. Mental Models** | The beliefs that create the structure. "What are our assumptions?" | "Maintenance is a cost center, not a value-creator." |

> [!NOTE]
> **Lean Application:** Lean tools like **5-Why Analysis** and **A3 Thinking** are designed to force practitioners to *dive below the event level* and address systemic structures and mental models.

### 2.3 Feedback Loops — The Engine of Systems

In any Lean system, every action triggers a response. These are called **feedback loops** and they come in two fundamentally different forms. This is the most heavily tested concept in Module 1.

#### A. Balancing (Negative) Feedback Loops
- **Behavior:** Self-correcting; seeks a **goal** or equilibrium.
- **Structure:** Goal → Current State → Gap → Action → adjusts Current State.
- **Lean Examples:**
  - **Kanban** replenishment system.
  - **Heijunka** (production leveling) — adjusts to demand.
  - **Thermostat** analogy: a room temperature control system.

#### B. Reinforcing (Positive) Feedback Loops
- **Behavior:** Self-reinforcing; produces exponential growth or collapse.
- **Structure:** Action → produces more of itself → amplifies.
- **Lean Examples:**
  - **Virtuous cycle:** More standard work → more consistency → better quality → fewer defects → more free capacity → more Kaizen time.
  - **Vicious cycle:** Equipment failure → overtime → operator fatigue → more mistakes → more defects → more rework → more overtime.

### 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quantitative Systems Metrics)

Although systems thinking is largely conceptual, Lean systems are governed by a few critical quantitative laws that *quantify* the system's behavior. **Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Foundational Laws of Lean Flow Systems**

**Law 1: Little's Law** (The Fundamental Theorem of Flow)
$$L = \lambda \times W$$
Where:
- $L$ = Work-In-Progress (WIP) in the system (units).
- $\lambda$ = **Throughput rate** (units arriving per unit time).
- $W$ = **Lead Time / Wait Time** (time units).

**Law 2: Lead Time Equation**
$$\text{Lead Time} = \text{Processing Time} + \text{Waiting Time}$$
$$\text{LT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( t_{p_i} + t_{w_i} \right)$$
Where $t_{p_i}$ is processing time and $t_{w_i}$ is wait time at station $i$.

**Law 3: Takt Time**
$$\text{Takt Time} = \frac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$$
$$T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D}$$

**Law 4: System Efficiency**
$$\eta_{system} = \frac{\sum t_{p}}{\text{Lead Time}} \times 100\%$$

**Law 5: Inventory Build-Up (from a perturbation)**
$$\Delta I = \lambda \times \Delta t_{down}$$
Where $\Delta I$ is the additional inventory created when a station is down for $\Delta t_{down}$ time.

### 2.5 Hard Systems vs. Soft Systems Thinking

A question that distinguishes top-grade answers in KTU valuation.

| Aspect | **Hard Systems Thinking** | **Soft Systems Thinking** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Engineering, Operational Research (1940s–50s). | Peter Checkland's **Soft Systems Methodology (SSM)**, 1970s. |
| **Problem Nature** | Well-defined, quantifiable. | Ill-defined, "wicked," human-centered. |
| **Goal** | Find the **optimal** solution. | Find a **culturally feasible** improvement. |
| **Method** | Mathematical modeling, simulation. | Rich pictures, root definitions, CATWOE. |
| **Lean Use** | Factory floor optimization, line balancing. | Change management, culture transformation, sustaining Lean. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for Lean:** A Lean transformation is roughly **20% technical** (hard systems — tools, layout) and **80% social** (soft systems — culture, behavior, leadership). A student who ignores soft systems thinking cannot explain *why* most Lean initiatives fail.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Relationship Between WIP, Lead Time, and Bottlenecks (Little's Law in Action)

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation**. You will be given a scenario and asked to prove or compute system behavior. The derivation below is the canonical "system behavior" problem.

> **Problem Statement (KTU Model):** A manufacturing cell produces a single product family. The average throughput rate is **8 units/hour**. The current WIP in the cell is **24 units**. Due to a proposed change in the layout, the throughput is expected to drop by **25%** temporarily while the system stabilizes, and the WIP will remain constant. Calculate the new average lead time and explain the systemic consequence.

**Step 1 — State Little's Law explicitly.**

$$L = \lambda \times W$$

This is an *invariant* of any stable queuing system. It holds regardless of the process distribution, number of servers, or arrival pattern.

**Step 2 — Identify the current state variables.**

Given:
- Current WIP: $L_{old} = 24$ units
- Current throughput: $\lambda_{old} = 8$ units/hour

**Step 3 — Compute the current Lead Time $W_{old}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{old} &= \frac{L_{old}}{\lambda_{old}} \\
W_{old} &= \frac{24 \text{ units}}{8 \text{ units/hour}} \\
W_{old} &= 3 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the perturbation to the new throughput.**

The throughput drops by 25%:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{new} &= \lambda_{old} \times (1 - 0.25) \\
\lambda_{new} &= 8 \times 0.75 \\
\lambda_{new} &= 6 \text{ units/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the new Lead Time $W_{new}$.**

Since WIP remains constant at 24 units during stabilization:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{new} &= \frac{L_{new}}{\lambda_{new}} \\
W_{new} &= \frac{24}{6} \\
W_{new} &= 4 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the change in Lead Time (system response).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta W &= W_{new} - W_{old} \\
\Delta W &= 4 - 3 \\
\Delta W &= 1 \text{ hour} \quad \text{(a 33.33\% increase in Lead Time)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Systems Thinking Interpretation (The "Why" the Examiner Wants).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A reductionist manager would see the 25% drop in throughput and expect a 25% drop in delivery performance. A systems thinker understands that **WIP acts as a "shock absorber" in the system**. While WIP remains constant, lead time must rise to absorb the throughput loss. The system is not "broken" — it is faithfully obeying Little's Law. The right systemic response is to *reduce WIP* (pull the kanban back) to lower lead time, not to push harder.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Effect of Setup Time Reduction on System Capacity (SMED & Flow)

This is a **"Show that..."** style 14-mark problem frequently asked in KTU Module 1.

> **Problem Statement:** A workstation processes 10 units/hour with a current setup time of 30 minutes per batch. The batch size is 50 units. Using the **Single Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)** philosophy, the setup time is reduced to 10 minutes. Demonstrate the systemic impact on inventory and lead time, assuming demand is 8 units/hour.

**Step 1 — State the batch-level inventory formula.**

Average inventory at the workstation during a batch cycle:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{batch} &= Q - D_{cycle} \\
\end{aligned}
$$
where $Q$ is the batch size and $D_{cycle}$ is the demand drained during one production run of one batch.

The cycle time to produce one batch is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= T_{setup} + Q \times T_{processing}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the processing time per unit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{processing} &= \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{10} \text{ hours} = 6 \text{ minutes/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Old system: $T_{setup} = 30$ min.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle,old} &= 30 \text{ min} + 50 \times 6 \text{ min} \\
T_{cycle,old} &= 30 + 300 = 330 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — New system: $T_{setup} = 10$ min.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle,new} &= 10 \text{ min} + 50 \times 6 \text{ min} \\
T_{cycle,new} &= 10 + 300 = 310 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the demand drained during the cycle (units consumed from inventory).**

Demand rate $D = 8$ units/hour $= \frac{8}{60}$ units/min.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{cycle,old} &= \frac{8}{60} \times 330 = 44 \text{ units} \\
D_{cycle,new} &= \frac{8}{60} \times 310 = 41.33 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute average inventory in the system.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg,old} &= \frac{Q}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ units (approx.)} \\
I_{avg,new} &= \frac{Q - D_{cycle,new}}{2} = \frac{50 - 41.33}{2} = 4.33 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Lead Time reduction via Little's Law.**

Using Little's Law with the same throughput ($\lambda = 8$ units/hr) and effective WIP:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{new} &= \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{4.33}{8} \approx 0.54 \text{ hours} = 32.5 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Systemic Insight for KTU:** A 67% reduction in setup time translated to a **~83% reduction in system inventory** and a corresponding collapse in lead time. This is the **classic SMED insight** of systems thinking: small, local improvements at the changeover point create **non-linear, system-wide leverage**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Reductionist vs. Systems-Thinking Decisions

This matrix is a frequent **Part-A 3-mark comparison question**.

| Decision Context | Reductionist Reaction | Systems-Thinking Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| Operator idle at machine 2. | Move operator to machine 3. | Identify *why* machine 2 is starved (system-wide flow break). |
| Defect at inspection. | Reject and re-queue. | Apply **Jidoka** — stop the line, find the root cause, build **Poka-Yoke**. |
| Low utilization at a workstation. | Increase batch size to "fill" the machine. | Reduce batch size, lower WIP, expose the true bottleneck. |
| Customer demand rises 20%. | Add 20% capacity everywhere. | Calculate new **Takt Time**; level the schedule (**Heijunka**). |
| Supplier late on delivery. | Find a backup supplier. | Reduce buffer inventory, shorten lead time, increase visibility (**VSM**). |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Generic Systems Thinking Model (Input → Process → Output → Feedback)

A foundational block diagram showing the four essential sub-systems of any Lean value stream. This is the **single most important diagram for KTU Module 1**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT: Raw Materials, Information, Demand Signal"] --> B["PROCESS: Value Adding Activities, Transformation, Flow"]
    B --> C["OUTPUT: Finished Goods, Services, Information to Customer"]
    C --> D["FEEDBACK: Customer Satisfaction, Quality Data, Kanban Signal, KPIs"]
    D --> A
    B -.-> E["SIDE EFFECT: Waste Mura Muri Muda"]
    E -.-> B
```

> **Reading the Diagram for KTU:** Every Lean system is a closed loop. The **feedback path** is what distinguishes a Lean system from a traditional production line. Without feedback (e.g., no kanban, no quality data, no customer pulse), the system is "open" and cannot self-correct — this is the hallmark of a non-Learning Organization.

---

### 4.2 The Iceberg Model — A Layered Causal Architecture

This diagram visualizes the four layers of the Iceberg Model discussed in Section 2.2, emphasizing that **only the top 10% is visible to managers**; the rest is submerged.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1: EVENTS - WHAT HAPPENED"]
        E1["Machine Breakdown on Tuesday"]
        E2["Customer Complaint on Friday"]
        E3["Quality Rejection on Monday"]
    end
    subgraph L2["LAYER 2: PATTERNS - WHAT IS HAPPENING"]
        P1["Same machine fails every 14 days"]
        P2["Defect rate rising for 3 months"]
        P3["Lead time increasing quarter over quarter"]
    end
    subgraph L3["LAYER 3: STRUCTURES - WHY IS IT HAPPENING"]
        S1["No Preventive Maintenance Plan"]
        S2["No Standard Work for New Operators"]
        S3["Push-based production with high WIP"]
    end
    subgraph L4["LAYER 4: MENTAL MODELS - WHAT WE BELIEVE"]
        M1["Maintenance is a cost; not a value creator"]
        M2["Speed is more important than quality"]
        M3["Inventory is a safety net; not a waste"]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 -.-> L1
```

> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why do Lean transformations fail?"*, draw this diagram. The answer is: *they only address Layer 1 (events) with quick fixes and never reach Layer 4 (mental models).*

---

### 4.3 Causal Loop Diagram — The Virtuous and Vicious Cycles of Lean

This is the systems-thinker's core tool. The same system can produce a virtuous cycle (Lean success) or a vicious cycle (Lean failure), depending on the **mental models** of the leadership.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph V1["VIRTUOUS CYCLE - R - Reinforcing Loop"]
        V1A["Standard Work Implemented"] --> V1B["Predictable Quality"]
        V1B --> V1C["Fewer Defects and Rework"]
        V1C --> V1D["More Free Capacity"]
        V1D --> V1E["Time for Kaizen Improvements"]
        V1E --> V1F["Better Standard Work"]
        V1F --> V1A
    end
    subgraph V2["VICIOUS CYCLE - R - Reinforcing Loop"]
        V2A["No Standard Work"] --> V2B["Inconsistent Quality"]
        V2B --> V2C["High Rework and Scrap"]
        V2C --> V2D["Operators Overworked and Stressed"]
        V2D --> V2E["No Time for Kaizen"]
        V2E --> V2F["Resistance to Standard Work"]
        V2F --> V2A
    end
```

> **Key Learning:** The *structure* of both loops is identical (5-node reinforcing feedback). The only difference is the **initial condition (mental model)**. This is why **Senge's Fifth Discipline** argues that systems thinking is inseparable from personal mastery and shared vision.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lean Transformation as a System

Since the physical transformation of a factory is too complex to draw, the diagram below maps the **functional architecture of a Lean transformation system** and its interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENABLERS["ENABLERS LAYER - Soft Systems"]
        EN1["Leadership Commitment and Hoshin Kanri"]
        EN2["Lean Culture and Shared Vision"]
        EN3["Training and Skill Development"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESS LAYER - Hard Systems"]
        P1["Value Stream Mapping VSM"]
        P2["Flow Design - Cells, Lines, Layout"]
        P3["Pull Systems - Kanban, Heijunka"]
        P4["Quality at Source - Jidoka, Poka-Yoke"]
        P5["Continuous Improvement - Kaizen, PDCA"]
    end
    subgraph OUTCOMES["OUTCOMES LAYER"]
        O1["Reduced Lead Time and WIP"]
        O2["Improved Quality and Cost"]
        O3["Customer Delight and On-Time Delivery"]
        O4["Engaged Employees and Learning Organization"]
    end
    ENABLERS --> PROCESS
    PROCESS --> OUTCOMES
    OUTCOMES -.Feedback to ENABLERS.-> ENABLERS
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 -.Feedback to VSM.-> P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This is the *system of systems* for Lean. The **Enablers** (soft, human) drive the **Process** (hard, technical) which produces the **Outcomes** (measurable). The outcomes feed back to *reinforce or break* the enablers. A Lean initiative that focuses only on Process and ignores Enablers will collapse after 6–12 months — this is empirically observed in 70% of failed transformations (a frequent KTU 14-mark essay point).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 1, Q1a]**
> **Q1. Define Systems Thinking. How does it differ from reductionist thinking in a manufacturing context? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Systems Thinking is a holistic analytical approach that studies a system by understanding the interrelationships among its components, the feedback loops, and the emergent behavior of the whole, rather than analyzing parts in isolation. In Lean, it means viewing the value stream as an end-to-end flow rather than a set of independent departmental KPIs.
>
> **Contrast with Reductionism (1.5 marks):** Reductionist thinking focuses on optimizing individual components (e.g., maximizing machine utilization), which often sub-optimizes the whole (e.g., creates WIP and longer lead time). Systems thinking, by contrast, focuses on the **goal of the whole system** (e.g., shortest lead time, highest customer value) and accepts that some local inefficiencies (e.g., idle time at a non-bottleneck) are correct decisions.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q1b]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between a balancing feedback loop and a reinforcing feedback loop with one Lean example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

> **Balancing Loop (1.5 marks):** A balancing (negative) feedback loop is self-correcting and seeks a target or equilibrium state. *Example:* The **Kanban replenishment system** — when WIP drops below the reorder point, a kanban card signals upstream production to replenish and bring the WIP back to the target level.
>
> **Reinforcing Loop (1.5 marks):** A reinforcing (positive) feedback loop amplifies the direction of change, producing growth or collapse. *Example:* The **virtuous cycle of Standard Work** — better standards → better quality → less rework → more capacity for Kaizen → even better standards.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 1, Q2]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** Explain Peter Senge's **Five Disciplines of a Learning Organization** with specific reference to how each discipline supports a Lean manufacturing transformation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A single-product assembly cell has a current throughput of **10 units/hour** and a WIP of **40 units**. A Kaizen event reduces the WIP to **15 units** without changing the processing time at any station. Using **Little's Law**, calculate the original and new lead times, and explain the systemic implications of the change using the Iceberg Model. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks (Senge's Five Disciplines + Lean Mapping)**

The Learning Organization, as conceptualized by Peter M. Senge in *"The Fifth Discipline"*, rests on five interrelated disciplines. Each plays a distinct role in a Lean manufacturing transformation:

| # | Discipline | Meaning | Role in Lean Transformation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Personal Mastery** | Continuous self-improvement and clarity of personal vision. | Operators learn to identify and solve problems at the gemba; the foundation of a **Kaizen culture**. |
| 2 | **Mental Models** | Surfacing and challenging deeply held assumptions. | Breaking beliefs like *"Inventory is a safety net"* that prevent pull-system adoption. |
| 3 | **Shared Vision** | A common, inspiring picture of the future state. | A **True North** vision (zero defects, zero WIP, zero lead time) that aligns the whole organization. |
| 4 | **Team Learning** | Dialogue and collective thinking, suspending assumptions. | **A3 problem-solving** and Quality Circles leverage the collective intelligence of teams. |
| 5 | **Systems Thinking** | The integrative discipline — understanding interrelationships. | Provides the **conceptual framework** for Value Stream Mapping, TOC, and policy deployment. |

> **Synthesis (for full marks):** Senge's central argument is that the **first four disciplines are necessary but insufficient** without systems thinking. A factory can train operators, change beliefs, and have a shared vision — but if it does not *see the system as a whole*, it will make locally optimal but globally harmful decisions. Hence, systems thinking is the **"fifth discipline"** that gives the other four their power.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Little's Law Numerical + Iceberg Model Interpretation)**

**Step 1: State Little's Law.**

$$L = \lambda \times W \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \frac{L}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2: Original Lead Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{old} &= \frac{L_{old}}{\lambda_{old}} = \frac{40 \text{ units}}{10 \text{ units/hour}} \\
W_{old} &= 4 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: New Lead Time.**

Since throughput is unchanged ($\lambda_{new} = 10$ units/hour) and WIP is now 15 units:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{new} &= \frac{L_{new}}{\lambda_{new}} = \frac{15}{10} \\
W_{new} &= 1.5 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Percentage Reduction in Lead Time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \Delta W &= \frac{W_{old} - W_{new}}{W_{old}} \times 100\% = \frac{4 - 1.5}{4} \times 100\% \\
\% \Delta W &= 62.5\% \text{ reduction in Lead Time}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Iceberg Model Interpretation (4 marks for this block).**

| Layer | Interpretation of the WIP Reduction |
|---|---|
| **Event** (Layer 1) | The Kaizen team physically removed 25 units of WIP from the cell. |
| **Pattern** (Layer 2) | Over the previous 6 months, WIP had been creeping up due to micro-stoppages. |
| **Structure** (Layer 3) | The cell was operating on a **push-based batch system** with no visual control; queues naturally built up. |
| **Mental Model** (Layer 4) | Management believed *"high WIP = high machine utilization = good performance"*. The 62.5% lead-time drop **disproved this belief** and is the moment of true organizational learning. |

> **Conclusion (for 7/7 marks):** The numerical exercise, properly interpreted through the Iceberg Model, demonstrates that **WIP is a structural symptom of an underlying mental model**, and that visible performance gains (62.5% lead-time reduction) are the *evidence* needed to transform that mental model — the deepest level of change.

---

### **Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice)**

> **(a)** With the aid of a Causal Loop Diagram, explain how the **virtuous cycle of Standard Work** can be transformed into a **vicious cycle of fire-fighting**. Identify the **balancing feedback** that management should introduce to break the vicious cycle. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A workstation produces **20 units/hour** in batches of **80 units**. Setup time is **45 minutes**. Calculate (i) the cycle time, (ii) the average inventory in the system, and (iii) the lead time. If a **SMED project** reduces the setup time to **10 minutes**, recalculate the three metrics and comment on the systemic impact. Demand rate is **15 units/hour**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline for (a) and (b):**

**Part (a) — Virtuous → Vicious Cycle Mapping (7 marks):**

- **Virtuous cycle (R loop, +):** Standard Work → Predictable Quality → Less Rework → Free Capacity → Time for Kaizen → Improved Standard Work.
- **Vicious cycle (R loop, +):** No Standard Work → Inconsistent Quality → Rework → Operator Fatigue → No Kaizen Time → Resistance to Standard Work.
- **The required balancing feedback (B loop, –):** A **visible performance dashboard** (e.g., OEE, First-Pass Yield) that *slows down or escalates* the moment rework hours exceed a threshold. This negative loop counters the vicious reinforcing loop.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Cycle time.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle,old} &= T_{setup} + Q \times T_{processing} \\
T_{cycle,old} &= 45 \text{ min} + 80 \times 3 \text{ min} = 45 + 240 = 285 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Demand drained per cycle.**
$$
D_{cycle} = \frac{15}{60} \times 285 = 71.25 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Average inventory.**
$$
I_{avg,old} = \frac{Q - D_{cycle}}{2} = \frac{80 - 71.25}{2} = 4.375 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4: Lead time (Little's Law with $\lambda = 15$ u/hr).**
$$
W_{old} = \frac{4.375}{15} \approx 0.29 \text{ hours} = 17.5 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5: SMED impact ($T_{setup,new} = 10$ min).**
$$
T_{cycle,new} = 10 + 240 = 250 \text{ min}
$$
$$
D_{cycle,new} = \frac{15}{60} \times 250 = 62.5 \text{ units}
$$
$$
I_{avg,new} = \frac{80 - 62.5}{2} = 8.75 \text{ units (transient) or smaller steady state}
$$
A 78% reduction in setup time yields a proportional reduction in non-value-added wait. The **systemic impact** is a *non-linear* improvement in responsiveness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write:**
> 1. **Confusing "Throughput" with "Lead Time":** Throughput is *units per time*; Lead Time is *time per unit*. Marks are lost when these are swapped in Little's Law.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Demand of 15 units/hour must be converted to units/minute (÷60) before multiplying with cycle time in minutes.
> 3. **Treating the Iceberg Model as a list, not a causal chain:** Examiners award marks only for showing *how* Layer 4 (mental model) drives Layer 3 (structure), which produces Layer 2 (pattern), which manifests as Layer 1 (event).
> 4. **Omitting the "balancing loop" answer:** When asked about a vicious cycle, students describe the problem but fail to prescribe a *balancing feedback mechanism* — a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Writing "Systems Thinking = seeing the whole"** without giving a *manufacturing-specific* example. Always anchor your answer in a factory context (value stream, kanban, takt time, etc.).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Systems Thinking in Lean**

- **Core Definition:** Systems thinking is a holistic discipline that views the *value stream* as an interconnected whole whose behavior emerges from feedback loops.
- **Peter Senge:** Systems thinking is the **"Fifth Discipline"** that integrates Personal Mastery, Mental Models, Shared Vision, and Team Learning.
- **The Iceberg Model:** 4 layers — *Events → Patterns → Structures → Mental Models*. Lean solutions must reach the mental-model layer to be sustainable.
- **Two Types of Feedback Loops:**
  - *Balancing (negative):* Self-correcting; targets equilibrium. Example: **Kanban**, **Heijunka**.
  - *Reinforcing (positive):* Amplifies direction; produces growth or collapse. Example: *Virtuous cycle of Standard Work* and *Vicious cycle of fire-fighting*.
- **Hard vs. Soft Systems:** Hard = engineering optimization; Soft = culture, people, change. A successful Lean transformation is **20% hard, 80% soft**.
- **Reductionism vs. Holism:** Reductionism optimizes parts → sub-optimizes the whole. Holism optimizes the whole → may leave local slack (a feature, not a bug, in a non-bottleneck).
- **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$. A foundational, *invariant* law of all flow systems. Use it to prove that WIP reduction collapses lead time even at constant throughput.
- **Takt Time:** $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D$. The *heartbeat* of a Lean system; sets the pace of every station.
- **System Efficiency:** $\eta = (\sum t_p / \text{Lead Time}) \times 100\%$. Typical factory efficiency is **<10%**, exposing massive systemic waste.
- **Causal Loop Diagrams (CLD):** Mandatory drawing skill. Always label loops as **R (reinforcing)** or **B (balancing)** with a polarity sign.
- **SMED as a Leverage Point:** A small local change (setup time) creates a non-linear system-wide impact (inventory, lead time, flexibility).
- **Sustainment Rule:** Lean tools without soft-systems thinking (culture, vision, mental-model change) collapse in 6–12 months.
- **Five-Discipline Mnemonic:** "**P**eople **M**ake **S**ustainable **T**ransformation" → **P**ersonal, **M**ental, **S**hared, **T**eam, **S**ystems.
- **Stock-and-Flow Reminder:** *Stock* (WIP, lead time) can only change via *Flow* (throughput). Inflows exceed outflows → stock grows; outflows exceed inflows → stock depletes.
- **Most Testable Concepts (Rank Order):** Iceberg Model → Feedback Loops → Little's Law → Senge's Disciplines → Hard vs. Soft → SMED impact on flow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles of Lean Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles of Lean Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Lean Production** (also called **Lean Manufacturing** or simply **Lean**) is a systematic, socio-technical methodology for the elimination of waste (*Muda*) and the maximization of value-added activities across an entire value stream, with the ultimate goal of providing perfect value to the end customer through a perfectly designed value stream that flows continuously on the pull of the customer.

The term was coined by **John Krafcik** in his 1988 MIT thesis, *"Triumph of the Lean Production System,"* and was later popularized in the landmark book *"The Machine That Changed the World"* (1990) by **James P. Womack, Daniel T. Jones, and Daniel Roos**, based on the famous **International Motor Vehicle (IMVP)** study at MIT.

Lean is rooted in the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, developed by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota Motor Corporation between 1948 and 1975, after the post-WWII Japanese economic crisis compelled engineers to do more with less.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** "Lean Production is a manufacturing philosophy that focuses on the systematic elimination of waste (Muda) and the creation of continuous flow to deliver maximum customer value with minimum resources."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are preparing a packed lunch for your friend in a small, cramped kitchen.
- A **non-lean** cook chops vegetables, then walks to the fridge to get milk, then returns to the stove, then looks for salt in three cupboards, then boils water, then forgets to add spices, and finally throws away half the ingredients.
- A **lean** cook first gathers *everything* (vegetables, spices, milk, water) in a small tray, chops in one motion, places spices within arm's reach, pours water immediately, and creates the perfect lunch in **half the time, half the space, and zero waste**.

Lean is the kitchen of engineering: **everything needed is at hand, in the right order, at the right time, in the right quantity**.

### 1.3 Core Lean Terminology (Must-Know Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Japanese Terms (Direct Exam Questions)**

| Japanese Term | Meaning | English Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Muda** | Wastefulness, futility | **Waste** |
| **Mura** | Unevenness, irregularity | **Unevenness / Variability** |
| **Muri** | Unreasonableness, overburden | **Overburden / Unreasonable Strain** |
| **Gemba** | The actual place | **The Shop Floor / Where Work Happens** |
| **Kaizen** | Continuous improvement | **Continuous Improvement** |
| **Poka-Yoke** | Fool-proofing | **Mistake Proofing** |
| **Heijunka** | Leveling | **Production Leveling** |
| **Kanban** | Sign/card | **Pull Signal Card** |
| **Jidoka** | Autonomation | **Autonomation (Automation with Human Touch)** |
| **Andon** | Paper lantern | **Visual Signal / Warning Light** |

### 1.4 The DNA of Lean — A Quick Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Lean is built on **5 foundational principles** (Womack & Jones, 1996):
> 1. **Value** — Define value from the customer's perspective.
> 2. **Value Stream** — Map the end-to-end flow that creates value.
> 3. **Flow** — Make value-creating steps flow without interruption.
> 4. **Pull** — Let the customer pull value, not push inventory.
> 5. **Perfection** — Pursue continuous, relentless improvement.

These five principles form the philosophical backbone of every KTU exam answer on Lean Production.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origins & Evolution of Lean Thinking

The history of lean is best understood as a **four-stage evolution**:

1. **Craft Production** (Pre-1900s): Skilled artisans, low volume, high cost, bespoke products.
2. **Mass Production** (1900s–1970s, Ford Model T): Standardization, assembly line, economies of scale, but with high inventory, low variety, and worker alienation.
3. **Toyota Production System** (1948–1975, Ohno): Japanese response to a resource-poor environment; born from the merging of two ideas — Henry Ford's flow concept and **Sakichi Toyoda's** automatic loom (Jidoka).
4. **Lean Production** (1990–present, Womack & Jones): Generic, globally applicable term for TPS principles; applicable beyond automotive (healthcare, services, software, education).

### 2.2 The Three Pillars of the Toyota Production System (TPS House)

Toyota conceptualized its system as a **"House of TPS"**, which KTU examiners frequently ask students to draw and label:

| Pillar | Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Just-In-Time (JIT)** | Producing only what is needed, only when needed, in only the amount needed | Minimizes WIP and finished goods inventory |
| **Jidoka (Autonomation)** | Stopping the line when a defect occurs, then solving the root cause | Builds quality in, prevents defect propagation |
| **Heijunka (Leveling)** | Leveling production by volume and mix over a fixed period | Prevents mura (unevenness) and overburden |

These pillars rest on the **foundation of Kaizen (continuous improvement)** and **standardized work**, and are **roofed by customer satisfaction / quality at the lowest cost with shortest lead time**.

### 2.3 The 7 (+1) Wastes of Lean — TIMWOODS + Skills

Waste in lean is any activity that consumes resources but creates **no value** for the customer. **Shigeo Shingo** (Toyota engineer) originally identified **7 wastes**, and a **8th waste** was added later.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the Acronym: TIMWOODS (or DOWNTIME)**

| # | Waste | Japanese | Definition | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **T**ransportation | Kido | Unnecessary movement of materials/products | Goods moved 5 times across 4 buildings |
| 2 | **I**nventory | Zaiko | Excess raw material, WIP, or finished goods | Piles of unsold stock in warehouse |
| 3 | **M**otion | Undo | Unnecessary movement of *people* | Worker bending/walking to reach tools |
| 4 | **W**aiting | Machi | Idle time waiting for info/materials/machines | Machine idle, worker idle, truck waiting |
| 5 | **O**verproduction | Seisan | Producing more than customer demand or earlier | Producing 1000 when only 800 are sold |
| 6 | **O**ver-processing | Tare | Doing more work than the customer values | Polishing a surface that will be painted |
| 7 | **D**efects | Furyo | Rework, scrap, warranty claims | Faulty products returned by customer |
| 8 | **S**kills (unused talent) | — | Failing to use people's mental/creative abilities | Engineers doing clerical work |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Worst Waste is OVERPRODUCTION** (Ohno): "Overproduction is the root of all other wastes." It hides problems, ties up capital, and forces all other wastes to exist.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Lean Metrics for Numerical Questions**

| Formula | Symbol | Standard Formula | Units | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Takt Time** | $T_t$ | $T_t = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | seconds/unit | Sets the pace of production to match demand |
| **Lead Time** | $L_t$ | $L_t = \text{Queue Time} + \text{Process Time} + \text{Wait Time} + \text{Transport Time}$ | minutes/hours/days | Total time from order to delivery |
| **Cycle Time** | $C_t$ | $C_t = \dfrac{\text{Net Available Time}}{\text{Number of Units Produced}}$ | seconds/unit | Time between successive units coming out |
| **Value-Added Ratio** | $VAR$ | $VAR = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time (VA)}}{\text{Total Lead Time (TLT)}}$ | % (0–100%) | Measures proportion of value-creating work |
| **Process Velocity** | $PV$ | $PV = \dfrac{1}{L_t} \quad \text{or} \quad PV = \dfrac{VA}{TLT}$ | units/time | Speed of value flow |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY$ | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Started}}$ | % | Quality at first attempt |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY$ | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | % | Cumulative quality across *n* processes |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | $OEE$ | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % (0–100%) | World-class = 85% |
| **Availability** | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | % | Measures downtime |
| **Performance** | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | % | Measures speed loss |
| **Quality** | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | % | Measures defect rate |
| **Value Stream Efficiency** | $VSE$ | $VSE = \dfrac{\sum VA}{\sum \text{NVA} + \sum VA} \times 100$ | % | Best-in-class industry target: ≥ 25% |
| **Inventory Turns** | $IT$ | $IT = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | times/year | Inventory health metric |
| **Throughput** | $TH$ | $TH = \dfrac{\text{Output Quantity}}{\text{Time Period}}$ | units/hour, units/day | Production rate |
| **Little's Law** | $WIP$ | $WIP = TH \times L_t$ | units | Fundamental flow law |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Lean Metrics

- **Takt Time** is the heartbeat of a lean line — every workstation's cycle time should *match* takt time, not exceed it.
- **OEE** is the most diagnostic metric in TPM (Total Productive Maintenance); even a 5% OEE jump can free 20% capacity.
- **Little's Law** ($WIP = TH \times L_t$) is the most powerful mental model: cutting WIP by half literally cuts lead time in half.
- **VAR** in service industries (e.g., a hospital patient journey) reveals that typically < 10% of the patient's time is value-added.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 1 — Takt Time & Cycle Time

> **Problem:** A factory operates **8 hours/day**, with a **60-minute lunch break** and **two 10-minute tea breaks**. The **daily customer demand is 200 units**.
> Find: (a) Takt Time, (b) If a workstation's Cycle Time is 145 seconds, is it a *bottleneck*? (c) Suggest a lean solution.

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert available time into a single unit (seconds):**

We have a known relation:
$$
T_{\text{available}} = 8 \text{ hrs} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr} = 28{,}800 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract all non-productive time:**

$$
T_{\text{lunch}} = 60 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 3{,}600 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{tea}} = 2 \times 10 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 1{,}200 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total break}} = 3{,}600 + 1{,}200 = 4{,}800 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Net Available Production Time:**

$$
T_{\text{net}} = 28{,}800 - 4{,}800 = 24{,}000 \text{ s/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Takt Time formula:**

$$
T_t = \dfrac{T_{\text{net}}}{D} = \dfrac{24{,}000 \text{ s}}{200 \text{ units}} = 120 \text{ s/unit}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_t = 120 \text{ seconds/unit}}
$$

**Step 5 — Bottleneck analysis:**

The station's Cycle Time ($C_t = 145$ s) **exceeds** Takt Time ($T_t = 120$ s). This means the station **cannot keep up with customer pace**, producing 1 unit every 145 s when 1 is needed every 120 s. Hence, it is a **bottleneck** and will cause **WIP pile-up and overburden (Muri)**.

**Step 6 — Lean solution:**

Lean tools to relieve this bottleneck:
- **Kaizen (Quick improvement):** reduce changeover time via **SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die)**.
- **Heijunka:** redistribute work to underutilized stations.
- **Yamazumi:** balance the line by recording each station's tasks.
- **Work standardization:** eliminate motion waste.
- If still unresolved: invest in additional capacity.

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 2 — OEE Calculation

> **Problem:** A CNC machine has the following data:
> - Planned Production Time = 480 min/shift
> - Downtime = 60 min
> - Ideal Cycle Time = 0.5 min/unit
> - Total Pieces Produced = 800
> - Defective Pieces = 40
> Find: (a) Availability, (b) Performance, (c) Quality, (d) OEE

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Operating Time:**

$$
T_{\text{oper}} = T_{\text{planned}} - T_{\text{down}} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Availability:**

$$
A = \dfrac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Performance:**

Theoretical maximum units in 420 min:

$$
N_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{oper}}}{C_{t,\text{ideal}}} = \dfrac{420}{0.5} = 840 \text{ units}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{N_{\text{actual}} \times C_{t,\text{ideal}}}{T_{\text{oper}}} = \dfrac{800 \times 0.5}{420} = \dfrac{400}{420} = 0.9524 = 95.24\%
$$

**Step 4 — Quality:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} = \dfrac{800 - 40}{800} = \dfrac{760}{800} = 0.95 = 95\%
$$

**Step 5 — OEE:**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.875 \times 0.9524 \times 0.95
$$

$$
OEE = 0.7917 = 79.17\%
$$

$$
\boxed{OEE \approx 79.17\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** World-class OEE = **85%**; Typical Indian SME = 50–65%. This factory is in the **"good but not world-class"** zone — focus on eliminating downtime (Availability) for biggest gains.

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 3 — Little's Law Application

> **Problem:** A hospital's emergency department processes 30 patients/day. Average patient Lead Time (door-to-discharge) = 6 hours. Find: (a) WIP, (b) If process is improved to reduce Lead Time to 3 hours while demand stays 30/day, what is the new WIP?

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert Throughput to per-hour:**

$$
TH = \dfrac{30 \text{ patients}}{24 \text{ hrs}} = 1.25 \text{ patients/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Little's Law:**

$$
WIP = TH \times L_t = 1.25 \times 6 = 7.5 \text{ patients}
$$

$$
\boxed{WIP_{\text{current}} \approx 8 \text{ patients in the system at any time}}
$$

**Step 3 — After improvement:**

$$
L_t' = 3 \text{ hrs}, \quad WIP' = 1.25 \times 3 = 3.75 \approx 4 \text{ patients}
$$

**Inference:** Lead time reduced by 50% ⇒ WIP reduced by 50%, **without adding any staff or equipment**. This is the power of lean — the same throughput, half the inventory, half the waiting.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Lean vs Mass vs Craft Production

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highly Exam-Relevant Comparison Table (Frequently asked for 7 or 14 marks)**

| Parameter | Craft Production | Mass Production | Lean Production |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin Era** | Pre-1900s | 1900s (Ford) | 1950s (Toyota) / 1990s (named) |
| **Workforce** | Highly skilled artisans | Semi-skilled labor | Multi-skilled, empowered teams |
| **Volume** | Low, custom | High, standardized | High, customized |
| **Lead Time** | Very high | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| **Inventory** | Low | Very high | **Lowest** |
| **Variety** | Infinite | Very low | **High** |
| **Wastage (Muda)** | Low (per unit) | High | **Lowest (systematic)** |
| **Focus** | Quality by master | Cost by scale | **Value by flow** |
| **Information Flow** | Tribal / oral | Top-down | **Two-way, transparent** |
| **Production Logic** | Job shop, make-to-order | Forecast-driven (push) | **Demand-driven (pull)** |
| **Defect Handling** | Rework by master | Inspection/sorting | **Built-in (Poka-Yoke)** |
| **Supplier Role** | Subcontractor | Adversarial | **Partnership / Family** |
| **Capital Required** | Low | Very high | **Lowest for same output** |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | **Highest** |
| **Worker Empowerment** | None (master decides) | None (line dictates) | **Maximum (Kaizen teams)** |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

> **Define Lean Production. Explain its origin in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

Lean Production is a manufacturing philosophy that aims to eliminate waste (Muda) and create continuous value flow by providing exactly what the customer wants, when they want it, in the right quantity. It is derived from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, developed in post-WWII Japan by **Taiichi Ohno** (1948–1975) under severe resource constraints. The term "Lean" was coined in 1988 by **John Krafcik** and popularized by **Womack, Jones, and Roos** in their 1990 MIT study *"The Machine That Changed the World,"* comparing global automotive assembly plants and finding Toyota's system to be the most efficient — combining the **flexibility of craft production** with the **low cost of mass production**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "Lean = JIT" — this is **wrong**. JIT is *one pillar* of Lean. Lean is a *broader system*. Marks deducted if JIT is given as the full definition.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

> **List and briefly explain any four types of waste (Muda) in Lean Production.**

**Model Answer:**

The 7 (+1) wastes of Lean are: **Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, and Unused Skills** (TIMWOODS).

1. **Defects:** Production of faulty products requiring rework or scrap — wastes material, labor, and time.
2. **Overproduction:** Producing more units or earlier than the customer demands — the *root* of all other wastes.
3. **Waiting:** Idle time of workers, machines, or information flow — adds no value.
4. **Motion:** Unnecessary movement of people (bending, walking, reaching) that does not add value.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Motion** (people movement) with **Transportation** (material movement). Examiners specifically test this distinction. Loss of 1 mark for confusion.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Each Part B question must follow the KTU ESE pattern: Choice-based, with two sub-parts (7 + 7).*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **5 Principles of Lean Production** as given by Womack and Jones, with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between **Mass Production and Lean Production** in 10 points. Discuss the **role of Ohno's "Three MU concepts"** (Muda, Mura, Muri) in lean transformation. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

The 5 Principles of Lean (Womack & Jones, *"Lean Thinking,"* 1996):

1. **Value:** Value is defined *only* by the end-customer — what they are willing to pay for. Producers must reorient all decisions around this.
   *Example:* A customer buying a mobile phone values camera, battery, and processor speed — not a free carrying case.

2. **Value Stream:** Identify the entire set of activities (value-adding + non-value-adding) required to deliver the product, from raw material to customer. Use **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** to expose waste.
   *Example:* In a car assembly plant, the value stream spans from steel mills → stamping → welding → painting → assembly → dealer.

3. **Flow:** After eliminating waste, arrange the remaining value-creating steps to occur in a *continuous, uninterrupted* flow (not in batches).
   *Example:* Re-layout of a sewing cell so that cut → stitch → finish → pack happen in sequence, not in separated departments.

4. **Pull:** Let the customer *pull* the product — produce only when the downstream operation signals demand (Kanban). This avoids overproduction.
   *Example:* A super-market shelf is restocked only when a customer removes an item (signal), not when a delivery truck decides to dump stock.

5. **Perfection:** As waste is removed and flow improves, repeat the cycle relentlessly. The target is **perfection (zero muda, zero defects, zero lead time)**.
   *Example:* Toyota's continuous Kaizen program, where every worker submits improvement ideas daily.

### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Differences (Mass vs Lean):**

| S.No. | Mass Production | Lean Production |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | High inventory buffers | Minimal inventory |
| 2 | Push system (forecast-driven) | Pull system (demand-driven) |
| 3 | Specialized, low-skilled labor | Multi-skilled, empowered teams |
| 4 | Functional departments | Product/flow cells |
| 5 | Long lead time | Short lead time |
| 6 | Centralized decision making | Decentralized |
| 7 | Inspection-based quality | Built-in quality (Jidoka) |
| 8 | Variability hidden by inventory | Variability exposed and attacked |
| 9 | Adversarial supplier relations | Long-term partnership |
| 10 | High capital intensity | Low capital intensity |

**Ohno's Three MU Concepts:**

- **Muda (Waste):** Any non-value-adding activity. 7+1 categories (TIMWOODS). Eliminating muda reduces cost and time.
- **Mura (Unevenness):** Variability in demand, supply, or production. Heijunka (leveling) tackles mura. Example: a 5-day production plan is leveled so Monday does not get a 500-unit demand spike.
- **Muri (Overburden):** Unrealistic demand placed on people or machines (e.g., asking a 90s station to do 60s work). Muri causes breakdowns, defects, and injuries. Eliminated by takt-time-based line balancing.

These three are deeply interconnected: **Muri causes Mura causes Muda.** Lean attacks all three simultaneously.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **House of Toyota Production System (TPS)**. Discuss the role of **Kaizen** and **Standardized Work** as the foundation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A garment unit has **9 working hours/day**, **demand = 450 shirts/day**, and the line has **2 breakdowns of 15 min each**. The line has **5 stations** with the following cycle times (in seconds): Station 1 = 65, Station 2 = 70, Station 3 = 50, Station 4 = 68, Station 5 = 75. Find **(i) Takt Time, (ii) Bottleneck station, (iii) Line efficiency.** **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**TPS House Diagram:**

(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram of the house.)

**Description:**

- **Roof — Customer Satisfaction** (Best Quality, Lowest Cost, Shortest Lead Time, High Morale).
- **Right Pillar — Just-In-Time (JIT):** Producing only what is needed, only when needed, in only the quantity needed. Enforced via *Kanban* and *Heijunka*.
- **Left Pillar — Jidoka (Autonomation):** "Automation with a human touch" — embedding intelligence into machines to stop when a defect occurs. Operators are empowered to halt the line (*Andon*).
- **Foundation — Standardized Work + Kaizen:** The bedrock. Standardized work establishes the *current best-known method*. Kaizen (continuous improvement) refines that standard daily. Without standardization, improvement is impossible; without Kaizen, standardization leads to stagnation.

**Role of Kaizen:** Continuous, incremental, employee-driven improvement. Cultural philosophy where every worker is a problem-solver. Tools: PDCA cycle, Gemba walks, suggestion systems, 5 Whys.

**Role of Standardized Work:** Defines the *best current method* for each task (takt time, sequence, standard in-process stock). It is the baseline for *any* improvement (Kaizen).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "Kaizen is a tool" — it is a **philosophy/culture**, not a tool. Marks deducted for misclassification.

### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Net Available Time:**

$$
T_{\text{net}} = (9 \text{ hrs} \times 60 \text{ min}) - (2 \times 15) = 540 - 30 = 510 \text{ min/day}
$$

$$
T_{\text{net}} = 510 \times 60 = 30{,}600 \text{ s/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Takt Time:**

$$
T_t = \dfrac{30{,}600}{450} = 68 \text{ s/shirt}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_t = 68 \text{ s/shirt}}
$$

**Step 3 — Bottleneck:**

The **longest cycle time** is at **Station 5 (75 s)**. This exceeds Takt Time (68 s) ⇒ **Station 5 is the bottleneck**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Bottleneck} = \text{Station 5}, \; C_t = 75 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Line Efficiency:**

The line's effective cycle time is dictated by the bottleneck:

$$
C_{\text{line}} = 75 \text{ s/unit}
$$

For 450 shirts at bottleneck rate, theoretical time required:

$$
T_{\text{required}} = 450 \times 75 = 33{,}750 \text{ s} = 562.5 \text{ min}
$$

But only 510 min is available. Hence the line is **overloaded**, and current efficiency in meeting demand:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{takt required}}}{T_{\text{net}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{450 \times 68}{30{,}600} \times 100 = \dfrac{30{,}600}{30{,}600} = 100\% \text{ (ideal baseline)}
$$

The actual line efficiency formula (alternative method):

$$
\eta_{\text{balance}} = \dfrac{\sum C_t}{n \times C_{\max}} \times 100
$$

where $n$ = number of stations = 5, $C_{\max} = 75$ s.

$$
\eta_{\text{balance}} = \dfrac{65 + 70 + 50 + 68 + 75}{5 \times 75} \times 100
$$

$$
= \dfrac{328}{375} \times 100 = 87.47\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{\text{line balance}} \approx 87.47\%}
$$

**Lean Recommendations:**
- Apply **Kaizen** at Station 5 to bring cycle time ≤ 68 s.
- Redistribute some work from Station 5 to Station 3 (only 50 s — has slack).
- Apply **SMED** to reduce setup time.
- After Kaizen, expect line efficiency to approach 100%.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Consolidated)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mark-Deduction Triggers — Lean Production Module 1**
>
> 1. **Confusing JIT with Lean:** JIT is a *subset* of Lean, not its synonym.
> 2. **Misnaming "Lean" as a Japanese invention:** Lean is the *Western name* for TPS; the system itself is Japanese (TPS).
> 3. **Forgetting the 8th waste:** Many textbooks mention only 7. Always write 7+1.
> 4. **Confusing Motion vs Transportation:** Motion = *people*; Transportation = *materials*. Exam trap.
> 5. **Writing Ohno's "Muda" as the only "MU":** Mura and Muri are equally important.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in TPS House question:** At least 2 marks reserved for diagram in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 7. **Confusing Takt Time with Cycle Time:** Takt = *demand-driven*; Cycle = *machine capability*. Common 14-mark trap.
> 8. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always write "s/unit," "min," "%," etc.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Basic Principles of Lean Production)**

- [x] **Definition:** Lean Production = systematic elimination of waste + creation of value flow.
- [x] **Origin:** Toyota Production System (TPS), 1948–1975, Taiichi Ohno, Toyota Motor Corporation.
- [x] **Lean coined by:** John Krafcik (1988); popularized by Womack, Jones & Roos (1990).
- [x] **5 Principles:** Value → Value Stream → Flow → Pull → Perfection (Womack & Jones, 1996).
- [x] **8 Wastes (TIMWOODS):** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects, (unused) Skills.
- [x] **Worst waste:** Overproduction — root of all other wastes.
- [x] **3 MUs (Ohno):** Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness), Muri (overburden). All must be tackled.
- [x] **TPS House:** Roof = Customer satisfaction; Pillars = JIT + Jidoka; Foundation = Kaizen + Standardized Work.
- [x] **JIT:** Right item, right time, right quantity — enabled by Kanban + Heijunka.
- [x] **Jidoka:** Autonomation — automation with intelligence to stop on defect.
- [x] **Heijunka:** Production leveling — kills Mura.
- [x] **Kaizen:** Continuous improvement (philosophy, not a tool).
- [x] **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proofing devices — prevent defects at source.
- [x] **Andon:** Visual signal system to call for help when abnormality occurs.
- [x] **Takt Time** = Available Time / Demand (set the production pace).
- [x] **Cycle Time** = Net Time / Units Produced (capability of a station).
