- [x] **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality; World-class ≥ 85%.
- [x] **Little's Law:** $WIP = TH \times L_t$ — cutting WIP halves lead time at constant throughput.
- [x] **Lean vs Mass:** Lean = low inventory, high variety, empowered workers, pull, flow. Mass = high inventory, low variety, divided labor, push.
- [x] **Lean = Mass + Craft:** Combines mass's low cost with craft's high variety and quality.

> **One-line takeaway for the exam hall:** *"Lean is not a tool — it is a way of thinking that systematically removes waste so that the customer's value flows uninterruptedly, pulled by their demand, and continuously perfected by every employee, every day."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Focus in Lean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Customer Focus in Lean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Customer Focus in Lean Manufacturing** is defined as the strategic and operational alignment of all organizational processes, resources, and decision-making frameworks toward the systematic identification, creation, and delivery of **value** as perceived and defined by the **end customer**. 

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, customer focus is treated as the foundational pillar of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and the broader Lean philosophy, where every activity in the value stream must be evaluated against a single criterion: *"Does this activity create value from the customer's perspective?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly identifies *Customer Focus* as the **first principle** of Lean (often the 5th principle of Lean Thinking after Value, Value Stream, Flow, and Pull, but logically the *starting* point because Value is defined by the customer).

The key constructs that operationalize customer focus include:
- **Value (V):** A capability provided to a customer at the right time at an appropriate price, as defined by the customer.
- **Voice of the Customer (VOC):** The structured process of capturing customer requirements, expectations, preferences, and dissatisfaction.
- **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ):** The translation of VOC statements into measurable, engineerable specifications.
- **Value-Added (VA) vs. Non-Value-Added (NVA) Activities:** Classification of work based on whether the customer is willing to pay for it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a small, family-run South Indian restaurant in Kerala called *"Appu's Dosa Kada."* 

In a traditional restaurant, the chef decides what to cook based on inventory — yesterday's leftover rice, vegetables about to spoil, and what is easy to prepare. The customer is forced to choose from this restricted menu. This is the **"push" model** of mass production: produce first, hope someone buys it.

Now contrast this with a **"Dosa-on-Demand"** stall at a wedding. The cook only prepares a dosa **after** the guest places an order (Pull system). The batter is freshly ground to match the guest's preference (Value defined by customer). The cook asks: *"Sir, masala or plain? Ghee or oil?"* — this is **Voice of the Customer (VOC)**. The ingredients are measured precisely — not too much, not too little (elimination of waste / **Muda**). The customer waits only 2 minutes (reduced lead time), gets a hot, customized product, and pays happily.

**Customer Focus in Lean works exactly like this dosa stall:** You don't produce blindly. You listen to the customer, translate their needs into precise specifications, design your process to deliver exactly that, and continuously improve based on their feedback. Anything the customer is **not willing to pay for** is waste, no matter how hard your team worked on it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight for Students:** Lean does not mean asking the customer "what do you want?" once. It is a *systematic, continuous, data-driven dialogue* with the customer at every stage of the value stream.

## 1.3 Standard Lean Metrics (Must Remember)

- **Takt Time** = $\dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand Rate}}$ — measured in **seconds/unit** or **minutes/unit**.
- **Lead Time (LT)** = Processing Time + Queue Time + Wait Time + Transport Time.
- **Value-Added Ratio** = $\dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}} \times 100\%$ — typically **< 5%** in un-lean systems.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** = $\dfrac{\text{Good Units Produced}}{\text{Total Units Started}} \times 100\%$.
- **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** typically ranges from **1 to 5** or **1 to 10** on a Likert scale.

All these metrics are **bolded as core KTU-defining constants** that examiners expect students to memorize and apply.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Customer Focus in Lean

Customer Focus in Lean is operationalized through **five interconnected pillars**. Each pillar answers a specific question in the value-creation process.

### Pillar 1: Identification of the "Right" Customer
- **Why:** Not all customers are the same. Lean demands segmentation.
- **How:** Use **SIPOC** (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers) and **Stakeholder Analysis**.
- **Output:** A clear definition of *who* the customer is — internal (next process) vs. external (end user).

### Pillar 2: Capturing the Voice of the Customer (VOC)
- **Why:** Without structured listening, "value" becomes a guess.
- **How:** Deploy multiple channels:
  * **Surveys** (quantitative Likert scale ratings)
  * **Interviews & Focus Groups** (qualitative depth)
  * **Complaint & Warranty Data Analysis**
  * **Field Observations / Gemba Walks at customer sites**
  * **Social Media & Review Mining**
- **Output:** A database of customer needs in their *own words* — called "**Customer Verbatim**" or "**VOC Statements**."

### Pillar 3: Translation of VOC into CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)
- **Why:** Customers say *"I want a fast laptop"*; engineers need *"Boot time < 12 seconds."*
- **How:** Build a **CTQ Tree** that branches from the customer need (Kano Model) → measurable specification (CTQ) → tolerance limits.
- **Output:** Engineerable specifications that the production system can deliver consistently.

### Pillar 4: Value Stream Mapping (VSM) Aligned to CTQ
- **Why:** You cannot improve what you cannot see.
- **How:** Map the current state → identify VA, NVA, and **Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA)** activities → design future state.
- **Output:** A blueprint of where waste exists relative to customer value.

### Pillar 5: Continuous Customer Feedback Loop (PDCA)
- **Why:** Customer expectations evolve; static systems become obsolete.
- **How:** Apply the **Deming Cycle (PDCA)** — Plan a customer-driven improvement, Do it on a small scale, Check the impact on CTQs, Act to standardize.
- **Output:** An evolving, self-correcting customer-centric system.

## 2.2 The Kano Model — Classifying Customer Needs

Professor Noriaki Kano (1984) classified customer requirements into three categories. KTU examiners **frequently test** this:

| Kano Category | Description | Customer Reaction if Present | Customer Reaction if Absent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Must-Be (Basic)** | Expected, taken for granted | Neutral (satisfied) | Extremely Dissatisfied |
| **One-Dimensional (Performance)** | More is better — linear satisfaction | More = More Satisfaction | Less = Dissatisfaction |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | Unexpected delighters | Highly Satisfied | Neutral (not noticed) |
| (Modern extension) **Indifferent** | Customer does not care | No effect | No effect |
| (Modern extension) **Reverse** | Some customers dislike it | Dissatisfied for some | Satisfied for some |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Takt Time (TT)** | $TT = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}$ | seconds/unit, where $T_{available}$ = available production time per period, $D$ = customer demand per period |
| 2 | **Lead Time (LT)** | $LT = T_{process} + T_{queue} + T_{wait} + T_{transport}$ | time units (seconds, minutes, hours, days) |
| 3 | **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $VAR = \dfrac{T_{VA}}{LT} \times 100\%$ | percentage; ideal: $\rightarrow 100\%$, typical un-lean: $< 5\%$ |
| 4 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percentage; $N_{good}$ = defect-free units |
| 5 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | product of stage yields; reveals hidden factory waste |
| 6 | **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** | $CSAT = \dfrac{\sum (\text{rating scores})}{N_{responses} \times \text{highest rating}} \times 100\%$ | percentage |
| 7 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | where $\%P$ = Promoters (9–10), $\%D$ = Detractors (0–6) |
| 8 | **Customer Effort Score (CES)** | $CES = \text{Average rating on 1-7 effort scale}$ | lower is better; KTU exam favorite |
| 9 | **Process Cycle Efficiency (PCE)** | $PCE = \dfrac{T_{VA}}{T_{VA} + T_{NVA}} \times 100\%$ | mirrors VAR; used interchangeably |
| 10 | **Demand Variation (σ)** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | standard deviation of customer demand |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When a question asks for Takt Time, **always state the assumption** — e.g., *"Assuming a single 8-hour shift, 60 minutes per hour, with two 10-minute breaks."* Examiners allocate **1 mark** for correct assumption identification.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors):** Takt time dictates every station's cycle time; pull systems (Kanban) trigger replenishment based on actual customer demand from dealerships.
- **Software (Agile Lean):** User Stories, Sprint Reviews, and Net Promoter Score (NPS) loops mirror VOC → CTQ → PDCA precisely.
- **Healthcare (Lean Hospitals):** Patient = customer; CTQ = "Time to first antibiotic < 60 minutes"; VOC = post-discharge surveys; VAR used to map emergency department value streams.
- **Civil Engineering & Construction:** Customer = building owner; VOC = design briefs; CTQ = load-bearing tolerance, finishing quality; Lean Construction (Last Planner System) applies this rigorously.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Takt Time from Customer Demand

The term *Takt* comes from the German word *Taktzeit*, meaning "rhythm" or "beat" — like a musical conductor setting the orchestra's pace. In Lean, the customer sets the rhythm.

### Step 1: Define the Production Window

Let the total **calendar time** available in one working day be $T_{cal}$. Subtract all non-productive time (breaks, meetings, changeovers, preventive maintenance) to obtain **Available Production Time** $T_{available}$.

$$T_{available} = T_{cal} - T_{breaks} - T_{meetings} - T_{changeovers}$$

### Step 2: Quantify Customer Demand

Let the customer demand for a planning period (say, one day) be $D$ units. This is **not** the production target — it is the rate at which customers are consuming the product (e.g., 240 cars sold per day across dealerships).

### Step 3: Apply the Takt Time Formula

The fundamental definition of Takt Time is the time available per unit of customer demand.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TT &= \frac{T_{available}}{D} \\
&= \frac{\text{seconds available in the day}}{\text{units demanded per day}} \\
&= \frac{\text{seconds}}{\text{unit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Standard Units

For consistency, $T_{available}$ is usually converted to seconds.

$$
T_{available} \text{ in seconds} = T_{available} \text{ in hours} \times 3600
$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

A Kerala-based SME producing packaged coconut oil works **one shift of 8 hours per day**. There is a 30-minute lunch break and two 10-minute tea breaks. Daily customer demand from retail distributors is **180 bottles**.

**Step 5a:** Compute net available time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cal} &= 8 \text{ hours} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ minutes} \\
T_{breaks} &= 30 + 10 + 10 = 50 \text{ minutes} \\
T_{available} &= 480 - 50 = 430 \text{ minutes} \\
T_{available} \text{ in seconds} &= 430 \times 60 = 25{,}800 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5b:** Apply the Takt Time formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
TT &= \frac{T_{available}}{D} \\
&= \frac{25{,}800 \text{ seconds}}{180 \text{ bottles}} \\
&= 143.33 \text{ seconds/bottle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5c:** Interpret the result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every 143.33 seconds, one bottle of coconut oil must be completed by the production line to match customer demand. If the line is faster, overproduction (waste) occurs. If slower, customer demand is unmet (lost sales / backorders).

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Value-Added Ratio (VAR)

Consider a Lean value stream with the following process step data:

| Step | Activity | Time (sec) | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Material Receiving | 600 | NVA |
| 2 | Cutting / Machining | 180 | VA |
| 3 | Waiting for QC | 1,200 | NVA |
| 4 | Quality Inspection | 90 | NNVA |
| 5 | Waiting for Assembly | 900 | NVA |
| 6 | Assembly | 240 | VA |
| 7 | Waiting for Painting | 1,500 | NVA |
| 8 | Painting | 300 | VA |
| 9 | Final Inspection | 60 | NNVA |
| 10 | Storage / Dispatch | 1,800 | NVA |

**Step 1:** Sum the Value-Added (VA) times.

$$
T_{VA} = 180 + 240 + 300 = 720 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the entire Lead Time (LT).

$$
\begin{aligned}
LT &= 600 + 180 + 1{,}200 + 90 + 900 + 240 + 1{,}500 + 60 + 1{,}800 + 300 \\
&\quad + (\text{wait times already included? no — re-sum cleanly}) \\
LT &= 600 + 180 + 1{,}200 + 90 + 900 + 240 + 1{,}500 + 60 + 1{,}800 \\
&= 6{,}570 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the VAR formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAR &= \frac{T_{VA}}{LT} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{720}{6{,}570} \times 100\% \\
&= 10.96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Only **~11%** of the total lead time is value-adding. The remaining **~89%** is pure waste (NVA) or necessary waste (NNVA). Customer focus demands that we *aggressively* attack the 89%.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CTQ Tree Construction

A **CTQ Tree** converts a vague VOC statement into measurable engineering targets. The full transformation has **three layers**:

**Layer 1 — Customer Need (VOC, in customer words):**
> *"I want my online food order to arrive hot and fresh."*

**Layer 2 — Customer Requirement (translated into operational terms):**
> *Minimize time between cooking completion and customer consumption; maintain temperature > 60°C.*

**Layer 3 — CTQ (measurable specification):**
> *Delivery time from kitchen dispatch to customer doorstep $\leq 25$ minutes; food temperature at delivery $\geq 65°C$.*

$$
\text{VOC} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Customer Requirement} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CTQ (measurable)}
$$

The transformation rule is: **Each VOC must be answered by a "How much / How fast / How accurate"** — this is what makes it engineerable.

## 3.4 Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI) — Aggregation Formula

Given $N$ customers rate a product on a scale of 1 to 5, where $n_k$ is the number of customers giving rating $k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CSI &= \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{5} k \cdot n_k}{N \times 5} \times 100\% \\
\text{where } N &= \sum_{k=1}^{5} n_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** 200 customers surveyed: 20 rated 1, 30 rated 2, 50 rated 3, 60 rated 4, 40 rated 5.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum k \cdot n_k &= (1)(20) + (2)(30) + (3)(50) + (4)(60) + (5)(40) \\
&= 20 + 60 + 150 + 240 + 200 = 670 \\
CSI &= \frac{670}{200 \times 5} \times 100\% = \frac{670}{1{,}000} \times 100\% = 67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

A CSI of **67%** indicates significant room for Lean improvement — a 100% score is the Lean ideal, but realistic targets hover around **85–90%** for mature systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Customer Focus Value Engine

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of how customer focus transforms a vague need into a delivered product, with feedback loops intact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Customer End User] --> B[Identify Right Customer Segmentation SIPOC]
    B --> C[Capture Voice of Customer VOC Channels]
    C --> C1[Surveys Likert Scale]
    C --> C2[Interviews and Focus Groups]
    C --> C3[Complaint and Warranty Data]
    C --> C4[Gemba Walk at Customer Site]
    C1 --> D[Translate VOC to CTQ Tree]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Kano Model Classification Must Be One Dimensional Attractive]
    E --> F[Define Measurable CTQ Specifications]
    F --> G[Value Stream Mapping Current State]
    G --> H[Identify VA NVA NNVA Activities]
    H --> I[Design Future State VSM]
    I --> J[Pull Production System Kanban Heijunka]
    J --> K[Deliver Value to Customer]
    K --> L[Customer Feedback Loop NPS CSAT CES]
    L --> C
    L --> M[Continuous Improvement PDCA Kaizen]
    M --> I
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Takt Time Drum-Beat Buffer-Buffer Model

This block diagram represents the **Drum-Buffer-Rope (DBR)** model from the **Theory of Constraints (Goldratt)**, which is tightly integrated with customer focus in Lean.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CustomerDemand[Customer Demand Rate D] --> TaktCalc[Takt Time Calculation]
    ShiftData[Available Time per Shift] --> TaktCalc
    TaktCalc --> Drum[Drum Pace Setter Takt Beat]
    Drum --> Constraint[Constraint Resource Bottleneck]
    Constraint --> Buffer1[Time Buffer Protects Drum]
    Buffer1 --> Assembly[Downstream Assembly Stations]
    Assembly --> Buffer2[Finished Goods Buffer Customer Decoupling]
    Buffer2 --> Shipping[Shipping to Customer]
    Shipping --> Feedback[Actual Sales Data]
    Feedback -.-> CustomerDemand
```

## 4.3 Kano Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Customer Need Received] --> Q1{Does absence of feature cause extreme dissatisfaction?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Does presence create high satisfaction?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is satisfaction linearly proportional to performance?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Attractive[Attractive Excitement]
    Q2 -->|No| MustBe[Must Be Basic Threshold]
    Q3 -->|Yes| OneDim[One Dimensional Performance]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Does customer even care?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Indifferent[Indifferent Ignore]
    Q4 -->|No| Reverse[Reverse Some Like Some Dislike]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block is a *function* in the customer-focus engine. Arrows represent information flow or material flow. The dotted line from `Customer Feedback` back to `VOC` represents the **closed-loop** nature of Lean — the system is never "done."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **"Value"** as understood in Lean Manufacturing. Explain why value must be defined **from the customer's perspective** and not from the producer's perspective. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Value is defined in Lean as **"a capability provided to a customer at the right time, at the right price, and with the right features"** that the customer is willing to pay for. Value must be defined from the customer's perspective because Lean fundamentally rejects the producer-centric "push" mentality; if the customer is not willing to pay for an activity, that activity — no matter how technically excellent — is **waste (Muda)**. The customer's willingness to pay is the ultimate arbiter of what constitutes value. The producer's expertise, effort, and intent are irrelevant in the absence of customer validation, because Lean defines the **purpose of any business as the creation of value for the customer** (Womack & Jones, 1996).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** and **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** with a suitable example. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
**VOC** is the **raw, unfiltered expression** of customer needs, expectations, and complaints captured through surveys, interviews, complaint logs, and field observations, expressed in the customer's own words. **CTQ** is the **engineered, measurable translation** of VOC into specific, quantifiable specifications with defined tolerance limits. 

For example: 
- VOC: *"I want my phone to charge very fast."* 
- CTQ: *"Phone battery must charge from 0% to 100% in $\leq 45$ minutes under standard 25°C ambient temperature."*

VOC is qualitative; CTQ is quantitative and engineerable.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **Kano Model** of customer satisfaction in detail. Discuss with examples how the three primary categories of customer requirements (Must-Be, One-Dimensional, and Attractive) influence Lean product design decisions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small-scale garment unit in Kerala operates **two shifts of 8 hours each per day**. The shift includes a **45-minute lunch break and two 15-minute tea breaks per shift**. The daily customer demand from retail outlets is **1,200 shirts**. Calculate the **Takt Time**. If the current production line's average cycle time is **22 seconds/shirt**, determine whether the line is meeting demand, overproducing, or underproducing. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Kano Model

**Introduction (1 mark):** The Kano Model, developed by Professor Noriaki Kano in 1984, is a framework for classifying customer preferences into distinct categories. It is foundational to customer-focused Lean design.

**Three Primary Categories (4 marks):**

1. **Must-Be (Basic / Threshold) Needs:** These are the minimum expectations the customer assumes will be fulfilled. Their absence causes extreme dissatisfaction, but their presence does not significantly increase satisfaction.
   - *Example for a car:* Functional brakes, working seatbelts, basic engine reliability. The customer does not *thank* the manufacturer for these, but would be furious if they were missing.
   - *Lean implication:* Must-Be needs are non-negotiable CTQs; they define the **entry ticket** to the market.

2. **One-Dimensional (Performance) Needs:** These have a **linear** relationship with satisfaction — more is better.
   - *Example for a car:* Fuel efficiency. A car giving 25 km/l satisfies more than one giving 15 km/l. The relationship is proportional and predictable.
   - *Lean implication:* These CTQs are optimized through continuous improvement; they are the focus of **Kaizen** activities.

3. **Attractive (Excitement / Delighter) Needs:** These are unexpected features that delight the customer when present, but cause no dissatisfaction when absent.
   - *Example for a car:* Heated seats with massage, voice-activated climate control, or a self-parking feature in a budget car.
   - *Lean implication:* Attractive needs are **value-add differentiators**; they justify premium pricing and brand loyalty.

**Influence on Lean Design (2 marks):** Lean designers use Kano analysis to **prioritize resources** — Must-Be needs consume resources to *meet baseline*, One-Dimensional needs consume resources to *compete*, and Attractive needs consume resources to *differentiate*. Misallocating effort (e.g., spending heavily on Attractive features while ignoring Must-Be) is a classic Lean failure that customer focus prevents.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Takt Time Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Two shifts × 8 hours = 16 hours/day
- Lunch break: 45 minutes/shift
- Tea breaks: 2 × 15 = 30 minutes/shift
- Total breaks per shift = 45 + 30 = 75 minutes
- Daily demand: $D = 1{,}200$ shirts

**Step 1: Total calendar time per day (1 mark)**

$$
T_{cal} = 16 \text{ hours} = 16 \times 60 = 960 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2: Total break time per day (1 mark)**

$$
T_{breaks} = 75 \times 2 = 150 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3: Available production time (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{available} &= T_{cal} - T_{breaks} \\
&= 960 - 150 = 810 \text{ minutes} \\
&= 810 \times 60 = 48{,}600 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Takt Time calculation (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TT &= \frac{T_{available}}{D} = \frac{48{,}600 \text{ seconds}}{1{,}200 \text{ shirts}} \\
&= 40.5 \text{ seconds/shirt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Comparison and Interpretation (2 marks)**
- Takt Time = **40.5 seconds/shirt**
- Current Cycle Time = **22 seconds/shirt**
- Since Cycle Time (22 s) **<** Takt Time (40.5 s), the line is **faster than customer demand**.

**Conclusion:** The line is **overproducing** by approximately $\dfrac{40.5 - 22}{40.5} \times 100\% = 45.7\%$ above customer demand. This is the classic Lean waste of **Overproduction (Muda of overproduction)** — the most dangerous waste, as it hides other wastes and increases inventory holding costs. The line should either reduce shifts, slow the conveyor (paced production), or use **Heijunka** (production leveling) to match Takt Time.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define **Value-Added (VA)**, **Non-Value-Added (NVA)**, and **Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA)** activities. With reference to a hospital's emergency department, classify any **six** activities under these three categories. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **CTQ Tree** for the VOC statement: *"I want my bank loan to be approved and disbursed quickly."* Identify at least **three measurable CTQ specifications** with units and target values. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — VA, NVA, NNVA Classification

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- **Value-Added (VA):** An activity that transforms the product/service in a way the customer is willing to pay for, done right the first time.
- **Non-Value-Added (NVA):** An activity that consumes resources but creates no value from the customer's perspective — pure waste (Muda).
- **Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA):** An activity that currently consumes resources without adding value, but is unavoidable under present regulatory, legal, or safety constraints (a candidate for future elimination through process redesign).

**Six Hospital Emergency Department Activities (4 marks):**

| # | Activity | Classification | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Doctor's diagnosis and treatment decision | **VA** | Patient is willing to pay; transforms health outcome |
| 2 | Patient waiting in queue for consultation | **NVA** | Patient unwilling to pay for waiting |
| 3 | Statutory documentation of patient records (per KPME Act) | **NNVA** | Legally required, but customer doesn't pay directly for it |
| 4 | Repeated registration at multiple counters | **NVA** | Redundant data entry; pure waste |
| 5 | Administering life-saving injection | **VA** | Customer (patient) values and pays for this |
| 6 | Mandatory bio-waste disposal as per BMW Rules 2016 | **NNVA** | Regulatory requirement; unavoidable in current setup |

#### Model Solution to (b) — CTQ Tree for Bank Loan

**Step 1: Write the VOC (verbatim) (1 mark)**
> *"I want my bank loan to be approved and disbursed quickly."*

**Step 2: Branch into Customer Requirements (2 marks)**
1. Time to receive initial approval decision
2. Total time from application submission to fund credit in account
3. Transparency of process (number of visits / status updates)

**Step 3: Convert each into measurable CTQs (4 marks)**

```
                    ┌── Time-to-Approval (≤ 4 working days) — days
VOC: Quick Loan ────┼── Disbursement Time (≤ 7 working days) — days
Approval &          └── Number of Branch Visits (≤ 2 visits) — count
Disbursement
                    ┌── Approval Rate for Eligible Applicants (≥ 90%) — %
                    └── SMS/Email Status Updates (≥ 3 updates) — count
```

**Three Measurable CTQ Specifications (formatted):**

1. **CTQ-1: Time to Initial Approval Decision**
   - Target: $\leq 4$ working days from application submission.
   - Unit: **days**.
   - Measurement: Time-stamped application receipt vs. approval letter generation.

2. **CTQ-2: End-to-End Disbursement Time**
   - Target: $\leq 7$ working days from submission to account credit.
   - Unit: **days**.
   - Measurement: Difference between application date and NEFT/RTGS credit timestamp.

3. **CTQ-3: Customer Branch Visits**
   - Target: $\leq 2$ visits per loan cycle.
   - Unit: **count of visits**.
   - Measurement: Gate register or footfall analytics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse VOC with CTQ.** VOC is in *customer words*; CTQ is in *engineer words* with units. Students who write *"fast" instead of "$\leq 4$ days"* lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Takt Time questions require assumptions.** Always state the shift hours, breaks, and demand clearly. Students who skip assumptions lose **1 mark** upfront.
> 3. **Do not call NNVA "value-added."** NNVA is **non-value-added but necessary** under current constraints. Confusing it with VA is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Kano Model — students forget Attractive category.** Always draw or list all three primary categories; missing one costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Units in CTQ are mandatory.** A CTQ without a unit (e.g., "short time" instead of "$\leq 30$ minutes") is incomplete. Examiners explicitly look for this.
> 6. **In Takt Time problems, do not use production quantity — use customer demand.** Many students mistakenly use last month's *production* instead of the *demand* from customers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Customer Focus in Lean):**

- ☐ **Customer Focus = the foundation of Lean.** Without identifying who the customer is and what they value, all Lean tools (5S, Kanban, Poka-Yoke) are meaningless.
- ☐ **Value is defined by the customer, not the producer.** Producer's effort ≠ value; customer's willingness to pay = value.
- ☐ **Three KTU-mandatory definitions to memorize verbatim:** Value, Value-Added Activity, and Non-Value-Added Activity.
- ☐ **VOC → CTQ Tree is a three-layer transformation:** Customer need (words) → Customer requirement (operational) → CTQ (measurable with units).
- ☐ **Kano Model has three primary categories:** Must-Be, One-Dimensional, Attractive. Two modern extensions: Indifferent, Reverse.
- ☐ **Takt Time formula:** $TT = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}$. Always state shift assumptions.
- ☐ **Cycle Time vs. Takt Time:** Cycle Time $<$ Takt Time = Overproduction; Cycle Time $>$ Takt Time = Underproduction / Lost Sales.
- ☐ **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** is typically **< 11%** in un-lean systems. Lean target: drive NVA to zero.
- ☐ **First Pass Yield (FPY)** vs. **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY):** $RTY = \prod FPY_i$ — RTY reveals hidden factory losses.
- ☐ **Customer Feedback loops** (NPS, CSAT, CES) must be **closed** — they feed back into VOC. Open loops = stagnant Lean.
- ☐ **PDCA (Deming Cycle)** is the engine of continuous customer focus: Plan-Do-Check-Act.
- ☐ **Pull (not Push):** Produce only when the downstream customer (internal or external) signals demand.
- ☐ **Gemba** — go to the actual place where value is created (shop floor, hospital ward, restaurant kitchen) to understand customer reality.
- ☐ **Heijunka** — level the production based on customer demand mix, not volume peaks.
- ☐ **KTU favorite integration:** Always link Takt Time to Heijunka, and CTQ to Kano, in 14-mark answers to demonstrate **holistic Lean thinking**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Waste (MUDA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Waste (MUDA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of MUDA (Waste)

In the context of Lean Manufacturing and the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, **MUDA** (Japanese: 無駄) refers to any human activity that absorbs resources but creates **no value** for the end customer. It is the foundational concept upon which the entire Lean philosophy is built, first systematically articulated by **Taiichi Ohno**, the architect of TPS.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim-aligned):**
> *Waste (Muda) is any activity in a process that does not add value from the customer's perspective. Value-added activities are those which the customer is willing to pay for; everything else is muda and must be systematically eliminated to achieve flow, quality, and cost efficiency.*

### 1.2 The Three Japanese Wastes (Muda, Mura, Muri)

Lean thinking does not stop at identifying single waste; it categorises inefficiency into three orthogonal families:

| Japanese Term | Literal Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|---------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **MUDA** (無駄) | Wastefulness, futility | Non-value-adding activities (the 7 wastes) |
| **MURA** (斑) | Unevenness, irregularity | Variability in demand, load, or production rate |
| **MURI** (無理) | Unreasonableness, overburden | Excessive strain on people or equipment |

> [!NOTE] **Examiners' Hot Tip:** Many students confuse MUDA with MURA and MURI. Remember: *Muda = wasteful steps, Mura = unevenness, Muri = overburden.* All three must be tackled, but MUDA is the primary focus of Module 1.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine you walk into a traditional restaurant and place an order for a single dosa. Behind the scenes:

- **The cook walks 8 metres to fetch batter** → *Motion waste (employee movement)*
- **He waits 6 minutes for the tawa to heat up** → *Waiting waste*
- **He makes 12 dosas at once "just in case"** → *Overproduction waste*
- **Half the dosas are thrown away at closing** → *Inventory + Defect waste*
- **He chops onions to a precision of 0.1 mm** → *Over-processing waste*
- **He uses a 3-kg ladle to serve one dosa** → *Transportation of materials*
- **A second helper stands idle** → *Non-utilised talent waste*

Only the act of **cooking and serving the dosa the customer eats** adds value. Everything else is MUDA. Now scale this analogy to a factory producing ₹10 crore worth of components per month — even a **5% muda** translates to **₹50 lakh** drained every month.

### 1.4 Value-Added (VA) vs Non-Value-Added (NVA) vs Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA)

A critical KTU distinction is that not all non-value-added activity is removable in the short term. The classification is:

> **Value-Added (VA):** Transforms the product/service in a way the customer is willing to pay for. Three criteria: (i) the activity changes the item, (ii) it is done right the first time, (iii) the customer cares.

> **Non-Value-Added (NVA):** Pure waste — consumes resources, adds zero customer value, and can theoretically be eliminated immediately (e.g., reworking a defective bolt, walking to a far-away tool crib).

> **Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA):** Currently required due to current process limitations (e.g., moving parts between two un-merged workstations, mandatory inspection under current regulations). Target for *future* elimination, but cannot be removed today.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven (Plus One) Wastes — TIMWOODS Framework

Taiichi Ohno originally identified **seven wastes**; modern Lean texts (Womack & Jones, Liker) often append an **eighth** — underutilised human talent. The standard English mnemonic is **TIMWOODS** or **DOWNTIME**.

| # | Waste (English) | Japanese | Real Factory Symptom | Typical % of Total Waste |
|---|------------------|----------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **T**ransportation | *Idō* | Conveyors, forklifts, moving WIP across the plant | ~10–15% |
| 2 | **I**nventory | *Zaiko* | Raw material piles, WIP racks, finished goods buffer | ~20–25% |
| 3 | **M**otion | *Ugoki* | Operator walking, reaching, bending, searching | ~15% |
| 4 | **W**aiting | *Mate* | Machine idle, operator idle, approval delays | ~15–20% |
| 5 | **O**verproduction | *Jikaseisan* | Making more than customer has ordered, "just-in-case" | ~25–30% (the worst) |
| 6 | **O**ver-processing | *Furyō-kōsaku* | Tighter tolerances than required, duplicate inspections | ~5–10% |
| 7 | **D**efects | *Furyō* | Scrap, rework, warranty claims, returns | ~10–15% |
| 8 | **Skills (Un-utilised talent)** | *Kufūryoku* | Underused ideas, talent, brain-power of workers | ~5% |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Overproduction is the "Mother of All Waste":**
> Overproduction directly *generates* the other six wastes. If you make 1,000 units when only 800 are needed, you immediately create:
> - **Inventory** (the 200 extras)
> - **Transportation** (moving them)
> - **Waiting** (operators waiting for the next operation on the surplus)
> - **Defects** (more units = more chances of failure)
> - **Motion & Storage** (handling them)
>
> Hence Ohno's quote: *"Overproduction is the worst kind of waste — it hides the other wastes."*

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every quantitative metric a KTU student must know for the *Overview of Waste* topic. All equations are stated in clean LaTeX with vertical bars rendered as `\vert`.

| # | Metric | Formula | Units / Interpretation |
|---|--------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $\text{VAR} = \dfrac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Lead Time}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \text{VAR} \le 1$ |
| 2 | **Waste Index (WI)** | $\text{WI} = \dfrac{\text{NVA Time}}{\text{VA Time}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{VAR}} - 1$ | Higher is worse |
| 3 | **Lead Time Compression Ratio** | $\text{LTCR} = \dfrac{\text{Current Lead Time} - \text{Lean Lead Time}}{\text{Current Lead Time}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage reduction achievable |
| 4 | **First-Time-Right (FTR) yield** | $\text{FTR} = \dfrac{\text{Units passed first time}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100\%$ | Defect-related waste |
| 5 | **Rolled-Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{Yield}_i$ | Product of station yields |
| 6 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{scrap}} + C_{\text{rework}} + C_{\text{warranty}} + C_{\text{downtime}}$ | Currency units (₹) |
| 7 | **Inventory Carrying Cost (ICC)** | $\text{ICC} = \dfrac{\text{Average Inventory} \times C_{u} \times T}{100}$ | where $C_u$ = unit cost (₹), $T$ = time (years) |
| 8 | **Total Process Efficiency (TPE)** | $\text{TPE} = \dfrac{\text{VA Time}}{\text{VA Time} + \text{NVA Time} + \text{NNVA Time}} \times 100\%$ | Equivalent to VAR |
| 9 | **Operator Utilisation (non-waste version)** | $\text{OU}_{\text{lean}} = \dfrac{\text{VA Work Time}}{\text{Total Available Time}}$ | Excludes waiting & rework |
| 10 | **Takt Time** | $T_{\text{takt}} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand per Period}}$ | seconds per unit |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Pitfall Avoided:** A common mark-loser is using $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table. In your answer script write-up, always use the $\lvert x \rvert$ LaTeX delimiters or the word "modulus" — never a raw vertical pipe — to prevent the markdown parser from breaking the column structure.

### 2.3 The Five-Step Waste Identification Methodology (Womack & Jones)

According to *Lean Thinking* (Womack & Jones, 1996), waste is identified using the following universal sequence:

1. **Specify Value** — from the customer's perspective, in product families.
2. **Identify the Value Stream** — map every action from raw material to customer.
3. **Make Value Flow** — remove interruptions and waiting.
4. **Pull on Customer Demand** — produce only when downstream pulls.
5. **Pursue Perfection** — repeat the cycle; the 7 wastes surface at step 2 of the next iteration.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Eliminating MUDA is the direct mechanism by which Lean improves measurable Key Performance Indicators:

- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** rises when *Breakdown* and *Setup* losses (a form of Muri/Setup muda) are cut.
- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** drops as *Defect* waste is engineered out via Poka-Yoke.
- **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** compresses as *Inventory* and *Waiting* wastes vanish.
- **On-Time-Delivery (OTD)** improves as *Transportation* and *Motion* wastes are removed via cell layout.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Lead-Time Components to the Waste Index

A KTU-favourite derivation is to express the **Waste Index** from first principles. Let us walk through it step-by-step.

Let the total **Lead Time (LT)** of a process be decomposed into three mutually exclusive time components:

$$
\text{LT} = T_{\text{VA}} + T_{\text{NVA}} + T_{\text{NNVA}}
$$

**Where:**
- $T_{\text{VA}}$ = time spent on **Value-Added** activities (e.g., actual machining, painting, assembling)
- $T_{\text{NVA}}$ = time spent on **pure waste** (e.g., reworking scrap, waiting for the supervisor, walking to a distant tool crib)
- $T_{\text{NNVA}}$ = time spent on **Necessary Non-Value-Added** work (e.g., mandatory quality inspection, moving parts between two non-merged cells)

> **Step 1 — Define the Value-Added Ratio (VAR):**
> By Lean convention, VAR is the fraction of total lead time that creates customer value. The most common definition used in KTU textbooks and ASQ/AME references is:

$$
\text{VAR} = \frac{T_{\text{VA}}}{T_{\text{VA}} + T_{\text{NVA}} + T_{\text{NNVA}}}
$$

> **Step 2 — Algebraic rearrangement to isolate the denominator:**

$$
\text{VAR} \cdot \big(T_{\text{VA}} + T_{\text{NVA}} + T_{\text{NNVA}}\big) = T_{\text{VA}}
$$

> **Step 3 — Subtract $\text{VAR} \cdot T_{\text{VA}}$ from both sides:**

$$
\text{VAR} \cdot T_{\text{NVA}} + \text{VAR} \cdot T_{\text{NNVA}} = T_{\text{VA}} - \text{VAR} \cdot T_{\text{VA}}
$$

> **Step 4 — Factor the right-hand side:**

$$
\text{VAR} \cdot \big(T_{\text{NVA}} + T_{\text{NNVA}}\big) = T_{\text{VA}} \cdot (1 - \text{VAR})
$$

> **Step 5 — Divide both sides by $\text{VAR} \cdot T_{\text{VA}}$:**

$$
\frac{T_{\text{NVA}} + T_{\text{NNVA}}}{T_{\text{VA}}} = \frac{1 - \text{VAR}}{\text{VAR}}
$$

> **Step 6 — Split the right side using $\dfrac{1-\text{VAR}}{\text{VAR}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{VAR}} - 1$:**

$$
\frac{T_{\text{NVA}} + T_{\text{NNVA}}}{T_{\text{VA}}} = \frac{1}{\text{VAR}} - 1
$$

> **Step 7 — Identify the Left Side as the Waste Index (WI):**
> The Waste Index is conventionally defined as the ratio of all non-value-added time to value-added time. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Waste Index (WI)} = \frac{1}{\text{VAR}} - 1\;}
$$

> **Step 8 — Boundary check:**
> - If a process is **100% value-added**, then $\text{VAR} = 1 \Rightarrow \text{WI} = 0$ ✓ (no waste)
> - If a process is **10% value-added**, then $\text{VAR} = 0.1 \Rightarrow \text{WI} = 9$ ✓ (90% waste)
> - If a process is **0% value-added**, then $\text{VAR} \to 0 \Rightarrow \text{WI} \to \infty$ ✓ (infinite waste)

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

A CNC shaft manufacturing cell records the following daily time distribution:

| Activity | Type | Time (minutes) |
|----------|------|----------------|
| Actual cutting on CNC | VA | 95 |
| Mandatory CMM inspection | NNVA | 30 |
| Walking to fetch raw stock | NVA | 40 |
| Waiting for tool change approval | NVA | 25 |
| Re-grinding a scrapped shaft | NVA | 20 |
| Moving parts between two cells | NNVA | 35 |

> **Step 1 — Sum each category:**

$$
T_{\text{VA}} = 95 \text{ min}, \quad T_{\text{NNVA}} = 30 + 35 = 65 \text{ min}, \quad T_{\text{NVA}} = 40 + 25 + 20 = 85 \text{ min}
$$

> **Step 2 — Total Lead Time:**

$$
\text{LT} = 95 + 65 + 85 = 245 \text{ min}
$$

> **Step 3 — Calculate VAR:**

$$
\text{VAR} = \frac{95}{245} = 0.3878 \approx 38.78\%
$$

> **Step 4 — Calculate Waste Index:**

$$
\text{WI} = \frac{1}{0.3878} - 1 = 2.579 - 1 = 1.579
$$

> **Step 5 — Interpretation:**
> For every **1 minute** of value-added work, the process wastes approximately **1.58 minutes**. Eliminating the 85 min of pure NVA alone would push VAR from 38.78% to **59.4%** — a **53% productivity improvement** with zero capital investment.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the 7 Wastes to KAIZEN Tools

| Waste | KAIZEN Counter-Tool | Key Metric Improved |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Overproduction | Heijunka, Just-in-Time, Takt-time alignment | Inventory turns |
| Waiting | One-piece flow, SMED, line balancing | Lead time |
| Transportation | Cellular layout, point-of-use delivery | Distance travelled |
| Over-processing | Quality at source, standard work | Process capability $C_p$ |
| Inventory | Kanban, FIFO lanes, supermarket pull | Days-on-hand |
| Motion | 5S, ergonomics, shadow boards | Cycle-time operator ratio |
| Defects | Poka-yoke, Jidoka, A3 thinking | FTR, COPQ |
| Unused talent | Suggestion systems, Kaizen circles | Engagement index |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The 7 Wastes of MUDA — Master Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["MUDA WASTE - 7 CATEGORIES"]
    A1["1 TRANSPORTATION"]
    A2["2 INVENTORY"]
    A3["3 MOTION"]
    A4["4 WAITING"]
    A5["5 OVERPRODUCTION"]
    A6["6 OVER PROCESSING"]
    A7["7 DEFECTS"]
    A8["8 UNUSED TALENT"]
    B1["Material movement between locations"]
    B2["Raw material, WIP, finished goods excess"]
    B3["Operator walking reaching bending"]
    B4["Idle time, machine downtime, approvals"]
    B5["Producing more than customer demand"]
    B6["Tighter tolerances and inspection than needed"]
    B7["Scrap, rework, warranty claims"]
    B8["Underutilised skills, ideas, creativity"]
    C1["Solution Cellular layout, point of use"]
    C2["Solution Kanban, JIT, FIFO lanes"]
    C3["Solution 5S, ergonomics, shadow boards"]
    C4["Solution SMED, one piece flow, line balancing"]
    C5["Solution Heijunka, Takt time, pull system"]
    C6["Solution Quality at source, standard work"]
    C7["Solution Poka yoke, Jidoka, A3 thinking"]
    C8["Solution Suggestion systems, Kaizen circles"]
    ROOT --> A1
    ROOT --> A2
    ROOT --> A3
    ROOT --> A4
    ROOT --> A5
    ROOT --> A6
    ROOT --> A7
    ROOT --> A8
    A1 --> B1 --> C1
    A2 --> B2 --> C2
    A3 --> B3 --> C3
    A4 --> B4 --> C4
    A5 --> B5 --> C5
    A6 --> B6 --> C6
    A7 --> B7 --> C7
    A8 --> B8 --> C8
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Waste Identification & Elimination Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Customer Demand Signal"]
    VSM["Value Stream Mapping Step 1 Identify Activities"]
    CAT["Step 2 Classify into VA, NVA, NNVA"]
    ID["Step 3 Tag the 7 Wastes TIMWOODS"]
    QNT["Step 4 Quantify Waste Index and VAR"]
    ACT["Step 5 Apply KAIZEN Counter Tools"]
    OUT["Step 6 Re-measure and Standardise"]
    FEED["Step 7 Pursue Perfection Loop back to Step 1"]
    INP --> VSM --> CAT --> ID --> QNT --> ACT --> OUT --> FEED
    FEED -. feedback .-> VSM
```

### 4.3 Process Boundary Schematic: Value-Added vs Non-Value-Added Time Along a Product's Journey

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Order Received"]
    B["Raw Material Stored - INVENTORY"]
    C["Move to Machine 1 - TRANSPORTATION"]
    D["Set up Machine - WAITING"]
    E["Machine Cutting - VALUE ADDED"]
    F["Move to Inspection - TRANSPORTATION"]
    G["Mandatory CMM Check - NNVA"]
    H["Rework Defective Part - DEFECT WASTE"]
    I["Move to Assembly - TRANSPORTATION"]
    J["Assemble - VALUE ADDED"]
    K["Move to Warehouse - INVENTORY"]
    L["Ship to Customer - DELIVERED"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K --> L
```

> **Reading the schematic:** The **VA blocks** (E, J) are the only activities a customer will pay for. Every other block is muda or NNVA — the Lean goal is to compress the diagram horizontally while keeping only E and J expanded.

### 4.4 Cause-and-Effect Summary: Why Overproduction Generates the Other 6 Wastes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP["OVERPRODUCTION - Making more than needed"]
    P1["Excess units must be stored - INVENTORY"]
    P2["Excess units must be moved - TRANSPORTATION"]
    P3["Operators wait between batches - WAITING"]
    P4["Excess units increase failure rate - DEFECTS"]
    P5["More handling means more operator movement - MOTION"]
    P6["Surplus units trigger extra inspection - OVER PROCESSING"]
    P7["Surplus masks capacity problems - UNUSED TALENT hidden"]
    OP --> P1
    OP --> P2
    OP --> P3
    OP --> P4
    OP --> P5
    OP --> P6
    OP --> P7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **MUDA** as understood in Lean Manufacturing. Mention any **three** of the seven wastes with a one-line example of each. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
*MUDA is a Japanese term meaning "waste" — any activity that consumes resources (time, material, labour, machine time) but does not add value as perceived by the end customer. It was systematically codified by Taiichi Ohno of Toyota within the Toyota Production System.*
*Three of the seven wastes are:*
*(i) **Transportation** — moving raw materials between distant storage bays when point-of-use delivery is feasible.*
*(ii) **Waiting** — an operator standing idle while a CNC machine runs an unattended cycle.*
*(iii) **Defects** — producing a casting with porosity that requires welding rework before shipment.*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **MUDA, MURA and MURI**. Why is Overproduction considered the "worst" waste? **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
***MUDA** is wastefulness — non-value-adding activity. **MURA** is unevenness — variability in demand, workload or production rate. **MURI** is overburden — excessive physical or mental strain on people and equipment.*
*Overproduction is considered the worst waste because it is the **root cause** that hides and generates the other six wastes: producing more than demanded creates excess inventory, requires extra transportation, generates waiting time, increases the chance of defects, causes additional motion in handling, and triggers over-processing through extra inspections. By producing exactly to takt-time and customer pull, all downstream wastes are simultaneously suppressed.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q3. (a)** List and explain the **seven wastes (MUDA)** identified by Taiichi Ohno with a manufacturing example for each. Discuss why each is a non-value-adding activity from the customer's viewpoint. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **seven wastes (TIMWOODS)** are:

1. **Transportation** — *Example:* Moving a forging from a central warehouse to a machining bay 200 m away by forklift. *Customer impact:* The customer never pays for the forklift trip; it only adds lead time and risk of damage.

2. **Inventory** — *Example:* Storing 6 months' worth of finished gearbox housings in a godown "just in case". *Customer impact:* Capital is locked, items may become obsolete, and the customer has no use for parts they did not order.

3. **Motion** — *Example:* An operator walking 15 m to a shared tool crib to fetch a wrench 50 times per shift. *Customer impact:* Movement does not change the product; the customer pays only for the assembly.

4. **Waiting** — *Example:* An operator idle for 12 minutes while a heat-treatment furnace completes its cycle. *Customer impact:* The wait period adds lead time without changing the part.

5. **Overproduction** — *Example:* Producing 1,200 units in an 8-hour shift when demand is for 800. *Customer impact:* Surplus ties up space, hides problems, and is never paid for.

6. **Over-processing** — *Example:* Machining a surface to 0.01 mm Ra when the drawing specifies 1.6 mm Ra. *Customer impact:* The extra precision is invisible to the user and consumes machine time.

7. **Defects** — *Example:* A welded chassis that fails leak-test and must be reworked. *Customer impact:* Resources spent on rework are not paid for; the customer is even willing to pay less because the first one was wrong.

In every case the activity either fails the **three tests of value-added** (does it change the item? is it done right the first time? does the customer care?), or fails at least one of them — hence the activity is non-value-added and qualifies as MUDA.

> **Q3. (b)** A process has the following time data: VA = 22 min, NNVA = 18 min, NVA = 40 min per unit. Calculate (i) the **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)**, (ii) the **Waste Index (WI)**, and (iii) the percentage **lead-time compression** achievable if the NVA is fully eliminated. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $T_{\text{VA}} = 22$ min, $T_{\text{NNVA}} = 18$ min, $T_{\text{NVA}} = 40$ min.

**Step 1 — Total Lead Time:**

$$
\text{LT} = 22 + 18 + 40 = 80 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Value-Added Ratio:**

$$
\text{VAR} = \frac{22}{80} = 0.275 = 27.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Waste Index:**

$$
\text{WI} = \frac{1}{0.275} - 1 = 3.636 - 1 = 2.636
$$

The process wastes **2.636 minutes** for every **1 minute** of value-added work.

**Step 4 — New Lead Time after eliminating NVA:**

$$
\text{LT}_{\text{new}} = 22 + 18 = 40 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 5 — Lead-time compression ratio:**

$$
\text{LTCR} = \frac{80 - 40}{80} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Eliminating the 40 minutes of pure NVA cuts the lead time by **half (50%)** and pushes the new VAR to $\dfrac{22}{40} = 55\%$, more than doubling process efficiency with **zero capital investment** — a classic Lean quick-win.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q4. (a)** Explain the concept of **Value-Added (VA), Non-Value-Added (NVA) and Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA)** activities. Why is the distinction between NVA and NNVA critical in a Lean implementation? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Value-Added (VA)** activity satisfies **three** criteria simultaneously:
- the product or service is physically **transformed**,
- the work is done **right the first time**, and
- the **customer is willing to pay** for it (e.g., drilling a hole, MIG-welding a joint, painting a panel).

A **Non-Value-Added (NVA)** activity consumes resources but creates **no value** and can, in principle, be **eliminated immediately** (e.g., reworking a defect, walking 30 m to a distant tool crib, waiting for an approval signature).

A **Necessary Non-Value-Added (NNVA)** activity is also non-value-adding but cannot be removed **today** due to current process or regulatory constraints (e.g., mandatory statutory safety inspection, moving parts between two non-merged cells, calibration of a CMM). It is a **target for future elimination** through kaizen.

**Why the distinction is critical:**
1. **Prioritisation** — engineers often waste effort trying to "value-engineer" tasks that are actually NNVA. Classifying correctly tells the team *where* to attack first.
2. **Roadmap building** — NVA goes into the "**eliminate now**" bucket; NNVA goes into the "**improve to remove later**" bucket.
3. **Motivation** — saying "everything is waste" demoralises workers. Recognising NNVA shows respect for activities that are *currently* essential.
4. **Measurement integrity** — the Waste Index (WI) can be split into WI(removable) and WI(necessary) so management sees the realistic scope of improvement.

> **Q4. (b)** A garment factory produces 1,200 shirts/day. Each shirt requires 18 minutes of VA sewing time, 22 minutes of NNVA finishing and ironing, and 35 minutes of NVA (waiting + rework + motion). The factory operates 8 hours/day. Calculate the **Takt Time** and comment on whether the process is **balanced** with respect to customer demand. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Available production time per day:**

$$
\text{Available time} = 8 \text{ hr} \times 60 = 480 \text{ min/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Takt Time:**

$$
T_{\text{takt}} = \frac{\text{Available time}}{\text{Customer demand}} = \frac{480 \text{ min}}{1200 \text{ shirts}} = 0.4 \text{ min/shirt} = 24 \text{ seconds/shirt}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Lead Time per shirt:**

$$
\text{LT} = 18 + 22 + 35 = 75 \text{ min/shirt}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Takt Time:**
The total lead time per shirt (**75 min**) is **187.5 times** the takt time (**24 s**). This means the production line is **severely unbalanced**: it is producing at a cadence far slower than the market rate.

**Step 5 — Lean recommendations:**
- Eliminate the 35 min of NVA first (waiting, rework, motion) — this can be done through 5S, cellular layout, and Poka-yoke.
- Convert NNVA (22 min) to VA-by-removing-need by in-line finishing and continuous-flow ironing.
- After elimination, the remaining 18 min of VA sewing is still **2,700% longer** than takt → the sewing station itself must be split into multiple parallel machines to meet demand.
- The Waste Index today is:

$$
\text{WI} = \frac{1}{18/(18+22+35)} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.24} - 1 = 4.167 - 1 = 3.167
$$

i.e., **3.17 minutes of waste for every 1 minute of value-added work**, confirming a huge Lean-improvement opportunity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Zones (and how to avoid them):**
> 1. **Confusing MUDA with MURA/MURI** — for 1 mark, write all three terms with their distinct meanings; examiners check the table.
> 2. **Forgetting the 8th waste** — Un-utilised talent was added post-2000; KTU 2024 syllabus often expects the "**7+1**" version. State the 8th even if not asked.
> 3. **Writing `|x|` inside a table** — breaks markdown rendering. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX or the word "modulus" inside answer scripts.
> 4. **Not showing units in calculations** — every numerical answer must carry units (min, %, ₹, pieces/day). Half-mark deducted otherwise.
> 5. **Missing the "Why Overproduction is the worst" angle** — KTU 2024 papers repeatedly reward the *reasoning* that overproduction generates the other six wastes; do not just list the seven.
> 6. **Treating NNVA same as NVA** — examiners award a full extra mark for correctly distinguishing.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MUDA = any non-value-adding activity** from the customer's viewpoint.
- **Muda + Mura + Muri** = the "3M" trilogy of inefficiency in TPS.
- **Seven (or 7+1) wastes** are remembered by **TIMWOODS** (or DOWNTIME).
- **Overproduction** is the **root waste** that hides and creates the other six.
- **VAR** ranges from 0 to 1; **WI** ranges from 0 to $\infty$.
- **NNVA ≠ pure NVA**: NNVA is *currently* necessary waste and is a future kaizen target.
- **Poka-yoke** addresses *Defects*; **Kanban** addresses *Inventory*; **5S** addresses *Motion*; **SMED** addresses *Waiting*; **Heijunka** addresses *Overproduction*; **Cellular layout** addresses *Transportation*; **Standard work** addresses *Over-processing*; **Suggestion systems** address *Unused talent*.
- **Lead-time compression of 50%** is typical after a focused muda-elimination project.
- The **mathematical link** $\text{WI} = \dfrac{1}{\text{VAR}} - 1$ is the single most tested formula on this topic — memorise the boundary conditions ($VAR=1 \Rightarrow WI=0$; $VAR \to 0 \Rightarrow WI \to \infty$).
- Always compute **Takt Time** first in any demand-vs-capacity question; it is the heartbeat of Lean flow.
- **Customer-centric definition** — if the customer would not pay for the activity, it is muda. This single rule settles 80% of classification disputes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools and Techniques for Lean Manufacturing: 5S System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tools and Techniques for Lean Manufacturing: 5S System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **5S System** is a structured, Japanese-origin workplace organization methodology and the foundational visual management tool of the Toyota Production System (TPS) and broader Lean Manufacturing framework. It is a systematic five-step methodology designed to optimize productivity, eliminate waste (muda), reduce variability, and establish a culture of continuous improvement (kaizen) by creating and maintaining an organized, clean, and disciplined workplace.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 5S system is the first and most fundamental tool in the Lean toolkit. The five Japanese terms — *Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu,* and *Shitsuke* — are universally accepted and often appear verbatim in KTU question papers without translation.

The term **5S** is an acronym for five Japanese words, each beginning with the letter "S":

| # | Japanese Term | English Translation | Core Function |
|---|---------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Eliminate unnecessary items |
| 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange items for easy access |
| 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace |
| 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish standards & consistency |
| 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain (Discipline) | Maintain & improve the system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5S is the foundation of Lean.** Without an organized, clean, and standardized workplace, every other Lean tool (Kanban, Poka-Yoke, SMED, TPM) becomes difficult or impossible to implement effectively. Toyota regards 5S as the "gateway" to all Lean activities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine your **own study room or kitchen** before and after a deep cleaning session:

- **Seiri (Sort):** You throw away old receipts, broken pens, magazines you will never read, and expired medicines. The goal is to keep ONLY what you actually need.
- **Seiton (Set in Order):** You arrange your books on a shelf by subject, place pens in a holder, and put spices in labeled jars near the stove. A guest could find any item in seconds.
- **Seiso (Shine):** You wipe the desk, mop the floor, and clean the window. Cleaning itself acts as a *visual inspection* — you discover a broken switch or a leaking tap.
- **Seiketsu (Standardize):** You create a rule: "Every book goes back to the shelf by subject after use. Every spice has a fixed jar." This rule is posted visibly.
- **Shitsuke (Sustain):** You make the habit stick. Every day you spend 5 minutes maintaining the system, and you teach your family to follow the same rules.

**The manufacturing workplace is no different.** A factory floor without 5S is full of unused tools, misplaced parts, oil spills, and no rules. 5S transforms chaos into a high-performance, visually-managed, and safe environment.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Industrial Analogy:** Think of the cockpit of an aircraft. Every switch, lever, and gauge is sorted, set in a specific order, polished/inspected daily, standardized across the fleet, and pilots are disciplined to maintain this exact arrangement. **5S is the "cockpit philosophy" for the factory floor.**

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Performance Indicators

- **5S Audit Score**: Typically expressed on a **0–100 point scale** or a **1–5 Likert scale** per S-element, with a passing threshold of **≥ 80 points** or **≥ 3.5/5**.
- **Red Tag Score (Seiri-specific metric)**: % of red-tagged items actually removed within 30 days. Target: **100%**.
- **Floor Space Utilization Ratio**: Usable space / Total space. Target: **≥ 85%** after Seiri.
- **Search/Retrieval Time Reduction**: Time to find a tool before vs. after 5S. Target reduction: **≥ 50%**.

> [!NOTE]
> All metrics above are **bold-typed standard KPIs** widely accepted in Lean assessment frameworks and commonly cited in KTU 2024 Scheme Part B answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Five Pillars — Exhaustive Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 SEIRI (Sort) — "The First S: Eliminate"

- **Definition:** Separating necessary from unnecessary items in the workplace and removing the unnecessary ones.
- **Core Logic:**
  1. Tag every item in the workspace.
  2. Categorize items as **Needed** or **Not Needed**.
  3. Apply the **Red-Tag Method** (see Section 2.2) to questionable items.
  4. Physically remove rejected items to a "red tag holding area."
  5. Dispose, recycle, or relocate unnecessary items.
- **Why It Matters:** Unnecessary items consume floor space, create clutter, increase search time, generate hidden costs (holding, obsolescence, insurance), and obscure process problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Tools in Seiri:** The "Used within last 30 days?" rule, the *Frequency-of-Use* matrix, and the *Cost-of-Keeping* analysis. If not used in the last 30 days and not expected in the next 30, it should be tagged red.

#### 2.1.2 SEITON (Set in Order / Straighten) — "A Place for Everything"

- **Definition:** Arranging necessary items in a logical, ergonomic, and visually identifiable manner so they can be easily found, used, and returned by anyone.
- **Core Logic:**
  1. Determine the **frequency of use** of each remaining item.
  2. Place **high-frequency** items at the **point of use (POU)**.
  3. Place **low-frequency** items in centralized stores.
  4. Apply **visual management** — shadow boards, floor markings, color codes, labels.
  5. Apply the **"30-second rule"** — any worker should locate any tool within 30 seconds.
- **Why It Matters:** Reduces *motion waste* (muda of motion), eliminates searching time, prevents loss of tools, and creates a self-explanatory workplace.

#### 2.1.3 SEISO (Shine / Sweep) — "Cleaning as Inspection"

- **Definition:** Cleaning the workplace, equipment, and surroundings to a spotless condition so that abnormalities (leaks, loose bolts, contamination) are immediately visible.
- **Core Logic:**
  1. Assign **cleaning responsibilities** to specific individuals (not "everyone").
  2. Integrate cleaning into the **daily work routine**, not as an occasional event.
  3. Treat cleaning as an **inspection activity** — defects surface during cleaning.
  4. Identify **root causes** of dirt, dust, and spills (sources, not symptoms).
  5. Eliminate these root causes.
- **Why It Matters:** A clean machine lasts longer, runs cooler, and signals operational discipline. It also forms the **basis of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**.

#### 2.1.4 SEIKETSU (Standardize) — "Make It a Habit"

- **Definition:** Establishing uniform procedures, visual standards, checklists, and SOPs so that the first three S's are maintained consistently across shifts, departments, and workers.
- **Core Logic:**
  1. Document best practices identified during Sort, Set-in-Order, and Shine.
  2. Create **visual standards** — photographs, diagrams, color codes, posters.
  3. Develop **checklists and SOPs** for the 5S activities.
  4. Use **5S boards, Andon displays, and audit sheets** to monitor compliance.
  5. Train every employee on the standards.
- **Why It Matters:** Without standardization, the first three S's degrade within weeks. Standardization is the **bridge** between short-term effort and long-term discipline.

#### 2.1.5 SHITSUKE (Sustain / Discipline) — "Make It a Culture"

- **Definition:** Embedding 5S into the organizational culture through training, audits, recognition, and leadership commitment so that the practice becomes a habit rather than a one-time event.
- **Core Logic:**
  1. Conduct **regular 5S audits** (daily, weekly, monthly).
  2. Implement **reward & recognition** programs.
  3. Provide **continuous training** and refreshers.
  4. Ensure **management commitment** and *Genchi Genbutsu* (go-and-see).
  5. Integrate 5S into the **employee performance evaluation** system.
- **Why It Matters:** The most common failure point in 5S implementations is the inability to sustain. Without Shitsuke, the initiative becomes a "campaign" that fades.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often list the 5 S's but fail to describe *how* to sustain them. The examiner expects explicit mention of **audits, training, and management commitment** under Shitsuke for full marks.

### 2.2 The Red-Tag Method (Detailed Sub-Concept)

A critical technique used in **Seiri** to identify items whose necessity is uncertain. A red tag is attached to the item with the following information:

- **Item name & quantity**
- **Date tagged**
- **Reason for tagging**
- **Person responsible**
- **Disposition (dispose, sell, relocate, or retain)**

Items are then moved to a "Red Tag Area" and given a **30-day disposition window**. If no one claims the item in 30 days, it is removed.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although 5S is qualitative in nature, several **quantitative metrics** are used for audit scoring and ROI calculation. These are highly valued in KTU 14-mark Part B questions.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Standard Unit | Target / Benchmark |
|---|------------------|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **5S Audit Score** | $\text{Score} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} S_i \times w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i} \times 100$ | Points (0–100) | $\geq$ **80** |
| 2 | **Red-Tag Disposal Rate** | $\text{RTDR} = \frac{\text{Red-tagged items removed}}{\text{Total red-tagged items}} \times 100$ | % | **100%** within 30 days |
| 3 | **Search Time Reduction** | $\text{STR} = \frac{T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}}{T_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq$ **50%** |
| 4 | **Floor Space Liberation** | $\text{FSL} = \frac{A_{\text{freed}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq$ **15–30%** typical |
| 5 | **Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC)** | $\text{CoNC} = C_{\text{search}} + C_{\text{motion}} + C_{\text{accident}} + C_{\text{scrap}}$ | Currency/yr | Minimized |
| 6 | **Inventory Carrying Cost Saved** | $\text{ICCS} = \Delta I \times i_{\text{rate}}$ | Currency/yr | Positive |
| 7 | **5S Compliance Rate** | $\text{CR} = \frac{\text{Checklist items passed}}{\text{Total checklist items}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq$ **90%** |
| 8 | **OEE Impact (post-5S)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Decimal (0–1) | $\geq$ **0.85 (85%)** |

Where:
- $S_i$ = score for the $i$-th S
- $w_i$ = weight assigned to the $i$-th S
- $T_{\text{before}}$, $T_{\text{after}}$ = search time before and after 5S (seconds)
- $A_{\text{freed}}$ = floor area freed by Seiri (m²)
- $\Delta I$ = inventory value reduction (currency)
- $i_{\text{rate}}$ = annual inventory carrying cost rate (decimal, typically **0.20–0.25**)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota, Ford, Tata Motors):** 5S underpins every assembly line. Floor markings, shadow boards for torque wrenches, and color-coded bins are direct 5S artifacts.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** FOD (Foreign Object Debris) prevention programs are essentially 5S + audits. A loose bolt in an aircraft engine can cause catastrophic failure.
- **Healthcare (Hospitals):** 5S is used to organize operating theatres, medication carts, and patient rooms — reducing medication errors and surgical delays.
- **Software/Digital: "5S for Code":** Sort (remove dead code), Set-in-Order (modular structure), Shine (refactor), Standardize (style guides), Sustain (CI/CD checks).
- **Warehousing & Logistics:** 5S drives slotting strategies, picking accuracy, and dock-door organization.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** In 14-mark Part B questions, always end your answer with a **real-world industry example** (e.g., "Toyota's assembly line at Bangalore plant uses 5S shadow boards for each tool type, achieving 100% tool accountability"). This leaves a strong impression on the examiner.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although 5S is primarily a qualitative methodology, KTU examiners frequently test **quantitative analytical ability** through metrics derivation and ROI calculations. This section provides exhaustive step-by-step derivations for the most commonly asked metrics.

### 3.1 Derivation: 5S Audit Score (Weighted Method)

**Problem Setup:** A manufacturing unit audits its 5S implementation. The auditor assigns the following raw scores (out of 5) and weights to each S:

| S-Element | Raw Score $S_i$ | Weight $w_i$ |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
| Seiri (Sort) | 3.5 | 0.20 |
| Seiton (Set in Order) | 4.0 | 0.20 |
| Seiso (Shine) | 4.5 | 0.20 |
| Seiketsu (Standardize) | 3.0 | 0.20 |
| Shitsuke (Sustain) | 2.5 | 0.20 |

**Goal:** Calculate the weighted 5S audit score and determine if it meets the **80-point passing benchmark**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Write the general weighted-score formula.

$$
\text{Weighted Score} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} S_i \cdot w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Verify the sum of weights.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00
$$

Since the sum equals 1.00, the formula simplifies to a direct weighted average.

**Step 3:** Compute the weighted sum.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{5} S_i \cdot w_i &= (3.5 \times 0.20) + (4.0 \times 0.20) + (4.5 \times 0.20) + (3.0 \times 0.20) + (2.5 \times 0.20) \\
&= 0.70 + 0.80 + 0.90 + 0.60 + 0.50 \\
&= 3.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final weighted score on a 0–100 scale.

$$
\text{Score} = 3.50 \times 100 = 70 \text{ points}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the benchmark.

$$
70 < 80 \implies \text{Fail — Improvement required, especially in Shitsuke (2.5)}
$$

**Result:** The organization scores **70/100**, falling short of the 80-point benchmark. The lowest-performing S is **Shitsuke**, indicating weak sustaining mechanisms. Management should focus on training, audits, and reward systems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Search Time Reduction (STR) with ROI Impact

**Problem Setup:** Before 5S, a tool crib required on average $T_{\text{before}} = 240$ seconds to locate a specific tool. After 5S (Seiton), the time reduced to $T_{\text{after}} = 60$ seconds. Each tool search is performed approximately $n = 50$ times per day. The labor cost is $C_{\text{labour}} = \text{₹}2/\text{second}$. The annual working days are $D = 300$.

**Goal:** Calculate the percentage STR, the daily time saved, the annual monetary saving, and the impact on productivity.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate the percentage Search Time Reduction.

$$
\text{STR} = \frac{T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}}{T_{\text{before}}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STR} &= \frac{240 - 60}{240} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{180}{240} \times 100 \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Search time reduced by **75%** — a strong outcome (benchmark: $\geq$ 50%).

**Step 2:** Calculate the time saved per search.

$$
\Delta t = T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}} = 240 - 60 = 180 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the daily time saved.

$$
T_{\text{day}} = \Delta t \times n = 180 \times 50 = 9{,}000 \text{ seconds/day}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
T_{\text{day}} = \frac{9{,}000}{3{,}600} = 2.5 \text{ hours/day}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the annual monetary saving.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= T_{\text{day}} \times D \times C_{\text{labour}} \\
&= 2.5 \text{ hr/day} \times 3{,}600 \text{ s/hr} \times 300 \text{ days} \times \text{₹}2/\text{s} \\
&= 2.5 \times 3{,}600 \times 300 \times 2 \\
&= 5{,}400{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}54{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Annual saving of **₹ 54,00,000** purely from reduced search time — a powerful justification for 5S investment.

**Step 5:** Productivity impact statement.

$$
\text{Equivalent FTE recovered} = \frac{T_{\text{day}}}{8 \text{ hours}} = \frac{2.5}{8} = 0.3125 \text{ FTE}
$$

This means **roughly 31% of one full-time worker's productive capacity** is recovered daily.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Floor Space Liberation & Inventory Carrying Cost Saved

**Problem Setup:** A 1,000 m² shop floor reduces its occupied inventory area by $A_{\text{freed}} = 200$ m² after Seiri. The inventory value removed is $\Delta I = \text{₹}20{,}00{,}000$, and the inventory carrying cost rate is $i_{\text{rate}} = 0.25$ (25% per year). The annual rent per m² is $R = \text{₹}5{,}000$.

**Goal:** Compute the Floor Space Liberation (FSL) percentage, the inventory carrying cost saved annually, and the rent equivalent.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute FSL percentage.

$$
\text{FSL} = \frac{A_{\text{freed}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100 = \frac{200}{1{,}000} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the inventory carrying cost saved.

$$
\text{ICCS} = \Delta I \times i_{\text{rate}} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the rent equivalent of freed floor space.

$$
\text{Rent Saving} = A_{\text{freed}} \times R = 200 \times 5{,}000 = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 4:** Total tangible benefit in the first year.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Benefit} &= \text{ICCS} + \text{Rent Saving} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}15{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Through a single Seiri exercise, the company saves **₹ 15,00,000 annually** and frees up **20% of its shop floor** — usable for new product lines, expansion, or just better workflow.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: 5S Activity-Waste Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix directly links each S to the seven **muda (wastes)** of Lean (TIMWOOD + skills underutilization). It is a high-yield answer structure for KTU Part B questions.

| S-Element | Defects | Overproduction | Waiting | Motion | Transport | Inventory | Over-Processing | Skills |
|-----------|:-------:|:--------------:|:-------:|:------:|:---------:|:---------:|:---------------:|:------:|
| **Seiri** |  |  | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |  |
| **Seiton** |  |  | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |  |  |  |
| **Seiso** | ✓ |  |  |  |  |  |  | ✓ |
| **Seiketsu** | ✓ |  | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |  |
| **Shitsuke** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

This matrix is **exhaustive evidence** that 5S addresses every form of waste when implemented in its entirety, which is why it is considered a **prerequisite to all other Lean tools**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
