## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

*Define the 5S system. List the five Japanese terms and their English equivalents.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **5S system** is a Japanese workplace organization methodology that forms the foundation of Lean Manufacturing and the Toyota Production System. It aims to create a clean, organized, and disciplined workplace while eliminating waste.

The five S's are:

1. **Seiri** — Sort (eliminate unnecessary items)
2. **Seiton** — Set in Order (arrange items logically)
3. **Seiso** — Shine (clean the workplace)
4. **Seiketsu** — Standardize (establish standards and procedures)
5. **Shitsuke** — Sustain (discipline to maintain the system)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for the 5 Japanese terms + 1 mark for correct English meanings.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*What is the Red-Tag Method? Why is it used in the Seiri step of 5S?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Red-Tag Method** is a visual identification technique used during the **Seiri (Sort)** phase of 5S. A red tag is attached to any item whose necessity in the workplace is uncertain. The tag records the item's name, quantity, date, reason for tagging, and the responsible person.

The item is then relocated to a designated "Red Tag Holding Area" for a **30-day observation period**. If no one claims the item within 30 days, it is disposed of, sold, recycled, or relocated.

**Purpose:**
- It provides a **physical, visual, and traceable** mechanism to identify unwanted items.
- It removes emotional bias — workers do not have to "decide" immediately; the system decides after 30 days.
- It creates a **standard, repeatable** Sort process that any employee can apply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for procedure + 1 mark for purpose/advantage.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Two independent alternative questions are provided below, following the standard KTU pattern of two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **5S system** in detail. Discuss the role of each of the five S's in eliminating waste and improving workplace efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company implements 5S in its tool crib. Before 5S, the average time to retrieve a tool was **180 seconds**. After implementing Seiton (Set in Order), the time reduced to **45 seconds**. Each tool is retrieved approximately **40 times per day**. The labor cost is **₹ 1.5 per second**, and the company operates **280 days per year**. Calculate the **percentage reduction in search time**, the **annual monetary saving**, and the **equivalent full-time employee (FTE) recovered**, assuming 8 working hours per day. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The 5S system is a structured, five-step Japanese workplace organization methodology developed as the foundation of the Toyota Production System. It is a prerequisite for every other Lean tool. The five S's are:

**1. Seiri (Sort):** Eliminates all unnecessary items from the workplace using techniques like the **Red-Tag Method** and a **30-day usage rule**. Removes inventory waste, motion waste, and transport waste. Frees up floor space and reduces clutter that hides process problems.

**2. Seiton (Set in Order):** Arranges the necessary items in a logical, ergonomic, and visually identifiable layout. Applies the **"30-second rule"** — any item must be locatable within 30 seconds. Uses **shadow boards, floor markings, and color codes** as visual management tools. Reduces search waste and motion waste.

**3. Seiso (Shine):** Cleans the workplace, machines, and surroundings as a daily activity, not an event. Cleaning doubles as **inspection** — leaks, loose fasteners, and contamination become visible. Forms the foundation of **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** and reduces defect waste.

**4. Seiketsu (Standardize):** Establishes uniform SOPs, visual standards, checklists, and audit procedures so the first three S's are applied consistently. Uses **photographs, 5S boards, and Andon displays** to maintain visual control. Prevents regression.

**5. Shitsuke (Sustain/Discipline):** Embeds 5S into the organization's culture through **regular audits, training, recognition programs, and management commitment** (*Genchi Genbutsu*). Without Shitsuke, the initiative fails within weeks. Shitsuke addresses the human/behavioral aspect of Lean transformation.

**Waste Elimination Linkage:**
The 5S system directly addresses all **seven muda** (defects, overproduction, waiting, motion, transport, inventory, over-processing) and the eighth waste (*skills underutilization*). The mapping matrix in Section 3.4 demonstrates that the most comprehensive coverage occurs when all five S's are deployed together.

> **Marks:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for each S's role (5 × 1 = 5) + 1 mark for waste-linkage statement = **7 marks**.

---

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Percentage Reduction in Search Time (STR)**

$$
\text{STR} = \frac{T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}}{T_{\text{before}}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{STR} &= \frac{180 - 45}{180} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{135}{180} \times 100 \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**The search time is reduced by 75%.**

**Step 2: Time Saved per Search**

$$
\Delta t = 180 - 45 = 135 \text{ seconds per search}
$$

**Step 3: Daily Time Saved**

$$
T_{\text{day}} = 135 \times 40 = 5{,}400 \text{ seconds/day} = \frac{5{,}400}{3{,}600} = 1.5 \text{ hours/day}
$$

**Step 4: Annual Monetary Saving**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= 1.5 \text{ hr/day} \times 3{,}600 \text{ s/hr} \times 280 \text{ days} \times \text{₹}1.5/\text{s} \\
&= 1.5 \times 3{,}600 \times 280 \times 1.5 \\
&= 22{,}68{,}000 \\
&= \text{₹}22{,}68{,}000 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Equivalent FTE Recovered**

$$
\text{FTE} = \frac{1.5 \text{ hr/day}}{8 \text{ hr/day}} = 0.1875 \text{ FTE} \approx 19\% \text{ of one worker}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 5S-driven Seiton implementation delivers an annual tangible saving of **₹ 22,68,000** and recovers **0.1875 FTE** of productive capacity, while reducing search time by **75%**. This is a powerful ROI justification for 5S investment.

> **Marks:** 1 mark for STR formula + 1 mark for substitution & answer + 1 mark for daily time + 1 mark for annual saving + 1 mark for FTE + 1 mark for conclusion + 1 mark for units/consistency = **7 marks**.

---

### **PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Red-Tag Method** in detail. Explain how it is applied during the Seiri phase, what information is captured on a red tag, and the criteria for final disposition of red-tagged items. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory conducted a 5S audit. The auditor scored the five S's on a scale of 1 to 5 as follows:

| S-Element | Score |
|-----------|-------|
| Seiri | 4.0 |
| Seiton | 3.5 |
| Seiso | 4.5 |
| Seiketsu | 3.0 |
| Shitsuke | 2.0 |

The company assigns **equal weight (0.20)** to each S. Calculate the **weighted 5S audit score** and assess whether the factory passes the **80-point benchmark**. Also identify the **weakest S** and recommend **two corrective actions**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **Red-Tag Method** is a structured visual technique used during the **Seiri (Sort)** phase of 5S to identify and isolate items whose necessity in the workplace is uncertain.

**Application Procedure:**

1. **Identification:** A red tag is attached to any item that fails the "Used in last 30 days?" test or whose purpose is unclear.
2. **Information Captured on the Red Tag:**
   - Item name, description, and quantity
   - Date of tagging
   - Department / location of origin
   - Reason for tagging (broken, obsolete, surplus, unidentified)
   - Name of the person who tagged the item
   - Disposition action (proposed)
3. **Relocation:** The tagged item is moved to a designated **"Red Tag Holding Area"** outside the active workspace.
4. **30-Day Observation Window:** During this period, any employee who genuinely needs the item may claim it by justifying its use.
5. **Final Disposition Criteria:**
   - **Claimed and used** → Returned to workplace.
   - **Not claimed** → Categorized as: (i) Dispose (scrap), (ii) Recycle, (iii) Sell, (iv) Relocate to a central store, or (v) Return to vendor.
6. **Documentation:** Inventory records and asset registers are updated to remove the item from the active workplace.

**Why the Red-Tag Method Works:**
- It creates a **physical and visual** decision boundary, removing the emotional bias of "I might need it someday."
- The 30-day window allows genuine use cases to surface.
- It is **standardized, repeatable, and auditable** — any worker can apply it.
- It enables **quantitative tracking** of waste removal.

> **Marks:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for procedure (steps) + 2 marks for information on the tag + 1 mark for final disposition criteria + 1 mark for significance = **7 marks**.

---

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Write the Weighted Score Formula**

$$
\text{Score} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} S_i \cdot w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i} \times 100
$$

**Step 2: Verify Sum of Weights**

$$
\sum w_i = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Weighted Sum**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum S_i \cdot w_i &= (4.0 \times 0.20) + (3.5 \times 0.20) + (4.5 \times 0.20) + (3.0 \times 0.20) + (2.0 \times 0.20) \\
&= 0.80 + 0.70 + 0.90 + 0.60 + 0.40 \\
&= 3.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Final Score**

$$
\text{Score} = 3.40 \times 100 = 68 \text{ points}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with Benchmark**

$$
68 < 80 \implies \text{Fail — Improvement Required}
$$

**Step 6: Identify the Weakest S**

The lowest score is **Shitsuke (Sustain) = 2.0**, indicating that while the workplace may be physically organized, the discipline to maintain it is missing.

**Step 7: Recommend Two Corrective Actions for Shitsuke**

1. **Implement a structured 5S audit schedule** — weekly audits by a cross-functional team, monthly audits by senior management, with results published on a 5S scoreboard visible to all employees. Tie audit scores to **team and individual KPIs**.
2. **Launch a 5S reward & recognition program** — recognize "5S Champions" monthly, conduct 5S competitions between cells/departments, and integrate 5S compliance into the **employee performance appraisal system**. Provide **refresher training** every quarter.

**Conclusion:** The factory scores **68/100**, falling below the **80-point benchmark**. The weakest link is **Shitsuke (Sustain)**, and focused efforts on audits, training, and recognition will elevate the score above benchmark within 2–3 audit cycles.

> **Marks:** 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for sum of weights + 1 mark for weighted sum + 1 mark for final score + 1 mark for comparison + 1 mark for weakest-S identification + 1 mark for corrective actions = **7 marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** list only the 5 Japanese terms without explaining *how* each S eliminates waste. The examiner expects a clear cause-effect linkage, e.g., "Seiri → eliminates inventory waste."
> 2. **Do NOT** skip the **units and final conclusion** in numerical problems. Marks are explicitly allocated for unit consistency and a concluding statement.
> 3. **Do NOT** forget to mention **Shitsuke** as a separate and equal S. Many students list only 4 S's, losing 1–2 marks immediately.
> 4. **Do NOT** confuse **5S with 5 Why's or 5M's** — they are different tools. 5S is workplace organization; 5 Why's is root cause analysis; 5M's (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement) is a fishbone diagram input.
> 5. **In numerical questions**, always show the formula first, then substitution, then the numerical result, then a concluding sentence. Examiners award "method marks" even if arithmetic slips occur.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bulletins — Read Twice Before Exam:**

- ✅ 5S stands for **Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke** — the foundation of Lean Manufacturing and Toyota Production System.
- ✅ **Seiri (Sort):** Remove unnecessary items. Tool: **Red-Tag Method** with a **30-day observation window**.
- ✅ **Seiton (Set in Order):** A place for everything. Tool: **Shadow boards, floor markings, color codes, 30-second rule**.
- ✅ **Seiso (Shine):** Clean as inspection. Foundation of **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)**.
- ✅ **Seiketsu (Standardize):** SOPs, checklists, visual standards, 5S boards. Prevents regression.
- ✅ **Shitsuke (Sustain):** Discipline, culture, audits, training, recognition. **Most failure-prone S** in real implementations.
- ✅ 5S addresses all **seven (or eight) muda** when implemented in full — refer to the analytical matrix in Section 3.4.
- ✅ Key quantitative metrics: **5S Audit Score (≥ 80 benchmark)**, **Search Time Reduction**, **Floor Space Liberation**, **Inventory Carrying Cost Saved**, **5S Compliance Rate**.
- ✅ 5S is the **gateway** to **Kanban, Poka-Yoke, TPM, SMED, Heijunka, Jidoka** — every other Lean tool sits on the 5S foundation.
- ✅ Real-world users: **Toyota, Ford, Boeing, Tata Motors, hospitals (5S in OTs), software firms (5S for code), warehouses (5S for slotting)**.
- ✅ **In KTU 14-mark answers**, structure your response as: Definition → Five S's explained individually with waste linkage → Real-world example → Conclusion (this is a KTU high-scoring pattern).
- ✅ **In KTU numericals**, remember the STR formula, the weighted audit-score formula, and the inventory-carrying-cost formula. These three alone cover 90% of numerical questions on 5S.
- ✅ **Mnemonic to remember the order:** **"Some Students In Exams Succeed"** (Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke). Or simply: **"Sort, Straighten, Shine, Standardize, Sustain"**.
- ✅ 5S is **qualitative-led, quantitative-supported** — your answer should always blend **conceptual explanation** with **measurable metrics** for maximum KTU marks.
- ✅ 5S is **not a one-time project** — it is a **continuous improvement loop** powered by the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle.
- ✅ **Origin:** Hiroyuki Hirano (Japanese industrial engineer) popularized 5S in the 1980s through his book *"5 Pillars of the Visual Workplace."*
- ✅ **Connection to other topics in Module 2:** 5S is the prerequisite for **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)**, **Poka-Yoke (Error Proofing)**, and **Visual Management** — all common KTU follow-up questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme values **applied understanding** over rote memorization. Always connect your 5S answer to a **real manufacturing scenario** (e.g., a CNC shop floor, an automobile assembly line, a hospital OT) and **quantify the benefit** wherever possible. This dual approach of *qualitative narrative + quantitative proof* is the hallmark of a high-scoring KTU answer script.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SORT (SEIRI) – The First 'S' of the 5S Methodology

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sort (Japanese: Seiri / 整理)** is the foundational step of the **5S Lean Methodology** that involves the systematic identification, segregation, and **elimination of all unnecessary items** from the workplace, retaining only those tools, materials, and equipment that are **genuinely required** for current operations. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Sort is the "clearing-out" discipline that distinguishes *value-adding* items from *non-value-adding* items in the **gemba** (the actual workplace).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sort** is also called *"Organisation"* or *"Tidiness"* in some Western adaptations of the 5S framework, though the Japanese term **Seiri** remains the universal academic reference in lean manufacturing literature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy: Cleaning Your Bedroom Closet**
> Imagine you open your wardrobe. You find clothes you haven't worn in **3 years**, broken accessories, expired cosmetics, and only a few outfits you regularly wear. Sort is the act of **removing everything you don't need** — the old, the broken, the rarely used — so that the items you actually need are **easy to find, accessible, and visible**. Now apply this to a factory floor: the unnecessary jigs, scrap materials, broken tools, and outdated documents are removed so that operators can work efficiently without hunting for what they need.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Outcome of Sort:** A workplace that contains **only the right items, in the right quantity, at the right location** — eliminating the **Seven Wastes (Muda)** in their physical form: defects, overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, and motion.

### 1.3 Why Sort is the *First* Step (Hierarchical Logic)

| Order | 5S Step | Japanese | Purpose |
|-------|---------|----------|---------|
| **1** | **Sort** | **Seiri** | **Remove what is NOT needed** |
| 2 | Set in Order | Seiton | Arrange what IS needed |
| 3 | Shine | Seiso | Clean the workspace |
| 4 | Standardize | Seiketsu | Establish norms |
| 5 | Sustain | Shitsuke | Maintain discipline |

> Sort **must precede** the other 4S steps because trying to organize, clean, or standardize a workplace **filled with unnecessary items** is fundamentally inefficient — you cannot "arrange" what should not be there in the first place.

### 1.4 Standard KTU Metrics & Key Performance Indicators

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Measured After Sort Implementation:**
> - **Floor Space Utilisation Efficiency:** Target improvement of **30%–50%**
> - **Search Time Reduction:** Typical reduction of **40%–60%** in time spent looking for tools
> - **Inventory Carrying Cost Reduction:** Standard industry benchmark of **20%–25%** annual savings
> - **Number of Red-Tagged Items Removed:** Direct measure of Sort activity
> - **Workplace Accident Rate:** Typical reduction of **15%–25%** post-Sort
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5-Step Sort Procedure (Operational Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Sort methodology follows a strict, sequential **5-step procedure** that is commonly tested in KTU board examinations:

### Step 1: Identify the Work Area & Define Scope
- Determine the **gemba** (target workplace) — workstation, tool crib, warehouse, office desk
- **Photograph the area "before"** Sort for baseline comparison
- Form a cross-functional **Sort team** (operators + supervisors + engineers)

### Step 2: Apply the "Red Tag" System
- Every item in the workspace is evaluated using a **Red Tag** (a standardized visual marker)
- Items are categorized based on **Frequency of Use (FoU)** and **Necessity (N)**

### Step 3: Decide Disposition
Each item receives one of three decisions:
- **KEEP** — needed frequently (used daily/weekly)
- **RELOCATE** — needed but not in this area (move to storage)
- **DISCARD** — broken, expired, obsolete, or rarely used

### Step 4: Remove Unnecessary Items
- Physically relocate or discard all items not classified as "KEEP"
- Update inventory records

### Step 5: Document & Validate
- Measure the space freed, count removed items
- Update **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**
- Photograph the area "after" Sort

## 2.2 The Red Tag Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Red Tag Decision Matrix** is a 2×3 evaluation grid used in Sort to classify every item:

| Frequency of Use \ Necessity | **Definitely Needed** | **Maybe Needed** | **Definitely Not Needed** |
|------------------------------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Used Daily** | KEEP (in work area) | KEEP (in work area) | DISCARD |
| **Used Weekly** | KEEP (in work area) | RELOCATE (to store) | DISCARD |
| **Used Rarely / Never** | RELOCATE (to store) | RELOCATE (to store) | DISCARD |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU examiners frequently ask: *"What is the action for an item that is 'Maybe Needed' and 'Used Daily'?"* — The correct answer is **KEEP** because usage frequency overrides uncertainty in necessity.

## 2.3 The Seven Categories of Unnecessary Items (Sort Targets)

> [!NOTE]
> Sort targets fall into **7 universal categories** that KTU students must memorize:

1. **Defective / Broken Tools** — non-functional equipment
2. **Obsolete / Outdated Documents** — old drawings, superseded procedures
3. **Excess Inventory / Surplus Raw Materials** — beyond reorder point
4. **Personal Items** — non-work belongings
5. **Scrap / Waste Material** — chips, offcuts, byproducts
6. **Duplicate Items** — multiple copies of the same tool
7. **Rarely Used Items** — used less than once per month

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU board exam numerical questions on Sort:

| # | Formula / Metric | LaTeX Expression | Description |
|---|------------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Space Liberation Ratio (SLR)** | $SLR = \dfrac{A_{\text{freed}}}{A_{\text{original}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of total floor space freed after Sort |
| 2 | **Item Removal Ratio (IRR)** | $IRR = \dfrac{N_{\text{removed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of items eliminated from the area |
| 3 | **Sort Cost-Benefit Ratio** | $CBR = \dfrac{C_{\text{remove}} + C_{\text{store}}}{S_{\text{space}} + S_{\text{search}} + S_{\text{safety}}}$ | Where $C$ = costs, $S$ = savings; must be $\geq 1$ for viability |
| 4 | **Pareto Vital Few / Useful Many** | $V\% = \dfrac{N_{\text{essential}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | The vital few items causing 80% of activity |
| 5 | **Search Time Reduction (STR)** | $STR = \dfrac{T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}}{T_{\text{before}}} \times 100\%$ | Time saved locating tools/materials |
| 6 | **Frequency Index (FI)** | $FI = \dfrac{U_{\text{period}}}{P_{\text{period}}}$ | Where $U$ = uses per period, $P$ = period length |
| 7 | **Inventory Carrying Cost (ICC)** | $ICC = P \times r \times t$ | $P$ = item price, $r$ = holding rate, $t$ = time |

## 2.5 Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule) Applied to Sort

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Logic in Sort:** Typically, **20% of items** in a workspace account for **80% of usage frequency**. Sort uses this principle to identify the **"Vital Few"** (items to KEEP at the workstation) versus the **"Useful Many"** (items to RELOCATE to stores).

$$\text{Vital Few Share} = \frac{\text{Items Used} \geq 80\% \text{ of Time}}{\text{Total Items in Work Area}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Applications of Sort:**
> - **Automotive Assembly Lines** (Toyota, Maruti) — eliminating excess WIP between stations
> - **CNC Machining Cells** — removing duplicate cutting tools, scrap chips
> - **Pharmaceutical Warehouses** — segregating expired drugs (FEFO/FIFO compliance)
> - **Aerospace Tool Cribs** — eliminating obsolete calibrated tools
> - **Hospital Operating Theatres** — keeping only instruments needed for scheduled surgeries
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Space Liberation Ratio (SLR) — Exhaustive Derivation

**Problem Context:** A workstation originally occupies **$A_{\text{original}} = 50$ m²**. After a Sort activity, **$A_{\text{freed}} = 18$ m²** of floor space becomes available for productive use. Calculate the **Space Liberation Ratio (SLR)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$SLR = \frac{A_{\text{freed}}}{A_{\text{original}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values:**

$$SLR = \frac{18 \text{ m}^2}{50 \text{ m}^2} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division:**

$$\frac{18}{50} = 0.36$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage:**

$$SLR = 0.36 \times 100\% = 36\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** **36%** of the original workstation floor space has been liberated by Sort, which exceeds the KTU benchmark of **30%–50%** for a successful Sort implementation.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Item Removal Ratio (IRR)

**Problem Context:** A tool crib contained **$N_{\text{total}} = 250$ items** before Sort. After applying the Red Tag system, **$N_{\text{removed}} = 75$ items** were discarded. Calculate the **Item Removal Ratio (IRR)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — State the governing formula:**

$$IRR = \frac{N_{\text{removed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$IRR = \frac{75}{250} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the fraction:**

$$\frac{75}{250} = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage:**

$$IRR = 0.30 \times 100\% = 30\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** **30%** of items in the tool crib were unnecessary — a typical industry benchmark confirming a productive Sort activity.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Search Time Reduction (STR)

**Problem Context:** Operators spent an average of **$T_{\text{before}} = 25$ minutes/day** searching for tools before Sort. After Sort and proper arrangement, the search time reduced to **$T_{\text{after}} = 10$ minutes/day**. Calculate the **Search Time Reduction (STR)** percentage.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$STR = \frac{T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}}{T_{\text{before}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator (time saved):**

$$T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}} = 25 - 10 = 15 \text{ minutes/day}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the equation:**

$$STR = \frac{15}{25} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Simplify and compute:**

$$\frac{15}{25} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$

$$STR = 0.60 \times 100\% = 60\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Operators now save **15 minutes/day** each — a **60% reduction** in non-value-adding search time, well within the KTU expected benchmark of **40%–60%**.

---

## 3.4 Derivative 4: Pareto Analysis for Sort (Vital Few Identification)

**Problem Context:** A workstation contains **100 items** in total. Frequency-of-use analysis reveals that **15 items** are used in 80% of operations, while the remaining **85 items** are used in only 20% of operations. Calculate:
- (a) Vital Few Share
- (b) Useful Many Share
- (c) Comment on the result using the 80/20 rule

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Apply the Vital Few formula:**

$$V\% = \frac{N_{\text{essential}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$$

$$V\% = \frac{15}{100} \times 100\% = 15\%$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Useful Many formula:**

$$U\% = \frac{N_{\text{non-essential}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \frac{85}{100} \times 100\% = 85\%$$

**Step 3 — Composite analytical matrix:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Vital Few (15\% of items)} &\rightarrow \text{Used 80\% of the time} \rightarrow \text{KEEP at workstation} \\
\text{Useful Many (85\% of items)} &\rightarrow \text{Used 20\% of the time} \rightarrow \text{RELOCATE to store}
\end{aligned}

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The 80/20 Pareto principle holds perfectly. Sort should retain the **15% Vital Few** in the immediate work area and relocate the **85% Useful Many** to a controlled store, dramatically improving visibility and accessibility.

---

## 3.5 Derivative 5: Cost-Benefit Analysis of Sort

**Problem Context:** A company is evaluating whether to implement Sort on a production line. The following data is available:

- Cost of removing & relocating items: **$C_{\text{remove}} = \text{₹} 50{,}000$**
- Cost of new storage racks: **$C_{\text{store}} = \text{₹} 30{,}000$**
- Annual savings from freed space rental: **$S_{\text{space}} = \text{₹} 60{,}000$**
- Annual savings from reduced search time: **$S_{\text{search}} = \text{₹} 25{,}000$**
- Annual savings from accident reduction: **$S_{\text{safety}} = \text{₹} 15{,}000$**

Calculate the **Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR)** and determine if Sort is viable.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the total cost:**

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{remove}} + C_{\text{store}} = 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 = \text{₹} 80{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total savings:**

$$S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{space}} + S_{\text{search}} + S_{\text{safety}} = 60{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 15{,}000 = \text{₹} 100{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cost-Benefit Ratio formula:**

$$CBR = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{S_{\text{total}}} = \frac{80{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.80$$

**Step 4 — Decision logic:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{If } CBR < 1 &\Rightarrow \text{Sort is ECONOMICALLY VIABLE (Savings > Costs)} \\
\text{If } CBR > 1 &\Rightarrow \text{Sort is NOT VIABLE (Costs > Savings)} \\
\text{If } CBR = 1 &\Rightarrow \text{Break-even}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Since $CBR = 0.80 < 1$, the Sort activity is **economically justified** with a **net annual benefit of ₹ 20,000**.

---

## 3.6 Composite Analytical Matrix — Sort Decision Outcomes

| Decision Category | Action | Storage Location | Typical Frequency | % of Items (Avg.) |
|------------------|--------|------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **KEEP** | Retain at workstation | Gemba | Daily / Weekly | 15%–25% |
| **RELOCATE** | Move to central store | Warehouse / Tool Crib | Monthly | 50%–65% |
| **DISCARD** | Dispose, scrap, recycle | Out of facility | Never / Rarely | 15%–30% |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** A well-executed Sort typically results in a **70/30 split** — approximately **70% of items are removed or relocated**, and only **30% are retained** in the immediate work area.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete Sort (Seiri) Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Define Work Area]) --> B[Photograph 'Before' State]
    B --> C[Form Cross-Functional Sort Team]
    C --> D[Inventory All Items in Gemba]
    D --> E{Apply Red Tag System}
    E --> F[Evaluate Frequency of Use]
    E --> G[Evaluate Necessity]
    F --> H{Decision Matrix}
    G --> H
    H --> I1[KEEP - In Work Area]
    H --> I2[RELOCATE - To Store]
    H --> I3[DISCARD - Recycle or Scrap]
    I1 --> J[Document Decisions]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Physically Remove Items]
    K --> L[Update Inventory Records]
    L --> M[Photograph 'After' State]
    M --> N[Calculate KPIs: SLR, IRR, STR]
    N --> O{Sort Successful?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Move to Step 2: Set in Order]
    O -->|No| D
    P --> Q([End: Sort Complete])
```

## 4.2 The Red Tag System — Information Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Red Tag Attached to Item] --> B[Item Identity & Description]
    A --> C[Date Tagged]
    A --> D[Department / Owner]
    A --> E[Reason for Tagging]
    A --> F[Decision Required]
    A --> G[Action Taken & Date]

    B --> B1[Name, Code, Qty]
    C --> C1[Tag Issue Date]
    D --> D1[Department Name]
    E --> E1[Broken, Unused, Obsolete, Excess]
    F --> F1[KEEP, RELOCATE, DISCARD]
    G --> G1[Completion Date & Signature]
```

## 4.3 Sort Decision Matrix — Visual Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Item in Work Area]) --> Q1{Is the item NEEDED?}
    Q1 -->|Definitely YES| A1[KEEP at Work Area]
    Q1 -->|Maybe| Q2{Used Daily or Weekly?}
    Q1 -->|Definitely NO| A3[DISCARD]

    Q2 -->|YES| A1
    Q2 -->|NO| A2[RELOCATE to Storage]

    A1 --> End1([Result: Item Stays in Gemba])
    A2 --> End2([Result: Item Goes to Store])
    A3 --> End3([Result: Item is Removed])

    style A1 fill:#90EE90
    style A2 fill:#FFD700
    style A3 fill:#FF6B6B
```

## 4.4 The 5S Pyramid — Position of Sort

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S5["Sustain (Shitsuke) - TOP"] --> S4["Standardize (Seiketsu)"]
    S4 --> S3["Shine (Seiso)"]
    S3 --> S2["Set in Order (Seiton)"]
    S2 --> S1["Sort (Seiri) - FOUNDATION"]

    style S1 fill:#FF6B6B
    style S2 fill:#FFA500
    style S3 fill:#FFD700
    style S4 fill:#90EE90
    style S5 fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram illustrates that **Sort is the FOUNDATION** of the entire 5S pyramid. Without a successful Sort, the subsequent steps (Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke) cannot be effectively executed.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define Sort (Seiri) in the context of 5S Lean Manufacturing. Mention any TWO benefits of implementing Sort on a shop floor.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Sort (Japanese: *Seiri*) is the first step of the 5S methodology that involves the **systematic identification, segregation, and removal of all unnecessary items** from the workplace, retaining only those items that are essential for current operations.

> [!TIP]
> **Two Benefits of Sort:**
> 1. **Improved Productivity:** Operators save significant time as essential tools become easier to locate, reducing non-value-adding search activity. Typical search time reduction is **40%–60%**.
> 2. **Enhanced Safety:** Removing clutter, broken tools, and obstructions significantly reduces the risk of workplace accidents and injuries, with typical accident rate reductions of **15%–25%**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is the "Red Tag" system in Sort? Explain its purpose with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Red Tag System Definition:** The Red Tag system is a **visual labelling technique** used during the Sort step to identify and mark items whose status in the workplace is **uncertain or unnecessary**. A red-coloured tag is attached to the item, and information such as item name, date tagged, reason, and proposed action is recorded.

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** In a CNC machine tool crib, an old calibration jig that has not been used in **18 months** is tagged red. The tag records: *Item: Dial Gauge Jig-07 | Date: 12-Jan-2024 | Reason: Not used for 18 months | Action: DISCARD*. After a designated waiting period (typically **30 days**) without a claim, the item is permanently removed.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with an **internal choice**. Students must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question A (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the step-by-step procedure for implementing Sort (Seiri) in a manufacturing organization. Discuss the Red Tag Decision Matrix in detail with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The Sort (Seiri) procedure is executed in **5 systematic steps**:

**Step 1 — Define the Work Area (Gemba) and Form a Team:**
The first step is to clearly define the boundary of the workplace that will undergo Sort — this may be a workstation, tool crib, warehouse, or entire shop floor. A **cross-functional team** is formed comprising operators, supervisors, maintenance staff, and a lean facilitator to ensure holistic evaluation.

**Step 2 — Inventory and Photograph the Area:**
Every item in the area is catalogued, and a **"before" photograph** is captured for visual documentation and post-implementation comparison.

**Step 3 — Apply the Red Tag System:**
Each item is evaluated based on two criteria — **Necessity (N)** and **Frequency of Use (FoU)** — and a red tag is attached to items that are questionable. The Red Tag Decision Matrix is as follows:

| FoU \ N | **Definitely Needed** | **Maybe Needed** | **Definitely Not Needed** |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Daily** | KEEP | KEEP | DISCARD |
| **Weekly** | KEEP | RELOCATE | DISCARD |
| **Rarely** | RELOCATE | RELOCATE | DISCARD |

**Step 4 — Dispose, Relocate, or Retain Items:**
Based on the matrix, items are sorted into three physical zones: **KEEP zone** (workstation), **RELOCATE zone** (central store), and **DISCARD zone** (scrap area).

**Step 5 — Document, Measure, and Validate:**
KPIs such as **Space Liberation Ratio (SLR)**, **Item Removal Ratio (IRR)**, and **Search Time Reduction (STR)** are computed. An "after" photograph validates the improvement.

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** A maintenance tool room contains 200 items. After Sort, 60 items (30%) are discarded as broken/obsolete, 100 items (50%) are relocated to a central store, and 40 items (20%) are kept in the tool room. The SLR achieved is **35%**, indicating a successful Sort.

---

**Question A (b) — 7 Marks:**
**A workstation originally occupied 80 m² of floor space. After a Sort activity, 28 m² of space was freed. Calculate the Space Liberation Ratio (SLR) and Item Removal Ratio (IRR) if 90 items were removed out of a total of 300 items. Comment on the effectiveness of the Sort activity.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Space Liberation Ratio (SLR):**

$$SLR = \frac{A_{\text{freed}}}{A_{\text{original}}} \times 100\% = \frac{28 \text{ m}^2}{80 \text{ m}^2} \times 100\%$$

$$SLR = 0.35 \times 100\% = 35\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Item Removal Ratio (IRR):**

$$IRR = \frac{N_{\text{removed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \frac{90}{300} \times 100\%$$

$$IRR = 0.30 \times 100\% = 30\%$$

**Step 3 — Effectiveness Analysis:**

| Metric | Calculated Value | KTU Benchmark | Verdict |
|--------|------------------|---------------|---------|
| SLR | 35% | 30%–50% | ✓ Within range |
| IRR | 30% | 25%–40% | ✓ Within range |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Both the SLR (35%) and IRR (30%) fall **within the KTU-accepted benchmark range** for a successful Sort activity. The Sort has effectively eliminated unnecessary items and freed productive floor space, validating the implementation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question B (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Discuss the concept of Pareto Analysis (80/20 Rule) as applied to the Sort (Seiri) step. How does it help in identifying the "Vital Few" vs "Useful Many"? Provide a numerical illustration.**

**Model Answer:**

**Conceptual Framework:**
The **Pareto Principle**, named after Italian economist Vilfredo Pareto, states that roughly **80% of effects come from 20% of causes**. In the context of Sort, this principle is applied to determine which items are used most frequently and should be retained at the workstation (Vital Few) versus those that are rarely used and should be relocated (Useful Many).

**Application to Sort:**
In any workplace, a small subset of items (**Vital Few** — approximately 15%–25% of total items) accounts for the majority of operational use (approximately 80% of activity frequency). The remaining larger subset (**Useful Many** — approximately 75%–85% of total items) is used infrequently (only 20% of activity frequency).

**Numerical Illustration:**

A CNC workstation contains **$N_{\text{total}} = 200$ items**. Analysis shows that **$N_{\text{vital}} = 40$ items** are used 80% of the time, while **$N_{\text{many}} = 160$ items** are used only 20% of the time.

**Step 1 — Compute the Vital Few Share:**

$$V\% = \frac{N_{\text{vital}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \frac{40}{200} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Useful Many Share:**

$$U\% = \frac{N_{\text{many}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \frac{160}{200} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

**Step 3 — Decision Outcome:**

\begin{aligned}
\text{Vital Few (20\% of items)} &\rightarrow \text{KEEP at the workstation (80\% of usage)} \\
\text{Useful Many (80\% of items)} &\rightarrow \text{RELOCATE to storage (20\% of usage)}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The Pareto analysis confirms that retaining only **40 vital items** at the workstation can satisfy **80% of operational needs**, while the remaining **160 items** can be safely relocated, dramatically improving workspace clarity and efficiency.

---

**Question B (b) — 7 Marks:**
**A company wants to evaluate the economic viability of implementing Sort on a shop floor. The estimated cost of removing items and creating new storage is ₹ 1,20,000. The expected annual savings are: rent from freed space ₹ 80,000; reduced search time ₹ 50,000; and reduced accidents ₹ 30,000. Calculate the Cost-Benefit Ratio and comment on the viability.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Total Cost:**

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{remove}} + C_{\text{store}} = \text{₹ } 1{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Total Annual Savings:**

$$S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{space}} + S_{\text{search}} + S_{\text{safety}} = 80{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1{,}60{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Cost-Benefit Ratio:**

$$CBR = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{S_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{1{,}60{,}000} = 0.75$$

**Step 4 — Viability Decision:**

\begin{aligned}
CBR = 0.75 &< 1 \Rightarrow \text{Sort is ECONOMICALLY VIABLE} \\
\text{Net Annual Benefit} &= S_{\text{total}} - C_{\text{total}} = 1{,}60{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 40{,}000
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** With a CBR of **0.75** (less than 1) and a **net annual benefit of ₹ 40,000**, the Sort implementation is **strongly recommended**. The investment will be recovered within the **first year**, after which the savings become pure profit.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Sort" with "Shine"** — Sort is about *removing unnecessary items*, NOT cleaning. Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by mixing up the 5S steps.
> 2. **Incorrect Red Tag Matrix Application** — The "KEEP" decision for "Maybe Needed + Daily Use" is often marked wrong. *Usage frequency overrides uncertainty*.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Questions** — Always specify **%** for SLR, IRR, STR and **₹** (or appropriate currency) for cost-benefit values. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
> 4. **Writing "Seiri" without explanation** — While the Japanese term is acceptable, KTU examiners expect the *English meaning* alongside the Japanese term in definitions.
> 5. **Skipping the "Team Formation" step** — Many students omit Step 1 (team formation) of the Sort procedure, losing 1 mark for incomplete procedural answers.
> 6. **Not comparing results with KTU benchmarks** — Numerical answers must always be **interpreted** (e.g., "SLR = 35% which is within the benchmark range of 30%–50%"). Uninterpreted answers lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SORT (Seiri):**

- **Definition:** Sort = Systematic removal of unnecessary items from the workplace; the *first* step of 5S.
- **Japanese Term:** Seiri (整理)
- **Purpose:** Retain only essential items in the gemba; eliminate all forms of waste (Muda) in physical form.
- **5-Step Procedure:** (1) Define area & form team → (2) Photograph & inventory → (3) Apply Red Tags → (4) Dispose/Relocate/Retain → (5) Document & measure.
- **Red Tag System:** Visual marker to identify questionable items; records item details, date, reason, and proposed action.
- **Three Dispositions:** KEEP (in workstation), RELOCATE (to store), DISCARD (scrap/recycle).
- **Pareto Application:** Vital Few (~20% of items) account for ~80% of usage; Useful Many (~80% of items) account for ~20%.
- **Key KPIs:** SLR (Space Liberation Ratio), IRR (Item Removal Ratio), STR (Search Time Reduction), CBR (Cost-Benefit Ratio).
- **KTU Benchmark Ranges:** SLR = 30%–50%, IRR = 25%–40%, STR = 40%–60%, Accident reduction = 15%–25%.
- **CBR Rule:** If $CBR < 1$ → Sort is **viable**; if $CBR > 1$ → Sort is **not viable**.
- **Sort is FOUNDATIONAL** — must precede Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, and Sustain.
- **Common Mistake:** Sort ≠ Cleaning. Sort is about *identification and removal*, not *cleaning*.
- **Real-world Impact:** Improved safety, reduced search time, lower inventory carrying cost, better visual management.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Set in Order
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEARN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Set in Order

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Set in Order (Seiton – 整頓)** is the **second pillar of the 5S methodology** in Lean Manufacturing, which follows *Sort* (Seiri). It is formally defined as a systematic workplace organization discipline that establishes **"a place for every item, and every item in its place"**, with the primary engineering objective of ensuring that all tools, materials, equipment, and documents required for a task can be **located, retrieved, and returned within a maximum of 30 seconds**, without any search, hesitation, or movement ambiguity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *Set in Order is the practice of arranging items in a manner that promotes the easy *selection, retrieval, return, and storage* of materials, tools, and documents, using visual controls such that the workplace becomes self-explanatory and self-ordering.*

The Japanese term **Seiton (整頓)** is composed of two kanji:
- **整 (Sei)** = Order, organize, arrange
- **頓 (Ton)** = Pause, settle, put down (tidily)

Together, the philosophy emphasizes *settling items in an orderly, pause-worthy way* so that they are always ready for use.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine opening your **wardrobe** in the morning.

- If all your clothes are **piled in a heap** (a "Sort" not done), you spend 10 minutes digging.
- If clothes are present but **mixed without order** (Sort done, but Set in Order *not* done), you still waste time deciding *where the black shirt is*.
- However, if **shirts are on the left hanger row, trousers on the right, ties color-coded, shoes on a labeled rack on the floor** — you find *everything in seconds*, without thinking.

That wardrobe is **"Set in Order."** Now apply the same logic to a CNC shop floor, an assembly line, or a hospital emergency room — and you have Seiton at industrial scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sort (Seiri)** asks: *"What do we need?"*
> **Set in Order (Seiton)** asks: *"Where is it, and how fast can I get it?"*
> Without Sort, Set in Order is impossible (you cannot order unnecessary items).

## 1.3 Core Engineering Metrics & Standards

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **"30-second rule"** | **30 s** | Maximum retrieval time for any tool/item |
| **Walking distance** | **Minimize** | Reduce operator motion waste |
| **Visual control compliance** | **100 %** | All items have designated, marked locations |
| **Search time reduction** | **> 80 %** | Compared to pre-Seiton baseline |
| **Floor space freed** | **20–30 %** | After combined Sort + Set in Order |

> [!TIP]
> The 5S philosophy treats the **workplace as an information carrier**. A well-Set-in-Order floor *talks* to the operator — no one needs to ask "where is X?" or "where do I put X back?" The visual environment itself answers the question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Seiton (Step-by-Step Breakdown)

Set in Order is not random tidying. It is a **structured engineering process** applied *after* Sort has removed unnecessary items. The logical sequence is:

### Step 1 — Frequency-of-Use Analysis (FUA)
Items are classified by **how often they are used** in a given shift.

- **High-frequency** (multiple times per hour) → Placed at the **operator's normal working arc (P-point)**, i.e., within 25–50 cm of the hands, at waist-to-shoulder height.
- **Medium-frequency** (multiple times per shift) → Placed at the **point of use** but on dedicated racks or shadow boards.
- **Low-frequency** (once per week or less) → Placed in **centralized storage** with clear labeling.

> **Why?** Because **70 % of motion waste** in a typical Indian SME shop floor comes from reaching, bending, and walking for *high-frequency* items. Fixing their position gives the highest ROI.

### Step 2 — Decide the Storage Location
Apply the **"nearest-neighbor" principle**: the *next user* of an item is the *current user*. Thus, the item should be located exactly where the next use occurs, not in a generic stockroom.

### Step 3 — Decide the Storage Method
Choose a method that makes the item's *presence, absence, and quantity* instantly visible:

- **Shadow boards** for hand tools.
- **Outlined floor markings** (tape, paint) for pallets, bins, machines.
- **Vertical racks** for long items (pipes, bars).
- **Color-coded bins** for fasteners.

### Step 4 — Apply Visual Controls
Visual controls (jidoka kaizen tools) are the *language* of Seiton. They include:

1. **Floor markings** (yellow/black safety zones, white walkways, green/blue work zones)
2. **Labels and signboards** (bilingual, photo + text)
3. **Shadow boards** (silhouette of tool painted on board — gap instantly shows missing tool)
4. **Color coding** (red = defective/scrap, blue = in-process, green = finished/OK, yellow = caution)
5. **Kanban cards / FIFO lanes** (for material flow)
6. **Andon lights** (status indicators)

### Step 5 — Standardize & Sustain
- Document the layout (a **"Seiton map"** or layout drawing).
- Photograph the standard.
- Train all operators.
- Integrate with the daily 5S audit.

## 2.2 The 30-Second Rule — Quantitative Formulation

The retrieval time for any item in a Set-in-Order workplace must satisfy:

$$
T_{\text{retrieve}} \leq T_{\text{max}} = 30 \text{ seconds}
$$

where:

$$
T_{\text{retrieve}} = T_{\text{see}} + T_{\text{decide}} + T_{\text{move}} + T_{\text{grasp}}
$$

- $T_{\text{see}}$ = time to *visually identify* the item's location (reduced by visual controls)
- $T_{\text{decide}}$ = decision time (reduced by standardized layout)
- $T_{\text{move}}$ = walking/reaching time (reduced by FUA + ergonomic placement)
- $T_{\text{grasp}}$ = picking/returning time (reduced by shadow boards, FIFO lanes)

A non-Seiton workplace typically exhibits $T_{\text{retrieve}} \geq 90$ s. Post-Seiton, this drops to **< 30 s**, giving a productivity gain of:

$$
\text{Gain \%} = \frac{T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}}{T_{\text{before}}} \times 100
$$

## 2.3 The Spaghetti Diagram (Before/After Visualization)

A **Spaghetti diagram** traces the operator's movement path on a layout. Crossing, looping paths indicate poor Set in Order.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exams, *spaghetti diagrams* are a frequent 5- or 7-mark sub-question. Memorize that **after Seiton, the path should approach a straight line**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

| Element | KTU-Standard Definition | Typical Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Seiton (整頓)** | "A place for everything, everything in its place" | 1 mark (definition) |
| **30-Second Rule** | Any item retrievable in ≤ 30 s | 2 marks |
| **Visual Control** | A device that conveys information at a glance | 3 marks |
| **Shadow Board** | Painted silhouette of a tool on a board; gap = missing tool | 3 marks |
| **Floor Marking Colors** | Yellow/black = caution, White = walkway, Green = safety, Red = defect | 2 marks |
| **FUA** | Frequency-of-Use Analysis | 3 marks |
| **Spaghetti Diagram** | Movement-trace diagram to detect motion waste | 5–7 marks |
| **FIFO Lane** | First-In-First-Out storage lane (inclined racks) | 2 marks |
| **P-point** | Prime Point — ergonomic placement of high-frequency items | 3 marks |

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Toyota)**: Tool shadow boards at every assembly station; a missing wrench shows up as a visible gap within 2 seconds.
- **Hospitals (AIIMS, Apollo)**: Color-coded crash carts — drugs arranged in identical order across all departments for muscle-memory retrieval.
- **Aviation (Boeing, HAL)**: Tool control programs (FOD prevention) use shadow boards + T-bar markings; no tool is left inside an engine.
- **Catering (5-star hotels)**: Kitchen mise-en-place mirrors Seiton precisely.

> [!TIP]
> Set in Order is the **only 5S pillar that directly generates "Information at a glance"** — a concept Jidoka and Heijunka depend upon.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Productivity Gain from Implementing Seiton

### Problem (KTU-Style Numerical)
A machining cell currently requires an operator to spend an average of **$T_{\text{before}} = 110$ seconds per cycle** searching for and retrieving tools, fixtures, and drawings. After implementing Set in Order, the retrieval time drops to **$T_{\text{after}} = 25$ seconds**. The cell completes **120 cycles per shift (8 hours)**. Calculate:

1. The percentage reduction in retrieval time.
2. The total time saved per shift.
3. The equivalent additional production capacity (in cycles).
4. The productivity gain factor.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Percentage reduction in retrieval time**

$$
\Delta T \% = \frac{T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}}}{T_{\text{before}}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\Delta T \% = \frac{110 - 25}{110} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta T \% = \frac{85}{110} \times 100 = 77.27 \,\%
$$

> *Interpretation: Set in Order eliminates ~77 % of search/retrieval waste — a typical figure in Indian manufacturing audits.*

**Step 2 — Total time saved per shift**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{before}} - T_{\text{after}} = 110 - 25 = 85 \text{ s/cycle}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{shift}} = \Delta T_{\text{cycle}} \times N_{\text{cycles}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{shift}} = 85 \times 120 = 10{,}200 \text{ s}
$$

Converting to minutes:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{shift}} = \frac{10{,}200}{60} = 170 \text{ minutes} = 2 \text{ hours } 50 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent additional production capacity**

If a single cycle of value-added work takes (for instance) $t_{\text{VA}} = 240$ s, the freed time enables:

$$
N_{\text{extra}} = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{shift}}}{t_{\text{VA}}} = \frac{10{,}200}{240} = 42.5 \approx 42 \text{ extra cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Productivity gain factor**

$$
k_{\text{gain}} = \frac{\text{New capacity}}{\text{Old capacity}} = 1 + \frac{\Delta T_{\text{shift}}}{N_{\text{cycles}} \cdot t_{\text{VA}}}
$$

$$
k_{\text{gain}} = 1 + \frac{10{,}200}{120 \times 240} = 1 + 0.354 = 1.354
$$

> *Conclusion: Productivity rises by **35.4 %** with no new machinery, no new labour — purely from organizing the workplace.*

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Workplace States

| Parameter | Pre-Sort, Pre-Seiton (Muda-heavy) | Post-Sort, Pre-Seiton | Post-Sort, Post-Seiton (Target) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Items present | All (needed + unneeded) | Only needed | Only needed, *arranged* |
| Item location | Undefined | Defined (loosely) | Defined + marked + visual |
| Retrieval time $T_{\text{retrieve}}$ | 90–180 s | 45–60 s | **≤ 30 s** |
| Decision time $T_{\text{decide}}$ | High | Medium | Near-zero |
| Walking distance / cycle | High | Medium | Minimal |
| Defect risk (wrong tool) | High | Medium | Low |
| Floor space utilization | Poor | Improved | Optimized |
| Safety incidents | Frequent | Reduced | Minimal |
| Information at a glance | None | Partial | **100 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the diagonal pattern — each cell improves as the 5S pillars mature. KTU board questions often test this *layered improvement* logic.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Frequency-of-Use (FUA) Placement Decision

A drilling station operator uses **6 tools** with the following frequencies per 8-hour shift:

| Tool | Use Frequency (times/shift) | Category |
|---|---|---|
| T1: 10 mm drill bit | 240 | **High** |
| T2: Center punch | 220 | **High** |
| T3: 6 mm drill bit | 80 | Medium |
| T4: Caliper | 15 | Medium |
| T5: Micrometer | 3 | Low |
| T6: Angle gauge | 1 | Low |

**Solution (FUA-based placement):**

- **T1 & T2 → P-point (waist height, within 25 cm of dominant hand)** — used > 200 times.
- **T3 & T4 → Adjacent tool tray / shadow board** — used 15–80 times.
- **T5 & T6 → Centralized shared tool crib** — used ≤ 3 times.

> *KTU Exam Tip:* When a question gives a frequency table, your answer must explicitly state the **P-point concept** and the **shared crib concept** — these are the two mark-fetching keywords.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Motion-Waste Quantification via Spaghetti Diagram

If a spaghetti diagram measures total operator walking distance $D_{\text{before}}$ over one full shift, and the same operator after Seiton walks $D_{\text{after}}$, the motion waste eliminated is:

$$
W_{\text{motion}} = D_{\text{before}} - D_{\text{after}} \;\; [\text{meters/shift}]
$$

If walking speed is $v_{\text{walk}} = 1.2$ m/s, time saved is:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{walk}} = \frac{W_{\text{motion}}}{v_{\text{walk}}}
$$

> *Note:* Most KTU problems give the data in distance form; conversion to time uses the standard walking speed. Always state the assumption explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Seiton Implementation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Sort Completed] --> B[Frequency of Use Analysis FUA]
    B --> C{High Use or Low Use}
    C -->|High Use 200+ per shift| D[Place at P point within 25 cm of hand]
    C -->|Medium Use 15 to 200| E[Place at Point of Use on Shadow Board]
    C -->|Low Use under 15| F[Place in Centralized Tool Crib]

    F --> G
    G --> H[Install Floor Markings and Signboards]

    J --> K{Meets 30s Standard}

    K -->|No| M[Re analyze Layout and Iterate]

```

## 4.4 Conceptual Mermaid Layout: Before vs After Seiton (Block Functional View)

## 4.5 Color-Coding Reference (Rendered as a Mermaid Table-Block)
