<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Set in Order" (Seiton) in the context of 5S. State the "30-second rule" associated with it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Set in Order (Seiton) is the second step of the 5S methodology that ensures **"a place for every item and every item in its place"** in the workplace. Its objective is to arrange tools, materials, and documents so that they can be **identified, retrieved, and returned quickly** without any search or hesitation. The **"30-second rule"** states that any required item must be obtainable in **30 seconds or less** by any operator familiar with the workstation. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a "shadow board"? How does it act as a visual control in Set in Order?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **shadow board** is a tool-storage board on which the **silhouette (outline) of each tool is painted or printed** at its designated location. It acts as a visual control because any **gap or missing silhouette instantly signals that a tool is absent**, enabling immediate detection of misplaced or missing tools. This is a classic Seiton tool that provides **"information at a glance"**, supporting FOD prevention and quick tool retrieval within the 30-second standard. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Examination Note:** KTU ESE Part B Module 2 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Two parallel options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Set in Order (Seiton)** in detail. Describe its **objectives, implementation procedure, and the role of visual controls** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **"30-second rule"** and the **Frequency-of-Use Analysis (FUA)** as tools of Seiton. With a suitable example, explain how FUA-based placement reduces motion waste. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to Q.A (a)

**Definition (1 mark):** Set in Order is the 5S principle that arranges items so that they are easily *identifiable, retrievable, and returnable* within a minimum time, with the help of visual controls.

**Objectives (2 marks):**
1. Eliminate search time and motion waste.
2. Enable the workplace to be *self-explanatory* and *self-ordering*.
3. Improve safety, ergonomics, and operator morale.
4. Free up floor space and reduce defects caused by wrong-item usage.

**Implementation Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Frequency-of-Use Analysis (FUA):** Categorize items as high-, medium-, and low-frequency.
2. **Decide the storage location:** P-point for high use, point-of-use for medium, shared crib for low.
3. **Apply visual controls:** shadow boards, floor markings, color codes, Kanban cards, FIFO lanes, signboards.
4. **Verify** the 30-second retrieval standard.
5. **Document** the standard (Seiton map + photographs) and integrate into daily audits.

**Role of Visual Controls (1 mark):** Visual controls convert *invisible information* (e.g., "is the tool there?") into *visible information* (e.g., a gap in the shadow board) — they enable **information at a glance**, which is the foundation of lean shop-floor management.

---

#### Model Answer to Q.A (b)

**30-Second Rule (3 marks):**
The rule states that any item required at a workstation should be **retrievable in ≤ 30 seconds** by an operator. Mathematically:

$$
T_{\text{retrieve}} = T_{\text{see}} + T_{\text{decide}} + T_{\text{move}} + T_{\text{grasp}} \leq 30 \text{ s}
$$

- $T_{\text{see}}$ is minimized by clear labels and shadow boards.
- $T_{\text{decide}}$ is minimized by standardized layout.
- $T_{\text{move}}$ is minimized by FUA-based placement.
- $T_{\text{grasp}}$ is minimized by ergonomic, vertical, FIFO storage.

**Frequency-of-Use Analysis (2 marks):**
FUA classifies items by usage frequency and places them at the **P-point (Prime Point)**, which is the ergonomic work zone — waist-to-shoulder height, within 25–50 cm of the operator's dominant hand. High-frequency items are placed at the P-point; medium at the point of use; low in a shared crib.

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**
Consider 6 tools with frequencies 240, 220, 80, 15, 3, 1.
- The first two (used > 200 times) → **P-point** placement.
- Next two (15–80 times) → **Point-of-use** shadow board.
- Last two (≤ 3 times) → **Shared tool crib**.

This placement reduces average walking distance from 12 m to 2 m per shift, eliminating roughly **83 % of motion waste**, which satisfies the 30-second rule.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a **spaghetti diagram**, explain how Set in Order eliminates motion waste. Compare the before-and-after operator paths. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the various **visual control tools** used in Set in Order. Explain the standard **color-coding convention** followed on Indian shop floors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to Q.B (a)

**Spaghetti Diagram — Definition (2 marks):**
A spaghetti diagram is a layout-overlay sketch on which the **operator's walking path during a work cycle** is traced using a continuous line. **Crossovers, loops, and backtracking** indicate poor workplace organization and unnecessary motion (one of the *7 Wastes / Muda*).

**Before Seiton — Path Description (2 marks):**
The operator's path resembles tangled spaghetti — multiple back-and-forth movements between the tool crib, machine, inspection desk, and raw-material rack. Typical distance: 40–60 m per cycle. Decision points: ambiguous. Items: scattered.

**After Seiton — Path Description (2 marks):**
The path straightens to a near-linear flow:
Machine → P-point tools → Inspection (adjacent) → Output rack. Typical distance: 5–8 m per cycle. All items have **named, marked, and visible** locations.

**Quantitative Conclusion (1 mark):**
If $D_{\text{before}} = 50$ m and $D_{\text{after}} = 7$ m, the motion waste eliminated is:

$$
W_{\text{motion}} = 50 - 7 = 43 \text{ m per cycle}
$$

This directly supports the 30-second rule and reduces operator fatigue.

---

#### Model Answer to Q.B (b)

**Visual Control Tools (4 marks):**

| Tool | Function |
|---|---|
| **Shadow boards** | Tool outlines; gaps signal missing tools |
| **Floor markings** | Define walkways (white), work zones (green/blue), hazard zones (yellow/black), and reject areas (red) |
| **Address labels / signboards** | Nameplate + photo of item; bilingual |
| **Kanban cards** | Trigger replenishment; show order quantity |
| **Andon lights** | Status indication (green = OK, yellow = call, red = stop) |
| **FIFO lanes (inclined racks)** | First-In-First-Out material flow |
| **Max–Min markers** | Indicate upper and lower inventory limits in bins |

**Indian Shop-Floor Color Convention (3 marks):**

- **Red** → Defective, scrap, or reject material.
- **Yellow** → Awaiting inspection, or caution zone.
- **Blue** → Work-in-Progress (WIP) storage.
- **Green** → Finished goods, approved items, or safety-related signage.
- **Orange** → Quarantined or "hold" material pending decision.
- **White** → Pedestrian walkways and aisles.
- **Black–Yellow stripes** → Hazard zones, machine bases, moving parts.

> [!NOTE]
> This color convention is the **Bharat Petroleum / Tata / Maruti standard** and is consistent with the **IS 9457:2005** safety-color guidelines, which Indian shop floors typically follow.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Sort and Set in Order:** Many students write the *Sort (Seiri)* red-tag procedure inside a Set-in-Order answer and lose **2–3 marks**. Sort ≠ Set in Order.
> 2. **Skipping the "30-second rule":** A Set-in-Order answer *without* mentioning the 30-second rule is considered incomplete by most KTU valuators.
> 3. **Forgetting visual control examples:** A bare definition without at least **two visual-control examples** (shadow board + floor marking) typically fetches only 3 out of 7 marks.
> 4. **No diagram:** KTU 2024 scheme awards 1–2 marks for a labelled diagram. A *spaghetti diagram sketch* or *shadow board sketch* is non-negotiable.
> 5. **Wrong color convention:** Mixing up red (defect) with red (safety) is a common error. Stick to the **IS 9457** convention above.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Seiton (整頓)** = 2nd pillar of 5S; *"a place for everything, everything in its place"*.
- **30-second rule:** Any item retrievable in ≤ 30 s.
- **P-point:** Ergonomic placement zone (waist height, 25–50 cm reach) for high-frequency items.
- **Frequency-of-Use Analysis (FUA):** High → P-point, Medium → point of use, Low → shared crib.
- **Visual controls** are the *language* of Seiton: shadow boards, floor markings, signboards, Kanban, Andon, FIFO lanes.
- **Color code (Indian standard):** Red = defect, Yellow = caution, Blue = WIP, Green = OK/finished, Orange = hold, White = walkway, Black-yellow = hazard.
- **Spaghetti diagram:** Traces motion path; tangled = bad, linear = good.
- **Shadow board:** Painted tool outline; visible gap = missing tool.
- **Pre-condition:** Sort (Seiri) must be completed *before* Seiton begins.
- **Mathematical check:**

$$
T_{\text{retrieve}} = T_{\text{see}} + T_{\text{decide}} + T_{\text{move}} + T_{\text{grasp}} \leq 30 \text{ s}
$$

- **Output metric:** Productivity gain typically **30–40 %** with zero new investment.
- **Sustenance:** Requires Seiton map + photo-standard + daily 5S audit.
- **KTU-favourite keywords for high marks:** *information at a glance, self-explanatory workplace, FUA, P-point, FIFO, jidoka interface, motion waste (Muda).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Shine (Japanese: 清掃 / Seiso)** is the **third pillar** of the **5S methodology** of Lean Manufacturing. It is formally defined as the systematic workplace practice of **cleaning, sweeping, scrubbing, washing, and polishing** the work environment, machinery, tools, and surrounding areas to eliminate dust, dirt, grease, waste, and contaminants.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *PERUT413 – Lean Manufacturing*, Shine is positioned not merely as an aesthetic or housekeeping activity, but as a **proactive engineering discipline** in which cleaning is repurposed as an **inspection tool** to identify abnormalities, defects, leaks, vibrations, loose fittings, and other latent failures before they escalate into major breakdowns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** *Shine (Seiso) is the workplace discipline of keeping the production environment, equipment, and surrounding areas in a state of cleanliness such that sources of contamination, friction, wear, and abnormality become immediately visible to the operator, thereby enabling early detection of defects and prevention of equipment deterioration.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car whose dashboard is completely covered by a thick, opaque cloth. You can press the accelerator and brake, but you cannot see the **fuel gauge, temperature meter, oil-pressure indicator, or warning lights**. The car still drives, but the moment something goes wrong, you find out only **after the engine has been damaged**. Now remove the cloth — every needle, light, and gauge is visible. Tiny fluctuations that previously hid under the cloth are now exposed, and the driver can act **before** the engine seizes.

**Shine in a factory is the act of "removing the cloth."**

- A clean machine **reveals** oil leaks, hairline cracks, loose bolts, abnormal vibrations, and micro-deposits of chips or dust.
- A clean floor **reveals** spilled coolant, dropped fasteners, and trip hazards.
- A clean tool board **reveals** a missing tool instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Shine transforms cleaning from a "janitorial chore" into an **engineering diagnostic exercise**. The operator becomes both the cleaner and the inspector — the two roles are inseparable in Lean.

---

### 1.3 Why Shine Exists: The Three Engineering Reasons

1. **Visibility of Abnormality:** Dirt hides defects. A clean surface makes leaks, wear, and misalignment immediately visible.
2. **Equipment Longevity:** Contaminants (dust, chips, oil mist, coolant residue) accelerate wear, corrosion, and electrical faults.
3. **Moral Ownership:** A worker who cleans a machine personally *cares* about it. Shine creates psychological ownership of the workstation.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlighted in Bold)

The following metrics are typically tracked before and after Shine implementation:

- **MTBF** – Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
- **MTTR** – Mean Time To Repair (hours)
- **OEE** – Overall Equipment Effectiveness (%)
- **Equipment Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)
- **Downtime Reduction (%)** post-Shine implementation
- **Number of Abnormalities Detected per Cleaning Cycle** (Tag count)
- **Mean Cleaning Cycle Time (T_c)** per workstation

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Quick-Fire:** If a KTU question gives you MTBF and MTTR, the first formula to write is **Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)**. Examiners reward this immediate recall.

---

### 1.5 Synonyms & Cross-Cultural Equivalents (Board-Expected Knowledge)

| Region / Framework | Term for Shine |
|---|---|
| Japan (origin) | Seiso (清掃) |
| English (5S) | Shine / Sweep |
| Western TPM | Cleaning + Inspection / Autonomous Maintenance Step 1 |
| ISO 9001 / 5S Audits | Workplace Cleanliness & Order |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Shine — Layered Breakdown

The practice of Shine is best understood in **six logically sequenced layers**. Each layer builds on the previous one and feeds the next.

**Layer 1 — Designate the Cleaning Scope**
- Decide *what* is to be cleaned: machines, floors, walls, windows, control panels, tool racks, conveyors, lighting fixtures, ceilings.
- Exclude nothing: a clean machine on a dirty floor is incomplete Shine.

**Layer 2 — Define Frequency and Responsibility**
- Assign every cleaning task a **fixed owner** (the operator of the machine, not a contract sweeper).
- Assign every task a **fixed frequency** (hourly, shift-end, daily, weekly, monthly).
- Use a **Cleaning Responsibility Matrix (CRM)** posted beside each machine.

**Layer 3 — Provide Tools and Consumables**
- Cleaning agents, mops, brushes, vacuum cleaners, air jets, lint-free wipes.
- Tools must be **dedicated, color-coded, and located near the point of use** (the previous 5S step *Set in Order* supports this).

**Layer 4 — Execute Daily Cleaning Routines**
- Conducted at **start-of-shift** and **end-of-shift** as a non-negotiable ritual.
- During cleaning, the operator physically touches every part of the machine — this is where **inspection** happens.

**Layer 5 — Tag Abnormalities (Red-Tag / White-Tag System)**
- Any defect noticed during cleaning is **immediately tagged** with a red or white card.
- The card carries: location, machine ID, defect type, date, and operator name.
- Tagged items enter the **abnormality register** for engineering follow-up.

**Layer 6 — Convert Cleaning into Inspection (the Core Principle)**
- Every cleaning action is paired with a **5-sense check**:
  - **Sight:** leaks, cracks, missing parts, dust accumulation.
  - **Sound:** abnormal vibration, whining bearings, irregular knocks.
  - **Touch:** abnormal heat, looseness, play in couplings.
  - **Smell:** burnt insulation, hot oil, gas leaks.
  - **Taste:** not used in industry, but chemical residue may indicate a coolant leak.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Shine:** If you clean without inspecting, you have done only 50% of Shine. The *real* value of Shine is unlocked **only** when cleaning is treated as inspection. This is the single most-tested KTU concept on this topic.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict formatting rule applied:** No vertical pipe `|` is used inside table rows. All absolute-value or conditional separators use `\vert` or `\mid` rendered in LaTeX.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equipment Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless (×100 for %) | Probability the equipment is operational when needed |
| 2 | MTBF | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{\text{total running}}}{\text{No. of failures}}$ | hours | Average uptime between two consecutive breakdowns |
| 3 | MTTR | $MTTR = \dfrac{T_{\text{total downtime}}}{\text{No. of failures}}$ | hours | Average repair time per failure |
| 4 | OEE | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless | World-class benchmark is **OEE ≥ 85%** |
| 5 | % Improvement in Availability | $\Delta A\% = \dfrac{A_{\text{new}} - A_{\text{old}}}{A_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | % | Used to quantify Shine impact |
| 6 | Cleaning Cycle Time | $T_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | minutes | Total time for one full cleaning sweep across *n* items |
| 7 | Cost of Neglect (dirtiness) | $C_n = D \times C_c \times T_e$ | currency | Where D = degree of dirtiness, $C_c$ = corrective cost, $T_e$ = exposure time |
| 8 | Tag Density | $\rho_t = \dfrac{N_{\text{tags}}}{A_{\text{zone}}}$ | tags / m² | Indicates abnormality-rich zones |
| 9 | Cycle-Time Reduction | $\Delta CT\% = \dfrac{CT_{\text{old}} - CT_{\text{new}}}{CT_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | % | Productivity gain from cleaner, faster workflow |
| 10 | Defect Detection Rate | $DDR = \dfrac{N_{\text{detected during cleaning}}}{N_{\text{total defects}}}$ | % | How effective Shine is as an inspection tool |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Shine in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Application of Shine |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Assembly** | Robotic weld tip cleaning; chip & oil removal from CNC beds |
| **Semiconductor / Cleanroom** | ISO Class 5/6 cleanliness; particle contamination control |
| **Food & Pharma** | Hygienic zoning; CIP (Clean-In-Place) systems |
| **CNC Machining** | Coolant filtration; chip evacuation; slideway lubrication |
| **Aviation MRO** | FOD (Foreign Object Debris) prevention — Shine is mandated |
| **Power Plants** | Boiler tube cleaning; turbine blade inspection via boroscope |

---

### 2.4 Linkage to TPM and the "Autonomous Maintenance" Pillar

Shine is the **operational twin** of **TPM's Autonomous Maintenance (AM) — Step 1: Initial Cleaning**. The Japanese term *Jishu Hozen* literally means "self-maintenance by the operator." Without a clean machine, the operator cannot master the equipment, because **abnormalities remain invisible**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"Seiso is to 5S what Initial Cleaning is to AM — they are the same philosophical act performed in two frameworks."*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example: Quantifying the Impact of Shine on Equipment Availability

This is a **typical 14-mark KTU Board numerical problem**. The derivation below is shown in *full*, with every algebraic step made explicit.

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A CNC machining cell has 4 identical machines. Over a 30-day observation period, the cell experienced the following data:
> - Total running time: 2,880 hours
> - Number of breakdowns: 24
> - Total downtime for repair: 720 hours
>
> After implementing a rigorous **Shine (Seiso) program** with operator-led daily cleaning and tagged-abnormality reporting, the following changes were observed:
> - Total running time: 2,880 hours (unchanged)
> - Number of breakdowns reduced to **15**
> - Total downtime reduced to **360 hours**
>
> **Calculate:**
> (i) Original **MTBF** and **MTTR**
> (ii) Original **Availability** $A_1$
> (iii) New **MTBF** and **MTTR** post-Shine
> (iv) New **Availability** $A_2$
> (v) **% Improvement in Availability**
> (vi) **OEE** before and after, assuming Performance = 0.90, Quality = 0.95 (assumed constant)

---

#### Step 1 — Compute Original MTBF and MTTR

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF_1 &= \frac{T_{\text{total running}}}{\text{No. of failures}} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{2880 \text{ hours}}{24} \\[4pt]
       &= 120 \text{ hours} \\[8pt]
MTTR_1 &= \frac{T_{\text{total downtime}}}{\text{No. of failures}} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{720 \text{ hours}}{24} \\[4pt]
       &= 30 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, each machine ran for 120 hours before a failure, and each repair consumed 30 hours.

---

#### Step 2 — Compute Original Availability $A_1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{MTBF_1}{MTBF_1 + MTTR_1} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{120}{120 + 30} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{120}{150} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.80 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 80.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Before Shine, the equipment was operationally available 80% of the time.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute New MTBF and MTTR Post-Shine

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF_2 &= \frac{2880}{15} = 192 \text{ hours} \\[4pt]
MTTR_2 &= \frac{360}{15} = 24 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Shine reduced the failure rate and the repair burden — operators detected minor issues during cleaning, so the *time-to-failure* grew (120 → 192 h) and the *time-to-repair* shrank (30 → 24 h).

---

#### Step 4 — Compute New Availability $A_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_2 &= \frac{MTBF_2}{MTBF_2 + MTTR_2} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{192}{192 + 24} \\[4pt]
    &= \frac{192}{216} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.8889 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 88.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 5 — Compute % Improvement in Availability

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta A\% &= \frac{A_2 - A_1}{A_1} \times 100 \\[4pt]
          &= \frac{0.8889 - 0.8000}{0.8000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
          &= \frac{0.0889}{0.8000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
          &= 11.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Availability improved by **11.11 percentage points** — a strong, board-worthy outcome.

---

#### Step 6 — Compute OEE Before and After (with $P = 0.90$, $Q = 0.95$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE_1 &= A_1 \times P \times Q = 0.80 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.684 = 68.4\% \\[4pt]
OEE_2 &= A_2 \times P \times Q = 0.8889 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.7600 = 76.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta OEE\% &= \frac{OEE_2 - OEE_1}{OEE_1} \times 100 \\[4pt]
            &= \frac{0.7600 - 0.6840}{0.6840} \times 100 \\[4pt]
            &= 11.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When Performance and Quality are held constant, **% improvement in OEE equals % improvement in Availability**. This is a useful shortcut for KTU board-checking of answers.

---

### 3.2 Pre-Shine vs. Post-Shine Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Pre-Shine | Post-Shine | Change | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of failures (per 30 d) | 24 | 15 | −37.5% | Early detection during cleaning |
| MTBF (hours) | 120 | 192 | **+60%** | Equipment survives longer between failures |
| MTTR (hours) | 30 | 24 | −20% | Less major repair; minor fixes suffice |
| Availability | 80.00% | 88.89% | **+8.89 pp** | Higher uptime |
| OEE (with P=0.90, Q=0.95) | 68.40% | 76.00% | +7.6 pp | World-class benchmark (≥85%) gets closer |
| Estimated annual downtime (h) | 8,760 | 6,580 | −2,180 h/yr | Direct revenue recovery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When the problem states "performance and quality are unaffected," always present OEE in the simplified form and explicitly state the assumption to earn full marks.

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Implementation Procedure (Procedural Derivation)

For a **7-mark descriptive sub-question**, examiners expect a clear 6-step sequence. The derivation below shows the logical *why* behind each step.

| Step | Action | Why (Engineering Rationale) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identify cleaning zones** (machine, floor, wall, panel, ceiling) | Defines the *scope* of Shine; without scope, accountability collapses |
| 2 | **Assign cleaning responsibilities** to operators, not external cleaners | Operators know the machine best; ownership is the *real* deliverable |
| 3 | **Define cleaning frequency** (hourly, daily, weekly, monthly) | Frequency must match the *rate of contamination*; one size fits none |
| 4 | **Provide dedicated tools and consumables** at the point of use | If tools are missing, the operator skips cleaning; *Set in Order* must precede this |
| 5 | **Execute cleaning + tagging** abnormalities | This dual act is what differentiates Shine from ordinary housekeeping |
| 6 | **Review tagged items weekly**, escalate unresolved tags to maintenance | Closes the **PDCA** loop: Act → Plan → Do → Check |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Shine Implementation Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Shine Implementation]) --> stepA1[Step 1: Identify Cleaning Zones and Equipment Surfaces]
    stepA1 --> stepA2[Step 2: Assign Cleaning Responsibility to Operators]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Step 3: Define Cleaning Frequency per Item]
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Step 4: Provide Dedicated Tools and Consumables]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[Step 5: Execute Daily Cleaning Routines]
    stepA5 --> stepA6[Step 6: Tag Every Abnormality Detected]
    stepA6 --> stepA7[Step 7: Log Tags in Abnormality Register]
    stepA7 --> stepA8[Step 8: Weekly Review and Engineering Escalation]
    stepA8 --> stepA9[Step 9: Convert Cleaning Time into Inspection Time]
    stepA9 --> decA{Is the workplace visibly clean and abnormality free?}
    decA -- No --> stepA5
    decA -- Yes --> stepA10[Step 10: Move to Standardize Seiketsu]
    stepA10 --> endA([Sustain Shitsuke Phase])
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Shine in the 5S Pillars Architecture (Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1Pillar[1S SORT Seiri] --> s2Pillar[2S SET IN ORDER Seiton]
    s2Pillar --> s3Pillar[3S SHINE Seiso]
    s3Pillar --> s4Pillar[4S STANDARDIZE Seiketsu]
    s4Pillar --> s5Pillar[5S SUSTAIN Shitsuke]
    s3Pillar --> tpmLink[TPM AUTONOMOUS MAINTENANCE JISHU HOZEN]
    tpmLink --> oeeBoost[Higher OEE Availability Performance Quality]
    s3Pillar --> inspectLoop[Cleaning Equals Inspection Cycle]
    inspectLoop --> abDetect[Abnormality Detection via 5 Senses]
    abDetect --> s3Pillar
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — The Cleaning-as-Inspection Cycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Machine Surface and Surroundings] --> procA[Operator Initiates Shine Activity]
    procA --> outA[Physical Cleaning Brushes Wipes Air Jets]
    outA --> outB[Visual Sight Check Leaks Cracks Missing Parts]
    outA --> outC[Auditory Sound Check Vibration Whining Knocks]
    outA --> outD[Tactile Touch Check Heat Looseness Play]
    outA --> outE[Olfactory Smell Check Burnt Oil Hot Insulation]
    outB --> tagA[Tag Abnormality Red White Card]
    outC --> tagA
    outD --> tagA
    outE --> tagA
    tagA --> logA[Abnormality Register Entry]
    logA --> engA[Engineering Maintenance Escalation]
    engA --> fixA[Defect Rectified]
    fixA --> oeeA[Availability MTTR MTBF OEE Improvement]
    oeeA --> inputA
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Pre-Shine vs Post-Shine State Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph preState[PRE SHINE STATE]
        p1[Dust and Chips Accumulated] --> p2[Leaks Hidden under Grime]
        p2 --> p3[Operator Cannot See Defects]
        p3 --> p4[High MTTR and Frequent Failures]
        p4 --> p5[Low OEE and Low Morale]
    end
    subgraph postState[POST SHINE STATE]
        q1[Surface Clean and Reflective] --> q2[Leaks Visible Immediately]
        q2 --> q3[Operator Detects Abnormality in 5 Seconds]
        q3 --> q4[Low MTTR and Reduced Failures]
        q4 --> q5[High OEE and Strong Ownership]
    end
    p5 -.Shine Implementation.-> q1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
Define **Shine (Seiso)** as a pillar of 5S. State **any four objectives** of implementing Shine in a manufacturing environment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Shine (Japanese: *Seiso*, 清掃) is the third pillar of 5S, defined as the systematic practice of cleaning the workplace, equipment, and surroundings to eliminate dirt, dust, and waste, so that abnormalities become immediately visible.

**Objectives (any four, 0.375 each):**
1. To keep the workplace clean and orderly.
2. To enable early detection of equipment defects and abnormalities.
3. To extend equipment life and reduce breakdowns.
4. To improve safety by removing slip/trip hazards and contaminants.
5. To create a sense of ownership and discipline among operators.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
What is meant by the principle **"Cleaning as Inspection"** in the context of Shine? Give **one industrial example**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** "Cleaning as Inspection" means that every cleaning activity is performed with a diagnostic mindset — the operator simultaneously cleans the surface and inspects the equipment for leaks, cracks, loose fittings, abnormal heat, vibration, or wear. The act of cleaning reveals what dirt had been hiding.

**Example (1 mark):** While wiping the bed of a CNC lathe with a clean cloth, the operator notices a thin oil trail and a hairline crack near the coolant nozzle — a defect that would have remained invisible under a layer of swarf and coolant. The operator tags it immediately, preventing a major spindle-bearing failure.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A — Module 2, 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 1)

**Q.A. (a) [7 Marks] — KTU Model Question**
Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for implementing Shine (Seiso)** in a manufacturing organization. Highlight the role of operator involvement and tagging systems.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Shine is not a one-time cleaning drive; it is a structured, recurring discipline with assigned ownership. Implementation requires a defined sequence.

**Step-by-Step Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Identify Cleaning Scope:** Mark all zones — machines, floors, walls, panels, ceilings, tools, conveyors. A clean machine on a dirty floor is incomplete Shine.
2. **Develop a Cleaning Responsibility Matrix (CRM):** For each zone, name the operator, the cleaning tool, the frequency (hourly/shift-end/daily/weekly), and the expected cycle time.
3. **Provide the Right Tools at the Point of Use:** Color-coded mops, brushes, lint-free wipes, vacuum cleaners, and air jets must be stationed *next to* the equipment. Without tools, cleaning is skipped.
4. **Set a Daily Cleaning Routine:** The first 10 minutes of every shift begin with a planned Shine activity — never optional, never postponed.
5. **Tag Every Abnormality:** During cleaning, if the operator observes a leak, loose bolt, abnormal sound, or wear, a **red/white tag** is fixed with date, machine ID, defect type, and operator signature. The tag enters an **Abnormality Register**.
6. **Weekly Review and Engineering Follow-Up:** The maintenance engineer reviews tags every week. Resolved tags are closed; unresolved ones are escalated with a target date.
7. **Audit and Feedback:** A monthly Shine audit scores each zone on a 0–5 scale, with results displayed on the **Visual Management Board**.

**Role of Operator Involvement (0.5 mark):** Operators *own* the cleanliness; this builds psychological ownership and converts the workforce into the first line of inspection — reducing reliance on external maintenance.

**Role of Tagging (0.5 mark):** Tagging converts *informal observations* into *formal data* that engineering can act upon. It closes the PDCA loop.

---

**Q.A. (b) [7 Marks] — KTU Model Question**
Discuss the **relationship between Shine (Seiso) and Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**, with specific reference to **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)**. How does Shine act as the foundation for the seven steps of Autonomous Maintenance?

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Shine and TPM share a common philosophical root: *the operator is the primary custodian of the equipment.* While Shine is a 5S housekeeping pillar, TPM's Autonomous Maintenance is a structured 7-step program for operator-led equipment care.

**The Seven Steps of Autonomous Maintenance (4 marks):**

1. **Initial Cleaning** — This is *operationally identical* to Shine.
2. **Countermeasures for Sources of Contamination** — Once clean, install guards, drip trays, and seals so dirt does not return.
3. **Formulation of Cleaning and Lubrication Standards** — Standardize *how* and *how often* cleaning/lubrication must happen.
4. **General Inspection** — Operators learn to inspect 5-sense checkpoints because the machine is now clean enough to inspect.
5. **General Tightening and Standardization** — Bolts, fittings, and connections are checked and torqued to spec.
6. **Maintaining the 5S Discipline in Full** — Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, and Sustain are sustained as background culture.
7. **Autonomous Management** — Operators run the AM program independently, with maintenance acting only as a coach.

**How Shine Acts as the Foundation (2 marks):**
- **Step 1 of AM *is* Shine.** No subsequent step is possible on a dirty machine.
- Shine makes **abnormalities visible** — without it, Steps 4–7 are blind.
- Shine creates **operator intimacy with the machine** — touching every part during cleaning builds the knowledge base needed for inspection.
- Shine provides **the data** for engineering improvement — tagged abnormalities become the inputs of *Why–Why* analysis and *Pokayoke* design.

**Conclusion:** Shine is therefore not a standalone housekeeping step — it is the **launchpad** of TPM.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B — Module 2, 14 Marks (Internal Choice Option 2)

**Q.B. (a) [7 Marks] — KTU Model Question**
Explain the concept of **"Cleaning as Inspection"** in detail. How does Shine help in **abnormality detection**? Discuss the **five-senses check** that operators perform during Shine.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Cleaning as Inspection (2 marks):** In conventional industries, cleaning is delegated to housekeeping staff and inspection is delegated to maintenance engineers. In Lean, the **operator performs both roles simultaneously** during the Shine activity. The act of physically touching and cleaning every surface forces the operator to notice what dirt had been hiding — leaks, cracks, wear, loose bolts, abnormal heat, and unusual sounds. Cleaning *is* inspection; inspection *is* cleaning.

**How Shine Helps in Abnormality Detection (2 marks):**
- Dirt, oil, and chips **mask** defects. Shine **exposes** them.
- Tagging converts observations into trackable data for engineering action.
- Regular cleaning produces a *baseline* of cleanliness; deviation from the baseline is itself an abnormality signal.
- A clean machine enables **predictive maintenance** — operators learn the "normal look, sound, and feel" of the machine, and instantly notice deviation.

**The Five-Senses Check During Shine (3 marks):**

| Sense | What the Operator Detects | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sight** | Leaks, cracks, missing parts, dust accumulation | Coolant puddle under hydraulic cylinder |
| **Sound** | Abnormal vibration, whining bearings, irregular knocks | Bearing whine when spindle runs |
| **Touch** | Abnormal heat, looseness, play in couplings | Hot motor housing; loose belt tension |
| **Smell** | Burnt insulation, hot oil, gas leaks | Acrid smell of overheating cable |
| **Taste** | Not used industrially, but residual chemical taste can indicate fluid type | Coolant leak on floor near beverage-grade line |

**Conclusion:** Shine, by integrating cleaning with a five-senses diagnostic scan, transforms a routine chore into the **most cost-effective inspection system in any factory**.

---

**Q.B. (b) [7 Marks] — KTU Model Question — Numerical**

A manufacturing cell consists of **4 machines**. The following data were recorded over a **30-day period**:
- Total running time of the cell: 2,880 hours
- Number of breakdowns: **24**
- Total downtime for repair: **720 hours**

After implementing a **rigorous Shine (Seiso) program** with operator-led cleaning, the data for the next 30 days became:
- Number of breakdowns: **15**
- Total downtime: **360 hours**

**Calculate:**
(i) Original MTBF and MTTR
(ii) Original Availability
(iii) New MTBF and MTTR
(iv) New Availability
(v) % Improvement in Availability
(vi) % Improvement in OEE, assuming Performance = 0.90 and Quality = 0.95 remain unchanged.

**Model Answer (Full Step-by-Step Solution):**

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{running}} = 2880$ h, $N_1 = 24$, $D_1 = 720$ h
- $N_2 = 15$, $D_2 = 360$ h
- $P = 0.90$, $Q = 0.95$

**(i) Original MTBF and MTTR (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF_1 &= \frac{2880}{24} = 120 \text{ hours} \\[4pt]
MTTR_1 &= \frac{720}{24} = 30 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Original Availability (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \frac{120}{120 + 30} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80 = 80.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) New MTBF and MTTR (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTBF_2 &= \frac{2880}{15} = 192 \text{ hours} \\[4pt]
MTTR_2 &= \frac{360}{15} = 24 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) New Availability (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_2 &= \frac{192}{192 + 24} = \frac{192}{216} = 0.8889 = 88.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) % Improvement in Availability (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta A\% &= \frac{0.8889 - 0.8000}{0.8000} \times 100 = 11.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(vi) % Improvement in OEE (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OEE_1 &= 0.80 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.684 = 68.40\% \\[4pt]
OEE_2 &= 0.8889 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.7600 = 76.00\% \\[4pt]
\Delta OEE\% &= \frac{0.7600 - 0.6840}{0.6840} \times 100 = 11.11\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result Summary:**

| Parameter | Before Shine | After Shine | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| MTBF | 120 h | 192 h | +60.0% |
| MTTR | 30 h | 24 h | −20.0% |
| Availability | 80.00% | 88.89% | +8.89 pp |
| OEE | 68.40% | 76.00% | +7.6 pp |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Writing only the formula but skipping the substitution step** — Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per skipped substitution. Always show $\dfrac{2880}{24} = 120$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing MTTR and MTBF** — MTBF uses *running* time in the numerator; MTTR uses *downtime* in the numerator. A frequent cause of zero marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — "120" without "hours" loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Using "Shine" as a generic cleaning term** — Examiners expect the operator-inspection linkage to be explicitly mentioned. Without it, even a correct answer caps at 60% of marks.
> 5. **Omitting the Assumption Statement** — In the OEE part, write *"Assuming Performance and Quality remain constant at 0.90 and 0.95 respectively."* This single sentence often earns the full 1 mark reserved for "validity of the model."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Shine (Seiso)** is the **3rd pillar of 5S** and means **"cleanliness"** in Japanese.
- It is **not housekeeping** — it is **cleaning + inspection** performed by the **operator**, not a sweeper.
- **The single most important principle:** *"Cleaning as Inspection."* Without inspection, Shine is half-done.
- **Five-Senses Check:** Sight, Sound, Touch, Smell, and (rarely) Taste — performed during every Shine activity.
- **Tagging System (Red/White Tags):** Converts observations into trackable engineering data.
- **Cleaning Responsibility Matrix (CRM):** Names an owner, a tool, a frequency, and a duration for every cleaning task.
- **Standard Cleaning Frequencies:** Hourly (spill-prone areas), Shift-end (machines), Daily (floors), Weekly (deep clean), Monthly (overhead & walls).
- **Equipment Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — write this first on any numerical.
- **OEE formula:** $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ — world-class benchmark is **≥ 85%**.
- **Tag Density metric** ($\rho_t$) helps identify abnormality-rich zones for engineering focus.
- **Shine is the foundation of TPM's Autonomous Maintenance Step 1** — *Initial Cleaning*.
- **Direct link between Shine and Safety:** Cleaner floors = fewer slip/trip incidents; cleaner panels = fewer electrical faults.
- **KTU Board-Magic Phrases to Use in Answers:**
  - *"Cleaning as Inspection"*
  - *"Operator-led autonomy"*
  - *"Visual Management of Abnormalities"*
  - *"Zero tolerance for non-tagged defects"*
  - *"From cleaning to condition monitoring"*
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Treating Shine as a one-time campaign. Examiners will mark down any answer that does not explicitly mention **recurring schedules and operator ownership**.
- **Numerical Shortcut:** When P and Q are constant, **% improvement in OEE = % improvement in Availability**. Use this for self-checking.
- **Diagram Expectation:** A 7-mark Shine answer without a *flowchart or process diagram* loses at least 1 mark on presentation. Always include a labelled flow showing *Clean → Inspect → Tag → Escalate → Improve*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STANDARDIZE (Lean Manufacturing Definition)**
> Standardize is the fourth stage of the **5S Methodology** and a foundational pillar of the Toyota Production System (TPS). It is the structured process of establishing precise, documented, and uniform **best-practice procedures, work instructions, visual controls, and operational benchmarks** so that every operator performs a task in the same correct way, every time, in every shift, and in every location.

In the broader **Lean/Toyota framework**, standardization is the mechanism that **freezes current best-known methods** so that deviations, abnormalities, and opportunities for further improvement become **immediately visible**. It is the essential bridge between an ad-hoc improvement (a one-time Kaizen) and an organization-wide, repeatable, controllable process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Standardize is studied as the cornerstone that converts the 3 preceding S's (Sort, Set in Order, Shine) from being "cleaned-up areas" into a **self-sustaining operating system**. Without standardization, the first three S's decay within weeks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **street-food cart in Kerala** that serves *kallappam and stew*. 

- **Before Standardize:** The owner makes the batter fresh every morning with his own secret proportion. When he is sick, his son makes a slightly different batter. The taste drops. The customer complains but cannot pinpoint why — the recipe, the resting time, the flame height, all vary daily.

- **After Standardize:** The owner writes the recipe on a laminated card pinned to the cart — **"150 g rice, 50 g coconut, 200 ml water, ferment 8 hours, medium flame, 2 minutes per side."** He marks the gas knob with paint to show "medium flame." Now *anyone* — his son, his wife, even a helper — can produce the same kallappam. If the batter ever tastes sour, the problem is no longer the "recipe" — it is now clearly the *fermentation time* or the *flame*, and the chef knows exactly what to fix.

**The standardized recipe did two things simultaneously:**
1. It **froze the current best method** so quality did not drift.
2. It **made future problems visible** by removing "operator variation" as a possible cause.

**That is exactly what Lean Standardization does in a factory.** It freezes the *current best-known way* so that:
- Quality is repeatable,
- Training of new operators is fast and identical,
- And any deviation is immediately recognized as a problem to be solved (not "just the way it is").

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary You Must Know

| Term | Meaning in Lean Context |
|------|--------------------------|
| **Standard** | A documented, agreed-upon, *best-known* way of doing a task. |
| **Standardization** | The *act* of creating, deploying, and maintaining those standards. |
| **Standardized Work** | The combination of *takt time*, *work sequence*, and *standard work-in-process* that defines how an operator performs a job. |
| **Visual Control** | A standard made obvious at a glance (colored tape, shadow boards, andon lights). |
| **Current Best Practice** | The baseline against which all improvement (Kaizen) is measured. |
| **One-Point Lesson (OPL)** | A 1-page visual teaching aid used to deploy a standard across a cell. |
| **Kamishibai Board** | A Japanese visual audit board used to verify standards are being followed. |

## 1.4 Why Standardize — The Lean "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Core Reasons Lean Organizations Standardize:**

1. **Variation Control** — To eliminate *muda* (waste) caused by operator-to-operator inconsistency.
2. **Problem Visibility** — A standard is the "before" picture. Any deviation is an abnormality worth investigating.
3. **Training & Onboarding** — New operators learn *the standard*, not the senior operator's habits.
4. **Improvement Foundation** — You cannot *improve* what you have not *defined*. Standardize establishes the baseline.
5. **Knowledge Preservation** — When an operator retires or leaves, the company's best practice stays in the company, not in one person's memory.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Components of Standardized Work (TPS)

According to the **Toyota Production System**, Standardized Work is built from exactly three ingredients. If any one is missing, the system collapses.

| # | Component | Definition | Typical Measure |
|---|-----------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Takt Time** | The available production time divided by customer demand rate. | seconds/unit |
| 2 | **Work Sequence** | The exact ordered list of tasks an operator performs, with hand and foot movements specified. | sequence diagram |
| 3 | **Standard Work-In-Process (SWIP)** | The minimum inventory of parts needed to keep the work sequence running without stoppage. | pieces / subassemblies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Distinction (Exam Favorite):**
> *Standardized Work ≠ Work Standards.*
> - **Work Standards** = generic time-study motion standards (predetermined times).
> - **Standardized Work** = the operator-specific, station-specific, currently-best-known procedure for *one* job in *one* cell.
> Standardized Work is **dynamic** — it changes after every successful Kaizen. Work standards are **static** and industrial-engineering driven.

## 2.2 The Standardization Process (Step-by-Step Logic)

The act of "standardizing" follows a deliberate 6-step logic flow. This is a *very common 14-mark question* in KTU ESE.

**Step 1 — Observe and Document the Current Best Method**
The team leader (Team Leader / Group Leader in TPS terminology) watches the *most experienced operator* perform the task in the *safest, easiest, lowest-muda* way. He writes it down — task by task — with hand and foot positions.

**Step 2 — Break the Task into Elements (Cycle Decomposition)**
The full cycle is divided into **work elements** (smallest distinct units of work), each timed in seconds. A typical Toyota cycle has 15–25 elements. This granularity exposes the *non-value-added* (NVA) steps.

**Step 3 — Plot the Work-Sequence Diagram**
Each element is drawn on a **Standardized Work Combination Table** with the x-axis = time (s) and the y-axis = parallel machine / worker operations. This shows hand-offs and wait time visually.

**Step 4 — Verify Takt Time Compliance**
For each element $t_i$, the sum of manual element times plus the *longest* machine cycle must not exceed takt time $T_{takt}$.

**Step 5 — Pilot and Validate**
Run the standard on the line for a trial shift. If it works, the standard is "frozen" as the *current best-known method.*

**Step 6 — Disseminate and Audit**
Deploy using **OPLs (One-Point Lessons)** and verify with the **Kamishibai Board** weekly.

## 2.3 The Standardization Cycle — PDCA Embedded

Standardization is not a one-time event. It is itself a **PDCA loop**:

- **Plan (P):** Identify the *current* best method → write the standard.
- **Do (D):** Train every operator on the standard; follow it exactly.
- **Check (C):** Audit (Kamishibai). Are deviations occurring? Is the standard still valid?
- **Act (A):** If a deviation is *better* → update the standard (Kaizen). If a deviation is *worse* → investigate root cause and re-train.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why Toyota says: *"Without a standard, there can be no Kaizen."* You cannot improve what you have not first defined.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All exam-relevant formulas on Standardize. Memorize the columns, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Meaning / Use |
|---|------------------|----------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | **Takt Time** | $$T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D}$$ | seconds / unit | Time per unit to meet customer demand. |
| 2 | **Available Time** | $T_{avail} = T_{shift} - T_{breaks} - T_{meetings}$ | seconds / shift | Effective working time in a shift. |
| 3 | **Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | seconds / unit | Sum of all element times in one operator cycle. |
| 4 | **Standard WIP (SWIP)** | $N_{WIP} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} d_j$ | pieces | Sum of minimum pieces between each process. |
| 5 | **Standard Efficiency** | $\eta_s = \dfrac{T_{takt}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\%$ | % | How tightly the cycle is matched to takt. $\eta_s \le 100\%$. |
| 6 | **Operator Load** | $L_{op} = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{T_{takt}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of takt the operator is busy. |
| 7 | **Line Balance Efficiency** | $\eta_{LB} = \dfrac{\sum T_{cycle}}{n_{sta} \cdot T_{takt}} \times 100\%$ | % | Use to re-balance after standard changes. |
| 8 | **Non-Value-Added (NVA) Ratio** | $R_{NVA} = \dfrac{\sum t_{NVA}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\%$ | % | Target $\le 10\%$ in Lean cells. |
| 9 | **Audit Compliance Rate** | $C_{audit} = \dfrac{N_{pass}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | From Kamishibai audits. Target $\ge 95\%$. |
| 10 | **Standard Adherence Ratio** | $R_{adh} = \dfrac{N_{obs}}{N_{std}}$ | dimensionless | Observed matches to documented standard. |

## 2.5 Relationship Map — Where Standardize Sits in Lean

Standardize is **not isolated** — it touches every other Lean tool:

- **With 5S:** Standardize is the *4th S*; it formalizes the gains of Sort, Set-in-Order, Shine.
- **With Just-in-Time (JIT):** Standardized work is the *prerequisite* for level production (Heijunka) and pull (Kanban).
- **With Jidoka (Autonomation):** Standards define the *normal* state so the Andon can detect the *abnormal*.
- **With Kaizen:** Standardize is the "frozen state" between Kaizen events.
- **With TPM:** Standards are the *cleaning*, *lubricating*, and *inspection* checklists.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **CNC machining cell**, Standardize manifests as: a one-page operator chart pinned at the machine showing spindle speed, feed rate, depth of cut, tool number, measurement point, and cycle time. In an **automotive assembly line**, it appears as the *time-wheel chart* on each station. In a **hospital OT**, it is the *Surgical Safety Checklist* — a medical standardized work. In a **software DevOps team**, it is the *runbook* for incident response.

> The discipline of "do it the same way every time" is the universal foundation of quality.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derived Worked Example — Building a Standardized Work Sheet

This is the *exact* type of 7-mark sub-question KTU asks. We will work it out **exhaustively**.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU-Style)

> A small assembly cell in a TV manufacturing plant operates in **two shifts of 8 hours each**. Each shift includes a **30-minute break** and a **10-minute morning meeting**. The cell assembles a circuit-board subassembly and the **daily demand is 480 units**. After time-study by the team leader, the operator's cycle was broken into the following elements (in seconds):

| Element | Description | Element Time $t_i$ (s) |
|:-------:|-------------|:----------------------:|
| A | Pick blank PCB from rack | 6 |
| B | Insert capacitor 1 | 8 |
| C | Insert capacitor 2 | 8 |
| D | Insert resistor 1 | 7 |
| E | Insert resistor 2 | 7 |
| F | Solder all joints (machine) | 22 |
| G | Visual inspection | 5 |
| H | Place finished unit in bin | 4 |

Required:
1. Compute the **Takt Time** and verify if the **current cycle satisfies takt**.
2. Compute **Standard Efficiency** $\eta_s$.
3. Compute **Operator Load** $L_{op}$.
4. Compute **Non-Value-Added Ratio** $R_{NVA}$, given that element F (soldering) is considered a necessary **value-added (VA)** machine operation, while elements A, G, H are **necessary NVA** (still required), and B, C, D, E are **VA manual work**.
5. If the demand rises to **600 units/day**, what is the new takt time, and is the standard still valid?

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Available Time $T_{avail}$

> [!NOTE]
> Always convert hours to **seconds** for takt-time consistency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{shift} &= 8 \text{ hr} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 28800 \text{ s} \\
T_{break} &= 30 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 1800 \text{ s} \\
T_{meeting} &= 10 \text{ min} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 600 \text{ s} \\
T_{avail, 1 shift} &= 28800 - 1800 - 600 = 26400 \text{ s} \\
T_{avail, 2 shifts} &= 2 \times 26400 = 52800 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic (explain in exam):** The available time is the *gross* time on the shop floor minus all *non-productive* allowances. This is the time during which value can be created.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute Takt Time $T_{takt}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 480 \text{ units / day} \\
T_{takt} &= \frac{T_{avail}}{D} = \frac{52800 \text{ s}}{480 \text{ units}} = 110 \text{ s / unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The line must produce one board **every 110 seconds** to meet demand. If the operator's cycle is longer, either we are short of capacity, or we need another operator.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Operator Cycle Time $T_{cycle}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= \sum_{i=A}^{H} t_i \\
T_{cycle} &= 6 + 8 + 8 + 7 + 7 + 22 + 5 + 4 \\
T_{cycle} &= 67 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Check Takt Compliance

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 67 \text{ s} \\
T_{takt} &= 110 \text{ s} \\
T_{cycle} < T_{takt} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{Cycle satisfies takt. Capacity is sufficient.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The difference $T_{takt} - T_{cycle} = 110 - 67 = 43$ s is the **operator's slack/wait time** within the takt. In Lean, this slack is used for kaizen activities, not for idling.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Standard Efficiency $\eta_s$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{T_{cycle}}{T_{takt}} \times 100\% \\
\eta_s &= \frac{67}{110} \times 100\% \\
\eta_s &= 60.91\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Only ~61% of the takt window is being used productively by the operator. The remaining 39% is slack — which signals an opportunity to either *add value* (more VA work), or to *combine stations* (line balancing). Target in mature Lean cells: $\eta_s \in [85\%, 95\%]$. Too close to 100% means the line is brittle; too low means over-staffed or unbalanced.

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Operator Load $L_{op}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{op} &= \frac{T_{cycle}}{T_{takt}} = \frac{67}{110} = 0.609
\end{aligned}
$$

$L_{op} = 0.609$ means the operator is busy 60.9% of the takt. Below 1.0 — the cell has surplus capacity.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Non-Value-Added Ratio $R_{NVA}$

Per the problem classification:

- **VA manual:** B, C, D, E → $t_{VA,manual} = 8+8+7+7 = 30$ s
- **VA machine (necessary):** F → $t_{VA,mach} = 22$ s
- **NVA (necessary):** A, G, H → $t_{NVA} = 6+5+4 = 15$ s

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 30 + 22 + 15 = 67 \text{ s (consistency check passes)} \\
R_{NVA} &= \frac{t_{NVA}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\% \\
R_{NVA} &= \frac{15}{67} \times 100\% \\
R_{NVA} &= 22.39\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Note:** $R_{NVA}$ here counts only *necessary* NVA (steps that are required for the process to function, e.g., picking a part). Lean targets reducing these to the absolute minimum, ideally $R_{NVA} \le 10\%$. Currently, ~22% of the cycle is *necessary-but-not-value-added* — a clear Kaizen target.

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Effect of Demand Rise to 600 units/day

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{new} &= 600 \text{ units / day} \\
T_{takt,new} &= \frac{52800}{600} = 88 \text{ s / unit} \\
T_{cycle} &= 67 \text{ s} \\
T_{cycle} < T_{takt,new} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{Standard is STILL valid (67 s < 88 s).} \\
\eta_{s,new} &= \frac{67}{88} \times 100\% = 76.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The standard still works, but operator load rises from 60.9% to 76.1%. The cell is becoming *tighter* — closer to needing additional resources.

### 3.1.10 Step 9 — The Demand Level at Which the Standard Breaks

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{takt} = T_{cycle} \Rightarrow T_{avail}/D_{crit} = 67 \\
D_{crit} = \frac{52800}{67} = 788 \text{ units/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** For any demand **above 788 units/day**, the current standardized work *fails* to meet takt and must be re-engineered (cycle-time reduction, additional operator, automation of the soldering step, etc.).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Standards vs. Non-Standards

| Dimension | With Standard | Without Standard |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Quality | Predictable, measurable | Variable, operator-dependent |
| Onboarding | 1–3 days with OPL | 2–6 weeks of apprenticeship |
| Problem Detection | Visible deviation = abnormal | "That's just how he does it" |
| Improvement (Kaizen) | Baseline clearly defined | No baseline → no comparison |
| Cost | Documentation time | Hidden waste, rework, scrap |
| Knowledge | Stays in organization | Leaves with the operator |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Standardization Pitfalls

| # | Pitfall | Consequence | Counter-Measure |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Standard written by an engineer, *not* by the operator | Rejected by the floor | Use **team-led** standard creation. |
| 2 | Standard frozen forever | No Kaizen possible | **Update after every improvement.** |
| 3 | Standard too detailed (e.g., 50-page SOP) | Operator ignores it | Keep it to **one page**, with visuals. |
| 4 | Standard not audited | Decay within weeks | **Kamishibai weekly audits.** |
| 5 | Standard imposed top-down | Resistance and rejection | Deploy using **OPLs** and floor training. |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Notes
- All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters (e.g., `nodeA1`, `stepB`).
- Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric only (no `**bold**`, no italics, no HTML).
- Reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`) are **never** used as node IDs.

## 4.2 The Standardization Workflow — 5S + PDCA Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA1["SORT S1"] --> nodeA2["SET IN ORDER S2"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3["SHINE S3"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4["STANDARDIZE S4"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5["SUSTAIN S5"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeB1["OBSERVE BEST METHOD"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2["DOCUMENT WORK SEQUENCE"]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3["COMPUTE TAKT TIME"]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4["PILOT AND FREEZE STANDARD"]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5["DEPLOY VIA OPL"]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6["KAMISHIBAI AUDIT"]
    nodeB6 --> nodeC1{"DEVIATION DETECTED"}
    nodeC1 -- YES BETTER --> nodeC2["KAIZEN UPDATE STANDARD"]
    nodeC1 -- YES WORSE --> nodeC3["ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS"]
    nodeC1 -- NO --> nodeB6
    nodeC2 --> nodeA4
    nodeC3 --> nodeA4
```

## 4.2.1 Reading the Flow

- The **outer loop** is the 5S staircase. Standardize is the *4th* step.
- The **inner loop** is the PDCA cycle embedded inside Standardize.
- The **decision diamond** is the *Kamishibai audit result*. Every deviation is either Kaizen (if the operator found a better way) or an abnormality (to be RCA'd).

## 4.3 Standardized Work Combination Table (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT0["TIME AXIS 0 SEC"] --> nodeT1["ELEMENT A PICK 6S"]
    nodeT1 --> nodeT2["ELEMENT B CAP1 8S"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["ELEMENT C CAP2 8S"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4["ELEMENT D RES1 7S"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5["ELEMENT E RES2 7S"]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6["MACHINE SOLDER 22S"]
    nodeT6 --> nodeT7["ELEMENT G INSPECT 5S"]
    nodeT7 --> nodeT8["ELEMENT H BIN 4S"]
    nodeT8 --> nodeT9["END CYCLE 67S"]
    nodeT9 -.->|REPEAT| nodeT0
    nodeT6 -.->|PARALLEL WAIT| nodeT7
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrow `nodeT6 -.->|PARALLEL WAIT| nodeT7` shows that during the 22-second machine solder, the operator *could* be working on the next unit (if WIP allows). This is the **operator-machine separation** that Toyota's Standardized Work Combination Table visualizes.

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Standards in a Toyota Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeH1["CORPORATE STANDARD"] --> nodeH2["DIVISION STANDARD"]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH3["PLANT STANDARD"]
    nodeH3 --> nodeH4["CELL STANDARD"]
    nodeH4 --> nodeH5["STATION STANDARD"]
    nodeH5 --> nodeH6["OPERATOR STANDARDIZED WORK"]
    nodeH6 --> nodeH7["OPL ONE POINT LESSON"]
    nodeH7 --> nodeH8["KAMISHIBAI AUDIT CHECK"]
```

## 4.5 Standardize vs. Kaizen Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeK1["CURRENT STANDARD FROZEN"] --> nodeK2["OPERATOR FINDS BETTER WAY"]
    nodeK2 --> nodeK3["TRIAL KAIZEN"]
    nodeK3 --> nodeK4{"VERIFIED IMPROVEMENT"}
    nodeK4 -- YES --> nodeK5["UPDATE STANDARD NEW BASELINE"]
    nodeK4 -- NO --> nodeK1
    nodeK5 --> nodeK1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most-asked diagram in KTU ESE for "Standardize." Memorize the cycle: *Standard → Kaizen → New Standard → Kaizen → Newer Standard* — infinite upward spiral of incremental improvement.
