# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define "Standardize" as the fourth S in 5S. Why is it considered the most critical of the five S's?

**Model Answer:**

Standardize is the fourth stage of the 5S methodology and refers to the process of establishing **uniform, documented, best-known methods** for performing tasks, maintaining layouts, and operating equipment, so that the gains achieved by the first three S's (Sort, Set-in-Order, Shine) are **locked in** and do not decay over time. It is considered the most critical of the five S's because it is the *bridge* between one-time cleanup and a **self-sustaining operating system**. Without standardization, the cleanliness and order created by Sort, Set-in-Order, and Shine would erode within weeks as new operators, shift changes, and time pressure reintroduce variation. Standardize converts the workplace from a *cleaned-up area* into a *disciplined system* where deviations are immediately visible, training of new operators is repeatable, and continuous improvement (Kaizen) has a stable baseline to measure against. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] List and briefly explain any three components of Standardized Work as defined in the Toyota Production System.

**Model Answer:**

According to the Toyota Production System, Standardized Work consists of three mandatory components:

1. **Takt Time** — The rate at which a unit must be produced to meet customer demand, computed as available production time divided by customer demand. It is the heartbeat of the line. **(1 mark)**

2. **Work Sequence** — The precise, ordered list of tasks an operator must perform in a given cycle, specifying the order, hand and foot positions, and decision points. It removes operator-to-operator variation. **(1 mark)**

3. **Standard Work-In-Process (SWIP)** — The minimum number of parts that must be present at and between each process step to allow the work sequence to proceed without starvation or stoppage. It is the controlled, minimal inventory needed for smooth flow. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Standardized Work" with "Work Standards."** Standardized Work is operator-specific, station-specific, *and* constantly updated after Kaizen. Work Standards are static, industrial-engineering-driven time-study norms. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Always show the takt-time computation in seconds**, not minutes or hours. Mixing units is the #1 cause of arithmetic errors in 14-mark answers.
> 3. **Mention PDCA explicitly** in any answer about Standardize. Standardize *is* a PDCA loop. Examiners allocate 2 marks for the PDCA linkage alone.
> 4. **Always verify takt compliance** ($T_{cycle} \le T_{takt}$). Students who compute but do not *interpret* the result lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Do not skip the "Why standardize" justification** in 7-mark sub-parts. Always state the Lean reason (variation control, problem visibility, training, Kaizen baseline, knowledge preservation).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Option A*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **standardization procedure** followed in a Lean cell after completion of the first three S's of 5S. Use a suitable block diagram in your answer.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A machining cell works **one shift of 8 hours** with a **45-minute break** and a **15-minute tool-meeting**. The daily demand is **96 units**. The team leader's time-study gives the following element times (in seconds):

| E1 | E2 | E3 | E4 | E5 | E6 | E7 |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| 12 | 18 | 15 | 22 | 14 | 19 | 10 |

Compute (i) takt time, (ii) cycle time, (iii) standard efficiency, (iv) operator load, and (v) state whether the current standard is valid. If demand rises to **120 units/day**, recompute the takt and comment on validity.

#### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Standardization Procedure (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> The complete procedure has **6 steps**. Examiners expect 5–6 steps with a brief justification of *why* each is performed.

**Step 1 — Observation of the Best-Known Method (1 mark):**
The Team Leader observes the most experienced and skilled operator performing the task in the *most efficient, safest, and least-wasteful* manner. The objective is to identify the *current best practice*, not the average practice. Only the best-known method qualifies as a standard.

**Step 2 — Cycle Decomposition into Elements (1 mark):**
The complete operator cycle is broken into small, distinct, measurable units called *work elements* (e.g., "pick part," "place part," "press button"). Each element is timed in seconds. This decomposition exposes non-value-added motion and supports future Kaizen.

**Step 3 — Construction of the Work-Sequence / Combination Table (1 mark):**
Each element is plotted on a *Standardized Work Combination Table* with the time-axis horizontal and parallel machine/worker operations on the vertical axis. This gives a single, visual, one-page representation of the entire cycle, including operator-machine parallelism and wait times.

**Step 4 — Takt-Time Verification (1 mark):**
The sum of manual element times plus the *longest* machine cycle (where applicable) is compared against takt time. If $T_{cycle} \le T_{takt}$, the standard is feasible. If not, the standard must be revised before freezing.

**Step 5 — Pilot Run and Standard Freezing (1 mark):**
The proposed standard is run for a trial shift. If the operator can sustain the cycle with no quality or safety deviation, the standard is *officially frozen* and becomes the *baseline* against which all future improvements will be measured.

**Step 6 — Deployment via OPL and Kamishibai Audit (2 marks):**
The frozen standard is deployed across the cell using **One-Point Lessons (OPLs)** — single-page, visually rich training aids. Compliance is verified through a **Kamishibai Board**, a Japanese visual audit system where red/green cards signal whether each standard is being followed. The PDCA loop then begins: deviations trigger either Kaizen (improvement) or root-cause analysis (abnormality), and the standard is updated accordingly.

**Block Diagram (within the 7 marks):**

```
Sort → Set-in-Order → Shine → [Observe Best Method]
                                  ↓
                          [Decompose into Elements]
                                  ↓
                          [Build Combination Table]
                                  ↓
                          [Verify against Takt Time]
                                  ↓
                          [Pilot Run on Shop Floor]
                                  ↓
                          [Freeze Standard]
                                  ↓
                  [Deploy via OPL + Kamishibai Audit]
                                  ↓
                      [PDCA → Kaizen → New Standard]
```

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$T_{shift} = 8$ hr, Break = 45 min, Tool-meeting = 15 min, $D = 96$ units/day.
Element times (s): 12, 18, 15, 22, 14, 19, 10.

**Step 1 — Available time in one shift:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{shift} &= 8 \times 60 \times 60 = 28800 \text{ s} \\
T_{break} &= 45 \times 60 = 2700 \text{ s} \\
T_{meeting} &= 15 \times 60 = 900 \text{ s} \\
T_{avail} &= 28800 - 2700 - 900 = 25200 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Takt Time (i):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{takt} &= \frac{T_{avail}}{D} = \frac{25200}{96} \\
T_{takt} &= 262.5 \text{ s / unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle Time (ii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 12 + 18 + 15 + 22 + 14 + 19 + 10 = 110 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Efficiency (iii):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{T_{cycle}}{T_{takt}} \times 100\% = \frac{110}{262.5} \times 100\% \\
\eta_s &= 41.90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Operator Load (iv):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{op} &= \frac{T_{cycle}}{T_{takt}} = \frac{110}{262.5} = 0.419
\end{aligned}
$$

The operator is busy 41.9% of the takt — a heavily under-loaded cell.

**Step 5 — Validity Check (v):**

$$
T_{cycle} = 110 \text{ s} < T_{takt} = 262.5 \text{ s} \Rightarrow \text{Standard is VALID.}
$$

**Step 6 — Effect of Demand Rise to 120 units/day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{takt,new} &= \frac{25200}{120} = 210 \text{ s / unit} \\
T_{cycle} &= 110 \text{ s} < 210 \text{ s} \Rightarrow \text{Standard is STILL VALID.} \\
\eta_{s,new} &= \frac{110}{210} \times 100\% = 52.38\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The standard remains valid for both demand levels, but operator efficiency rises to 52.38% as demand increases. The cell has substantial *slack* that should be used for Kaizen activities (cycle-time reduction, multi-process training, or combining with an adjacent station).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Option B*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the **relationship between Standardize and Kaizen**. Why does Toyota say *"Without a standard, there can be no Kaizen"*? Discuss any three benefits and three challenges of implementing standardization on the shop floor.

**(b)** [7 Marks] In a paint-shop of an automotive assembly plant, the **takt time is 75 seconds**. The operator's standardized work consists of seven elements with the following times (in seconds): 8, 12, 10, 15, 9, 11, 7. One of the elements (15 s) is a **necessary machine-aided drying** step during which the operator is free. (i) Identify the *parallel-wait* opportunity. (ii) Calculate the standard efficiency and operator load. (iii) If the line introduces a **second operator** who shares the cycle equally, compute the new standard efficiency. (iv) Comment on the trade-off.

#### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Standardize ↔ Kaizen Relationship (7 marks)**

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```
        +------------------------+
        |  CURRENT STANDARD      |  (frozen best-known method)
        +------------------------+
                   |
                   |  Operator finds better way
                   v
        +------------------------+
        |  TRIAL KAIZEN          |  (small, focused improvement)
        +------------------------+
                   |
                   v
            +--------------+
            |  VERIFIED?   |
            +--------------+
             |          |
       YES (better)   NO
             |          |
             v          v
   +-----------------+   (back to standard)
   |  UPDATE         |
   |  STANDARD       |
   +-----------------+
             |
             v
   (New baseline; cycle repeats)
```

**Why "Without a standard, there can be no Kaizen" (2 marks):**

Kaizen means *improvement*. But improvement must be measured *relative to a baseline*. If there is no standard, every operator does the task differently, and there is no agreed "current best" to improve upon. Toyota's philosophy is: *first* standardize the best-known method (freezing the *As-Is*), *then* improve it incrementally (creating the *To-Be*), *then* re-freeze the new standard. The standard is the *ruler* without which Kaizen is unmeasurable. The cycle of standard-kaizen-new-standard is the *engine* of continuous improvement.

**Three Benefits (3 marks):**

1. **Variation Control** — Quality, safety, and cycle time become predictable across operators and shifts.
2. **Faster Training** — A new operator can reach full productivity in days (using OPLs) rather than months.
3. **Visible Abnormalities** — Any deviation from the standard is immediately a problem signal, enabling fast root-cause response.

**Three Challenges (3 marks):**

1. **Resistance from Senior Operators** — They may feel their "experience" is being replaced by a piece of paper. *Mitigation:* make them *authors* of the standard.
2. **Documentation Decay** — Standards become outdated quickly; without a Kamishibai audit cycle, they become fiction. *Mitigation:* weekly audits, mandatory Kaizen updates.
3. **Over-Standardization** — Excessive detail (50-page SOPs) makes the standard unusable. *Mitigation:* keep to one page with pictures and minimal text.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $T_{takt} = 75$ s; elements = 8, 12, 10, 15, 9, 11, 7 s; the 15-s element is machine-aided drying during which the operator is *free* (parallel wait).

**Step 1 — Total cycle time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= 8 + 12 + 10 + 15 + 9 + 11 + 7 = 72 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Active operator work time** (excluding the 15-s parallel wait, since during drying the operator is free to do other things — but in *one-operator* mode, that 15 s is wait, not active work). However, in strict Standardized Work analysis, the *effective* operator busy time is the *cycle time* if no overlap is possible, or the *cycle time minus parallel-wait overlap* if overlap is engineered. Here we treat the 15 s as **non-overlapping wait** in single-operator mode:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{active} &= 72 - 15 = 57 \text{ s (active manual work)} \\
T_{wait} &= 15 \text{ s (parallel wait during drying)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Parallel-Wait Opportunity (1 mark):**
The 15-second drying step is the **parallel-wait opportunity**. In single-operator mode, the operator idles for 15 s. In two-operator mode, this is the natural *hand-off* point.

**(ii) Standard Efficiency and Operator Load (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_s &= \frac{T_{cycle}}{T_{takt}} \times 100\% = \frac{72}{75} \times 100\% = 96.0\% \\
L_{op} &= \frac{72}{75} = 0.960
\end{aligned}
$$

The operator is busy 96% of takt — a *tight* line, with very little slack. (1 mark) 

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Reflection:** Although the operator is *active* for only 57 s, the *cycle time* is 72 s because of the machine wait. In single-operator mode, this 96% takt utilization is misleading — the operator is *forced to wait* 15 s. The cell is **machine-bound**, not **operator-bound**.

**(iii) Two-Operator Mode (2 marks):**
With two operators sharing the cycle equally, the per-operator workload becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle,new} &= \frac{72}{2} = 36 \text{ s per operator} \\
\eta_{s,new} &= \frac{36}{75} \times 100\% = 48.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Trade-Off Comment (2 marks):**

| Aspect | 1 Operator | 2 Operators |
|--------|------------|-------------|
| Labor cost | Lower | ~2× higher |
| Line efficiency (takt basis) | 96% (tight) | 48% (slack) |
| Output capability | 75 s/unit | 75 s/unit (unchanged) |
| Operator load | High (stressful) | Lower (healthier) |
| Use of slack | None | Kaizen / cross-training / multi-process |

**Conclusion:** Two operators do **not** increase output (takt is still 75 s); they only *redistribute* the work. The trade-off is **cost vs. ergonomics vs. Kaizen-time**. In Lean, the 1-operator cell is preferred when the cycle is < 60 s, but the 2-operator split is preferred when the cycle exceeds the *operator-fatigue threshold* (~ 50–60 s) or when the 15 s of waiting can be eliminated entirely by re-arranging the layout so that the drying oven and the next assembly are in parallel at *adjacent* stations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read 2 Hours Before Exam**

**1. One-Line Definitions**
- **Standardize** = freezing the *current best-known method* so deviation becomes visible.
- **Standardized Work** = Takt Time + Work Sequence + Standard WIP.
- **Takt Time** = Available time / Customer demand (seconds per unit).
- **Work Sequence** = the documented, ordered, hand-and-foot-specified set of tasks.
- **SWIP** = the *minimum* inventory between stations for the cycle to run.
- **OPL (One-Point Lesson)** = 1-page visual training aid.
- **Kamishibai Board** = visual red/green audit board for standard compliance.

**2. The 6-Step Standardization Procedure (Memorize Order)**
1. Observe best method
2. Decompose cycle into elements
3. Build Standardized Work Combination Table
4. Verify takt-time compliance ($T_{cycle} \le T_{takt}$)
5. Pilot run and freeze the standard
6. Deploy via OPL + Kamishibai audit (PDCA loop begins)

**3. Formulas You Must Memorize (with units)**
- $T_{takt} = T_{avail} / D$ (s/unit)
- $T_{cycle} = \sum t_i$ (s/unit)
- $\eta_s = (T_{cycle} / T_{takt}) \times 100\%$ (%)
- $L_{op} = T_{cycle} / T_{takt}$ (dimensionless)
- $R_{NVA} = (\sum t_{NVA} / T_{cycle}) \times 100\%$ (%)
- $C_{audit} = (N_{pass} / N_{total}) \times 100\%$ (%)

**4. Standardize ↔ Kaizen Cycle (Draw this in 30 seconds if asked)**
Standard → Try Improvement → Verify → Update Standard → (repeat).

**5. Three Mandatory Components of Standardized Work**
Takt Time, Work Sequence, Standard WIP. *Never* miss any one in an exam answer.

**6. The PDCA Inside Standardize**
Plan (write the standard) → Do (train and follow) → Check (Kamishibai) → Act (Kaizen or RCA). Examiners allocate marks for explicitly stating this.

**7. Why Standardize? (5 reasons)**
Variation control, problem visibility, training, Kaizen baseline, knowledge preservation.

**8. Three Common Pitfalls (to avoid in answers)**
(1) Confusing Standardized Work with Work Standards. (2) Forgetting to convert hours to seconds. (3) Writing standards *for* operators instead of *with* them.

**9. The Golden Quote (Memorize)**
*"Without a standard, there can be no Kaizen."* — Taiichi Ohno, Toyota.

**10. Visual Reminder — 5S Order**
Sort → Set-in-Order → Shine → **Standardize** → Sustain. (Standardize is the *4th S*.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sustain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sustain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **5S Methodology** of Lean Manufacturing, **"Sustain"** (also called **"Shitsuke"** in Japanese, meaning *discipline* or *self-discipline*) is the **fifth and final 'S'** that consolidates and preserves the gains achieved through the previous four steps: **Sort (Seiri)**, **Set in Order (Seiton)**, **Shine (Seiso)**, and **Standardize (Seiketsu)**.

According to KTU 2024 scheme terminology aligned with the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and **Lean Enterprise Institute** standards, *Sustain* is defined as:

> **"The disciplined commitment by all employees to consistently follow, review, and improve the established 5S standards, transforming 5S from an event-based project into a sustainable organizational habit and culture."**

The objective of **Sustain** is to embed 5S into the **DNA of the organization** so that it becomes a self-sustaining, behaviour-driven system rather than a one-time cleanliness drive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2 (which covers workplace organization and standardisation tools), *Sustain* is treated as the **most difficult and most critical** step of 5S. Board examiners frequently allot dedicated questions on the **failure modes of Sustain** and **auditing mechanisms**, because it is the step where 90% of 5S implementations fail.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Sustain** like the **"maintenance phase" after losing weight**.

You can start a strict diet (Sort unwanted items), organize your kitchen (Set in Order), clean it daily (Shine), and write a meal plan on the fridge (Standardize). But unless you **discipline yourself to follow the plan every single day**, you will eventually slip back into old habits, the kitchen becomes messy again, and the diet fails. **Sustain** is the daily habit, the willpower, the routine — not the act of cooking, but the commitment to *keep cooking right*.

In a factory context, **Sustain** is the difference between:
- A workplace that is clean **for the audit day** (unsustained), and
- A workplace that is clean **every day of the year** (sustained).

## 1.3 Position in the 5S Framework

The 5S pillars and Sustain's role can be summarized as:

| Step | Japanese | English | Role of *Sustain* |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1S | Seiri | Sort | Requires discipline to *not* bring items back |
| 2S | Seiton | Set in Order | Requires discipline to *return* items to their place |
| 3S | Seiso | Shine | Requires discipline to *clean* daily |
| 4S | Seiketsu | Standardize | Sets the *rules* that discipline follows |
| **5S** | **Shitsuke** | **Sustain** | **Ensures rules are followed habitually** |

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics of Sustain

- **5S Audit Score (%)** — a quantitative measure of compliance.
- **Number of 5S Non-Conformances (NCs)** raised per audit cycle.
- **Audit Cycle Adherence** — frequency at which scheduled audits are actually completed.
- **Employee Suggestion Rate** for 5S improvements.
- **Mean Time Between 5S Deterioration (MTBD)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU exams, when asked "What is the most important 'S' in 5S?", the textbook answer is **Sustain (Shitsuke)**, because without it, the previous four 'S's decay rapidly. Always quote this as: *"Sustain is the cornerstone of 5S — it converts the first four 'S's from a campaign into a culture."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" of Sustain — Failure Analysis

The **5S Institute** and **JIPM (Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance)** data consistently show that **70–90% of 5S implementations fail to sustain beyond 12 months**. The reasons, structured for KTU-style answers, are:

1. **Lack of Management Commitment** — Top management treats 5S as a worker-level activity, not a leadership system.
2. **Audit Fatigue** — Audits become a formality, and scores are inflated to avoid blame.
3. **No Linkage to Daily Work** — 5S is treated as a separate activity rather than embedded in **Standard Work**.
4. **Absence of Visual Management** — Standards are in a manual no one reads.
5. **No Recognition/Reward System** — Good work is invisible; bad work is tolerated.
6. **Cultural Mismatch** — Top-down imposition without employee ownership.

## 2.2 The "How" of Sustain — Seven Pillars

The **7 Pillars of Sustaining 5S** (high-yield KTU content):

1. **Leadership Commitment (Genchi Genbutsu)** — Managers walk the floor (Gemba walks) regularly.
2. **5S Audits** — Scheduled, transparent, and tracked.
3. **Visual Management (VM)** — Standards posted at point-of-use using **shadow boards, floor markings, Kanban signals, and Andon boards**.
4. **Training & Re-training** — Every new joiner is inducted into 5S; existing staff re-trained quarterly.
5. **Integration with Standard Work** — 5S checks become part of the operator's daily start-up checklist.
6. **Suggestion System (Kaizen Teian)** — Employees generate ideas to improve 5S.
7. **Recognition & Accountability** — Best 5S area award, photographs of best/worst zones, and escalation matrix for non-conformances.

## 2.3 The 5S Audit Mechanism

Sustain is operationalized through a **structured audit cycle** that typically follows the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** logic:

- **Plan:** Define audit checklist, schedule, and auditor.
- **Do:** Conduct the audit (typically 30–60 minutes per area).
- **Check:** Score the area, photograph non-conformances, compare with previous scores.
- **Act:** Issue Corrective Action Requests (CARs), verify closure, and re-audit.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Question Focus:** Examiners frequently ask students to **compute the 5S Audit Score** for a given area. Memorize the formulas below.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **5S Element Score** (Eᵢ) | $E_i = \dfrac{R_i}{R_{max,i}} \times 100$ | Percentage score of *i*-th S-element (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain) |
| 2 | **Item Score** (Sⱼ) | $S_j = \dfrac{r_j}{r_{max,j}}$ | Rating of a single audit item (e.g., floor cleanliness) |
| 3 | **Weighted Element Score** | $E_i^{w} = E_i \times w_i$ | Element score multiplied by weightage of that 'S' |
| 4 | **Overall 5S Audit Score** | $S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} E_i \times w_i$ | Weighted sum of all 5 element scores |
| 5 | **Audit Score (%)** | $S_\% = \dfrac{\sum r_j^{obtained}}{\sum r_j^{max}} \times 100$ | Direct percentage formula (commonly used) |
| 6 | **Audit Cycle Compliance** | $C_{ac} = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{N_{scheduled}} \times 100$ | % of audits actually performed |
| 7 | **Improvement Index** | $II = \dfrac{S_{current} - S_{previous}}{S_{previous}} \times 100$ | Trend of audit scores across cycles |
| 8 | **MTBD (Mean Time Between Deterioration)** | $MTBD = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Average time the area maintains its score before re-deteriorating |

**Standard Weightage Convention (used in Indian automotive & KTU exam settings):**

| Element | Suggested Weightage (wᵢ) |
|:---|:---:|
| Sort (1S) | 15% |
| Set in Order (2S) | 20% |
| Shine (3S) | 20% |
| Standardize (4S) | 20% |
| **Sustain (5S)** | **25%** |

> The *Sustain* element carries the **highest weightage** because it measures the cultural maturity of the system.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive (Toyota, Maruti, Tata Motors):** Sustain is audited monthly; a plant below 70% audit score loses its "Model Line" status.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, HAL):** Sustain is integrated with **FOD (Foreign Object Debris)** programs — discipline of cleanliness is mission-critical.
- **Hospitals:** Sustain prevents Hospital-Acquired Infections (HAI) through disciplined cleaning protocols.
- **IT/Software:** Sustain maps to **DevOps discipline** — keeping code, infrastructure, and documentation clean long-term.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing the Overall 5S Audit Score

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing cell was audited on five 5S elements. Each element has a maximum rating of **5 points**, and the auditor assigned ratings as follows:

| Element | Weightage (wᵢ) | Rating obtained (rⱼ) | Maximum possible (Rₘₐₓ) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1S – Sort | 0.15 | 4 | 5 |
| 2S – Set in Order | 0.20 | 3 | 5 |
| 3S – Shine | 0.20 | 4 | 5 |
| 4S – Standardize | 0.20 | 4 | 5 |
| 5S – Sustain | 0.25 | 2 | 5 |

**Find:** (i) The percentage score of each element, (ii) The overall weighted 5S audit score, (iii) Interpret the result with respect to Sustain failure.

### Step 1 — Compute Each Element's Percentage Score

Using the formula:

$$
E_i = \frac{R_i}{R_{max,i}} \times 100
$$

**Element 1S (Sort):**

$$
E_1 = \frac{4}{5} \times 100 = 80.0\%
$$

**Element 2S (Set in Order):**

$$
E_2 = \frac{3}{5} \times 100 = 60.0\%
$$

**Element 3S (Shine):**

$$
E_3 = \frac{4}{5} \times 100 = 80.0\%
$$

**Element 4S (Standardize):**

$$
E_4 = \frac{4}{5} \times 100 = 80.0\%
$$

**Element 5S (Sustain):**

$$
E_5 = \frac{2}{5} \times 100 = 40.0\%
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Weighted Contribution of Each Element

Using the formula:

$$
E_i^w = E_i \times w_i
$$

$$
E_1^w = 80 \times 0.15 = 12.00
$$

$$
E_2^w = 60 \times 0.20 = 12.00
$$

$$
E_3^w = 80 \times 0.20 = 16.00
$$

$$
E_4^w = 80 \times 0.20 = 16.00
$$

$$
E_5^w = 40 \times 0.25 = 10.00
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Overall Weighted Audit Score

Using the formula:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} E_i^w
$$

$$
S_{total} = 12.00 + 12.00 + 16.00 + 16.00 + 10.00 = 66.00\%
$$

### Step 4 — Interpret the Result

> **Overall 5S Audit Score = 66% → Below the 70% industry benchmark.**

Despite Sort, Shine, and Standardize being strong (80%), the **Sustain score of only 40%** has dragged the overall score down by 10 percentage points. This is a classic **"campaign-driven 5S"** failure pattern — the workplace looks clean on audit day, but **discipline (Sustain) is weak**, indicating the previous four 'S's will deteriorate rapidly.

**Recommended Corrective Action:** Re-train all operators, install visual standards at point-of-use, increase audit frequency from monthly to weekly for 3 months, and assign a **5S Champion** to the area.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Computing the Improvement Index (II)

**Problem Statement:**
The same cell was re-audited 3 months later. The new Sustain rating was 4 out of 5. Compute the **Improvement Index (II)** for the Sustain element.

### Step 1 — Compute the Previous Sustain Score (Sₚᵣₑᵥ)

$$
S_{prev} = \frac{2}{5} \times 100 = 40.0\%
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Current Sustain Score (S꜀ᵤᵣ)

$$
S_{cur} = \frac{4}{5} \times 100 = 80.0\%
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Improvement Index Formula

$$
II = \frac{S_{cur} - S_{prev}}{S_{prev}} \times 100
$$

$$
II = \frac{80 - 40}{40} \times 100
$$

$$
II = \frac{40}{40} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

> **The Sustain element improved by 100% — a doubling of the score.** This indicates that the corrective actions taken were effective and the cell is now on a sustainable 5S path.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — 5S Sustain Diagnostic

| Symptom Observed on Shop Floor | Root Cause | Sustain Tool to Apply |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Items re-appearing on floor after Sort | No discipline to maintain red-tag boundaries | **Gemba Walks** + **5S Patrol** |
| Tools missing from shadow boards | Operators not trained on 2S rules | **Visual Standards** + re-training |
| Spillage not cleaned within shift | Shine not in standard work | **Daily 5-Minute Cleaning at Shift End** |
| Audit scores vary wildly month-to-month | No consistent audit standard | **Standardized Audit Checklist** |
| Workers say "5S is extra work" | 5S not linked to daily work | **Embed 5S in Standard Work Combination Sheet** |
| Top management never seen on shop floor | Lack of leadership commitment | **Top-Down Gemba Walks (monthly)** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 5S Cycle with Sustain as the Engine

The following **Mermaid flow diagram** illustrates how the first four 'S's feed into *Sustain*, and how Sustain drives the entire system forward via the **PDCA audit cycle**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["1S SORT Seiri"] --> B["2S SET IN ORDER Seiton"]
    B --> C["3S SHINE Seiso"]
    C --> D["4S STANDARDIZE Seiketsu"]
    D --> E["5S SUSTAIN Shitsuke"]
    E --> F["5S AUDIT PDCA Cycle"]
    F --> A
    F --> G["GEMBA WALK Management"]
    F --> H["VISUAL MANAGEMENT Standards at Point of Use"]
    F --> I["KAIZEN TEIAN Suggestion System"]
    G --> E
    H --> E
    I --> E
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Sustain System

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of how the Sustain element operates within a manufacturing organization. Each block represents a critical function, and the arrows show information/control flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEAD["LEADERSHIP LAYER"]
        L1["Plant Head"]
        L2["Department Managers"]
    end

    subgraph EXEC["EXECUTION LAYER"]
        E1["5S Coordinators"]
        E2["Area Supervisors"]
        E3["Operators"]
    end

    subgraph SYS["SUSTAIN SYSTEM CORE"]
        S1["5S Audit Schedule"]
        S2["Standardized Checklist"]
        S3["Score Tracking Database"]
        S4["Corrective Action Tracker"]
    end

    subgraph TOOLS["SUPPORT TOOLS"]
        T1["Visual Boards"]
        T2["Shadow Boards"]
        T3["Floor Markings"]
        T4["Andon Display"]
    end

    subgraph FEED["FEEDBACK LOOP"]
        F1["Monthly Audit Report"]
        F2["Kaizen Suggestions"]
        F3["Recognition Awards"]
    end

    L1 --> S1
    L2 --> S1
    S1 --> E1
    E1 --> S2
    E2 --> S3
    S2 --> E3
    E3 --> T1
    E3 --> T2
    E3 --> T3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> E2
    S3 --> F1
    F1 --> L1
    E3 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F3 --> E3
    T4 --> L1
```

## 4.3 The 5S Audit PDCA Loop (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["PLAN Define audit checklist, schedule, and auditor"] --> D1["DO Conduct 5S audit 30 to 60 min per area"]
    D1 --> C1["CHECK Score area, photograph NCs, compare trend"]
    C1 --> A1["ACT Issue CAR, verify closure, re-audit in 30 days"]
    A1 --> P1
    C1 --> S1["Sustain Score Trends"]
    S1 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The arrows in all three diagrams form a **closed loop**, which is the engineering essence of *Sustain*. A *closed loop* means feedback continuously improves the system — this is mathematically identical to a **negative feedback control system** in classical control engineering, where the error signal (audit deviation) drives corrective action until error → 0.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**"What is meant by 'Sustain' (Shitsuke) in the 5S methodology? Why is it considered the most difficult 'S' to implement?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sustain (Shitsuke) is the fifth step of 5S, meaning **discipline** — the commitment by all employees to consistently follow the 5S standards established in the previous four steps. It transforms 5S from a one-time campaign into a daily habit. (1.5 marks)
It is the most difficult 'S' because it requires **long-term behavioural and cultural change** rather than physical activity — involving leadership commitment, regular audits, and continuous reinforcement. Without discipline, the gains of Sort, Set, Shine, and Standardize decay within weeks. (1.5 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
**"List any four tools/mechanisms used to sustain 5S in a manufacturing organization."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Scheduled 5S Audits** (PDCA-based) with standardized checklists. (0.75)
2. **Gemba Walks** by top management to demonstrate leadership commitment. (0.75)
3. **Visual Management** — shadow boards, floor markings, photo standards. (0.75)
4. **Kaizen Teian (Suggestion System)** linked to 5S improvements, with recognition awards. (0.75)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**"Explain in detail the concept of 'Sustain' (Shitsuke) in the 5S methodology. Discuss the seven pillars of sustaining 5S and the role of the 5S audit as a feedback mechanism."**

#### (a) Concept of Sustain and its Significance (7 marks)

Sustain (Shitsuke) is the fifth 'S' of the 5S framework, derived from the Japanese word meaning **self-discipline**. While the first four 'S's (Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize) focus on **physical and procedural changes** in the workplace, Sustain focuses on **human behaviour and culture** — ensuring that the standards established are followed habitually by every employee, every day, without external enforcement. (2 marks)

Sustain is significant because studies by the **5S Institute** and **JIPM** show that **70–90% of 5S implementations deteriorate within 12 months** if Sustain is weak. A Sustain failure manifests as: items returning to the floor (Sort failure), tools missing from shadow boards (Set in Order failure), cleaning skipped on non-audit days (Shine failure), and standards being ignored (Standardize failure). Thus, Sustain acts as the **"glue"** that holds the entire 5S system together. (2 marks)

The **three cultural shifts** required for Sustain are: (i) **from compliance to commitment** — employees follow standards because they believe in them, not because they are watched; (ii) **from event to routine** — 5S becomes part of daily work, not a special project; (iii) **from blame to improvement** — non-conformances are treated as improvement opportunities, not punishable offences. (3 marks)

#### (b) Seven Pillars of Sustaining 5S and the 5S Audit (7 marks)

The **seven pillars** of Sustain are: (1) **Leadership Commitment** — top management conducts regular Gemba walks and allocates budget; (2) **5S Audits** — structured, transparent, and scheduled; (3) **Visual Management** — standards posted at the point of use using shadow boards, floor markings, and photo boards; (4) **Training and Re-training** — 5S induction for new joiners and quarterly refreshers; (5) **Integration with Standard Work** — 5S checks embedded in the operator's daily start-up checklist; (6) **Suggestion System (Kaizen Teian)** — every employee is encouraged to submit 5S improvement ideas; (7) **Recognition and Accountability** — monthly best-area awards and an escalation matrix for repeated non-conformances. (4 marks)

The **5S Audit** is the primary feedback mechanism. It follows a **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle: Plan the audit schedule and checklist; Do the audit (typically 30–60 min per area with photographs); Check by computing the weighted audit score using the formula:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} E_i \times w_i, \quad \text{where} \quad E_i = \frac{R_i}{R_{max,i}} \times 100
$$

Act by issuing Corrective Action Requests (CARs) and verifying closure within a defined time (e.g., 30 days). The audit score trend is plotted on a **Pareto chart** to identify the weakest 'S', and management reviews the score in monthly review meetings. Audits are conducted by a **cross-functional team** (not the area's own supervisor) to ensure objectivity. (3 marks)

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**"A manufacturing cell was audited on the five 5S elements. The maximum rating per element is 5. The auditor assigned the following ratings: Sort = 4, Set in Order = 3, Shine = 4, Standardize = 4, Sustain = 2. The weightages are 15%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 25% respectively. (a) Calculate the overall weighted 5S audit score. (b) Interpret the result and recommend corrective actions to sustain 5S in the cell."**

#### (a) Calculation of Overall Weighted 5S Audit Score (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute each element's percentage score using $E_i = (R_i / R_{max,i}) \times 100$. (1 mark)

| Element | Rating | Eᵢ (%) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1S Sort | 4/5 | 80% |
| 2S Set in Order | 3/5 | 60% |
| 3S Shine | 4/5 | 80% |
| 4S Standardize | 4/5 | 80% |
| 5S Sustain | 2/5 | 40% |

**Step 2:** Multiply each Eᵢ by its weightage wᵢ to get the weighted contribution $E_i^w = E_i \times w_i$. (2 marks)

| Element | Eᵢ | wᵢ | Eᵢᵂᵂ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1S | 80 | 0.15 | 12.00 |
| 2S | 60 | 0.20 | 12.00 |
| 3S | 80 | 0.20 | 16.00 |
| 4S | 80 | 0.20 | 16.00 |
| 5S | 40 | 0.25 | 10.00 |

**Step 3:** Sum the weighted contributions:

$$
S_{total} = 12.00 + 12.00 + 16.00 + 16.00 + 10.00 = 66.00\%
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4:** State the formula explicitly:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} E_i \times w_i = 66.00\%
$$

(2 marks)

#### (b) Interpretation and Corrective Actions (7 marks)

**Interpretation:** The overall score of **66%** is **below the 70% industry benchmark** for a "Sustained 5S" cell. The Sort, Shine, and Standardize elements are healthy (80%), but **Sustain has collapsed to 40%** and **Set in Order is borderline (60%)**. This is a classic **"campaign 5S"** signature — the cell was cleaned up for the audit, but discipline has not set in. Without intervention, the score will drop below 50% within 2–3 months. (3 marks)

**Recommended Corrective Actions to Sustain 5S:** (4 marks)

1. **Top-Down Gemba Walks:** Plant Head and Department Manager must walk the cell at least **twice a week** for 30 minutes each, with a visible checklist. This signals leadership commitment.
2. **Audit Frequency Increase:** Shift from monthly to **weekly audits** for 3 months, then revert to monthly once the score exceeds 80%.
3. **Visual Management Upgrade:** Install **photo-based standard boards** at every workstation, and a **"5S Score Dashboard"** visible from the shop floor.
4. **Embedded 5S in Standard Work:** Add a **5-Minute 5S Check** at the start and end of every shift, signed by the operator and counter-signed by the supervisor.
5. **Recognition & Accountability:** Announce a **"Best 5S Cell of the Month"** award with a small reward (certificate + memento) tied to a sustained score > 80% for 3 consecutive months.
6. **Targeted Sustain Training:** Conduct a **2-hour refresher workshop** on Shitsuke for all 12 cell operators, with case studies of 5S failures.
7. **Re-audit in 30 days** and plot the Improvement Index to verify recovery.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in 'Sustain' Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating Sustain with Standardize** — These are NOT the same. Standardize = writing the rules; Sustain = following the rules. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if these are mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by weightage** — In numerical problems, students often compute simple averages (66% would become 62% in the unweighted average). The KTU key explicitly requires **weighted** scoring using the given weightages. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting management commitment** — In 14-mark theory questions, a common pitfall is to list "audits" and "visual management" but forget that **Sustain starts at the top**. Examiners expect the phrase *"without Top Management commitment, Sustain cannot exist"* — losing this costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **No closure of PDCA loop** — In audit mechanism answers, students often describe Plan-Do-Check but **skip Act (corrective action verification)**. Always close the loop explicitly.
> 5. **Cultural vs. Procedural confusion** — Sustain is a **behavioural/cultural** concept, not a procedural one. Writing procedural answers (e.g., "paint the floor") loses conceptual marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Sustain (Shitsuke) = Discipline** — the 5th and most critical 'S' of 5S.
- ✅ **Definition:** Sustained, disciplined adherence to the 5S standards by all employees, transforming 5S from a campaign into a culture.
- ✅ **Why it's hardest:** It requires **behavioural change**, not just physical change; most 5S failures (70–90%) occur at the Sustain stage.
- ✅ **Seven Pillars of Sustain:** Leadership Commitment, 5S Audits, Visual Management, Training, Integration with Standard Work, Kaizen Teian, Recognition & Accountability.
- ✅ **5S Audit Formula (MUST memorize):**

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} E_i \times w_i, \quad E_i = \frac{R_i}{R_{max,i}} \times 100
$$

- ✅ **Standard Weightage (industry norm):** Sort 15%, Set 20%, Shine 20%, Standardize 20%, **Sustain 25%** (highest — reflecting its importance).
- ✅ **Audit Threshold:** < 70% = Red zone (action required), 70–85% = Yellow (monitor), > 85% = Green (sustained).
- ✅ **PDCA Loop:** Plan → Do → Check → Act — must be **closed-loop** to be effective.
- ✅ **Cross-functional audit teams** are mandatory to avoid auditor bias.
- ✅ **Sustain is NOT the same as Standardize** — one sets rules, the other follows them.
- ✅ **Cultural shift required:** From compliance → commitment, from event → routine, from blame → improvement.
- ✅ **Real-world anchor:** Toyota, Maruti, and Tata Motors plants lose their "Model Line" status if 5S audit score drops below 70% — a perfect KTU example to quote.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **JIPM Definition of TPM (Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance)**
> *Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) is a productive maintenance system involving a total concept for the entire organization — from top management to shop-floor workers — with the objective of achieving **Zero Breakdown**, **Zero Defect**, and **Zero Accident** through the promotion of preventive maintenance, autonomous maintenance, and focused improvement activities across the entire equipment life cycle.*

In a KTU examiner's context, TPM is **not** just a maintenance strategy — it is a **company-wide cultural system** where every operator becomes the owner of the equipment they touch. It was formally introduced by **Seiichi Nakajima** in **1971** at **Nippondenso** (a Toyota group company) and later institutionalized by **JIPM** through the prestigious **TPM Excellence Award (formerly PM Award)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "TPM Is Preventive Healthcare for Machines"

> [!NOTE] **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a human body. Doctors don't wait for a heart attack to act — they recommend **daily exercise, regular check-ups, and healthy food** to keep you running for 80+ years. Your car is the same: an **oil change every 5,000 km** prevents engine seizure.
>
> TPM applies this **preventive healthcare philosophy** to factory machines. Instead of waiting for a breakdown (a "heart attack"), operators and engineers **clean, lubricate, inspect, and tighten (CLIT)** machines daily. The result: machines that run for decades without major failure.
>
> **Bonus Analogy:** Just as a company cannot run on the CEO alone, a factory cannot run on the maintenance department alone. In TPM, **the production operator is the first-line doctor** of the machine.

## 1.3 The Three Zeros of TPM — The Holy Grail

| Zero | Meaning | Real-World Goal |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| **Zero Breakdown** | Equipment never fails unexpectedly | OEE $\geq$ **85%** (World Class) |
| **Zero Defect** | Every part produced is right-first-time | Quality Rate = **100%** |
| **Zero Accident** | Safe workplace, no injuries | LTIR = **0** |

## 1.4 Origin and Evolution Timeline

- **1951** — Preventive Maintenance (PM) concept introduced in USA.
- **1960s** — Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) emerged.
- **1971** — Seiichi Nakajima develops **TPM** at Nippondenso (Toyota Group).
- **1974** — **Total Productive Maintenance** term registered.
- **1980s** — JIPM launches the **PM/TPM Excellence Award**.
- **1990s–Today** — Global adoption; Lean + TPM + Six Sigma convergence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8 Pillars of TPM (JIPM Framework) — The Most Frequently Asked Topic

The 8 pillars are divided into **2 categories**: **4 Foundation Pillars** (must be done first) and **4 Implementation Pillars** (deliver the results).

### A. Foundation Pillars (Done Before Production Activities)

1. **Pillar 1 — 5S (Housekeeping)**
   - The literal base. Without a clean workplace, no TPM can survive.
   - *Seiri (Sort), Seiton (Set), Seiso (Shine), Seiketsu (Standardize), Shitsuke (Sustain).*

2. **Pillar 2 — Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement)**
   - Small-group, cross-functional teams eliminate the **6 Big Losses** systematically.

3. **Pillar 3 — Education & Training**
   - Build multi-skilled operators and engineers. "**No machine should operate without an educated operator.**"

4. **Pillar 4 — Safety, Health & Environment (SHE)**
   - **Zero accident** culture. Crosses with all other pillars.

### B. Implementation Pillars (Deliver Tangible Results)

5. **Pillar 5 — Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)**
   - **Operators** own daily maintenance: **CLIT** (Clean, Lubricate, Inspect, Tighten).
   - The most powerful pillar — it transfers ownership.

6. **Pillar 6 — Planned Maintenance (PM)**
   - Engineering-driven **preventive + predictive** maintenance schedules.

7. **Pillar 7 — Quality Maintenance (QM)**
   - Eliminate defects at the source by maintaining **Condition-Based** standards.

8. **Pillar 8 — Office TPM (Admin TPM)**
   - Extends TPM logic to **information, supply chain, sales, and admin** flows.

## 2.2 The 6 Big Losses (Six Major Losses) — Heart of OEE

> [!IMPORTANT] These 6 losses are the **enemies of OEE**. Memorize them — they appear in almost every TPM question.

| Category | The 6 Big Losses | Impact Area |
|----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Availability Losses** | 1. Equipment Failure / Breakdown | Stops machine |
| | 2. Setup & Adjustment Loss | Changeover time |
| **Performance Losses** | 3. Idling & Minor Stoppages | Brief halts |
| | 4. Reduced Speed Loss | Running below designed speed |
| **Quality Losses** | 5. Defect / Rework Loss | Reject and rework |
| | 6. Startup / Yield Loss | Bad pieces at start of run |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **OEE is the single most important metric in TPM.** It is asked almost every year.

| Metric | Formula | Range | World-Class |
|--------|---------|-------|-------------|
| **Availability** | $\text{A} = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | 0 to 1 | $\geq$ **90%** |
| **Performance Rate** | $\text{P} = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | 0 to 1 | $\geq$ **95%** |
| **Quality Rate** | $\text{Q} = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | 0 to 1 | $\geq$ **99.9%** |
| **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | 0 to 1 | $\geq$ **85%** |
| **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours | Maximize |
| **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours | Minimize |
| **MTTF** | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours | Maximize |
| **MTBA** | $\text{MTBA} = \text{MTBF} - \text{MTTR}$ | Hours | Maximize |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of TPM

- **Automotive:** Toyota, Honda, Maruti Suzuki use TPM to achieve OEE > 85%.
- **Pharma:** GMP-compliant plants use TPM for audit-ready equipment.
- **FMCG & Bottling:** Reduces changeover losses in high-mix production.
- **Semiconductor:** TPM + predictive maintenance drives fab utilization > 90%.
- **Cost Impact:** Industry studies show TPM reduces maintenance cost by **30–50%** and breakdowns by **70–90%**.

## 2.5 Autonomous Maintenance vs Planned Maintenance — Examiner's Favourite Contrast

| Feature | Autonomous Maintenance (AM) | Planned Maintenance (PM) |
|---------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Owner** | Machine **operator** | **Maintenance engineer** |
| **Frequency** | **Daily** (per shift) | Weekly / Monthly / Yearly |
| **Activities** | Clean, Lubricate, Inspect, Tighten (CLIT) | Overhaul, calibration, predictive checks |
| **Skill Level** | Basic — trained by maintenance | Advanced — engineering judgement |
| **TPM Pillar** | Pillar 5 | Pillar 6 |
| **Goal** | Detect abnormalities early | Prevent failures systematically |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of OEE — From Losses to Metric

> [!NOTE] **Why does OEE exist?** Because traditional metrics (machine utilization, plant efficiency) hide losses. OEE exposes **every** wasted second.

### Step 1 — Define Planned Production Time (PPT)

PPT is the time the factory is **scheduled** to run, excluding breaks, weekends, and planned holidays.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PPT} = \text{Total Calendar Time} - \text{Planned Downtime (lunch, breaks, holidays)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Subtract Unplanned Downtime to get Operating Time (OT)

Operating Time excludes **breakdown loss** and **setup/adjustment loss**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OT} = \text{PPT} - \text{Equipment Failure Time} - \text{Setup/Changeover Time}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Availability (A)

Availability captures the **Availability Losses** (Losses 1 & 2 of the 6 Big Losses).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \frac{\text{OT}}{\text{PPT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Performance Rate (P)

Performance captures the **Performance Losses** (Losses 3 & 4).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** If the machine is *supposed* to make 1 piece every 10 seconds, then for $N$ pieces it should take $10N$ seconds. Anything above that is **Performance Loss**.

### Step 5 — Calculate Quality Rate (Q)

Quality captures the **Quality Losses** (Losses 5 & 6).

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces (passed final inspection)}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Combine into OEE

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **OEE is multiplicative, not additive.** A 90% × 90% × 90% line gives **72.9%**, not 270%. This is why small losses are devastating in TPM.

## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU Numerical Problem

### **Problem:**
A CNC machine in a KTU model factory is scheduled to run for **480 minutes** per shift. During the shift, the machine **breaks down for 30 minutes**, and **15 minutes** are lost to changeover. The **ideal cycle time is 2 minutes/piece**, the machine actually produces **180 pieces** in operating time, and **10 pieces** are rejected at the end of the line. Calculate **OEE** and identify which category needs improvement.

### **Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1 — Planned Production Time (PPT):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PPT} = 480 \text{ minutes (given)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Operating Time (OT):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OT} = \text{PPT} - \text{Breakdown} - \text{Setup} = 480 - 30 - 15 = 435 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability (A):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \frac{\text{OT}}{\text{PPT}} = \frac{435}{480} = 0.90625 = 90.625\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Pieces and Good Pieces:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Pieces} &= 180 \\
\text{Good Pieces} &= 180 - 10 = 170
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Performance Rate (P):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ideal Run Time} &= \text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces} = 2 \times 180 = 360 \text{ minutes} \\
P &= \frac{360}{435} = 0.8276 = 82.76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Quality Rate (Q):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \frac{170}{180} = 0.9444 = 94.44\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Overall OEE:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.90625 \times 0.8276 \times 0.9444
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} = 0.7083 = 70.83\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Conclusion:**
OEE = **70.83%** (below world-class 85%). The **Performance Rate (82.76%)** is the weakest link — the factory should run a **Kobetsu Kaizen** project to attack **idling** and **reduced speed losses**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — OEE Loss Tree

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE Loss} &= 1 - \text{OEE} = 1 - (A \times P \times Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Loss Category | Source | Reduction Pillar |
|---------------|--------|------------------|
| **1 - A** | Equipment failure, setup | Pillar 5, 6 |
| **1 - P** | Idling, reduced speed | Pillar 2 (Kaizen) |
| **1 - Q** | Defects, startup rejects | Pillar 7 (QM) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.3 The OEE Loss-Reduction Architecture (Pillars Mapped to Losses)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeGoal["ACHIEVE WORLD CLASS OEE 85 PERCENT"]

    nodeOEE["OEE METRIC"]
    nodeLoss1["LOSS 1 BREAKDOWN"]
```
