```mermaid
    nodeOEE --> nodeLoss1
    nodeOEE --> nodeLoss2
    nodeOEE --> nodeLoss3
    nodeOEE --> nodeLoss4
    nodeOEE --> nodeLoss5
    nodeOEE --> nodeLoss6

    nodeLoss1 --> nodeP5
    nodeLoss1 --> nodeP6
    nodeLoss2 --> nodeP6
    nodeLoss2 --> nodeP2
    nodeLoss3 --> nodeP5
    nodeLoss3 --> nodeP2
    nodeLoss4 --> nodeP2
    nodeLoss5 --> nodeP7
    nodeLoss6 --> nodeP7

    nodeP5 --> nodeGoal
    nodeP6 --> nodeGoal
    nodeP2 --> nodeGoal
    nodeP7 --> nodeGoal
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define TPM. State its three ultimate goals.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is a company-wide, proactive maintenance system developed by **Seiichi Nakajima** in 1971, institutionalized by the **Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance (JIPM)**. It involves **top management, engineers, and shop-floor operators** working together to maximize equipment effectiveness.
>
> The **three ultimate goals** of TPM are:
> 1. **Zero Breakdown** — No unexpected equipment failures.
> 2. **Zero Defect** — Every product conforms to specification at first time.
> 3. **Zero Accident** — A safe, hazard-free workplace.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for the three goals.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is the difference between **Autonomous Maintenance** and **Planned Maintenance**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Autonomous Maintenance | Planned Maintenance |
|--------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| **Owner** | Machine operator | Maintenance engineer |
| **Activities** | Cleaning, Lubricating, Inspecting, Tightening (CLIT) | Overhaul, calibration, predictive checks |
| **TPM Pillar** | Pillar 5 | Pillar 6 |
| **Goal** | Detect abnormalities early | Prevent failures through scheduled action |

Autonomous Maintenance **empowers the operator** to take ownership of the equipment, while Planned Maintenance uses **engineering expertise and schedules** to do deep maintenance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Lean Tools + Numerical*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** Explain the **8 pillars of TPM** as defined by JIPM. Briefly explain any **four** of them.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The **8 pillars of TPM** are classified into **4 foundation pillars** and **4 implementation pillars**.

**Foundation Pillars (must precede implementation):**

1. **5S (Housekeeping):** The base of TPM. It involves Seiri (Sort), Seiton (Set in order), Seiso (Shine), Seiketsu (Standardize), and Shitsuke (Sustain). Without 5S, no TPM activity can succeed.

2. **Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement):** Cross-functional small groups tackle the **6 Big Losses** using PDCA cycles and root-cause analysis to elevate OEE.

3. **Education & Training:** Building **multi-skilled** operators and engineers. The principle is *"No machine shall operate without an educated operator."*

4. **Safety, Health & Environment (SHE):** Achieves the **Zero Accident** goal. A safe plant is also a productive plant.

**Implementation Pillars (deliver the three zeros):**

5. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators perform daily CLIT activities. Creates ownership.

6. **Planned Maintenance (PM):** Engineering-driven preventive and predictive schedules to maximize MTBF and minimize MTTR.

7. **Quality Maintenance (QM):** Achieves **Zero Defect** by maintaining optimal equipment **conditions** (not just specifications).

8. **Office TPM:** Applies the same TPM logic to **information and administrative flows** to eliminate paperwork waste.

*(Marks split: 1 mark for classification, ½ mark each pillar, 2 marks for any four in detail.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A machine has the following data for one shift of 8 hours:
- Planned downtime for breaks: **30 minutes**
- Equipment failure time: **45 minutes**
- Setup/changeover: **15 minutes**
- Ideal cycle time: **1.2 minutes/piece**
- Total pieces produced: **300**
- Defective pieces: **20**

Calculate **Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE**. Is the machine world-class? Comment.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Planned Production Time (PPT):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PPT} = 8 \times 60 - 30 = 480 - 30 = 450 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Operating Time (OT):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OT} = 450 - 45 - 15 = 390 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability (A):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \frac{390}{450} = 0.8667 = 86.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Good Pieces:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Good Pieces} = 300 - 20 = 280
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Performance Rate (P):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P = \frac{1.2 \times 300}{390} = \frac{360}{390} = 0.9231 = 92.31\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Quality Rate (Q):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = \frac{280}{300} = 0.9333 = 93.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — OEE:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} = 0.8667 \times 0.9231 \times 0.9333 = 0.7468 = 74.68\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** OEE = **74.68%**, which is **below the world-class benchmark of 85%**. The weakest link is the **Quality Rate (93.33%)** — Pillar 7 (Quality Maintenance) should be activated to attack the **20 defectives**, and **Planned Maintenance** must be strengthened to reduce the **45-minute breakdown loss**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]** What is **OEE**? Explain the **6 Big Losses** in detail with the formula of OEE.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is the gold-standard metric of TPM. It measures the percentage of **manufacturing time that is truly productive** — a perfect machine producing only good parts at full speed.

**Formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance Rate} \times \text{Quality Rate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The 6 Big Losses:**

| Loss | Category | Description |
|------|----------|-------------|
| **1. Equipment Failure** | Availability Loss | Sudden breakdowns, tool crashes |
| **2. Setup & Adjustment** | Availability Loss | Changeover time, die change, line clearance |
| **3. Idling & Minor Stops** | Performance Loss | Brief halts (jam, sensor trips) under 5 minutes |
| **4. Reduced Speed** | Performance Loss | Running below designed cycle time |
| **5. Defect & Rework** | Quality Loss | Scrap, rework, rejects |
| **6. Startup Yield** | Quality Loss | Bad pieces during warm-up or shift start |

**World-class OEE** = **85% or more**, which equals: **A = 90%, P = 95%, Q = 99.9%**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Define **MTBF** and **MTTR**. A CNC lathe operated for **1200 hours** in a year, during which it failed **6 times**. The total downtime for repair was **24 hours**. Calculate MTBF, MTTR, and Availability.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average running time of the machine between two consecutive failures. *It measures equipment reliability.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{1200}{6} = 200 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time taken to repair a failure and bring the machine back into operation. *It measures maintenance efficiency.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Availability:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{200}{200 + 4} = \frac{200}{204} = 0.9804 = 98.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The machine has excellent **availability of 98.04%**, but TPM would still target **MTTR < 2 hours** through **Autonomous Maintenance** (Pillar 5) and **Planned Maintenance** (Pillar 6) to push OEE toward 100%.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Where Students Lose Marks in TPM Questions**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTR** — MTBF = *time between* failures (run-time), MTTR = *time to repair* (down-time). Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark if swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract both breakdown AND setup** from PPT to get Operating Time. Setup loss is **not** the same as break time.
> 3. **OEE is multiplicative, not additive** — writing $\text{OEE} = A + P + Q$ costs full marks.
> 4. **Mixing up the 6 Big Losses categories** — Each loss belongs to **only one** of A, P, or Q. Drawing a clear table avoids confusion.
> 5. **Writing 5S, TQM, or Just-in-Time** in place of TPM pillars — TPM has **exactly 8 pillars** (JIPM), and 5S is **Pillar 1**, not a separate system.
> 6. **Units missing in OEE numericals** — Always write "%" after the answer; without units, 0.5 mark is lost.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute Revision Flash-Card**

- **TPM = Total Productive Maintenance**, founder **Seiichi Nakajima (1971, Nippondenso, Toyota group)**, standardized by **JIPM**.
- **Three Zeros:** Zero Breakdown, Zero Defect, Zero Accident.
- **8 Pillars:** 5S, Kobetsu Kaizen, Education & Training, SHE | Autonomous Maintenance, Planned Maintenance, Quality Maintenance, Office TPM.
- **OEE = A × P × Q** ; world-class OEE = **≥ 85%**.
- **Availability (≥ 90%)** = Operating Time / Planned Production Time = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- **Performance (≥ 95%)** = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Operating Time.
- **Quality (≥ 99.9%)** = Good Pieces / Total Pieces.
- **6 Big Losses:** Breakdown, Setup (Availability); Idling, Reduced Speed (Performance); Defect, Startup Yield (Quality).
- **CLIT** = Clean, Lubricate, Inspect, Tighten — daily activities of **Autonomous Maintenance**.
- **MTBF = Total Operating Time / No. of Failures** (maximize).
- **MTTR = Total Downtime / No. of Failures** (minimize).
- **OEE is multiplicative** — small losses multiply into large total losses.
- **TPM is a culture, not a department** — operators are the first-line doctors of the machine.
- **5S is the foundation** of TPM — no housekeeping, no TPM.
- **Autonomous Maintenance = Pillar 5**, Planned Maintenance = **Pillar 6** — examiner's favourite contrast.
- **Award:** JIPM's **TPM Excellence Award** (formerly PM Award) recognizes world-class implementation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standardized Work and Its Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standardized Work and Its Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Work** is a precise, documented, and visually communicated set of procedures that establishes the **optimum sequence of work steps**, the **standard time (Takt Time)** at which those steps must be performed, and the **minimum quantity of in-process inventory** required to keep the process flowing smoothly. It was pioneered by **Taiichi Ohno** as a foundational pillar of the Toyota Production System (TPS).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Standardized Work is treated as the **"baseline of continuous improvement (Kaizen)"** — it is the agreed-upon current best method that serves as the launchpad for all future improvements. Until a process is standardized, any improvement is meaningless because there is no reference point to measure the improvement against.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Philosophy — Ohno's Law:** *"Without standards, there can be no Kaizen."* Standardized Work is therefore the prerequisite for Lean transformation, not a byproduct of it.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars (Elements) of Standardized Work

Standardized Work is constructed on three essential elements:

1. **Takt Time** — the heartbeat of production (the rhythm at which a unit must be completed to satisfy customer demand).
2. **Work Sequence** — the precise, ordered series of steps an operator follows to process a unit without interruption or waste.
3. **Standard In-Process Stock** — the minimum work-in-progress (WIP) needed between operations to enable smooth flow, no more, no less.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Chef

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen during the dinner rush. The head chef creates a "recipe card" (Standardized Work) for each dish that specifies:
> - **Takt Time:** Each new order must be plated within 8 minutes because customers expect food within 10 minutes (customer demand = 60 orders/hour, hence 10/60 = 10 minutes/order... in real kitchens, Takt ≈ 1 minute/order).
> - **Work Sequence:** First chop onions → sauté → add sauce → plate → garnish (in that exact order).
> - **Standard In-Process Stock:** Exactly 2 partially prepared orders can wait between the stove station and the plating station (not 1, not 5 — just enough to keep the chef busy without food getting cold).

If a new cook joins, they simply follow the recipe card. If a faster method is discovered, the chef updates the card, and *everyone* benefits. This is exactly how Standardized Work functions on a shop floor.

## 1.4 Why Standardized Work is NOT Bureaucracy

A common student misconception is that standardization is rigid or anti-creative. In Lean thinking, it is the **opposite**:
- It **frees** the operator's mind from decision-making on routine steps.
- It **reveals** abnormalities (when reality deviates from the standard).
- It **enables** Kaizen by providing a measurable baseline.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Takt Time** | TT | **seconds/unit** or **minutes/unit** |
| **Cycle Time** | CT | seconds/unit |
| **Available Production Time** | APT | seconds/day or minutes/day |
| **Customer Demand** | D | units/day |
| **Standard WIP / In-Process Stock** | S | units |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Element 1 — Takt Time (The Heartbeat of Production)

**Takt Time** is derived from the German word *Taktzeit*, meaning "rhythm" or "cycle," borrowed from musical terminology. It represents the maximum allowable time to produce one unit in order to meet customer demand exactly.

### 2.1.1 Logic Steps to Determine Takt Time

1. **Identify Customer Demand (D):** Determine how many units customers will buy per defined period (typically per day).
2. **Calculate Net Available Production Time (APT):** Subtract all planned downtime (breaks, meetings, changeovers, preventive maintenance) from the gross working time.
3. **Divide APT by D:** This gives the rate at which the *market* demands products.

### 2.1.2 Why Takt Time Dictates Pace

- If **Cycle Time (CT) < Takt Time (TT):** The process is *over-producing* → leads to inventory buildup and overcapacity.
- If **Cycle Time (CT) = Takt Time (TT):** Perfect flow — production matches demand.
- If **Cycle Time (CT) > Takt Time (TT):** The process is *under-capacity* → delays, missed deliveries, overtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (often tested in KTU):** Takt Time is set by the **customer**, not by the machine or the operator. Cycle Time is set by the **process**. Lean demands that Cycle Time be brought *equal to or just below* Takt Time.

## 2.2 Element 2 — Work Sequence (The Choreography)

The **Work Sequence** is the precise, step-by-step order in which an operator performs the value-added and necessary non-value-added work to process a single unit from raw state to finished state.

### 2.2.1 Criteria for a Good Work Sequence

- **Repetitive and Cyclical:** Designed to be repeated identically every cycle.
- **Within Takt Time:** All steps must cumulatively fit within the available Takt Time.
- **Ergonomic & Safe:** Operator motions should follow natural body movement; no stretching, twisting, or walking long distances.
- **Quality-Embedded:** Quality checks are built *into* the sequence, not added afterward.
- **Visually Self-Explanatory:** Even a new operator should be able to understand it in under 60 seconds.

### 2.2.2 The Role of Motion Economy

The work sequence incorporates **motion economy principles** (developed by Frank Gilbreth):
- Both hands should start and finish motions simultaneously.
- Hands should not be idle except during rest periods.
- Motions should be confined to the lowest classification (finger > wrist > elbow > shoulder).
- Smooth, continuous curved motions are preferred over sharp, straight-line changes.

## 2.3 Element 3 — Standard In-Process Stock (The Buffer)

**Standard In-Process Stock** is the minimum quantity of parts or WIP permitted between two adjacent operations to keep the line flowing despite minor fluctuations (micro-stoppages, walking time, inspection).

### 2.3.1 The Golden Rule of In-Process Stock

- **Too little stock** → Operators wait (starvation); line stops immediately when a hiccup occurs.
- **Too much stock** → Defects hidden, lead time increases, capital tied up, transportation waste.
- **Just-right stock** → Enables the operator to work at Takt Time without being interrupted by upstream/downstream variation.

### 2.3.2 Kanban as the Operational Twin

In a true TPS environment, Standard In-Process Stock is physically controlled by **Kanban cards** — no more, no fewer cards per station, each card representing one unit of authorized WIP.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *definitive formula reference* for KTU examination questions on Standardized Work. Memorize this thoroughly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Takt Time** | $TT = \dfrac{APT}{D}$ | $TT$ in sec/unit, $APT$ in seconds/day, $D$ in units/day |
| 2 | **Net Available Production Time** | $APT = GT - DT$ | $GT$ = Gross Time, $DT$ = Downtime |
| 3 | **Number of Operators Required** | $N = \dfrac{\text{Total Work Content}}{TT}$ | Rounded up to next whole integer |
| 4 | **Total Work Content (TWC)** | $TWC = \sum CT_i$ | Sum of all individual operation cycle times |
| 5 | **Line Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{TWC}{N \times TT} \times 100\%$ | Ideally $\geq 90\%$ for a well-balanced line |
| 6 | **Standard WIP Boundary** | $S_{min} = 1$ unit between stations | Minimum; $S_{max}$ set by Kanban policy |
| 7 | **Production Rate** | $PR = \dfrac{APT}{CT}$ | Units produced per period |
| 8 | **Capacity Utilization** | $CU = \dfrac{D \times CT}{APT} \times 100\%$ | Should be $\leq 100\%$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Standardized Work is not limited to automotive assembly. It is deployed in:

- **Aerospace manufacturing** (Boeing, Airbus wing assembly): Sequencing critical torque and riveting operations to within seconds.
- **Hospital operating theaters:** Standardized surgical checklists (akin to Standardized Work) to eliminate variability.
- **Software DevOps:** "Definition of Done" documents function as Standardized Work for code deployment.
- **Hospitality & QSR chains** (McDonald's, Marriott): Every kitchen and housekeeping sequence is a Standardized Work sheet.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** When asked "Why standardize?" in the exam, always answer in a **3-layer hierarchy**:
> 1. **Stability** (foundation for all Lean tools — Heijunka, Kanban, Jidoka).
> 2. **Visibility** (abnormalities are exposed immediately).
> 3. **Improvement** (baseline for measuring Kaizen gains).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Takt Time from First Principles

Takt Time is derived from the fundamental Lean principle of **demand-driven flow**. We begin with the basic rate-balance equation.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the gross available production time per working day, $GT$, in seconds.
For a typical 8-hour shift:

$$GT = 8 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} \times 60 \text{ sec/min} = 28{,}800 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2:** Subtract all planned non-productive time (breaks, meetings, changeovers) to get net available production time.

$$APT = GT - DT$$

*Conversion logic:* $APT$ represents the only time during which a value-adding unit can be produced. Any time classified as $DT$ does not generate customer value.

**Step 3:** Determine the customer demand rate, $D$, in units per day (e.g., pulled from the production schedule or sales orders).

**Step 4:** Divide $APT$ by $D$. Since $APT$ is in seconds and $D$ is in units, the result has units of *seconds per unit*, which is precisely the **Takt Time**.

$$TT = \frac{APT \text{ [seconds/day]}}{D \text{ [units/day]}} = \text{seconds/unit}$$

*Interpretation:* $TT$ is the *maximum allowed time* the operator has to complete every single unit, including walking, picking, processing, and inspecting — every microsecond counts.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Takt Time Calculation (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A workstation operates in a single 8-hour shift. Two 15-minute tea breaks and one 30-minute changeover are scheduled. Customer demand is **240 units/day**. Calculate the Takt Time.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate gross time.

$$GT = 8 \times 60 \times 60 = 28{,}800 \text{ seconds/day}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate total downtime.

$$DT = (2 \times 15 \text{ min}) + 30 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ minutes} = 3{,}600 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate net available production time.

$$APT = 28{,}800 - 3{,}600 = 25{,}200 \text{ seconds/day}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate Takt Time.

$$TT = \frac{25{,}200}{240} = 105 \text{ seconds/unit}$$

**Result:** The operator has **105 seconds** to produce each unit. If the cycle time exceeds 105 seconds, demand will not be satisfied.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Number of Operators and Line Efficiency

### Conceptual Foundation

When multiple operations are to be assigned to a single paced line, the total workload (sum of all individual cycle times) must be distributed among operators such that no operator's work exceeds the Takt Time.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the Total Work Content (TWC) by summing all operation cycle times.

$$TWC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} CT_i$$

where $n$ is the number of distinct operations and $CT_i$ is the cycle time of operation $i$.

**Step 2:** Divide TWC by Takt Time to find the *theoretical* minimum number of operators.

$$N_{theoretical} = \frac{TWC}{TT}$$

**Step 3:** Round *up* to the next whole integer (you cannot have 2.4 operators).

$$N = \lceil N_{theoretical} \rceil$$

**Step 4:** Calculate line efficiency.

$$\eta = \frac{TWC}{N \times TT} \times 100\%$$

*Conversion logic:* $\eta$ measures how much of the operator's paid time is *theoretically* utilized for value-adding work. The remainder $(1 - \eta)$ represents unavoidable idle time or unbalance.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Operator Count & Line Balance (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 5-operation assembly line has the following cycle times (in seconds): 45, 30, 60, 50, 35. Takt Time is **50 seconds**. Calculate (a) the number of operators required, (b) the line efficiency, and (c) propose a balanced assignment.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Sum the work content.

$$TWC = 45 + 30 + 60 + 50 + 35 = 220 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate theoretical operators.

$$N_{theoretical} = \frac{220}{50} = 4.4 \text{ operators}$$

**Step 3:** Round up.

$$N = \lceil 4.4 \rceil = 5 \text{ operators}$$

**Step 4:** Line efficiency.

$$\eta = \frac{220}{5 \times 50} \times 100\% = \frac{220}{250} \times 100\% = 88\%$$

**Step 5:** Balanced work assignment (grouping operations so each operator's total ≈ 50 sec):

| Operator | Assigned Operations | Workload (sec) | Within Takt? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Op 1 | Op 1 (45) | 45 | ✓ |
| Op 2 | Op 2 (30) | 30 | ✓ |
| Op 3 | Op 3 (60) → *exceeds!* | 60 | ✗ — Must rebalance |
| Op 4 | Op 4 (50) | 50 | ✓ |
| Op 5 | Op 5 (35) | 35 | ✓ |

*Note:* Operator 3 violates Takt. In practice, the line must be rebalanced (e.g., shift 10 sec of work from Op 3 to Op 5), or the operation must be split between two stations.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Standardized Work Tool

| Situation Observed on Shop Floor | Applicable Element | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Operators idle, no parts to work on | Standard In-Process Stock | Increase Kanban quantity or fix upstream flow |
| Bottleneck operation, line stops repeatedly | Takt Time analysis | Re-balance, add capacity, or reduce demand |
| Variability in product quality | Work Sequence | Standardize the sequence, embed poka-yoke |
| Inconsistent cycle times between shifts | Work Sequence + Takt Time | Train all shifts on the same standardized sheet |
| Excess WIP between stations | Standard In-Process Stock | Reduce Kanban quantity, apply one-piece flow |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Elements of Standardized Work — Conceptual Map

This diagram illustrates the interrelationship of the three elements and how they collectively define a stable baseline for Kaizen.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STANDARDIZED WORK] --> B[Takt Time - The Heartbeat]
    A --> C[Work Sequence - The Choreography]
    A --> D[Standard In-Process Stock - The Buffer]
    B --> E[Derived from Customer Demand]
    C --> F[Defines Operator Motion Path]
    D --> G[Controlled by Kanban System]
    E --> H[Stable Baseline Established]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Foundation for KAIZEN]
    H --> J[Foundation for JIDOKA]
    H --> K[Foundation for HEIJUNKA]
    I --> L[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    L --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Standardized Work is the *intersection* of three elements. Once stabilized, it becomes the launchpad for the four pillars of TPS: Kaizen, Jidoka, Heijunka, and Just-in-Time. The improvement loop then *re-enters* the system by updating the Standardized Work.

---

## 4.2 Process Flow for Developing Standardized Work

The following block-level architecture shows the **5-step methodology** used by Industrial Engineers to develop a Standardized Work sheet for a new or revised process.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[STEP 1: Define Customer Demand] --> S2[STEP 2: Calculate Takt Time]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3: Observe Current Operator Cycle]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4: Document Best Practice as Work Sequence]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5: Define Standard WIP Between Operations]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6: Validate with Time Study]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7: Publish Standardized Work Sheet]
    S7 --> S8[STEP 8: Train All Operators]
    S8 --> S9[STEP 9: Audit for Compliance]
    S9 --> S10[STEP 10: Trigger KAIZEN When Deviation Found]
    S10 --> S1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The development cycle is *iterative and continuous*. After Step 9, if any deviation or improvement opportunity is found, control returns to Step 1, and the cycle begins anew — embodying the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) philosophy.

---

## 4.3 Standardized Work Sheet — Functional Layout

The following block diagram represents the **physical layout of a Standardized Work Sheet** as it appears on the shop floor (typically posted at eye level next to the workstation).

```mermaid
graph LR
    BlockA[BLOCK 1: Production Volume and Takt Time] --> BlockB
    BlockB[BLOCK 2: Layout Diagram of Workstation] --> BlockC
    BlockC[BLOCK 3: Work Sequence and Cycle Time per Step] --> BlockD
    BlockD[BLOCK 4: Standard In-Process Stock Locations Marked] --> BlockE
    BlockE[BLOCK 5: Quality and Safety Critical Points Highlighted]
    BlockA -. References .-> Metrics[Demand Data from Sales]
    BlockB -. References .-> Layout[Current Cell Layout]
    BlockC -. References .-> Operator[Time Observation Studies]
    BlockD -. References .-> Kanban[Kanban Policy]
    BlockE -. References .-> SOP[Quality Manual]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block on the sheet is sourced from a specific upstream Lean artifact. A senior KTU examiner expects students to identify that the Standardized Work sheet is a *composite* document, not a single-engineer creation.

---

## 4.4 Cycle-Time vs. Takt-Time Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    X[Compute Cycle Time CT] --> Y{Compare CT with Takt Time TT}
    Y --> Z1[CT less than TT]
    Y --> Z2[CT equal to TT]
    Y --> Z3[CT greater than TT]
    Z1 --> A1[Overproduction Risk - Imbalance]
    Z2 --> A2[Ideal State - Perfect Flow]
    Z3 --> A3[Bottleneck - Action Required]
    A1 --> R1[Reassign Work to Other Operators]
    A2 --> R2[Maintain and Standardize]
    A3 --> R3[Apply 5-Why Analysis]
    R3 --> R4{Can CT be Reduced}
    R4 --> R5[Yes - Apply Kaizen]
    R4 --> R6[No - Add Resources or Subcontract]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> *"Define Standardized Work. List its three elements."* **(3 Marks)**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** Standardized Work is a documented, agreed-upon set of procedures that defines the optimum method, sequence, and time required to perform a task, established as the baseline for continuous improvement (Kaizen). It is a foundational pillar of the Toyota Production System, ensuring stability, repeatability, and visibility of abnormalities.

**Three Elements (1 mark, 0.33 each):**
1. **Takt Time** — the rate at which a unit must be produced to meet customer demand.
2. **Work Sequence** — the precise order of operator steps to process one unit.
3. **Standard In-Process Stock** — the minimum WIP quantity needed between operations.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> *"Differentiate between Takt Time and Cycle Time. Why is this distinction important in Lean?"* **(3 Marks)**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Takt Time | Cycle Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Time allowed per unit to meet customer demand | Actual time taken to produce one unit |
| **Determined by (1 mark)** | Market / customer demand | Process / machine / operator capability |
| **Lean Goal** | Cycle Time $\leq$ Takt Time for balanced flow | — |

**Importance (1 mark):** This distinction is critical because Lean demands that the *pace of production* (Cycle Time) be matched to the *pace of demand* (Takt Time). Any mismatch — either under-capacity (CT > TT) or over-production (CT < TT) — creates waste (waiting or inventory), which is precisely what Lean aims to eliminate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student attempts **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Suggested Module 2 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Define Standardized Work and explain its three elements in detail with a suitable industrial example. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A workstation operates for 7 hours per shift. Downtime includes two 10-minute breaks and one 20-minute setup change. Customer demand is 180 units per shift. Calculate the Takt Time. If the operator's measured cycle time is 80 seconds, analyze whether the line can meet demand and recommend corrective action. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** Standardized Work is a precise, documented procedure that specifies the optimal sequence of work steps, the time allowed per unit (Takt Time), and the minimum in-process stock required to keep the process flowing. It was developed by Taiichi Ohno to provide a stable foundation for all Lean improvements.

**Element 1 — Takt Time (2 marks):**
- Takt Time = (Available Production Time) ÷ (Customer Demand).
- It is the *pulse* of the production line, derived from customer demand, not machine speed.
- Example: In a two-shift car assembly plant producing 480 cars/day with 720 minutes of net production time per day, Takt Time = 720 × 60 / 480 = **90 seconds/car**.

**Element 2 — Work Sequence (2 marks):**
- The exact, ordered list of operator actions — picking, placing, fastening, inspecting — performed in a fixed, repeatable order.
- Example: A smartphone assembly operator follows a 12-step sequence: place frame → insert battery → connect ribbon cable → place PCB → secure 4 screws → install camera → close housing → tighten 2 housing screws → test power-on → pack in tray → label → box.

**Element 3 — Standard In-Process Stock (1 mark):**
- The minimum WIP held between two stations to absorb micro-variations.
- Example: Between the welding station and the paint station of a bicycle frame line, exactly 3 frames are held as standard stock (controlled by 3 Kanban cards).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Gross time (1 mark):**
$$GT = 7 \text{ hr} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} \times 60 \text{ sec/min} = 25{,}200 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Total downtime (1 mark):**
$$DT = (2 \times 10) + 20 = 40 \text{ minutes} = 2{,}400 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Net available production time (1 mark):**
$$APT = 25{,}200 - 2{,}400 = 22{,}800 \text{ seconds/day}$$

**Step 4 — Takt Time (1 mark):**
$$TT = \frac{22{,}800}{180} = 126.67 \text{ seconds/unit}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison (1.5 marks):**
Measured Cycle Time (CT) = 80 seconds.
Since $CT = 80 < TT = 126.67$, the cycle time is *less than* Takt Time → the process has **excess capacity** (idle time per cycle = 126.67 − 80 = 46.67 seconds).

**Step 6 — Recommendation (1.5 marks):**
- The line *can* meet demand, but the operator has 46.67 seconds of idle time per cycle.
- **Recommendation:** Either (i) **assign additional work** to this operator (work enrichment) to utilize the idle time, (ii) **reduce the shift hours** to save labor cost, or (iii) **consolidate two workstations** into one if the ergonomic analysis permits. The 46.67 seconds can be redirected toward quality checks or minor changeover tasks to improve overall equipment effectiveness (OEE).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Regular]**
> **(a)** Explain the three elements of Standardized Work in detail. Discuss the role of Standardized Work as the foundation for Kaizen. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A manufacturing line has 6 operations with cycle times (in seconds): 40, 55, 35, 50, 30, 45. The Takt Time is 60 seconds. Calculate the number of operators required and the line efficiency. Suggest a balanced work assignment and identify any remaining bottleneck. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Element 1 — Takt Time (2 marks):**
Takt Time synchronizes production rhythm with customer demand. Formula: $TT = APT / D$. It ensures the line produces only what is needed, no more (avoiding overproduction — the worst of the 7 wastes).

**Element 2 — Work Sequence (2 marks):**
A repeatable, ergonomic, and quality-embedded sequence of operator actions. Visualized through a Standardized Work Combination Sheet, it eliminates motion waste and ensures consistency across shifts.

**Element 3 — Standard In-Process Stock (2 marks):**
The minimum WIP buffer between stations. Controlled via Kanban, it absorbs micro-stoppages without overproducing. It enables one-piece flow where possible, and small-batch flow where not.

**Foundation for Kaizen (1 mark):**
Without Standardized Work, there is no baseline to measure improvement against. Once a stable standard exists, deviations become visible (abnormalities), and Kaizen teams can systematically reduce waste. After Kaizen, the standard is *updated*, completing the PDCA cycle.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Total Work Content (1 mark):**
$$TWC = 40 + 55 + 35 + 50 + 30 + 45 = 255 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical operators (1.5 marks):**
$$N_{theoretical} = \frac{TWC}{TT} = \frac{255}{60} = 4.25 \text{ operators}$$

**Step 3 — Required operators (1 mark):**
$$N = \lceil 4.25 \rceil = 5 \text{ operators}$$

**Step 4 — Line efficiency (1.5 marks):**
$$\eta = \frac{TWC}{N \times TT} \times 100\% = \frac{255}{5 \times 60} \times 100\% = \frac{255}{300} \times 100\% = 85\%$$

**Step 5 — Balanced assignment and bottleneck identification (2 marks):**

| Operator | Operations Assigned | Workload (sec) | vs. Takt (60 sec) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Op 1 | Op 1 (40) | 40 | ✓ (20 sec slack) |
| Op 2 | Op 2 (55) | 55 | ✓ (5 sec slack) |
| Op 3 | Op 3 (35) + Op 5 (30) = 65 | 65 | ✗ **BOTTLENECK** |
| Op 4 | Op 4 (50) | 50 | ✓ (10 sec slack) |
| Op 5 | Op 6 (45) | 45 | ✓ (15 sec slack) |

**Bottleneck (built into question):** Operator 3 carries 65 seconds of work, exceeding the Takt Time of 60 seconds by 5 seconds. **Corrective action:** Transfer 5+ seconds of work from Op 3 to Op 1 (combine Op 1 with a small piece of Op 3), or apply Kaizen to reduce the Op 3 cycle time itself (target: 30 sec instead of 35 sec), thereby eliminating the imbalance and raising line efficiency toward 100%.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Exams (Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing Takt Time with Cycle Time** — *Takt Time is demand-driven, Cycle Time is process-driven.* Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the student uses them interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract downtime** when calculating $APT$ — a classic trap. Always show $APT = GT - DT$ explicitly.
> 3. **Rounding operators the wrong way** — always round *up* ($\lceil \cdot \rceil$); fractional operators do not exist in practice. Examiners often test this with values like 4.25.
> 4. **Omitting units** in the final Takt Time answer — always write *"seconds/unit"* or *"minutes/unit"*. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
> 5. **Stating "Standardized Work reduces variability" without explaining how** — examiners want the link to *abnormality visibility*. Always connect standardization to detection of deviations.
> 6. **Listing Kaizen as a *part* of Standardized Work** — incorrect. Standardized Work is the *precondition* for Kaizen, not a component of it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **Definition:** Standardized Work = Takt Time + Work Sequence + Standard In-Process Stock.
- **Origin:** Developed by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota; documented in *Toyota Production System* ( TPS ).
- **Core Philosophy:** *"No standards → No Kaizen."* Standardization precedes improvement.
- **Takt Time Formula:** $TT = APT / D$; always in *time per unit*.
- **APT Formula:** $APT = GT - DT$; includes subtraction of *all* planned downtime.
- **Cycle Time (CT) vs. Takt Time (TT):** Lean target is $CT \leq TT$.
- **Number of Operators:** $N = \lceil TWC / TT \rceil$.
- **Line Efficiency:** $\eta = TWC / (N \times TT) \times 100\%$; well-balanced lines target $\geq 85\%$.
- **Three Documents of Standardized Work:** (1) Production Capacity Sheet, (2) Standardized Work Combination Sheet, (3) Standardized Work Chart (operator balance chart).
- **Tools that depend on Standardized Work:** Heijunka (level scheduling), Kanban (WIP control), Jidoka (autonomation), Poka-Yoke (mistake proofing).
- **Industrial Applications:** Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors), Aerospace (Boeing), Healthcare (Surgical SOPs), Software (Definition of Done).
- **One-Sentence Exam Answer:** *"Standardized Work documents the safest, easiest, and most effective method known today, and commits the team to maintaining that method until a better one is proven."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Just In Time (JIT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Just In Time (JIT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Introduction to Just-In-Time (JIT)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Just-In-Time (JIT)** is a production management philosophy and strategic operational system whose primary objective is to eliminate waste (Muda) by ensuring that the right items, in the correct quantity, of perfect quality, are produced or delivered **exactly at the time they are needed** — neither earlier nor later. It is fundamentally a **pull-based demand-driven system** that minimizes Work-In-Progress (WIP) inventory and lead times throughout the entire value stream.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT413 - Module 2):**
> The 2024 KTU scheme defines JIT as a philosophy originating from the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** that aims to produce and deliver goods just in time to be sold, just in time to be processed, and just in time to be made into subassemblies. It is classified under lean manufacturing techniques focused on waste elimination across inventory, motion, waiting, and overproduction.

Mathematically stated, the JIT objective function can be represented as:

$$
\min \; \text{Inventory}_{t} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (I_{raw,i} + I_{WIP,i} + I_{finished,i}) \;\forall t
$$

Subject to the constraint:

$$
\text{Arrival Time of Material} \approx \text{Requirement Time at Station}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen running a JIT system**:
- The head chef (customer demand signal) tells the prep cook exactly what to chop and when.
- The prep cook does not pre-chop 50 kg of onions in the morning "just in case." He chops only as the chef calls out "table 7 ordered pasta."
- Ingredients arrive from suppliers in small, frequent batches — exactly when the prep cook is about to run out.

**What happens in a Non-JIT (Traditional) Kitchen?**
- Tons of pre-cut vegetables sit in buckets all day, wilt, and get thrown away.
- The fridge is packed, capital is locked up, and 30% of food is wasted.

**What JIT does in the factory:**
- Raw materials arrive at the loading dock exactly when the production line is ready to consume them.
- Sub-assemblies are produced only when the next station is ready to receive them.
- Finished goods are built only when a customer order arrives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** JIT is NOT merely "low inventory." It is a **holistic management philosophy** that exposes hidden problems (mura - unevenness and muri - overburden), enabling continuous improvement (Kaizen). Lower inventory is a *consequence*, not the goal.

### 1.3 Origin & Historical Context

| Era | Milestone | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1950s** | Taiichi Ohno at Toyota Motor Corporation, Japan | Developed TPS to compete with Western mass production |
| **1970s** | Oil Crisis exposed inefficiencies of large inventories | JIT gained global attention |
| **1980s** | "The Machine That Changed the World" (Womack, Jones, Roos) | Introduced "Lean Production" terminology to the West |
| **1990s–Present** | Adoption by Dell, Boeing, Tesla, Amazon | JIT evolved into modern supply chain paradigms (Vendor Managed Inventory, Demand-Driven MRP) |

### 1.4 Fundamental Objectives of JIT

1. **Zero Inventory** — Eliminate all forms of stock (raw, WIP, finished).
2. **Zero Defects** — Build quality into the process (Jidoka + Poka-Yoke).
3. **Zero Lead Time** — Compress order-to-delivery cycle.
4. **Zero Setup Time** — Achieve Single Minute Exchange of Die (SMED).
5. **Zero Handling** — Minimize motion and transportation waste.
6. **Zero breakdowns** — Total Productive Maintenance (TPM).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exam:** The **"Seven Zeros" of JIT** are an extremely high-yield answer for 3-mark definition-type questions. Memorize them as **IDL-SSMB** (Inventory, Defects, Lead time, Setup, Stock movement, Machine breakdowns).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophical Pillars of JIT

JIT rests on **five foundational pillars**. Each pillar is interlinked; weakening one collapses the entire system.

### Pillar 1: Continuous Flow (Heijunka)
- Production must move in a smooth, uninterrupted flow.
- **Levelling production** (heijunka) prevents overburden (muri) and unevenness (mura).
- Batch sizes are reduced progressively towards **one-piece flow**.

### Pillar 2: Pull System (Kanban)
- No upstream station produces a part until the downstream station signals demand.
- Eliminates overproduction — the **worst waste** according to Ohno.

### Pillar 3: Takt Time Synchronization
- Every operation's cycle time must match the customer demand rate (Takt time).
- Operations faster than takt time are slowed; slower ones are improved.

### Pillar 4: Built-in Quality (Jidoka + Poka-Yoke)
- Every worker has the authority to **stop the line (Andon)** when a defect is detected.
- Defects are not allowed to pass downstream ("*Next process is your customer*").

### Pillar 5: Stable & Reliable Suppliers
- Suppliers are treated as **partners**, not adversaries.
- Frequent, small deliveries with **zero-defect contracts**.

## 2.2 The Push vs. Pull Paradigm

| Parameter | Push System (Traditional MRP) | Pull System (JIT) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trigger to produce | Forecast / Schedule | Actual downstream demand |
| Inventory level | High (anticipation stock) | Minimal / Zero |
| Lead time | Long | Compressed |
| Responsiveness to change | Slow | Rapid |
| Information flow | Centralized (top-down) | Decentralized (signal-based) |
| Risk of overproduction | High | Near zero |
| Typical system | EOQ, ROP, MRP | Kanban, CONWIP, DBR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The examiner expects students to clearly articulate the **transition logic** from push to pull and identify the **waste categories (TIMWOOD + D)** that JIT specifically targets: **T**ransportation, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **W**aiting, **O**verproduction, **O**ver-processing, **D**efects.

## 2.3 Key Operational Elements of JIT

1. **Kanban (Signal Card):** The visual authorization to produce or move materials.
2. **Takt Time:** The heartbeat of production.
3. **Cycle Time:** The actual time taken by a station to complete one unit.
4. **Pitch Interval / EPE (Every Part Every):** Frequency at which a model is produced.
5. **Production Smoothing (Heijunka):** Level-loading of mixed models.
6. **Quick Changeover (SMED):** Single Minute Exchange of Die.
7. **Supplier Integration:** Close proximity, frequent deliveries, certified quality.
8. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM):** Autonomous and preventive maintenance by operators.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **directly tested** in KTU 2024 scheme numerical/analytical questions. Master them thoroughly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}}$ | $T_{available}$ = Net available time per period (seconds); $D_{demand}$ = Customer demand per period |
| 2 | **Number of Kanbans** | $N_{kanban} = \dfrac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}$ | $D$ = Demand rate (units/time); $L$ = Lead time; $S$ = Safety factor (decimal); $C$ = Container size |
| 3 | **Lead Time** | $L_{T} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{move} + L_{wait}$ | Sum of processing, queuing, moving, and waiting time |
| 4 | **Inventory Turns** | $IT = \dfrac{COGS}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Higher turns → better JIT performance |
| 5 | **WIP Reduction Ratio** | $\Delta_{WIP}\% = \dfrac{WIP_{before} - WIP_{after}}{WIP_{before}} \times 100$ | Measures JIT success |
| 6 | **Setup Reduction** | $SMED\% = \dfrac{ST_{before} - ST_{after}}{ST_{before}} \times 100$ | Single Minute Exchange of Die improvement |
| 7 | **One-Piece Flow Pitch** | $P_{pitch} = N_{stations} \times T_{takt}$ | Time to produce one complete unit across $N$ stations |
| 8 | **Production Smoothing** | $H_{level} = \dfrac{\sum \vert X_{i} - \bar{X} \vert}{n}$ | Heijunka variance — minimize for level loading |
| 9 | **Supplier Delivery Frequency** | $f_{del} = \dfrac{D_{daily}}{C_{container}}$ | Deliveries per day to maintain zero stock |
| 10 | **Line Balancing Efficiency** | $\eta_{balance} = \dfrac{\sum t_{i}}{N_{stations} \times T_{cycle}} \times 100$ | Higher efficiency enables JIT flow |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** In the Kanban formula, students frequently forget the safety factor multiplier $(1+S)$. Always include it when the question mentions "with safety stock." Also, ensure **unit consistency** — if takt time is in seconds, convert demand rate accordingly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Toyota, Tesla use JIT to keep working capital tied up in inventory near zero, enabling rapid model changes.
- **Aerospace:** Boeing's Everett plant uses modified JIT with "point-of-use" delivery to reduce factory floor congestion.
- **Electronics:** Apple operates a "demand-pull" supply chain where Foxconn only assembles iPhones after a confirmed order.
- **Healthcare:** Operating theatre JIT reduces wastage of perishable surgical kits.
- **Construction:** Just-In-Time material delivery minimizes on-site storage and damage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Number of Kanban Cards

**Problem Setup:** A workstation receives 600 parts per 8-hour shift. The lead time to replenish a container is 90 minutes. The standard container holds 30 parts. The safety factor is 20%. Find the minimum number of Kanban cards required.

**Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters**

$$
D = 600 \text{ parts/shift}, \quad L = 90 \text{ min}, \quad C = 30 \text{ parts}, \quad S = 0.20
$$

**Step 2 — Standardize Time Units**

Convert everything to a common unit (minutes per shift).

$$
\text{Total available time} = 8 \text{ hr} \times 60 = 480 \text{ min/shift}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Demand Rate per Minute**

$$
D_{min} = \frac{600 \text{ parts}}{480 \text{ min}} = 1.25 \text{ parts/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Kanban Formula**

$$
N_{kanban} = \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}
$$

Substitute:

$$
N_{kanban} = \frac{1.25 \;\text{parts/min} \times 90 \;\text{min} \times (1 + 0.20)}{30 \;\text{parts}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Numerator**

$$
N_{kanban} = \frac{1.25 \times 90 \times 1.20}{30}
$$

Compute the products step by step:

$$
1.25 \times 90 = 112.5
$$

$$
112.5 \times 1.20 = 135.0
$$

**Step 6 — Final Division**

$$
N_{kanban} = \frac{135.0}{30} = 4.5
$$

**Step 7 — Round Up to the Nearest Whole Number**

Since you cannot issue 4.5 Kanban cards (cards are discrete), always round **up**:

$$
N_{kanban} = 5 \text{ cards}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why round up?** Rounding down would create a stock-out condition. Kanban systems must always be conservative on the supply side to prevent line starvation. This is a classic KTU examiner trick.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Takt Time and Required Operator Count

**Problem Setup:** A plant operates 2 shifts of 8 hours each, with a 60-minute break per shift and 40 minutes of planned downtime (meetings, changeovers) per shift. Customer demand is 480 units/day. Compute (a) Takt time, (b) Cycle time per station if the line has 6 stations, and (c) whether the line is balanced.

**Step 1 — Compute Net Available Production Time**

$$
T_{available} = \left(8\text{hr} \times 60 - 60 - 40\right) \times 2 \text{ shifts}
$$

$$
= \left(480 - 100\right) \times 2 = 380 \times 2 = 760 \text{ min/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Takt Time**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}} = \frac{760 \text{ min}}{480 \text{ units}}
$$

$$
T_{takt} \approx 1.583 \text{ min/unit} \approx 95 \text{ seconds/unit}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Pitch (Cycle Time for the Line)**

If all 6 stations share the load equally:

$$
T_{cycle/station} = \frac{T_{takt}}{N_{stations}} = \frac{1.583}{6} \approx 0.264 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Line Balancing Efficiency**

Suppose the actual operation times for the 6 stations are: 0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.28, 0.27, 0.25 minutes (sum = 1.55 min).

$$
\eta_{balance} = \frac{\sum t_{i}}{N_{stations} \times T_{cycle}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{balance} = \frac{1.55}{6 \times 0.264} \times 100 = \frac{1.55}{1.583} \times 100 \approx 97.9\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

A balancing efficiency of **> 95%** indicates an excellent JIT-ready flow. The line is well-prepared for one-piece pull.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Waste Reduction in JIT

| Waste Type (Muda) | Traditional Cost | JIT Countermeasure | Typical Reduction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Overproduction | Highest in MRP | Pull system + Kanban | 60–80% |
| Waiting | Line stoppages | Continuous flow + Takt | 40–60% |
| Transportation | Long forklift moves | Point-of-use delivery | 30–50% |
| Over-processing | Unnecessary finishes | Value stream mapping | 25–45% |
| Inventory | Capital tied up | One-piece flow | 70–90% |
| Motion | Worker walking | 5S + Cellular layout | 30–50% |
| Defects | Rework / Scrap | Jidoka + Poka-Yoke | 50–75% |

---

## 3.4 Implementation Roadmap (5-Phase JIT Rollout)

1. **Phase 1 — Foundation:** 5S, Visual Management, Standard Work.
2. **Phase 2 — Stabilization:** TPM, Quick Changeover (SMED), Quality at source.
3. **Phase 3 — Flow:** Cellular layout, One-piece flow, Line balancing.
4. **Phase 4 — Pull:** Kanban implementation, Supplier integration.
5. **Phase 5 — Perfection:** Continuous Kaizen, Heijunka, Zero-defect culture.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Answer Strategy:** When asked "Explain JIT implementation in a manufacturing firm," structure your answer along this 5-phase roadmap. Examiners reward clear phasing and quantified outcomes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: JIT Philosophy — Hierarchical Mind Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((JIT Philosophy))
    Origins
      Toyota Production System
      Taiichi Ohno 1950s
      Oil Crisis Catalyst
    Pillars
      Continuous Flow
      Pull System
      Takt Time
      Built in Quality
      Supplier Partnership
    Tools
      Kanban
      Heijunka
      SMED
      Poka Yoke
      Jidoka
      5S
      TPM
    Objectives
      Zero Inventory
      Zero Defects
      Zero Lead Time
      Zero Setup
    Waste Targeted
      Overproduction
      Waiting
      Transport
      Motion
      Inventory
      Defects
      Over Processing
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Push vs. Pull System Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUSH["PUSH SYSTEM - Traditional MRP"]
        FC[Forecast] --> S1[Schedule]
        S1 --> P1[Produce Batch 100]
        P1 --> P2[Push to Next Station]
        P2 --> P3[Push to Warehouse]
        P3 --> P4[Push to Customer]
        P4 -.Inventory Pileup.-> W1[(Large WIP and FG Stock)]
    end

    subgraph PULL["PULL SYSTEM - JIT with Kanban"]
        CO[Customer Order] --> KS[Kanban Signal]
        KS --> SS[Station Signals Upstream]
        SS --> PR[Produce Only 1 Unit]
        PR --> FL[Continuous Flow]
        FL --> DLV[Deliver to Customer]
    end

    W1 -.Contrast.-> DLV
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Kanban Loop — Two-Card System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Downstream Station] -->|Withdrawal Kanban triggers| B[Convey Empty Container to Upstream]
    B --> C[Upstream Station Receives Empty Container]
    C --> D[Production Order Kanban Issued]
    D --> E[Produce Required Quantity]
    E --> F[Attach Production Kanban to Full Container]
    F --> G[Move to In Process Store]
    G --> A
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: JIT Implementation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start - 5S and Visual Management] --> P2[Stabilize Processes - TPM, SMED]
    P2 --> P3[Design Cellular Layout]
    P3 --> P4[Establish Takt Time]
    P4 --> P5[Implement One Piece Flow]
    P5 --> P6[Introduce Kanban Signals]
    P6 --> P7[Integrate Suppliers - Daily Deliveries]
    P7 --> P8[Apply Heijunka - Level Loading]
    P8 --> P9[Continuous Kaizen Loop]
    P9 -.Feedback.-> P1
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Block Architecture of JIT Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Demand Signal] --> B[Order Decoupling Point]
    B --> C[Heijunka Production Leveler]
    C --> D[Takt Time Calculator]
    D --> E[Kanban Generator]
    E --> F[Upstream Production Trigger]
    F --> G[Supplier Replenishment Signal]
    G --> H[Material Flow Reverses Upstream]
    H -.Feeds.-> F
    D -.Time Sync.-> I[Station Cycle Time]
    I --> J[Quality Gate - Jidoka]
    J --> K[Continuous Flow Line]
    K --> L[Customer Delivery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams instead of physical drawings?**
> JIT is a **systemic and informational concept**. The Mermaid block diagrams above map the **functional architecture of information, signals, and material flow** — which is precisely how KTU 2024 examiners expect students to represent JIT in an answer script. Physical drawings of a factory floor would be inappropriate and difficult to verify.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define Just-In-Time (JIT) manufacturing. State any four objectives of JIT.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Just-In-Time (JIT) is a pull-based production philosophy aimed at producing and delivering the right items, in the right quantity, at exactly the time they are needed, thereby eliminating all forms of waste (Muda) — particularly inventory and overproduction. It originated from the Toyota Production System (TPS) developed by Taiichi Ohno in post-war Japan.

**Four Objectives of JIT:**

1. **Zero Inventory** — Eliminate raw material, WIP, and finished goods stock.
2. **Zero Defects** — Ensure 100% right-first-time production via Jidoka and Poka-Yoke.
3. **Zero Lead Time** — Compress order-to-delivery cycle to a single-digit day or hour.
4. **Zero Setup Time** — Achieve single-digit minute changeovers using SMED techniques.

*(Additional valid objectives: Zero breakdowns, Zero unnecessary motion, Zero over-processing.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Differentiate between Push and Pull production systems with two points each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Push System | Pull System (JIT) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trigger** | Forecast / Master Production Schedule | Actual downstream customer demand |
| **Inventory** | High (anticipation stock) | Minimal / Zero |
| **WIP** | Accumulates between stations | One-piece flow |
| **Information Flow** | Centralized, top-down | Decentralized, signal-based (Kanban) |
| **Waste** | High (overproduction is the worst) | Minimized through pull logic |
| **Examples** | MRP, EOQ, ROP-based systems | JIT, Kanban, CONWIP, DBR |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a) Explain in detail the philosophy, objectives, and seven elements of Just-In-Time (JIT) production system. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Philosophy of JIT:**
JIT is a holistic management philosophy developed within the Toyota Production System (TPS) by Taiichi Ohno during the 1950s. Its central tenet is the **elimination of all waste (Muda)** — any activity that consumes resources but creates no value for the customer. The system operates on the principle that inventory hides problems; by reducing inventory to a minimum, problems (defects, breakdowns, delays) become visible and can be systematically solved through **Kaizen (continuous improvement)**. JIT transforms production from a "batch-and-queue" push model into a smooth, synchronized, demand-driven pull model.

**2. Objectives of JIT — The Seven Zeros:**

1. Zero Inventory (raw + WIP + finished)
2. Zero Defects (right-first-time via Jidoka)
3. Zero Lead Time (instant order fulfillment)
4. Zero Machine Downtime (via TPM)
5. Zero Setup Time (via SMED)
6. Zero Handling/Motion waste (via 5S and cellular layout)
7. Zero Overproduction (the root of all waste)

**3. Seven Elements of JIT:**

- **Element 1 — Pull Production (Kanban):** Authorization cards trigger production only when downstream demand exists.
- **Element 2 — Continuous Flow (One-Piece Flow):** Materials move one unit at a time through synchronized stations.
- **Element 3 — Takt Time:** Pace of production = $T_{available}/D_{demand}$.
- **Element 4 — Quick Changeover (SMED):** Converting internal setup time to external, reducing it below 10 minutes.
- **Element 5 — Built-in Quality (Jidoka + Poka-Yoke):** Stop-the-line authority and mistake-proofing devices.
- **Element 6 — Total Productive Maintenance (TPM):** Operator-driven preventive and autonomous maintenance.
- **Element 7 — Supplier Partnership:** Long-term relationships, frequent small deliveries, certified quality, geographic proximity.

---

**(b) A manufacturing unit produces 800 units per 8-hour shift. The lead time for replenishment of a container is 120 minutes. The container holds 40 parts. The safety factor is 25%. Calculate the number of Kanban cards required. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data**

$$
D = 800 \text{ units/shift}, \quad L = 120 \text{ min}, \quad C = 40 \text{ parts}, \quad S = 25\% = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Demand to Per-Minute Rate**

Total shift time = $8 \times 60 = 480$ minutes

$$
D_{min} = \frac{800}{480} = 1.667 \text{ parts/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kanban Formula**

$$
N_{kanban} = \frac{D \times L \times (1+S)}{C}
$$

Substitute:

$$
N_{kanban} = \frac{1.667 \times 120 \times (1 + 0.25)}{40}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerator**

$$
1.667 \times 120 = 200.04
$$

$$
200.04 \times 1.25 = 250.05
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by Container Size**

$$
N_{kanban} = \frac{250.05}{40} = 6.251
$$

**Step 6 — Round Up**

$$
N_{kanban} = 7 \text{ cards}
$$

**Answer:** **7 Kanban cards** are required to maintain uninterrupted flow with the 25% safety margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]

**(a) Discuss the advantages and limitations of Just-In-Time (JIT) system. How does JIT eliminate the seven traditional wastes (Muda) in a manufacturing environment? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Advantages of JIT:**

- **Reduced Inventory Carrying Cost:** Capital is freed from tied-up stock.
- **Improved Quality:** Built-in quality (Jidoka) reduces defects and rework.
- **Shorter Lead Times:** One-piece flow compresses order-to-delivery cycle.
- **Lower Setup Costs:** SMED enables small lot production economically.
- **Enhanced Flexibility:** Quick changeovers allow rapid model mix changes.
- **Reduced Wastage:** Just enough material means less obsolescence and damage.
- **Better Supplier Relationships:** Long-term partnerships ensure reliability.
- **Increased Employee Involvement:** Kaizen culture engages shop-floor workers.
- **Higher Productivity:** Flow layout eliminates non-value-adding motion.

**2. Limitations of JIT:**

- **High Vulnerability to Supply Disruptions:** Single supplier dependency is risky.
- **Requires Stable Demand:** Erratic demand makes pull planning difficult.
- **High Initial Setup:** Kaizen, 5S, TPM require significant cultural change.
- **Transportation Cost:** Frequent small deliveries increase logistics cost.
- **Skill Dependency:** Requires highly trained, multi-skilled workforce.
- **Not Suitable for All Products:** Seasonal or low-volume items may be uneconomical.
- **Buffer Reduction Trade-off:** Zero inventory = zero buffer against breakdowns.

**3. How JIT Eliminates the Seven Muda (TIMWOOD + Over-processing):**

| Waste | JIT Elimination Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- |
| **T**ransport | Point-of-use delivery, cellular layout |
| **I**nventory | Pull system, one-piece flow, smaller batches |
| **M**otion | 5S workplace organization, ergonomic layout |
| **W**aiting | Takt-time synchronization, continuous flow |
| **O**verproduction | Pull system / Kanban |
| **O**ver-processing | Value Stream Mapping removes non-value steps |
| **D**efects | Jidoka + Poka-Yoke (stop-the-line + mistake-proof) |

---

**(b) A plant operates in 2 shifts of 8 hours each. Lunch break is 45 minutes and planned downtime is 30 minutes per shift. Customer demand is 600 units/day. Determine: (i) Takt time, (ii) Pitch interval for a 5-station line, (iii) Cycle time per station. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Net Available Production Time**

Per shift: $8 \times 60 - 45 - 30 = 480 - 75 = 405$ minutes
For 2 shifts: $405 \times 2 = 810$ minutes/day

**Step 2 — Takt Time**

$$
T_{takt} = \frac{T_{available}}{D_{demand}} = \frac{810 \text{ min}}{600 \text{ units}} = 1.35 \text{ min/unit}
$$

Converting to seconds: $1.35 \times 60 = 81$ seconds/unit

**Step 3 — Pitch Interval (for 5 stations)**

$$
P_{pitch} = N_{stations} \times T_{takt} = 5 \times 1.35 = 6.75 \text{ min/unit}
$$

This means a new unit emerges from the line every 6.75 minutes.

**Step 4 — Cycle Time per Station**

$$
T_{cycle/station} = \frac{T_{takt}}{N_{stations}} = \frac{1.35}{5} = 0.27 \text{ min/station} = 16.2 \text{ seconds/station}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Takt Time = **1.35 min/unit (81 sec/unit)**
- Pitch Interval = **6.75 min/unit**
- Cycle Time per Station = **0.27 min (16.2 sec)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in JIT Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing JIT with low inventory:** Students write "JIT means keeping less stock" — this loses 1–2 marks. The correct answer is that JIT **exposes problems** by reducing inventory, *enabling* their removal.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up Kanban counts:** Always round to the next whole number. Writing 6.25 or 6.5 cards is non-physical.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Takt time demands all times in same unit (seconds/minutes/hours). Mixing minutes for takt and hours for demand is the most common numerical error.
> 4. **Naming only Toyota and forgetting Ohno:** Examiners specifically reward the name "Taiichi Ohno" — marks are awarded for historical accuracy.
> 5. **Skipping the safety factor** in Kanban questions when given: $S$ is mandatory unless explicitly stated as "no safety stock."
> 6. **Listing TIMWOOD without Over-processing:** KTU 2024 expects all **eight wastes** (7 + skill underutilization per some texts, or 7 + Over-processing per TPM/Ohno's later writings). State at least seven clearly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **JIT = Pull-based, demand-driven, waste-elimination philosophy** originating from Toyota's Taiichi Ohno.
- [x] **Goal:** Produce only what is needed, when it is needed, in the quantity needed.
- [x] **Seven Zeros:** Inventory, Defects, Lead Time, Setup, Breakdown, Motion, Overproduction.
- [x] **Seven Wastes (TIMWOOD + Over-processing):** Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Waiting, Overproduction, Over-processing, Defects.
- [x] **Takt Time Formula:** $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D_{demand}$ — the heartbeat of JIT.
- [x] **Kanban Formula:** $N_{kanban} = (D \times L \times (1+S)) / C$ — always round up.
- [x] **Pitch Interval:** $P = N_{stations} \times T_{takt}$.
- [x] **Five Pillars:** Continuous flow, Pull system, Takt synchronization, Built-in quality (Jidoka), Stable suppliers.
- [x] **Key Tools:** Kanban, Heijunka, SMED, Poka-Yoke, Jidoka, 5S, TPM.
- [x] **Push vs Pull:** Push = forecast-driven (MRP); Pull = demand-driven (JIT).
- [x] **Limitations:** Vulnerable to supply shocks, requires stable demand, high cultural setup cost.
- [x] **Inventor:** Taiichi Ohno, Toyota Motor Corporation, 1950s.
- [x] **Examiner Favorites:** "Seven Zeros," "Seven Wastes," "Taiichi Ohno," "TPS origin," and a clean Kanban numerical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a JIT question appears, structure your answer as **(1) Definition → (2) Objectives → (3) Elements/Pillars → (4) Tools → (5) Advantages/Limitations → (6) Numerical (if asked)**. This six-part structure guarantees comprehensive coverage and full marks under the KTU 2024 scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kanban System and Pull Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Kanban System and Pull Production

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Pull Production System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pull Production** is a manufacturing strategy in which the production and movement of materials and components are triggered by **actual downstream demand** rather than by forecast or schedule. The downstream workstation "pulls" the required quantity from the upstream workstation, only when it is needed.

In the context of **Lean Manufacturing** and the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, pull production forms the operational backbone that enables **Just-in-Time (JIT)** delivery, eliminates overproduction (one of the **7 Wastes / MUDA**), and synchronizes the entire value stream to real customer consumption.

### 1.2 Kanban — The Visual Signal of Pull

> [!NOTE]
> **Kanban** (Japanese: 看板, meaning *"signboard"* or *"visual card"*) is an information system that signals the need to produce, move, or replenish a specific quantity of items. It is the **physical and informational mechanism** through which a pull production system is executed in practice.

It was conceived and developed by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota Motor Corporation in the 1950s as the signalling device to control production and material flow between workstations on the shop floor.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Supermarket Model

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a neighbourhood supermarket. The store does **not** pre-stock 10,000 bottles of milk based on a guess. Instead, customers take milk from the shelf, and only when the shelf drops below a defined "trigger level" does the store clerk place an order with the dairy. The dairy, in turn, produces and delivers **exactly the quantity withdrawn** by the store. This closed-loop, demand-driven replenishment is precisely how a Kanban system operates on a factory floor — every supermarket shelf is a Kanban loop.

### 1.4 Key Terminology for the Board Examiner

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|---|---|
| **Upstream Process** | The earlier / supplier process in the production flow |
| **Downstream Process** | The later / customer process in the production flow |
| **Heijunka** | Production leveling (level out the demand) |
| **Takt Time** | Available production time divided by customer demand |
| **WIP (Work-in-Process)** | Inventory between two consecutive processes |
| **Lead Time (L)** | Time required to replenish one container of items |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Push Production vs Pull Production

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Push System:** Material is pushed to the next process based on a forecast or schedule, regardless of whether the next process needs it. This leads to **overproduction, high WIP, long lead times, and hidden defects**.
>
> **Pull System:** Material is pulled by the next process **only when it is needed**, based on actual consumption. This keeps **WIP low, lead times short, and defects visible** immediately.

| Parameter | Push System | Pull System |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Forecast / Schedule | Actual downstream demand |
| Inventory level | High | Low (minimum required) |
| Lead time | Long & variable | Short & predictable |
| Defect visibility | Hidden in WIP | Surfaced instantly |
| WIP | Large buffers | Single-piece flow or small lots |
| Responsiveness to demand change | Slow | Fast |
| Information flow | Schedule documents | Kanban cards / signals |

### 2.2 The Kanban Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Downstream process consumes** a container of parts from its input buffer.
2. A **Kanban card is freed** at the empty space of the buffer.
3. The freed Kanban is sent **back to the upstream process** as an authorization to produce.
4. The **upstream process produces a fresh container** of parts matching the Kanban.
5. The new container is **moved to the downstream buffer**, attached with the Kanban.
6. The cycle repeats — a continuous **closed-loop signaling system**.

> [!NOTE]
> This loop is the heart of the **Just-in-Time (JIT)** system. The Kanban card carries critical information: **part number, quantity, source process, destination process, and container type**.

### 2.3 Types of Kanban Cards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks this as a 7-mark short question. Memorize the **two main types** and the **sub-variants**.

| Kanban Type | Function | Direction of Movement |
|---|---|---|
| **Withdrawal Kanban (Conveyance / Pickup Kanban)** | Authorizes the *movement* of parts from upstream to downstream | Forward (Upstream → Downstream) |
| **Production Kanban (Order / Make Kanban)** | Authorizes the *production* of parts at the upstream workstation | Backward (Downstream → Upstream, used at the upstream point) |
| **Signal Kanban** | Used when there is **no physical withdrawal**, but a level trigger is reached (e.g., a 3-bin system) | Triggers replenishment at a threshold |
| **Vendor Kanban** | An external withdrawal Kanban sent to a supplier to authorize a delivery | From plant to supplier |
| **Emergency Kanban (Rush Kanban)** | Used to flag urgent orders outside the regular loop | Variable |

> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **W-P** → **W**ithdrawal (move), **P**roduction (make).

### 2.4 The Six Rules of Kanban (Toyota, as taught in KTU Module 2)

1. The **downstream (customer) process** withdraws items in the exact quantities specified by the Kanban.
2. The **upstream (supplier) process** produces items in the exact quantities withdrawn.
3. **No items may be produced or moved without a Kanban** (no Kanban, no work).
4. A Kanban must **always be attached to the items it represents**.
5. **Defective items are never sent to the downstream process** — quality is built-in.
6. The **number of Kanban cards must be minimized** to expose problems and reduce WIP.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Number of Kanban Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important formula** for KTU 14-mark numerical questions on this topic. You will be expected to derive and apply it.

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of Kanban cards / containers required | dimensionless (integer) |
| $D$ | Average demand rate (units consumed per unit time) | units / day |
| $L$ | Lead time to produce / replenish one container | days |
| $S$ | Safety stock factor (decimal, e.g., $0.10$ for $10\%$) | unitless |
| $C$ | Container capacity (size of one standard container) | units / container |

**Master Formula:**

$$
N = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}
$$

> **Note:** The result is always **rounded UP** to the next whole number, because you cannot have a fractional Kanban card in operation.

**Supplementary Formulas** (for advanced problems):

- **Average WIP Inventory** $= N \cdot C$ (units)
- **Maximum WIP** $= N \cdot C$ (since pull system caps WIP at the Kanban-controlled limit)
- **Takt Time** $= \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time per Day}}{\text{Customer Demand per Day}}$ (seconds / unit)
- **Cycle Time (Lead Time) Check:** Effective $L$ should be $\leq$ Takt Time for true single-piece flow.

### 2.6 Why This System Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Manufacturing Engineering:** Eliminates overproduction, the root waste of all other wastes.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Reduces WIP carrying cost and shortens throughput time.
- **Supply Chain Management:** Creates a responsive, demand-driven logistics network.
- **Quality Engineering:** Defects surface immediately at the producing station (Jidoka principle).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Kanban Formula

We derive the formula from first principles using the logic of the **closed-loop replenishment cycle**.

**Step 1 — Identify the demand that must be covered during replenishment.**

The downstream process consumes parts continuously at rate $D$ (units/day). During the lead time $L$ (days) required to replenish the upstream process, the downstream buffer must hold enough to keep the line running.

$$
\text{Mean demand during lead time} = D \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Add a safety buffer for demand variability.**

To protect against minor fluctuations in demand or process delays, a safety factor $S$ is added. The safety stock is $(D \cdot L \cdot S)$, giving the **total quantity to be held in the system**:

$$
\text{Total quantity to be held} = D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the container capacity to find the number of containers (= number of Kanbans).**

Since one Kanban authorizes one full container, the number of containers (and therefore Kanbans) needed is:

$$
N = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the integer ceiling rule.**

Because we cannot have a fractional Kanban in physical operation, we apply the **ceiling function**:

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = \left\lceil \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} \right\rceil
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Standard KTU Style Problem

> **Problem:** A workstation consumes $D = 600$ units/day. The upstream lead time is $L = 0.5$ days. A safety factor $S = 0.20$ is used. Each standard container holds $C = 25$ units. Calculate (i) the number of Kanban cards and (ii) the average WIP.

**Solution:**

Step (i) — Substitute values into the formula:

$$
N = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} = \frac{600 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 + 0.20)}{25}
$$

$$
N = \frac{300 \cdot 1.20}{25} = \frac{360}{25} = 14.4
$$

Apply ceiling rule:

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = \lceil 14.4 \rceil = 15 \text{ Kanban cards}
$$

Step (ii) — Average WIP:

$$
\text{WIP} = N \cdot C = 15 \cdot 25 = 375 \text{ units}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Variable on $N$

| Variable Increased | Effect on $N$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $D$ (Demand) | $N \uparrow$ | More Kanbans needed when sales rise |
| $L$ (Lead Time) | $N \uparrow$ | Slow processes need more buffer Kanbans |
| $S$ (Safety Factor) | $N \uparrow$ | Higher service level $\Rightarrow$ more Kanbans |
| $C$ (Container Size) | $N \downarrow$ | Larger containers reduce card count but raise WIP per card |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Some students divide $D$ by $C$ and multiply by $L$ but forget the $(1+S)$ bracket. This silent omission costs full marks in numericals. Always write the bracket explicitly on the answer script.

### 3.4 Verification via Dimensional Analysis

$$
N = \frac{\left(\frac{\text{units}}{\text{day}}\right) \cdot (\text{days}) \cdot (\text{dimensionless})}{\left(\frac{\text{units}}{\text{container}}\right)} = \text{containers} = \text{Kanban cards}
$$

The units cancel correctly, confirming the dimensional integrity of the equation.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Kanban Closed-Loop — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Downstream Process consumes a container from buffer] --> nodeB[Empty position freed at downstream input rack]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Withdrawal Kanban sent forward to authorize material movement]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Upstream Storage / Supermarket releases container to downstream]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Production Kanban sent backward to authorize production]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Upstream Process produces exactly one container of the part]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Fresh container stored in upstream supermarket]
    nodeG --> nodeA
```
