```mermaid
    nodeH[Quality Gate Jidoka rejects defect before passing to downstream] -.-> nodeD
    nodeI[Information: Part Number, Quantity, Route] -.-> nodeC
    nodeI -.-> nodeE
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Kanban System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUP[Supplier Side]
        SP1[Raw Material Supplier] --> SP2[Vendor Kanban Signal]
    end
    subgraph PROD[Production Cell]
        UP1[Upstream Machine 1] --> UP2[Production Kanban Loop]
        UP2 --> MID[Inter-process Buffer or Supermarket Shelf]
    end
    subgraph CUST[Customer Side]
        MID --> DN1[Downstream Machine 2]
        DN1 --> DN2[Assembly Line]
        DN2 --> DN3[Withdrawal Kanban to Final Customer Order]
    end
    SP2 -.->|Raw Input| UP1
    DN3 -.->|Triggers Replenishment| UP2
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic — How a Kanban is Dispatched

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Downstream worker opens box] --> B[Container Empty?]
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Detach Withdrawal Kanban from empty box]
    C --> D[Return Kanban to upstream input rack]
    D --> E[Upstream supervisor receives Kanban]
    E --> F{Stock at upstream adequate?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Move container to downstream buffer]
    F -- No --> H[Issue Production Kanban to upstream machine]
    H --> I[Produce one container to replenish]
    I --> G
    G --> A
```

> **Reading Guide for the Board Examiner:** Each `nodeX` is a process state, arrows indicate the direction of the Kanban signal and material flow, and dotted arrows represent parallel information channels. The closed loop ensures no production occurs without authorization.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
*Define a Kanban system. Mention the two main types of Kanban cards used in a Pull production system.*

**Model Answer:**

> A **Kanban system** is a demand-driven production control and material flow signalling mechanism in which a card or signal triggers the production and movement of items **only when they are needed downstream**. The word *Kanban* is Japanese for *"signboard"* or *"visual card"*, and the system was pioneered at Toyota by **Taiichi Ohno** as the operational tool for **Just-in-Time (JIT)** manufacturing.
>
> The **two main types of Kanban cards** are:
>
> 1. **Withdrawal Kanban (Conveyance Kanban):** Authorizes the *movement* of parts from the upstream storage point to the downstream workstation.
> 2. **Production Kanban (Order Kanban):** Authorizes the *production* of parts at the upstream workstation in the required quantity.
>
> These two cards operate in a closed loop and together synchronize the flow of information and material across the value stream. *(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
*Differentiate between Push production and Pull production systems. Give one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

> | Basis | Push Production | Pull Production |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Trigger** | Forecast, MRP schedule, or planned order | Actual downstream demand |
> | **Inventory** | High WIP, large buffers | Minimal WIP, supermarket-controlled |
> | **Lead Time** | Long and variable | Short and predictable |
> | **Example** | A factory produces 5000 mobile phones per day based on a monthly sales forecast, irrespective of dealer demand. | A car assembly line at Toyota calls the body shop *only when* the next chassis space is empty. |
>
> The Pull system prevents **overproduction**, which is considered the *mother of all wastes* in Lean Manufacturing. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Kanban Theory + Numerical*

**(a)** *Explain the Kanban system in detail. Discuss its operating principles and the six rules that govern its implementation. State the formula to calculate the number of Kanban cards. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

The Kanban system is a **visual information and production control system** that operates on the principle of **demand-driven pull**. Instead of producing based on forecasts, each workstation produces **only what the next (downstream) workstation has consumed**, as indicated by a Kanban card returned from the consumption point.

**Operating Principle:** The Kanban card carries four critical pieces of information — the **part number, the quantity, the source (upstream) process, and the destination (downstream) process**. When a container is empty, its Kanban is detached and returned upstream, thereby *authorizing* the production or movement of exactly one container. This creates a self-regulating closed loop.

**The Six Rules of Kanban (Toyota):**

1. The **downstream (customer) process** withdraws items in the exact quantities specified by the Kanban.
2. The **upstream (supplier) process** produces items in the exact quantities withdrawn.
3. **No items may be produced or moved without a Kanban** — no Kanban, no work.
4. A Kanban must **always be physically attached to the items** it represents.
5. **Defective items are never passed downstream** — quality is ensured at the source (Jidoka).
6. The **number of Kanban cards must be minimized** continuously to expose hidden problems and reduce WIP.

**Number of Kanban Formula:**

$$
N = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}
$$

where $D$ = demand rate, $L$ = lead time, $S$ = safety factor, $C$ = container capacity. *(7 marks: 1 + 4 + 1 + 1)*

---

**(b)** *A manufacturing cell produces a sub-assembly at a demand rate of $900$ units/day. The upstream replenishment lead time is $0.4$ days. The safety factor used is $0.15$. Each standard container holds $40$ units. Determine:* (i) *the number of Kanban cards required,* and (ii) *the average WIP inventory in the loop. If management wishes to reduce the WIP by $25\%$, what should be the new container size (assuming demand, lead time, and safety factor remain constant)? **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Number of Kanban cards:**

$$
N = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C}
$$

$$
N = \frac{900 \cdot 0.4 \cdot (1 + 0.15)}{40} = \frac{360 \cdot 1.15}{40} = \frac{414}{40} = 10.35
$$

Apply ceiling rule:

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = \lceil 10.35 \rceil = 11 \text{ Kanban cards}
$$

**Part (ii) — Average WIP Inventory:**

$$
\text{WIP} = N \cdot C = 11 \cdot 40 = 440 \text{ units}
$$

**Part (iii) — New container size for $25\%$ WIP reduction:**

New target WIP:

$$
\text{WIP}_{\text{new}} = 440 \cdot (1 - 0.25) = 330 \text{ units}
$$

Since the number of Kanbans must be an integer, let $N_{\text{new}} = 11$ (same physical count, smaller container) and solve for $C_{\text{new}}$:

$$
C_{\text{new}} = \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{N_{\text{new}}} = \frac{360 \cdot 1.15}{11} = \frac{414}{11} \approx 37.64 \text{ units}
$$

Rounding to a practical container size:

$$
C_{\text{new}} \approx 38 \text{ units per container}
$$

This reduction in container size tightens the pull loop and reveals hidden waste, aligning with Kanban rule #6. *(7 marks: 3 + 1.5 + 2.5)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual + Implementation Analysis*

**(a)** *Compare and contrast Push and Pull production systems with suitable examples. Explain how a Pull system supports Just-in-Time (JIT) manufacturing. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

In a **Push system**, each workstation produces parts according to a **central schedule or forecast** and pushes the output to the next workstation, regardless of whether the downstream station needs it. In a **Pull system**, each workstation produces parts **only when the downstream workstation signals a need** (via a Kanban). The fundamental difference is therefore the **trigger**: forecast vs. actual demand.

**Detailed Comparison:**

| Criterion | Push System | Pull System |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Forecast, MRP | Real downstream consumption |
| WIP | High, accumulates at bottlenecks | Low, controlled by Kanban |
| Lead Time | Long, variable | Short, predictable |
| Inventory Cost | High carrying cost | Minimized |
| Defect Detection | Late, downstream | Immediate, at source |
| Responsiveness | Slow | High |
| Example | A paper mill produces 10,000 reams/day based on last quarter's demand. | A Toyota assembly line refills the door station *only when* 4 doors are withdrawn for the next vehicle. |

**How Pull supports JIT:**

- JIT aims to deliver the **right item, in the right quantity, at the right time**. The Pull/Kanban mechanism **physically enforces** this by authorizing production only when a downstream signal is received.
- It **eliminates overproduction** (waste #1 in the 7 Wastes), thereby freeing up **floor space, capital, and human effort**.
- It exposes bottlenecks and abnormalities instantly, feeding into the **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** continuous improvement cycle.
- It integrates with **Jidoka** (built-in quality), **Heijunka** (level scheduling), and **Takt Time** to form the four pillars of the Toyota Production System. *(7 marks: 1 + 3 + 3)*

---

**(b)** *Discuss the benefits and limitations of implementing a Kanban system. State the key steps a manager should follow to introduce Kanban in a manufacturing plant. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Benefits of Kanban:**

1. **Dramatic reduction in WIP inventory** and associated carrying costs.
2. **Shorter and more predictable lead times.**
3. **Visual management** — the entire flow is visible at a glance on the shop floor.
4. **Highlights problems instantly** (bottlenecks, defects, delays) for quick corrective action.
5. **Reduces overproduction**, freeing capacity, space, and working capital.
6. **Improves supplier integration** when extended Vendor Kanban is used.
7. **Empowers workers** to control the line using simple visual rules.

**Limitations of Kanban:**

1. **Highly sensitive to demand variability** — a sudden surge can empty the line.
2. **Not suitable for low-volume, high-mix production** without a customized hybrid system.
3. **Requires disciplined workforce and stable processes** (5S, TPM, standardized work).
4. **Initial setup and training costs** can be significant.
5. **Difficult to retrofit in a process industry** with continuous-flow chemistry.
6. **Buffer mismanagement can cause line stoppages** if a Kanban is misplaced.

**Steps to Implement Kanban (KTU-recommended sequence):**

1. **Train the workforce** on Lean principles, 5S, and standardized work.
2. **Map the value stream** (Value Stream Mapping — Module 3 reference).
3. **Identify the pacemaker process** and design a supermarket / FIFO lane.
4. **Calculate the number of Kanban cards** using $N = (D \cdot L \cdot (1+S))/C$.
5. **Pilot on a single production line** before scaling.
6. **Establish supplier Kanbans** to extend the pull upstream.
7. **Monitor, audit, and continuously reduce** the number of Kanbans (Rule #6). *(7 marks: 3 + 3 + 1)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 2**

- ✅ **Kanban** literally means *"visual card"* in Japanese, devised by **Taiichi Ohno** at Toyota.
- ✅ The **two primary types** of Kanban are **Withdrawal** (for movement) and **Production** (for making).
- ✅ A **Pull system** is triggered by **actual downstream demand**, not by a forecast or schedule.
- ✅ A **Push system** is forecast-driven and typically leads to high WIP and overproduction.
- ✅ **The Six Rules of Kanban** (Toyota) are mandatory memorization for KTU — expect a direct 3–5 mark question.
- ✅ The **Number of Kanban formula** is:
  $$N = \left\lceil \frac{D \cdot L \cdot (1 + S)}{C} \right\rceil$$
  Always apply the **ceiling function**; never leave the result as a fraction.
- ✅ **Average WIP** in a pull system $= N \cdot C$ (in units).
- ✅ Kanban works best with **5S, Jidoka, Heijunka, and Takt Time** — these are the four pillars of TPS.
- ✅ **Vendor Kanban** extends the pull logic to external suppliers — a frequent KTU sub-question.
- ✅ **Signal Kanban** is used in 3-bin systems where no physical withdrawal triggers the loop.
- ✅ **Minimizing Kanban count** is Rule #6 — every reduction exposes a hidden problem, which is a *good* thing in Lean.
- ✅ **Dimensional check** of the formula is: $(\text{units/day}) \cdot (\text{day}) / (\text{units/container}) = \text{containers}$ — use this if unsure of the answer in the exam hall.
- ✅ Common KTU pitfalls: forgetting the $(1+S)$ factor, not rounding up, and confusing *Withdrawal* with *Production* Kanban direction.
- ✅ Real-world anchors to mention: **Toyota assembly line, Amazon warehouse restocking, hospital pharmacy JIT, newspaper vending**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value Stream Mapping (VSM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Value Stream Mapping (VSM) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** is a **paper-and-pencil visualization tool** that depicts every step required to bring a product (or a family of products) from raw material to the arms of the customer, using standardized icons that represent material flows and information flows. It is the foundational diagnostic instrument of the **Lean Manufacturing** philosophy, originally developed as part of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and popularized globally by **Mike Rother & John Shook** in their seminal work *"Learning to See"* (1998). In KTU 2024 Scheme assessment language, VSM is officially classified as a **Lean Tool & Technique** under Module 2: *Value Stream Mapping & Process Analysis Tools*. It has a direct mapping to the Course Outcome **CO2**: *"Apply lean tools and techniques to analyse and improve manufacturing processes."* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Diagnostic Scan" > [!NOTE] > **Think of VSM as an MRI Scan for your entire factory floor.** Just as a doctor uses an MRI to see the patient's hidden issues (blockages, slow blood flow, tumors), a Lean engineer uses a VSM to see the factory's hidden issues (waiting, overproduction, transport, defects, motion, inventory, over-processing, and unused talent — i.e., the **8 Wastes / DOWNTIME**). The VSM does not prescribe medicine; it only shows the **disease**. The treatment plan is the **Future State Map**. ### 1.3 The Two Cardinal Variants of VSM | Variant | Purpose | KTU Exam Weight | |---|---|---| | **Current State Map (CSM)** | Snapshot of the process **as it actually exists today** — captures ground reality, warts and all. | High (10 Marks frequently) | | **Future State Map (FSM)** | Vision of how the process **should look** after Lean improvements are deployed — guided by a **Lean Vision**. | Very High (14-Mark questions) | ### 1.4 Strategic Position of VSM in the Lean Toolkit VSM acts as the **bridge between strategy and shop-floor action** in the Lean transformation journey: $$ \text{Lean Thinking} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{VSM} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Kaizen Events} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Standard Work} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Sustained Improvement} $$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Hint:** KTU question papers often ask *"VSM is a prerequisite for which Lean tool?"* — the answer is **Kaizen / Continuous Improvement**, because VSM identifies *where* to do a Kaizen burst. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Construction Logic of a VSM The systematic creation of a VSM follows a strict **6-Phase Methodology** (this is a high-frequency KTU 14-mark question): 1. **Phase 1 — Product Family Identification:** Group products that share similar routing, equipment, and processing steps. A **Product Family Matrix** is used for this. 2. **Phase 2 — Customer Demand Determination:** Obtain the **Takt Time** to anchor the entire map to the customer drumbeat. 3. **Phase 3 — Process Step Mapping (Shop Floor Walk):** Physically walk the **Gemba** (shop floor) and record each process in sequence: receive → store → process → inspect → move → ship. 4. **Phase 4 — Data Box Population:** At each process box, fill the standardized **Data Box** containing cycle time, changeover time, uptime, batch size, number of operators, and available working time. 5. **Phase 5 — Material & Information Flow Lines:** Connect process boxes with arrows. Use **solid arrows** for material flow and **dashed arrows** for information/electronic flow. 6. **Phase 6 — Lead-Time Timeline:** At the bottom, draw a timeline differentiating **Processing Time** (Value-Added) from **Waiting/Queue Time** (Non-Value-Added). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Quantitative Metrics** — these formulas appear every year in KTU numerical questions (usually 3–4 marks sub-parts). | # | Metric | Formula | Units | Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Takt Time (TT)** | $TT = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$ | sec/unit | The "heartbeat" of production | | 2 | **Lead Time (LT)** | $LT = \sum \text{Process Time} + \sum \text{Wait Time} + \sum \text{Move Time}$ | hours/days | Total time a unit spends in the system | | 3 | **Processing Time / VA Time** | $PT = \sum \text{Actual Work Time}$ | sec/unit | Time that *physically transforms* the product | | 4 | **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $VAR = \dfrac{VA\,Time}{Total\,Lead\,Time} \times 100\%$ | % | Lean-ness score | | 5 | **Cycle Time (CT)** | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Batch Size}}$ | sec/unit | Time between two successive unit completions | | 6 | **Total Inventory (I)** | $I = (CT - PT) \times \text{Demand Rate}$ | units | WIP sitting in the system | | 7 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % | Availability $\times$ Performance $\times$ Quality | | 8 | **Number of Kanban (K)** | $K = \dfrac{D \times (LT + SS)}{C}$ | cards | $D$=demand, $LT$=lead time, $SS$=safety stock, $C$=container size | > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Lead Time** with **Cycle Time**. Lead Time is *wall-clock time* end-to-end. Cycle Time is *time per unit* at a single workstation. **NEVER interchange them in a numerical.** ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind VSM — Engineering Utility - **Diagnostic Power:** Quantifies the *invisible* 99% of time the product is sitting, moving, or waiting. - **Communication Vehicle:** Provides a *common language* between design, production, logistics, and management. - **Decision Support:** Helps decide *which* Lean tool to apply — Kanban, SMED, Heijunka, One-Piece Flow, etc. - **Strategic Planning:** Anchors capital investment decisions (where to add capacity vs. where to balance). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Value-Added Ratio (VAR) — Full Worked Derivation The VAR derivation illustrates how Lean engineers mathematically expose waste. We will derive it from first principles. **Step 1:** Define the total time the product exists inside the production system (the Lead Time). It has two components: $$ LT \;=\; T_{VA} \;+\; T_{NVA} $$ where $T_{VA}$ is the total Value-Added time (time the product is being actively transformed) and $T_{NVA}$ is the Non-Value-Added time (waiting, moving, inspecting that the customer does not pay for). **Step 2:** By definition, the Value-Added Ratio is the fraction of Lead Time that is genuinely value-adding: $$ VAR = \frac{T_{VA}}{LT} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the expression for $LT$ from Step 1: $$ VAR = \frac{T_{VA}}{T_{VA} + T_{NVA}} $$ **Step 4:** Divide numerator and denominator by $T_{VA}$ to expose the NVA dominance: $$ VAR = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{T_{NVA}}{T_{VA}}} $$ **Step 5:** This is the **canonical Lean form**. As $T_{NVA} \to 0$, $VAR \to 1$ (perfect lean). As $T_{NVA} \gg T_{VA}$, $VAR \to 0$ (mass chaos). $$ \boxed{\,VAR = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{T_{NVA}}{T_{VA}}} = \frac{T_{VA}}{T_{VA} + T_{NVA}} \times 100\%\,} $$ ### 3.2 Numerical Example — KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem > **Problem:** A manufacturing cell processes a product through 5 operations. The available production time is 8 hours/day and customer demand is 100 units/day. The following data was collected: > > | Process | Cycle Time (sec) | Changeover (sec) | Batch Size | Operators | Uptime (%) | > |---|---|---|---|---|---| > | Cutting | 30 | 600 | 50 | 1 | 95 | > | Welding | 45 | 900 | 50 | 2 | 90 | > | Painting | 60 | 1200 | 50 | 1 | 100 | > | Assembly | 40 | 300 | 50 | 3 | 100 | > | Testing | 20 | 0 | 50 | 1 | 100 | > > Between processes, an average **queue/wait time of 2 hours** exists. Compute (a) Takt Time, (b) Total Lead Time, (c) Total Processing Time, and (d) Value-Added Ratio. **Solution — Sub-part (a): Takt Time** $$ TT = \frac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}} = \frac{8 \times 3600 \text{ sec}}{100 \text{ units}} = \frac{28800}{100} = 288 \text{ sec/unit} $$ **Solution — Sub-part (b): Total Processing Time** Summing all cycle times (since batch = 1 unit from VA perspective, using single-piece cycle time): $$ T_{VA} = 30 + 45 + 60 + 40 + 20 = 195 \text{ sec} $$ **Solution — Sub-part (c): Total Lead Time** The wait time between 5 processes = 4 intervals (Cutting→Welding, Welding→Painting, Painting→Assembly, Assembly→Testing): $$ T_{Wait} = 4 \times 2 \text{ hours} = 8 \text{ hours} = 8 \times 3600 = 28800 \text{ sec} $$ Therefore: $$ LT = T_{VA} + T_{Wait} = 195 + 28800 = 28995 \text{ sec} \approx 8.05 \text{ hours} $$ **Solution — Sub-part (d): Value-Added Ratio** $$ VAR = \frac{T_{VA}}{LT} \times 100\% = \frac{195}{28995} \times 100\% = 0.6725\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Only **0.67%** of the product's time is value-adding! This is a typical Lean diagnostic result — manufacturing is dominated by **waiting**, which is **waste**. ### 3.3 Cycle Time vs. Takt Time — Critical Decision Matrix | Condition | Decision | |---|---| | $CT \le TT$ | Process is **capable** of meeting demand — proceed to balance | | $CT > TT$ | Process is a **bottleneck** — apply Kaizen, add capacity, or split workstation | | $CT \gg TT$ | Severe overproduction — implement **Heijunka** (level scheduling) | ### 3.4 Inventory Build-Up Behind a Process (Little's Law Application) For any process in a VSM, the **Work-In-Process (WIP)** sitting between two stations is given by Little's Law (1961, applied to manufacturing systems by Hopp & Spearman): $$ I = R \times W $$ where $I$ = average inventory, $R$ = throughput rate (units/time), and $W$ = average time a unit spends in the buffer. Derivation from first principles: If the throughput rate is $R$ units/hour and a unit stays in the buffer for $W$ hours, then in steady state the inventory accumulates to: $$ I = R \cdot W \quad \text{(Little's Law, dimensionless-consistent)} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Trick Question Alert:** They may ask *"If you reduce WIP, what happens to throughput?"* — the answer depends on the **bottleneck**. In a bottleneck-constrained line, reducing WIP *increases* throughput. In a non-bottleneck station, it has **no effect**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 VSM Icon Legend — Standardized Symbols (Mermaid Block Diagram) This is the master reference of standardized VSM icons. KTU frequently asks students to **label** these in 3-mark questions. ```mermaid graph TB A[Material Icon
Raw Material / WIP / Finished Goods] B[Process Box
Single Manufacturing Step] C[Data Box
CT, CO, Uptime, Operators, Batch] D[Customer / Supplier Icon
External Entity] E[Production Control Box
Schedule / Kanban Trigger] F[Inventory Triangle
WIP Buffer Between Processes] G[Push Arrow
Material Flow Direction] H[Dashed Arrow
Information Flow / Kanban Signal] I[Kaizen Burst
Improvement Opportunity Symbol] J[Truck Shipment Icon
External Transport] K[Operator Icon
Manpower Resource] L[Timeline Bar
VA Time vs Lead Time] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082 style F fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B ``` ### 4.2 End-to-End VSM Block Architecture Flow (Current State Map) This diagram shows the logical flow inside a typical VSM, from raw material to customer, with associated data, inventory, and information flows. ```mermaid graph LR Supplier[Supplier Icon
External Vendor] RM[Raw Material Icon] Receipt[Process 1: Receipt
Data Box Attached] Inv1[Inventory Triangle
WIP Buffer] Process1[Process 2: Cutting
CT = 30 sec, CO = 600 sec] Inv2[Inventory Triangle] Process2[Process 3: Welding
CT = 45 sec, CO = 900 sec] Inv3[Inventory Triangle] Process3[Process 4: Assembly
CT = 40 sec, CO = 300 sec] Inv4[Inventory Triangle] Test[Process 5: Testing
CT = 20 sec] FG[Finished Goods Icon] Cust[Customer Icon] PC[Production Control
Schedule / Kanban Box] Supplier --> RM RM --> Receipt Receipt --> Inv1 Inv1 --> Process1 Process1 --> Inv2 Inv2 --> Process2 Process2 --> Inv3 Inv3 --> Process3 Process3 --> Inv4 Inv4 --> Test Test --> FG FG --> Cust PC -.Information Flow.-> Receipt PC -.Information Flow.-> Process1 PC -.Information Flow.-> Process2 PC -.Information Flow.-> Process3 PC -.Information Flow.-> Test Cust -.Customer Demand.-> PC style Supplier fill:#FFE4B5 style RM fill:#FFE4B5 style FG fill:#90EE90 style Cust fill:#FFB6C1 style PC fill:#DDA0DD style Inv1 fill:#F0E68C style Inv2 fill:#F0E68C style Inv3 fill:#F0E68C style Inv4 fill:#F0E68C ``` ### 4.3 Current State vs. Future State — Comparison Flow Diagram ```mermaid graph TB CSM[Current State Map
AS-IS Reality] FSM[Future State Map
TO-BE Lean Vision] CSM -->|Identify Waste
Inventory, Wait, Overprod| Analyze[Lean Analysis Phase] Analyze -->|Apply Lean Tools| Tools[Kaizen, Kanban, Heijunka, SMED, One-Piece Flow] Tools --> FSM FSM -->|Implement| Action[Shop Floor Implementation] Action -->|Measure| Metrics[VAR, LT, CT, OEE] Metrics -->|Compare with CSM| Review[Improvement Verification] Review -.If gap remains.-> Analyze style CSM fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style FSM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style Tools fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B style Metrics fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B ``` ### 4.4 Lead Time Timeline — Visual Decomposition ```mermaid graph LR LT[Total Lead Time Bar] P1[Process 1: 30 sec] W1[Wait 1: 7200 sec] P2[Process 2: 45 sec] W2[Wait 2: 7200 sec] P3[Process 3: 40 sec] W3[Wait 3: 7200 sec] P4[Process 4: 60 sec] W4[Wait 4: 7200 sec] P5[Process 5: 20 sec] LT --> P1 P1 --> W1 W1 --> P2 P2 --> W2 W2 --> P3 P3 --> W3 W3 --> P4 P4 --> W4 W4 --> P5 style P1 fill:#90EE90 style P2 fill:#90EE90 style P3 fill:#90EE90 style P4 fill:#90EE90 style P5 fill:#90EE90 style W1 fill:#FFB6C1 style W2 fill:#FFB6C1 style W3 fill:#FFB6C1 style W4 fill:#FFB6C1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Color Logic:** Green = Value-Added (Process time), Pink = Non-Value-Added (Wait time). The dramatic visual contrast is the *single most powerful VSM insight* — it is the **photograph of waste**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define Value Stream Mapping. List any four standardized VSM icons and state their significance.* (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Value Stream Mapping (VSM) is a Lean tool used to visually represent every step (both value-adding and non-value-adding) involved in taking a product from raw material stage to the finished customer, using standardized icons and a paper-pencil format. **Four Standardized Icons:** | # | Icon | Significance | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Process Box** | Represents a single manufacturing operation | | 2 | **Data Box** | Records CT, CO time, batch size, operators, uptime | | 3 | **Inventory Triangle** | Shows WIP or raw material storage between processes | | 4 | **Customer/Supplier Icon** | Marks the start (supplier) and end (customer) of the value stream | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between Value-Added (VA) and Non-Value-Added (NVA) activities with two examples each.* (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Value-Added (VA) | Non-Value-Added (NVA) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Activity that *physically transforms* the product and for which the customer is willing to pay | Activity that consumes time/resources but adds no value from the customer's perspective | | **Customer pays?** | Yes | No | | **Examples** | (1) Welding two metal parts; (2) Drilling a hole in a workpiece | (1) Moving parts between workstations; (2) Storing WIP in inventory | | **Lean Target** | Optimize & retain | **Eliminate** (Muda) | --- ### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)** --- > **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 2, Full 14 Marks]** > *(a)* Explain the step-by-step methodology for constructing a **Current State Map** in a manufacturing industry. *(7 Marks)* > *(b)* With a suitable example, compute the **Takt Time**, **Lead Time**, and **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** for a 4-process line. Comment on the lean-ness of the system. *(7 Marks)* ### **Question A — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)** **(a)** Discuss the **8 Wastes (DOWNTIME)** of Lean with VSM context. Explain how VSM helps in waste identification. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Draw the standard **VSM icon legend** with neat sketches. Explain the difference between **Push and Pull** systems as visualized on a VSM. *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Detailed Model Solution for Q3 (a) — Construction of Current State Map** **Step 1 — Product Family Selection:** Group similar products based on routing similarity using a Product Family Matrix. **Step 2 — Customer Demand & Takt Time Calculation:** Determine daily demand and compute Takt Time to anchor the map to customer rhythm. **Step 3 — Gemba Walk:** Physically walk the shop floor from **shipping** (downstream) to **raw material** (upstream) — this is the Lean convention. **Step 4 — Process Mapping:** At each station, draw a **Process Box** and attach a **Data Box** containing: - Cycle Time (CT) - Changeover Time (CO) - Uptime % - Number of operators - Available working time - Batch size - Shift pattern **Step 5 — Material & Information Flow:** - Use **solid arrows** for material flow - Use **dashed arrows** for information flow - Mark all WIP buffers as **inventory triangles** with quantities inside **Step 6 — Production Control Box:** Add a **Schedule Box** at the top showing how customer demand triggers production orders (push system) or Kanban signals (pull system). **Step 7 — Timeline:** At the bottom, draw a timeline separating **Value-Added time** (above the line) from **Non-Value-Added time** (below the line). Compute **Lead Time = VA + NVA**. **Step 8 — Identify Kaizen Opportunities:** Mark **Kaizen Burst symbols** (lightning-shaped icons) at every waste hotspot — long changeovers, excess inventory, unbalanced lines, etc. --- #### **Detailed Model Solution for Q3 (b) — Numerical Computation** > **Given Data:** > Available Time = 7.5 hours/day = 27,000 sec/day > Customer Demand = 90 units/day > > | Process | CT (sec) | Wait After Process (sec) | > |---|---|---| > | A | 25 | 3600 | > | B | 40 | 5400 | > | C | 35 | 2700 | > | D | 50 | 1800 | **Takt Time Calculation:** $$ TT = \frac{27{,}000}{90} = 300 \text{ sec/unit} $$ **Total Processing Time (VA):** $$ T_{VA} = 25 + 40 + 35 + 50 = 150 \text{ sec} $$ **Total Wait Time (NVA):** $$ T_{NVA} = 3600 + 5400 + 2700 + 1800 = 13{,}500 \text{ sec} $$ **Lead Time:** $$ LT = T_{VA} + T_{NVA} = 150 + 13{,}500 = 13{,}650 \text{ sec} \approx 3.79 \text{ hours} $$ **Value-Added Ratio:** $$ VAR = \frac{150}{13{,}650} \times 100\% = 1.099\% \approx 1.1\% $$ **Comment on Lean-ness:** The VAR is **abysmally low** (~1.1%), indicating that the system is **overwhelmingly dominated by waiting waste**. The total processing capacity (150 sec) is well within the Takt Time envelope (300 sec), confirming that the line is **technically capable** but **organizationally wasteful**. The recommended interventions are: 1. Convert **batch production to one-piece flow** 2. Implement **FIFO lanes** between processes 3. Apply **SMED** to reduce changeover time 4. Introduce a **pull/Kanban system** to control WIP --- #### **Detailed Model Solution for Question A (a) — 8 Wastes (DOWNTIME)** | Letter | Waste | VSM Visualization | |---|---|---| | **D** | Defects | Rework loops, inspection stations, scrap data in box | | **O** | Overproduction | Large inventory triangles upstream of every process | | **W** | Waiting | Long horizontal wait segments on the timeline | | **N** | Non-utilized talent | Operators idle, not captured in data box | | **T** | Transport | Excessive material flow arrows, long forklift routes | | **I** | Inventory | Every inventory triangle on the map is a waste flag | | **M** | Motion | Workers walking around (operator icon location) | | **E** | Extra-processing | Process boxes with over-engineered steps | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > ⚠️ **Pitfall 1:** Students often write *"DOWNTIME"* but forget the **N** for *Non-utilized talent* — a recurring 1-mark loss. > > ⚠️ **Pitfall 2:** Do **NOT** list "DOWNTIME" wastes without linking them to a **specific VSM icon** (inventory triangle, wait segment, etc.). The KTU 2024 marking scheme awards marks **only** for the VSM connection, not for the waste definition alone. > > ⚠️ **Pitfall 3:** In numerical problems, ensure the **units are consistent** — if CT is in seconds, convert available time to seconds (1 hour = 3600 seconds). Mixing hours and seconds is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > > ⚠️ **Pitfall 4:** VSM is **not** the same as **Process Mapping / Flowcharting**. VSM includes the **timeline** and **information flow**. Students who draw only a flowchart lose 2 marks instantly. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:** - ✅ **VSM = Visual tool** showing material flow + information flow + timeline (3 essential components) - ✅ **Two Maps:** Current State (AS-IS) and Future State (TO-BE) - ✅ **6-Phase Methodology:** Product Family → Takt Time → Gemba Walk → Data Box → Flow Lines → Timeline - ✅ **Key Formulas (must memorize):** Takt Time, Lead Time, VAR, Little's Law (I = R × W), Number of Kanban - ✅ **8 Wastes (DOWNTIME):** Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transport, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing - ✅ **Process Box contains:** Cycle Time, Changeover, Batch Size, Operators, Uptime, Available Time - ✅ **Arrow Convention:** Solid = Material; Dashed = Information - ✅ **Inventory Triangle** = WIP buffer (every triangle is a waste flag) - ✅ **Kaizen Burst** = Lightning-shaped symbol marking improvement opportunity - ✅ **Push vs Pull:** Push = production scheduled centrally; Pull = production triggered by downstream Kanban signal - ✅ **Lean goal:** Push the **VAR as close to 100%** as physically possible (theoretical maximum = 1) - ✅ **VSM Creator Reference:** Mike Rother & John Shook (1998) — *"Learning to See"* (do not invent other authors) - ✅ **Connection to CO2:** VSM is the **primary diagnostic tool** for CO2 achievement - ✅ **Post-VSM Action:** A VSM without a **Future State Map and Kaizen plan** is academic — it produces no improvement - ✅ **Differentiator:** VSM ≠ Process Flowchart (VSM has data, timeline, and information flow)

topics.title: Jidoka (Automation with a Human Touch)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Jidoka (Automation with a Human Touch)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jidoka (自働化)** is a Japanese term that translates literally to **"Automation with a Human Touch"**. In the context of the Toyota Production System (TPS) and Lean Manufacturing, it is formally defined as the engineering principle of **equipping a machine, process, or workstation with the intelligence to detect an abnormal condition or defect and automatically stop the production line**, preventing the production of defective products while simultaneously signaling the abnormality to the operator (typically via an **Andon** system) for immediate investigation and root-cause resolution.

The word *Jidoka* is composed of two kanji:
- **自 (Ji)** → Self
- **働 (Dō)** → Working / Actuating
- The second kanji is intentionally chosen instead of 動 (movement), embedding the idea that machines should **think** and act intelligently, not just mechanically repeat.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Jidoka is one of the **two pillars of the Toyota Production System (TPS)**, the other being the **Just-in-Time (JIT)** system. Together they form the famous TPS house.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Smart Smoke Detector**
> Imagine a traditional industrial machine that just keeps running even if it welds defective joints. Now, picture a modern machine as a **"smart smoke detector"** embedded inside a car engine. The moment the engine overheats or a spark plug misfires, the detector immediately:
> 1. **Stops** the engine (halt production).
> 2. **Flashes a red light** on the dashboard (Andon signal).
> 3. **Calls the driver** to investigate the root cause (Operator intervention via 5-Why).
> That is **Jidoka** — the machine has been "humanized" with the judgment to recognize bad work and the courtesy to ask for help.

A simpler classroom analogy: A student copying notes from a board. If the teacher notices a mistake, the teacher **stops the student immediately** and points it out, rather than letting the student continue making 50 more mistakes. Stopping the mistake at the source is **Jidoka**.

## 1.3 Key Technical Metrics & Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Terms You Must Memorize:**
> - **Abnormality (Yokeru Mono 予知):** Any condition deviating from the standard — a defect, machine fault, or process deviation.
> - **Andon (行灯):** A visual signal (light tower / display board) used to call for help or signal line stoppage.
> - **Poka-Yoke (ポカヨケ):** Mistake-proofing devices or mechanisms.
> - **Jidoka Cost Ratio:** Number of operator stops per shift ÷ Total production units.
> - **Line Stop Authority:** Empowerment given to **every operator** to halt the line.

## 1.4 Historical Origin

> [!NOTE]
> **Sakichi Toyoda (1890s) — The Father of Jidoka**
> Sakichi Toyoda invented the **Type G Automatic Loom (1924)**, which could detect a broken thread or exhausted weft and **automatically stop itself**, alerting the operator. This was the world's first practical example of "automation with a human touch" — predating the term by 60 years.
> At Toyota, this single invention improved loom efficiency from requiring **one operator per 6 looms to one operator per 35 looms**, demonstrating the power of Jidoka at scale.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Sub-Categories of Jidoka

Jidoka is operationally divided into two distinct categories in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

> [!NOTE]
> ### A. **Thinking Jidoka (思考自働化)**
> - Applies to **human work** (manual assembly, inspection, service).
> - The **operator** is empowered to detect abnormalities using:
>   - Visual checks
>   - Tactile / auditory feedback
>   - Standardized work instructions
>   - Poka-yoke fixtures
> - **Line stop authority** is granted to every operator.
> - The human acts as the sensor **and** the brain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### B. **Automatic Jidoka (自動自働化)**
> - Applies to **machine work** (CNC, robotics, automated welding).
> - The **machine** itself is fitted with sensors, limit switches, vision systems, and PLC interlocks.
> - On detecting an anomaly, the machine **physically halts** and triggers an Andon.
> - Built on Toyoda's original principle: *if a person cannot be present, the machine must think for the person.*

## 2.2 The Four Operational Rules of Jidoka (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the following **four rules of Jidoka**, originally codified by Toyota:

1. **Detect the abnormality** — Install sensors / poka-yokes to identify deviations from standard in real time.
2. **Stop the process** — Halt work-in-progress; do not let defects pass downstream.
3. **Fix the immediate condition** — Restore the machine or process to standard condition right at the workstation.
4. **Investigate the root cause and install countermeasures** — Apply the **5-Why analysis** to prevent recurrence.

## 2.3 Poka-Yoke (Mistake-Proofing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poka-Yoke** is the engineering discipline that *makes it impossible to make a mistake*, rather than relying on humans to catch mistakes.
> - **Shigeo Shingo** (1909–1990) is credited as the pioneer.
> - Three types of poka-yoke:
>   1. **Contact method** — physical shape, pin, or fixture blocks wrong insertion.
>   2. **Fixed-value method** — counter or sensor ensures the correct number of actions.
>   3. **Motion-step method** — sequence sensors ensure steps occur in the correct order.

## 2.4 Andon System — The Voice of Jidoka

> [!NOTE]
> The **Andon** is the communication heart of Jidoka. It transforms silent stoppages into loud, visible signals.
> - **Green Andon** → Normal operation.
> - **Yellow Andon** → Operator needs assistance (quality issue, minor stop).
> - **Red Andon** → Line stoppage (critical abnormality, defect, safety issue).
> Modern Andons are **digital displays** integrated with Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES), automatically logging every stop event with timestamp and operator ID.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the key analytical metrics, formulas, and units used in Jidoka problem-solving questions in KTU University Exams.

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit | Boundary / Note |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **First-Time-Through Yield** | FTY | $\text{FTY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units at First Pass}}{\text{Total Units Entered}}$ | $\vert$ ratio $\vert$ | $0 \le \text{FTY} \le 1$; higher is better |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield** | RTY | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FTY}_{i}$ | $\vert$ ratio $\vert$ | Product of all stage FTYs |
| **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}$ | defects per million | D = defects, U = units, O = opportunities |
| **Line Stop Frequency** | LSF | $\text{LSF} = \dfrac{N_{\text{stops}}}{T_{\text{shift}}}$ | stops per hour | Tracked per shift for Jidoka maturity |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | MTTR | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_{\text{repair}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | minutes | Lower is better |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{uptime}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | hours | Higher is better |
| **Availability (OEE)** | A | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $\vert$ ratio $\vert$ | Core OEE availability component |
| **OEE (Overall)** | OEE | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | $\vert$ ratio $\vert$ | P = Performance, Q = Quality |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Jidoka in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications:**
> - **Automotive:** Toyota assembly lines — any worker pulls the Andon cord; the entire line halts within 60 seconds. This is called the **"Andon Cord"** system.
> - **Electronics:** Foxconn's SMT lines use vision-based poka-yoke for PCB component placement.
> - **Aerospace:** Boeing wing-fuselage join uses laser-based dimensional poka-yoke.
> - **Healthcare:** "Smart IV pumps" that stop infusion on detecting air bubbles — *Jidoka in hospitals*.
> - **Software:** CI/CD pipelines that auto-rollback on test failure — *Jidoka in DevOps*.

Jidoka fundamentally transforms a **detection-based quality system** (inspect at the end) into a **prevention-based quality system** (stop the defect at birth), saving the **1-10-100 rule** cost of rework, scrap, and warranty claims.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Relating Jidoka Maturity to Defect Rate (DPMO)

This derivation shows the **Six Sigma relationship** between process defects and Jidoka-enabled quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that as Jidoka maturity (and poka-yoke coverage) increases, the Defect Per Million Opportunities (DPMO) decreases.

**Step 1 — Define the baseline defect rate.**

Let $D$ be the number of defects, $U$ be the number of units produced, and $O$ be the number of opportunities for defect per unit. The DPMO is defined as:

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{baseline}} = \frac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce a Jidoka effectiveness factor $J \in [0, 1]$.**

A value of $J = 0$ means no Jidoka is implemented (defects pass downstream). A value of $J = 1$ means perfect Jidoka — every defect is stopped at the workstation. The surviving defect rate after Jidoka is reduced proportionally:

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{Jidoka}} = \text{DPMO}_{\text{baseline}} \times (1 - J)$$

**Step 3 — Express the effective yield.**

The rolled throughput yield after Jidoka at $k$ sequential stages:

$$\text{RTY}_{\text{Jidoka}} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} \left(1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}_{i}}{1{,}000{,}000}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Suppose a process produces $U = 10{,}000$ units per day, each with $O = 5$ defect opportunities, and currently $D = 50$ defects per day. Management installs poka-yokes achieving $J = 0.80$.

**Step 4a — Baseline DPMO calculation:**

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{baseline}} = \frac{50 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000 \times 5} = \frac{50{,}000{,}000}{50{,}000} = 1{,}000$$

**Step 4b — DPMO after Jidoka installation:**

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{Jidoka}} = 1{,}000 \times (1 - 0.80) = 200$$

**Step 4c — Effective yield at $k = 4$ sequential stages:**

$$\text{RTY}_{\text{Jidoka}} = (1 - 0.0002)^{4} = (0.9998)^{4} \approx 0.9992$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

A Jidoka effectiveness of $J = 0.80$ (achievable with mature poka-yokes) reduces DPMO by 5× and pushes RTY to **99.92%**, equating to **4.4 sigma** quality.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — OEE Improvement via Jidoka

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Quantify the gain in **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** when Jidoka is implemented.

**Step 1 — Recall the OEE identity.**

$$\text{OEE} = \underbrace{\frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}}_{A} \times \underbrace{\frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Theoretical Output}}}_{P} \times \underbrace{\frac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units}}}_{Q}$$

**Step 2 — Express the pre-Jidoka state.**

Pre-Jidoka, minor defects are produced and discovered late, causing long MTTR (mean time to repair) since the failure must be back-tracked. Let:

$$\text{MTTR}_{\text{before}} = 60 \text{ min}, \quad \text{MTBF} = 240 \text{ min}, \quad P = 0.90, \quad Q = 0.92$$

**Step 3 — Express the post-Jidoka state.**

With Andon + poka-yoke, the same defect is caught at birth, slashing MTTR by 80%. Minor stops become *micro-stops* that the operator clears in 12 minutes:

$$\text{MTTR}_{\text{after}} = 12 \text{ min}, \quad \text{MTBF} = 240 \text{ min}, \quad P = 0.95, \quad Q = 0.99$$

**Step 4 — Compute OEE before Jidoka.**

$$\text{OEE}_{\text{before}} = \frac{240}{240 + 60} \times 0.90 \times 0.92 = \frac{240}{300} \times 0.828 = 0.80 \times 0.828 = 0.6624$$

**Step 4a — Convert to percentage:**

$$\text{OEE}_{\text{before}} = 66.24\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute OEE after Jidoka.**

$$\text{OEE}_{\text{after}} = \frac{240}{240 + 12} \times 0.95 \times 0.99 = \frac{240}{252} \times 0.9405 = 0.9524 \times 0.9405 \approx 0.8957$$

**Step 5a — Convert to percentage:**

$$\text{OEE}_{\text{after}} = 89.57\%$$

**Step 6 — Net gain from Jidoka.**

$$\Delta \text{OEE} = 89.57\% - 66.24\% = 23.33 \text{ percentage points}$$

This is the single largest OEE gain achievable in any shop-floor intervention — a benchmark consistent with the **World-Class OEE target of ≥ 85%** set by SEMI (Semiconductor Equipment and Materials International).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Thinking vs. Automatic Jidoka

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix as your **KTU answer-writing guide** for descriptive questions on Jidoka classification.

| Decision Parameter | Thinking Jidoka (Human Sensor) | Automatic Jidoka (Machine Sensor) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Operator count at station** | $\vert$ Many (assembly, food, service) $\vert$ | $\vert$ Few (highly automated) $\vert$ |
| **Defect visibility** | $\vert$ Easy to see/hear/feel $\vert$ | $\vert$ Invisible or microscopic $\vert$ |
| **Cycle time** | $\vert$ Long (seconds to minutes) $\vert$ | $\vert$ Short (sub-second) $\vert$ |
| **Capital investment** | $\vert$ Low (training, checklists) $\vert$ | $\vert$ High (sensors, PLC, vision) $\vert$ |
| **Skill requirement** | $\vert$ High — operator must be multi-skilled $\vert$ | $\vert$ Low — operator monitors only $\vert$ |
| **5-Why application** | $\vert$ By operator immediately $\vert$ | $\vert$ By maintenance engineer $\vert$ |
| **Example** | $\vert$ Manual wiring harness assembly $\vert$ | $\vert$ CNC spindle load monitoring $\vert$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Jidoka Operational Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the end-to-end **Jidoka Stop-Call-Fix-Prevent** logic, including Andon signaling and 5-Why root-cause analysis. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain only raw uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Process Starts at Station] --> detectA[Sensor or Operator Detects Abnormality]
    detectA --> decisionA{Is Condition Within Standard?}
    decisionA -- YES --> continueA[Continue Production]
    decisionA -- NO --> stopA[STOP THE PROCESS IMMEDIATELY]
    stopA --> andonA[Trigger Andon Signal Yellow or Red]
    andonA --> callA[Call Team Leader and Quality Engineer]
    callA --> containA[Contain Defective WIP at Station]
    containA --> restoreA[Restore Machine or Process to Standard]
    restoreA --> whyA[Apply 5-Why Root Cause Analysis]
    whyA --> counterA[Install Permanent Countermeasure Poka Yoke]
    counterA --> verifyA[Verify Countermeasure on Sample Run]
    verifyA --> resumeA[Resume Production After Sign Off]
    resumeA --> standardA[Update Standard Work Document]
    standardA --> startA
    continueA --> startA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every abnormal condition triggers a self-contained PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) micro-cycle. The process is **never resumed** until a permanent countermeasure is installed — this is what differentiates Jidoka from conventional quality control.

## 4.2 Jidoka Maturity Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block-level architecture shows the **five maturity levels** a manufacturing unit climbs through when adopting Jidoka, mapped to Industry 4.0 readiness.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    level1[Level 1 Manual Detection] --> level2[Level 2 Poka Yoke Fixtures]
    level2 --> level3[Level 3 Andon Light and Cord]
    level3 --> level4[Level 4 PLC Sensor Auto Stop]
    level4 --> level5[Level 5 IoT AI Vision Auto Detect and Correct]
    level1 --> desc1[Operators check visually and tactilely]
    level2 --> desc2[Physical fixtures prevent wrong assembly]
    level3 --> desc3[Visual and audible call for help installed]
    level4 --> desc4[Machine halts itself on limit switch trip]
    level5 --> desc5[Camera vision detects defect and robot self corrects]
```

## 4.3 Jidoka vs. Conventional Automation — Comparative Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram contrasts **Conventional Automation** (left) with **Jidoka** (right) at the workstation level.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONV[Conventional Automation]
        c1[Raw Input] --> c2[Machine Runs Continuously]
        c2 --> c3[End of Line Inspection]
        c3 --> c4{Defect Found}
        c4 -- YES --> c5[Rework or Scrap at End]
        c4 -- NO --> c6[Pass to Next Station]
    end
    subgraph JIDO[Jidoka Station]
        j1[Raw Input] --> j2[Sensor Checks at Each Step]
        j2 --> j3{Any Abnormality}
        j3 -- NO --> j4[Pass to Next Station]
        j3 -- YES --> j5[Stop and Trigger Andon]
        j5 --> j6[Operator 5-Why Fix at Source]
        j6 --> j2
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In Conventional Automation, **defects are caught after value is added** (wasted labour + materials). In Jidoka, defects are caught **at the exact moment of creation**, eliminating downstream waste — the foundation of the famous Toyota cost advantage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank is structured exactly as per the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Marks are tagged according to the official **CO (Course Outcome)** mapping for PERUT413 Module 3.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. Define Jidoka. How does it differ from conventional automation? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Jidoka, meaning *Automation with a Human Touch*, is the principle of giving a machine or operator the intelligence to detect an abnormal condition and **stop the process immediately** while signalling for help via an Andon system. It was developed by Sakichi Toyoda in the 1920s and is one of the two pillars of the Toyota Production System.

| Aspect | Conventional Automation | Jidoka |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Defect detection | After completion (end-of-line) | At the moment of occurrence |
| Line stoppage | Machine keeps producing | Machine halts itself |
| Operator role | Passive monitor | Active decision-maker |
| Quality cost | High (rework/scrap) | Low (prevention at source) |

Thus, Jidoka converts a *detection-based* system into a *prevention-based* system.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. What is Poka-Yoke? List its three types with one industrial example each. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
Poka-Yoke is a Japanese term meaning *mistake-proofing* — a device or procedure that **prevents an error from occurring** or **immediately detects it** so it cannot pass to the next step. It was developed by **Shigeo Shingo**.

**Three Types:**
1. **Contact Method** — A physical pin or shape prevents wrong insertion. *Example: SIM card trays that physically block wrong-side insertion.*
2. **Fixed-Value Method** — A counter ensures the correct number of actions. *Example: A pill bottle counter that stops at exactly 30 tablets.*
3. **Motion-Step Method** — A sensor ensures steps occur in the correct sequence. *Example: A car dashboard that prevents shifting from Park to Drive unless the brake pedal is pressed.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | CO3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **four rules of Jidoka** as practiced in the Toyota Production System. How do they support built-in quality? (7 Marks)

**(b)** A CNC turning cell produces 5,000 shafts per shift. Each shaft has 4 critical dimensions inspected. The cell currently records 80 defects per shift. Management installs Jidoka poka-yokes and achieves an effectiveness of $J = 0.75$. Calculate the **baseline DPMO**, **post-Jidoka DPMO**, and the **rolled throughput yield (RTY) for 5 sequential operations** assuming the same post-Jidoka defect rate. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

The four operational rules of Jidoka — sometimes called the *"Jidoka Cycle"* — are:

1. **Detect the Abnormality:** Every workstation is fitted with sensors, limit switches, vision systems, or operator check points that identify any deviation from the defined standard condition in real time.
2. **Stop the Process:** The moment an abnormality is detected, the machine (or operator) **physically halts the line**. The Japanese call this *nigen saku gen ryō* (two workers producing the right quantity rather than one producing the wrong quantity). This prevents defective WIP from travelling downstream.
3. **Fix the Immediate Condition:** The operator, with the help of a Team Leader, restores the machine or process to standard *at the workstation itself*. This is *jikoda* (self-rescue) — the worker is multi-skilled to handle the recovery.
4. **Investigate Root Cause and Install Countermeasure:** The team applies the **5-Why analysis** to reach the systemic root cause and installs a **poka-yoke** or revised standard work to prevent recurrence. The new standard is documented and shared across the plant.

**How it supports built-in quality:** By stopping at the source, Jidoka makes it **impossible to pass a defect downstream**. Quality is no longer dependent on a final inspection gate; it is *engineered into* the process. This is the essence of *built-in quality (jidō hinshitsu)*, which Shigeo Shingo called the **"Zero Quality Control"** approach.

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $U = 5{,}000$ shafts/shift, $O = 4$ dimensions/shaft, $D = 80$ defects/shift, $J = 0.75$, $k = 5$ sequential operations.

**Step 1 — Baseline DPMO calculation:**

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{baseline}} = \frac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O} = \frac{80 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{5{,}000 \times 4} = \frac{80{,}000{,}000}{20{,}000} = 4{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Post-Jidoka DPMO calculation:**

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{Jidoka}} = 4{,}000 \times (1 - 0.75) = 4{,}000 \times 0.25 = 1{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Per-stage yield after Jidoka:**

$$\text{FTY} = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000} = 1 - \frac{1{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.999$$

**Step 4 — Rolled Throughput Yield over 5 stages:**

$$\text{RTY} = (0.999)^{5}$$

**Step 4a — Compute the power:**

$$(0.999)^{5} = 0.999 \times 0.999 \times 0.999 \times 0.999 \times 0.999 \approx 0.99501$$

**Step 5 — Result:**

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO}_{\text{baseline}} = 4{,}000, \quad \text{DPMO}_{\text{Jidoka}} = 1{,}000, \quad \text{RTY} = 99.501\%}$$

The Jidoka intervention improves sigma level from approximately **4.4σ to 4.6σ** and pushes 5-stage cumulative yield above 99.5%.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **concept of Automatic Jidoka**. Differentiate it from **Thinking Jidoka** with a comparison table. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A packaging machine has $\text{MTBF} = 300 \text{ min}$ and $\text{MTTR} = 45 \text{ min}$. Performance is $P = 0.85$ and Quality is $Q = 0.90$. After installing an Andon and a vision-based poka-yoke, $\text{MTTR}$ drops to 10 min, $P$ rises to 0.94, and $Q$ rises to 0.98. Calculate the OEE before and after, and comment on the Jidoka impact. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Automatic Jidoka (自働化):** It is the practice of embedding **mechanical, electronic, or computer-based intelligence** into a machine so that it can detect an abnormal condition *without* human presence, stop itself, and signal the operator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram (described in text for the answer sheet):**
> `Input Material → Sensor Array (Limit, Vision, Force, Temperature) → PLC Decision Block → Machine Halt Relay + Andon Trigger → Operator Notification via HMI`
> The PLC acts as the "brain", sensors as the "eyes and skin", and the relay as the "muscle" that enforces the stop.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Automatic Jidoka | Thinking Jidoka |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Sensor | Machine sensor | Human operator |
| Best for | High-speed / hazardous tasks | Manual assembly / service |
| Investment | High (sensors, PLC, vision) | Low (training, standard work) |
| Reaction time | Milliseconds | Seconds to minutes |
| 5-Why owner | Maintenance engineer | Production operator |
| Industry 4.0 link | Cyber-physical system | Lean labour empowerment |

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**

| State | MTBF | MTTR | P | Q |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Before | 300 | 45 | 0.85 | 0.90 |
| After | 300 | 10 | 0.94 | 0.98 |

**Step 1 — OEE Before calculation:**

$$A_{\text{before}} = \frac{300}{300 + 45} = \frac{300}{345} = 0.8696$$

$$\text{OEE}_{\text{before}} = 0.8696 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 = 0.8696 \times 0.765 = 0.6652$$

$$\boxed{\text{OEE}_{\text{before}} = 66.52\%}$$

**Step 2 — OEE After calculation:**

$$A_{\text{after}} = \frac{300}{300 + 10} = \frac{300}{310} = 0.9677$$

$$\text{OEE}_{\text{after}} = 0.9677 \times 0.94 \times 0.98 = 0.9677 \times 0.9212 = 0.8914$$

$$\boxed{\text{OEE}_{\text{after}} = 89.14\%}$$

**Step 3 — Net improvement:**

$$\Delta \text{OEE} = 89.14\% - 66.52\% = 22.62 \text{ percentage points}$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**

The OEE jumped from **66.52% (typical for Indian SME average)** to **89.14% (World-Class benchmark ≥ 85%)**. The biggest contributor is the MTTR drop of 77.8% (from 45 → 10 min), demonstrating that Jidoka's primary ROI lies in **fast failure detection and recovery**, not just quality.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout
>
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize (–2 to –5 marks):**
> 1. **Confusing Jidoka with Just-in-Time** — Jidoka is the *quality pillar*; JIT is the *flow pillar*. Do not interchange them.
> 2. **Writing "Jidoka means automation"** — Partial credit only. Always add the qualifier *"...with a human touch / intelligence to stop on abnormality"*.
> 3. **Omitting the 5-Why step** — A common 2-mark loss. The fourth rule of Jidoka is **NOT optional**; without root-cause analysis, Jidoka collapses into simple line stopping.
> 4. **Formula errors in DPMO** — Writing $D \times O$ instead of $D \times 1{,}000{,}000$ in the numerator loses 1 mark immediately.
> 5. **Skipping the Andon discussion** — Any Jidoka answer without mentioning the Andon signalling system is considered incomplete by KTU evaluators.
> 6. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — Causes table render failure in digital answer sheets; always use $\vert$ for absolute values.
> 7. **Not converting OEE to percentage in the final line** — Examiners want `89.14%`, not `0.8914`. Always end with the percentage.
> 8. **Skipping the comparison table** — For "differentiate" questions, a table earns 2 marks; a paragraph earns 0–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Jidoka
>
> - **Definition:** "Automation with a human touch" — the machine/operator detects and stops on abnormality.
> - **Origin:** Sakichi Toyoda, Type G Automatic Loom, 1924.
> - **Two Pillars of TPS:** Jidoka (quality) + Just-in-Time (flow).
> - **Two Sub-Types:** Thinking Jidoka (human) vs Automatic Jidoka (machine sensor).
> - **Four Rules of Jidoka:** Detect → Stop → Fix → Investigate root cause.
> - **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proofing; 3 types = Contact, Fixed-Value, Motion-Step.
> - **Pioneer of Poka-Yoke:** Shigeo Shingo.
> - **Andon System:** Visual signalling — Green (ok), Yellow (help), Red (stop).
> - **5-Why Analysis:** Always paired with the 4th Jidoka rule.
> - **Key Metric:** OEE = A × P × Q; world-class target ≥ 85%.
> - **DPMO Formula:** $\dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{U \times O}$ — must memorize.
> - **RTY Formula:** $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FTY}_i$ — product of all stage yields.
> - **Operator Empowerment:** Every operator has the authority to pull the Andon cord.
> - **Industry 4.0 Link:** Jidoka is the philosophical ancestor of cyber-physical systems.
> - **Jidoka vs Automation:** Detection (Jidoka) vs Prevention-through-speed (traditional automation).
> - **Cost Impact:** Aligns with the **1-10-100 rule** — fixing at source is 100× cheaper than warranty claims.
> - **KTU Tagline to Memorize:** *"Build quality in, do not inspect it in."* — Shigeo Shingo.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing) Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing) Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Poka-Yoke** (ポカヨケ) is a Japanese quality engineering term coined by **Shigeo Shingo** in the 1960s as a core pillar of the Toyota Production System (TPS). Literally translated, "Poka" (pronounced "POH-kah") means *inadvertent mistakes* and "Yoke" (pronounced "YOH-keh") means *to prevent*. Thus, Poka-Yoke is formally defined as:

> **Poka-Yoke** is any mechanism in a lean manufacturing process that **prevents an inadvertent human error from becoming a defect**, or **detects the error and stops the process immediately** before the defect propagates downstream.

The original term Shingo used was **"Baka-Yoke"** (idiot-proofing), but it was renamed to **"Poka-Yoke"** (mistake-proofing) to be culturally respectful, recognizing that errors stem from process design flaws rather than worker incompetence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Poka-Yoke is positioned in Module 3 of PERUT413 as the "Zero Defects Mechanism." It is the *operational weapon* that enables the **Jidoka** (autonomation) philosophy taught earlier in the module. Every KTU exam question on this topic tests the student's ability to classify poka-yoke devices, design them for given scenarios, and articulate the four-step implementation procedure.

## 1.2 The Foundational Philosophy: The Error–Mistake–Defect Chain

Poka-Yoke is built on a simple but profound chain of causation:

$$\text{Defect} \;\leftarrow\; \text{Mistake} \;\leftarrow\; \text{Error} \;\leftarrow\; \text{Unavoidable Human Condition}$$

- **Error** → The mental slip or oversight (humans are not 100% reliable).
- **Mistake** → The physical incorrect action resulting from the error.
- **Defect** → The non-conforming product reaching the customer.

Shingo's revolutionary insight was: *Since errors are inevitable, design the process so that mistakes cannot become defects.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Tracked:** In lean factories, the most common performance indicator is the **First Pass Yield (FPY)** expressed as a percentage. The target after poka-yoke deployment is **100% (Zero Defects)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy 1: The USB Connector.** Have you ever tried to insert a USB plug the wrong way? It just will not go in. The asymmetric internal pin layout is a *physical poka-yoke*—it prevents you from making a "mistake." The connector was deliberately designed so that the only possible action is the correct one.

**Analogy 2: The Washing Machine Door Lock.** While the wash cycle is running, the door mechanically interlocks. You literally cannot open it. This is a *motion-step poka-yoke*—it enforces the correct sequence (wait, then open).

**Analogy 3: The Car Reverse Beep.** When you engage reverse gear, an automatic chime sounds. This is a *warning/attention poka-yoke*—it does not physically stop you but alerts you that a potentially dangerous action is in progress.

**Analogy 4: ATM Card Slot Orientation.** The card slot is shaped so the chip must face a specific direction. A wrong insertion is physically impossible. This is a *contact poka-yoke*.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Answers:** Whenever you are asked to "give an example" of a poka-yoke, prefer **everyday engineering examples** (USB, SIM card tray, 3-pin plug, car gear interlocks, microwave door switches) over factory examples. They are universally understood by examiners and earn full marks.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Poka-Yoke Device** | The physical or procedural mechanism that prevents/detects the error | Core noun in all answers |
| **Source Inspection** | Checking the part *as it is being made* | Preferred inspection mode in poka-yoke |
| **100% Inspection** | Every single unit is checked (not just samples) | The goal poka-yoke enables |
| **Fail-Safe (Foolproof)** | Old English synonym; the process shuts down safely upon error | Often used in 14-mark answers |
| **Jidoka** | The broader concept of autonomy that *uses* poka-yoke as a tool | Compare-and-contrast questions |
| **Zero Quality Control (ZQC)** | Shingo's system that combines source inspection + poka-yoke | Conceptual framework |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Major Functional Categories of Poka-Yoke

Poka-Yoke devices are classified by **what they do** when an error occurs. This is the *most heavily tested* classification in KTU exams.

### Category A: Prevention Poka-Yoke (Stop the Process)
The device makes the wrong action **physically impossible** or **logically blocked**. The mistake is intercepted *before* it can be made.

- **Mechanism:** Shape, geometry, sequence, or software lock.
- **Advantage:** Zero chance of defect propagation.
- **Example:** Three-prong electrical plug (cannot insert into a two-slot socket the wrong way).

### Category B: Detection Poka-Yoke (Catch and Stop)
The device allows the action to begin but **detects the mistake in real time** and triggers an immediate stop, alarm, or signal.

- **Mechanism:** Sensor, counter, light curtain, weight check.
- **Advantage:** Catches errors in existing processes that cannot be redesigned.
- **Example:** A bin on a line that holds exactly 10 bolts; if the operator removes 11, a sensor halts the conveyor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shingo's Hierarchy:** In his original framework, *prevention* is always superior to *detection* because it eliminates the defect opportunity entirely. KTU questions sometimes ask you to justify why prevention is preferred.

## 2.2 The Three (or Four) Method-Based Types

This is the *second* classification KTU tests. It describes **how** the poka-yoke operates mechanically.

### Type 1: Contact Method
The device has a physical feature (pin, groove, cam) that engages with the part. If the part is missing, misaligned, or wrong, the contact feature either triggers a sensor or physically blocks the operation.

- **Sub-form:** *Contact + Sensor* (e.g., a microswitch under a fixture).

### Type 2: Fixed-Value (Count) Method
The device ensures a specific **number** of actions, items, or repetitions occurs. Used when errors arise from "too few" or "too many" actions.

- **Example:** A magazine of 10 springs. If the line operator does not place all 10, the empty magazine blocks the next station.

### Type 3: Motion-Step (Sequence) Method
The device enforces a specific **sequence** of operations. Skipping a step or reversing the order triggers the stop.

- **Example:** A two-handed control on a press—both hands must press simultaneously, keeping them out of the danger zone.

### Type 4: Warning / Attention Method (sometimes included as a fourth)
A **signal** (light, beep, voice prompt) is given to alert the operator. It does not stop the process; it relies on human response.

- **Example:** "Door Ajar" warning in a car.
- **KTU Caveat:** Some examiners consider this a *weak* poka-yoke because the operator can ignore the warning. The best answers note that warning methods are only acceptable when prevention/detection is impossible.

## 2.3 Poka-Yoke vs. Traditional Inspection

| Dimension | Traditional Inspection | Poka-Yoke |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | After the operation is complete | During the operation (source) |
| **Coverage** | Often statistical sampling | **100%** of units |
| **Action on Error** | Rework or scrap (defect already exists) | Process stops; no defect produced |
| **Cost Driver** | Labor-intensive inspectors | One-time device cost |
| **Reliability** | Depends on human vigilance | Depends on device + design |
| **Philosophy** | "Find and fix bad parts" | "Make it impossible to make a bad part" |
| **Worker Role** | Inspector (separate function) | Operator becomes the inspector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Line:** *"Inspection does not improve quality; it merely identifies defects. Poka-Yoke improves quality at the source."* — paraphrase of W. Edwards Deming. This single sentence is worth **2 marks** in any KTU 14-mark essay on Poka-Yoke.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

Although Poka-Yoke is qualitative, several *quantitative* metrics surround its deployment. These appear in calculation parts of KTU questions.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Meaning in Poka-Yoke Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Opportunities} \times \text{Units Produced}} \times 10^{6}$ | defects / million | Quality level *before* poka-yoke vs *after* |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units from First Run}}{\text{Total Units Started}} \times 100\%$ | percent (%) | Should rise to 100% after poka-yoke |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | decimal / percent | Multi-station yield; poka-yoke raises each $\text{FPY}_i$ |
| **Sigma Level (Six Sigma view)** | $\sigma = 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(\text{DPMO})}$ | sigma | Industry target is 6$\sigma$ (3.4 DPMO) |
| **Error Rate Reduction %** | $\Delta\% = \dfrac{E_{\text{before}} - E_{\text{after}}}{E_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Justifies ROI of poka-yoke investment |
| **Poka-Yoke Effectiveness Ratio** | $\text{PER} = \dfrac{\text{Defects Prevented}}{\text{Devices Deployed}}$ | defects / device | Engineering design optimization metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety:** Notice that *all* mathematical "divided by" or "such that" symbols above use **LaTeX `\dfrac`, `\times`, `\ln`** instead of vertical pipes `\vert` or `\mid`. KTU PDF renderers and most markdown engines **break** the table row when a raw `\vert` appears inside. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in KTU handouts.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Poka-Yoke is not limited to assembly lines. It permeates every engineered system:

- **Automotive Safety:** Airbag sensor circuits, brake-transmission interlock (cannot shift out of Park without foot on brake), seatbelt buckle sensors.
- **Medical Devices:** Epinephrine auto-injectors that fire only when pressed against skin, syringe dose-limiters, MRI interlocks.
- **Electronics Hardware:** USB-C, RJ45 keyed connectors, RAM module notches, SIM tray eject mechanism.
- **Software/UX:** "Are you sure?" dialogs, undo buttons, two-factor authentication, mandatory field validation in forms.
- **Civil Engineering:** Manhole covers that cannot fall through the opening, asymmetric bridge bearings that only fit one orientation.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Module Link:** In **Module 2 (Jidoka)**, poka-yoke is the *technical means*. In **Module 4 (Kaizen)**, poka-yoke is the *result* of problem-solving. Memorize this triangle: **Jidoka → Poka-Yoke → Kaizen**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Implementation Procedure

## 3.1 Shingo's Zero Defects Control (ZQC) Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation

Poka-Yoke is the operational arm of Shingo's **Zero Quality Control (ZQC)** system. The derivation below shows *why* poka-yoke is mathematically necessary, not just a "nice-to-have."

### Step 1: Define the variables

Let:
- $N$ = Total units produced in a shift.
- $p$ = Probability of a single human error per operation.
- $n$ = Number of operations per unit.

### Step 2: Probability of *zero* errors in a unit

Assuming independent operations (worst-case simplification), the probability that a single unit passes perfectly through all $n$ operations is:

$$P_{\text{pass}} = (1 - p)^{n}$$

### Step 3: Probability of *at least one* defect per unit

$$P_{\text{defect}} = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$$

### Step 4: Taylor expansion for small $p$ (engineering approximation)

For $p \ll 1$, we can expand $(1 - p)^{n}$ using the binomial series:

$$(1 - p)^{n} = 1 - np + \frac{n(n-1)}{2}p^{2} - \cdots$$

Retaining only the first-order term gives the **Shingo Linear Approximation**:

$$P_{\text{defect}} \approx np \quad \text{(for small } p \text{)}$$

### Step 5: Introduce poka-yoke as a probability modifier

A poka-yoke device reduces the effective error probability from $p$ to $p^{\prime}$ where $p^{\prime} = p \cdot (1 - k)$ and $k$ is the *device effectiveness* ($0 \le k \le 1$).

Substituting:

$$P^{\prime}_{\text{defect}} \approx n \cdot p \cdot (1 - k)$$

### Step 6: The improvement ratio

$$\text{Improvement Ratio} = \frac{P_{\text{defect}}}{P^{\prime}_{\text{defect}}} = \frac{np}{np(1-k)} = \frac{1}{1-k}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If a poka-yoke device is 90% effective ($k = 0.9$), defects are reduced by a factor of $\frac{1}{1-0.9} = 10\times$. If $k = 0.99$, the factor is $100\times$. This is the *engineering justification* for investing in poka-yoke and is a guaranteed high-yield 14-mark KTU essay point.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A line produces 5,000 units/day across 8 operations. Historical error rate per operation is $p = 0.005$ (0.5%). A contact-method poka-yoke is installed with effectiveness $k = 0.95$. Calculate (a) expected defects/day *before* poka-yoke, (b) expected defects/day *after* poka-yoke, (c) the DPMO improvement.

### Solution

**(a) Defects before:**

$$P_{\text{defect}} \approx np = 8 \times 0.005 = 0.04$$

$$\text{Defects/day} = N \cdot P_{\text{defect}} = 5000 \times 0.04 = 200 \text{ defects/day}$$

**(b) Defects after:**

$$P^{\prime}_{\text{defect}} \approx np(1-k) = 0.04 \times 0.05 = 0.002$$

$$\text{Defects/day} = 5000 \times 0.002 = 10 \text{ defects/day}$$

**(c) DPMO before:**

Assume 1 opportunity per operation, so total opportunities = $N \times n = 5000 \times 8 = 40{,}000$.

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{before}} = \frac{200}{40{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 5{,}000{,}000$$

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{after}} = \frac{10}{40{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 250{,}000$$

$$\text{Improvement} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000 - 250{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% = 95\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always show *all six* intermediate steps in numerical answers. KTU examiners award *method marks* generously when the logic is visible, even if the final number is miscalculated.

## 3.3 The Five-Step Poka-Yoke Implementation Procedure

This is the most-frequently tested procedural answer in KTU Part B. Memorize in order:

1. **Identify the Defect** — Use Pareto charts, customer complaints, or SPC charts to find the dominant defect type.
2. **Analyze the Cause** — Apply **5-Whys** or **Ishikawa fishbone** to reach the root cause of the defect.
3. **Design the Poka-Yoke** — Choose the right category (Prevention vs Detection) and method (Contact, Fixed-Value, Motion-Step, Warning). Brainstorm with operators.
4. **Test on the Line** — Pilot the device on a single station. Confirm 100% detection and zero false-positives (an alarm when there is no error).
5. **Standardize Across the Line/Plant** — Update SOPs, train operators, and replicate the device at every applicable workstation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write *three* or *four* steps. The complete procedure is **five** steps. Examiners specifically check for the inclusion of **Standardize** as the final step (linkage to Kaizen in Module 4).

## 3.4 The Four-Category Defect Classification Matrix

Poka-Yoke devices are designed against one of these four defect classes. KTU 14-mark questions often give a *scenario* and ask which class applies.

| Defect Class | Trigger Event | Typical Poka-Yoke Solution | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
