| **Task Defect** | A required processing step is *forgotten* or *skipped* | Motion-step method, checklist jig, andon | Omitted welding on a chassis |
| **Treatment Defect** | A part is processed using the *wrong method* or *wrong parameters* | Contact method (fixture guides the tool) | Drill depth set automatically by jig |
| **Tangible Defect** | A part is *missing* from an assembly | Fixed-value method (counter or weight check) | Missing screw in a sub-assembly |
| **Information Defect** | Operator receives the *wrong data* (drawing, code, batch) | Barcode scan, color-coded traveler, digital display | Wrong routing slip attached to batch |

## 3.5 Poka-Yoke vs. Jidoka vs. SPC — The Critical Comparison

| Dimension | Poka-Yoke | Jidoka | SPC (Statistical Process Control) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Shigeo Shingo, Toyota | Taiichi Ohno, Toyota | Walter Shewhart, Bell Labs |
| **Core Idea** | Prevent *human* errors | Stop the line on *any* abnormality | Detect *process* variation statistically |
| **Trigger** | Specific mistake (count, sequence, fit) | Worker judgment or sensor on abnormality | Control chart rule violation |
| **Granularity** | Per unit (100% check) | Per workstation | Per subgroup (sample-based) |
| **Best For** | Discrete assembly errors | Any abnormality (quality + equipment) | Continuous processes, chemistry |
| **Relationship** | A *tool* within Jidoka | A *philosophy* that *uses* Poka-Yoke | A *complementary* quality method |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cross-Question Alert:** A common 7-mark sub-question asks: *"How is Poka-Yoke different from SPC?"* The single most important distinction: **Poka-Yoke eliminates the opportunity for error; SPC detects when the process has drifted.** SPC is statistical; Poka-Yoke is deterministic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Poka-Yoke Master Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Poka Yoke System"]:::root --> B["By Function"]
    A --> C["By Method"]
    A --> D["By Defect Class"]
    B --> B1["Prevention"]
    B --> B2["Detection"]
    B1 --> B1a["Physical Shape Block"]
    B1 --> B1b["Sequence Lock"]
    B2 --> B2a["Sensor Based Alarm"]
    B2 --> B2b["Counter Based Stop"]
    C --> C1["Contact Method"]
    C --> C2["Fixed Value Method"]
    C --> C3["Motion Step Method"]
    C --> C4["Warning Method"]
    D --> D1["Task Defect"]
    D --> D2["Treatment Defect"]
    D --> D3["Tangible Defect"]
    D --> D4["Information Defect"]
    D1 --> R1["Solution: Checklist Jig"]
    D2 --> R2["Solution: Tooling Fixture"]
    D3 --> R3["Solution: Weight Counter"]
    D4 --> R4["Solution: Barcode Scan"]
    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef level1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff;
    classDef level2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000000;
    classDef leaf fill:#deebf7,stroke:#9dc3e6,color:#000000;
    class B,C,D level1;
    class B1,B2,C1,C2,C3,C4,D1,D2,D3,D4 level2;
    class B1a,B1b,B2a,B2b,R1,R2,R3,R4 leaf;
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: The Error-to-Defect Prevention Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Human Worker"]:::actor --> E["Error Occurs"]:::err
    E --> M["Mistake Made"]:::mistake
    M --> Q{"Poka Yoke Present"}:::gate
    Q -->|Yes| D1["Process Stops Defect Prevented"]:::good
    Q -->|No| D2["Defect Propagates Downstream"]:::bad
    D2 --> S["Scrap or Rework Cost"]:::cost
    D1 --> K["Kaizen Improvement Standardize"]:::kaizen
    K --> P
    classDef actor fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    classDef err fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#bf6500,color:#000000
    classDef mistake fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#bf6500,color:#000000
    classDef gate fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000
    classDef good fill:#70ad47,stroke:#548235,color:#ffffff
    classDef bad fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    classDef cost fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    classDef kaizen fill:#70ad47,stroke:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Five-Step Implementation Procedure (Sequential Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1 Identify Defect Pareto SPC"]:::step --> S2["Step 2 Analyze Root Cause 5 Whys Ishikawa"]:::step
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Design Poka Yoke Contact Fixed Motion Warning"]:::step
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Test on Line 100 Percent Detection No False Positives"]:::step
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Standardize Update SOP Train Replicate"]:::step
    S5 --> LOOP["Continuous Kaizen Loop"]:::loop
    LOOP -.-> S1
    classDef step fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
    classDef loop fill:#70ad47,stroke:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Functional Architecture of a Contact-Method Poka-Yoke

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Workpiece on Fixture"]:::part --> B["Locating Pin and Slot Contact"]:::contact
    B --> C{"Part Correctly Seated"}:::decision
    C -->|Yes| D["Micro Switch Closes Process Continues"]:::good
    C -->|No| E["Micro Switch Open ANDON Light Alarm Buzzer"]:::alarm
    E --> F["Line Stop Operator Rectifies"]:::stop
    F --> A
    classDef part fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    classDef contact fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000
    classDef good fill:#70ad47,stroke:#548235,color:#ffffff
    classDef alarm fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#bf6500,color:#000000
    classDef stop fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Each block represents a *functional element* of the poka-yoke system, not a physical device shape. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks in a 14-mark answer for including a "flowchart or block diagram of a poka-yoke you have studied." Use the patterns above as templates; replace labels with your own factory example.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Define Poka-Yoke. Who developed it and under which production system?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Poka-Yoke is a Japanese term meaning "mistake-proofing." It refers to any mechanism designed to either prevent human errors from occurring in a process or detect them immediately so that defects are not produced. It was developed by **Shigeo Shingo** in the 1960s as a core component of the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**. Originally called Baka-Yoke, it was renamed to be culturally sensitive, recognizing that errors stem from poor process design rather than worker incompetence. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between *Prevention* and *Detection* types of Poka-Yoke with one example each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Prevention Poka-Yoke* makes the wrong action **physically impossible**, stopping the mistake before it occurs; example: a three-prong electrical plug that cannot be inserted into a two-slot socket the wrong way. *Detection Poka-Yoke* allows the action to begin but **identifies the error in real time** and stops the process, e.g., a parts counter that triggers an alarm when 11 bolts are picked instead of 10. Prevention is preferred because it eliminates defect opportunity entirely. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Essay Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question B1]**

### Question A — 14 Marks (CHOICE OPTION 1)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four categories of defects** that Poka-Yoke systems are designed to address. For each category, give **one industrial example** and the **type of Poka-Yoke device** you would deploy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **five-step procedure** for implementing a Poka-Yoke system in a manufacturing plant. Illustrate your answer with a **flowchart**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The four defect categories defined by Shigeo Shingo are:

1. **Task Defects** — A required step in the process is forgotten or skipped. *Example:* Omitting a torque check on a wheel after assembly. *Poka-Yoke Solution:* Motion-step method — a torque wrench fitted with a counter that will not release the next job card until the required number of torques is recorded. **(1.75 marks)**

2. **Treatment Defects** — The part is processed using the wrong method, tool, or parameters. *Example:* Drilling a hole too deep in a casing. *Poka-Yoke Solution:* Contact method — a drill jig with an integral depth-stop bushing that physically limits tool travel. **(1.75 marks)**

3. **Tangible Defects** — A part is missing from the assembly. *Example:* A car produced with only three brake pads instead of four. *Poka-Yoke Solution:* Fixed-value method — a weight scale on the bin that triggers a line stop if the bin is returned lighter than the reference weight. **(1.75 marks)**

4. **Information Defects** — The operator receives the wrong drawing, code, or routing. *Example:* A batch labeled with the wrong routing slip. *Poka-Yoke Solution:* Warning method combined with detection — a barcode scanner at the workstation that reads the part code and compares it to the active work order; mismatch halts the line. **(1.75 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

The five-step implementation procedure is:

1. **Identify the Defect.** Use Pareto analysis of rejection data, customer complaints, and warranty claims to pinpoint the dominant defect. SPC charts confirm the defect is *systematic* and not a random spike. **(1.4 marks)**

2. **Analyze the Cause.** Apply the **5-Whys** technique to drill from symptom to root cause. Complement with an **Ishikawa fishbone** diagram spanning the 6Ms (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother-Nature). **(1.4 marks)**

3. **Design the Poka-Yoke.** Choose between Prevention and Detection, and between the methods (Contact, Fixed-Value, Motion-Step, Warning). Brainstorm with operators and maintainability staff. The device must be simple, inexpensive, and reliable. **(1.4 marks)**

4. **Test on the Line.** Pilot at a single station. Verify three metrics: *detection rate = 100%*, *false-positive rate = 0%*, and *mean time between failures > 1 month*. **(1.4 marks)**

5. **Standardize.** Update Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), train all operators, and replicate the device at every station with the same defect. Document in the **Single-Minute Exchange of Die (SMED)** and **Visual Management** systems. **(1.4 marks)**

A typical flowchart representation:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Identify Defect"] --> B["Analyze Cause"]
    B --> C["Design Device"]
    C --> D["Test on Line"]
    D --> E["Standardize Plant Wide"]
    E -.-> A
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only **three or four steps**, omitting the final "Standardize" step. Examiners explicitly deduct **1.4 marks** (proportional to the 1.4-mark step weight) when Standardize is missing, because it represents the **bridge to Kaizen (Module 4)**. Always include five steps.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (CHOICE OPTION 2)

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Poka-Yoke** with **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** and **Jidoka**. In what situations would you prefer Poka-Yoke over SPC? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing line produces **4,000 units/day** with **6 operations** per unit. The historical error rate per operation is **0.008**. A motion-step Poka-Yoke is installed with effectiveness **$k = 0.92$**. Calculate:
   (i) The expected defects/day **before** Poka-Yoke.
   (ii) The expected defects/day **after** Poka-Yoke.
   (iii) The DPMO **before** and **after**, assuming 1 opportunity per operation.
   (iv) Comment on the **ROI** of the device if it costs ₹2,00,000 and each scrap unit costs ₹150. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

Poka-Yoke, SPC, and Jidoka are three distinct quality tools in the lean toolbox. They differ in origin, mechanism, and application.

- **Origin:** Poka-Yoke was developed by Shigeo Shingo (Toyota, 1960s). SPC was developed by Walter Shewhart (Bell Labs, 1920s). Jidoka was formalized by Taiichi Ohno (Toyota, 1950s). **(1 mark)**

- **Mechanism:** Poka-Yoke is *deterministic* — every single unit is checked against a fixed rule. SPC is *statistical* — samples are plotted on control charts and rules flag abnormal variation. Jidoka is *autonomous* — the machine or worker halts the line upon detecting any abnormality. **(1.5 marks)**

- **Coverage:** Poka-Yoke achieves 100% inspection. SPC typically uses 3–5 samples per subgroup. Jidoka covers all units at the workstation but depends on the operator's judgment or sensor sensitivity. **(1.5 marks)**

- **Target Errors:** Poka-Yoke targets *human* errors (omission, wrong sequence, missing parts). SPC targets *process* variation (drift in temperature, pressure, dimension). Jidoka targets *both*, plus equipment breakdowns. **(1.5 marks)**

- **When to prefer Poka-Yoke over SPC:** Prefer Poka-Yoke when (i) errors are *discrete* and *human-driven* (assembly, packaging, manual operations), (ii) the cost of a single defect is high (medical, aerospace), (iii) 100% inspection is economically justifiable, and (iv) the process is repetitive and rules can be encoded. SPC is preferred for *continuous* processes with *inherent* variation (chemical, thermal). **(1.5 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $N = 4000$ units/day, $n = 6$ operations/unit, $p = 0.008$, $k = 0.92$.

**(i) Defects before Poka-Yoke:**

$$P_{\text{defect}} \approx np = 6 \times 0.008 = 0.048$$

$$\text{Defects/day}_{\text{before}} = 4000 \times 0.048 = 192 \text{ defects/day}$$

**(1.75 marks)**

**(ii) Defects after Poka-Yoke:**

$$P^{\prime}_{\text{defect}} \approx np(1-k) = 0.048 \times 0.08 = 0.00384$$

$$\text{Defects/day}_{\text{after}} = 4000 \times 0.00384 \approx 15.36 \approx 15 \text{ defects/day}$$

**(1.75 marks)**

**(iii) DPMO calculation:**

Total opportunities per day: $N \times n = 4000 \times 6 = 24{,}000$.

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{before}} = \frac{192}{24{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 8{,}000{,}000$$

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{after}} = \frac{15.36}{24{,}000} \times 10^{6} \approx 640{,}000$$

Sigma level improvement: from roughly $2.5\sigma$ to $4.4\sigma$. **(1.75 marks)**

**(iv) ROI comment:**

Defects avoided per day = $192 - 15.36 \approx 177$ units.

Daily scrap savings = $177 \times ₹150 = ₹26{,}550$.

Annual savings (300 working days) = $₹26{,}550 \times 300 = ₹79{,}65{,}000$.

Payback period = $\frac{₹2{,}00{,}000}{₹26{,}550/\text{day}} \approx 7.5 \text{ days}$.

The device pays for itself in **under 8 days**, and yields an annual ROI of $\frac{79{,}65{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 3{,}882\%$. This is a textbook example of a high-lean investment. **(1.75 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Numerical Error:** Students often forget to *multiply $N$ by $n$* when computing DPMO opportunities. Each *operation* is an *opportunity*; one unit with six operations has six opportunities, not one. Examiners specifically check this step for **0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Exam Hall (Read 5 Minutes Before the Paper)**

- **Originator:** Shigeo Shingo, 1960s, Toyota Production System. Original name: Baka-Yoke. **(1 mark guaranteed)**
- **Definition:** Any device/mechanism that prevents human errors from becoming defects, OR detects and stops them at source. **(1 mark guaranteed)**
- **Two Functional Categories:** *Prevention* (stops the wrong action) and *Detection* (catches and halts).
- **Three/Four Methods:** *Contact*, *Fixed-Value (Count)*, *Motion-Step (Sequence)*, and *Warning*. Warning is the weakest; prefer the other three.
- **Four Defect Classes:** *Task, Treatment, Tangible, Information*. Map each to a specific Poka-Yoke method for full marks.
- **Five-Step Implementation:** Identify → Analyze → Design → Test → **Standardize**. Never forget Standardize!
- **Inspection vs. Poka-Yoke:** Inspection is *after-the-fact* (finds defects). Poka-Yoke is *at-source* (prevents defects).
- **Poka-Yoke vs. SPC:** Poka-Yoke is deterministic, 100% check, for human errors. SPC is statistical, sampled, for process drift.
- **Poka-Yoke vs. Jidoka:** Poka-Yoke is a *tool*; Jidoka is the *philosophy* that uses it. Jidoka covers equipment + quality; Poka-Yoke covers only human-error quality.
- **Quantitative Anchor:** Poka-Yoke effectiveness $k$ enters the formula $P^{\prime}_{\text{defect}} = np(1-k)$. Improvement ratio is $\frac{1}{1-k}$.
- **Standard Metrics to Quote:** FPY (target 100%), DPMO, Sigma Level. Always state the *target* (100% / 6$\sigma$) in essays.
- **Cross-Module Bridges:** Jidoka (Module 2) uses Poka-Yoke (Module 3) to enable Kaizen (Module 4). Memorize this triangle.
- **High-Yield Examples to Memorize:** USB plug (Contact), Magazine with 10 springs (Fixed-Value), Two-handed press control (Motion-Step), Door-ajar beep (Warning).
- **Examiner's Pet Phrase:** *"Inspection does not improve quality; Poka-Yoke does."* — write this line in any 14-mark Poka-Yoke essay.
- **Diagram Always Helps:** A simple 5-block flowchart of the implementation procedure earns **1–2 marks** in a 14-mark answer.

> **End of Module 3 — Poka-Yoke Notes | PERUT413 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma: Concepts and Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma: Concepts and Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma** is a **disciplined, data-driven methodology** and **statistical quality management philosophy** aimed at eliminating defects, reducing process variation, and improving profitability by identifying and removing the causes of defects (errors) and minimizing variability in manufacturing and business processes.

The term "Six Sigma" refers to the goal of having **no more than 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** in a process — i.e., a process operating at **±6 standard deviations (σ)** from the mean, where both tails of the normal distribution lie entirely within the customer specification limits.

Six Sigma was pioneered at **Motorola Inc. in 1986** by engineer **Bill Smith** under the leadership of CEO **Bob Galvin**. It was later popularized globally by **General Electric (Jack Welch, 1995)** and adopted as an industry-standard methodology under the umbrella of **Lean Six Sigma**.

### 1.1.1 Statistical Meaning of "Sigma"
In a Six Sigma process, the distance between the process mean and the nearest specification limit is **6σ**. This implies that even with a **1.5σ drift** (industry accepted long-term mean shift), the failure rate is only **3.4 DPMO**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistical Benchmark (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **3.4 DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities)
> - **99.99966%** Yield
> - **±6σ** from the process mean

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Airline Safety Standard**
> Imagine you are an airline CEO. Every flight carries 200 passengers. In a "Three Sigma" airline, statistically, **66,800 crashes could occur per million flights** (allowable). In a "Six Sigma" airline, only **3.4 crashes per million flights** are tolerated. That is why Six Sigma is often associated with near-perfection — like a parachuter jumping and the parachute failing only 3.4 times out of a million jumps. Six Sigma pushes the bell curve so far away from the "failure zone" that defects become virtually impossible.

### 1.2.1 Simple Process View
Think of a coffee shop. If the specification says "coffee must be served between 60°C and 70°C":
- A **1-Sigma** process will fail roughly **317,000 times per million cups** (too hot or too cold).
- A **3-Sigma** process will fail roughly **2,700 times per million cups**.
- A **6-Sigma** process will fail only **3.4 times per million cups** — virtually perfection.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Six Sigma Vocabulary You MUST know for KTU Exams:**
> - **DPMO** – Defects Per Million Opportunities
> - **CTQ** – Critical to Quality (key customer-driven metric)
> - **VOC** – Voice of the Customer
> - **COPQ** – Cost of Poor Quality
> - **SIPOC** – Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers
> - **DMAIC** – Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control
> - **DMADV** – Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify
> - **Champion / Black Belt / Green Belt / Master Black Belt** – Project roles
> - **COPQ** – Cost of Poor Quality (prevention + appraisal + internal + external failure costs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Core Six Sigma Methodologies

Six Sigma is implemented using two complementary problem-solving roadmaps:

### 2.1.1 DMAIC (for Existing Processes Needing Improvement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DMAIC = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control**
> Used when a process exists but is not performing at acceptable quality levels. It is a **closed-loop** problem-solving cycle.

| Phase | Purpose | Key Tools / Deliverables |
|---|---|---|
| **D – Define** | Identify the problem, customer (VOC), project scope, and CTQ | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, COPQ, Problem Statement |
| **M – Measure** | Quantify current performance, baseline the process | Data Collection Plan, Process Mapping, MSA (Measurement System Analysis), Cpk |
| **A – Analyze** | Identify root causes of variation and defects | Pareto Chart, Fishbone (Ishikawa), 5-Whys, Regression, Hypothesis Testing |
| **I – Improve** | Develop, pilot, and implement solutions to remove root causes | DOE (Design of Experiments), Pilot Runs, FMEA, Poka-Yoke |
| **C – Control** | Sustain the gains via standardisation, SOPs, and monitoring | Control Plans, SPC Control Charts, Mistake-Proofing, Documentation |

### 2.1.2 DMADV (for New Process / Product Design)

> [!NOTE]
> **DMADV = Define → Measure → Analyze → Design → Verify**
> Also called **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS)**. Used when a new product, service, or process is being created from scratch, or when an existing one requires a complete redesign. A common variant is **IDOV** (Identify, Design, Optimize, Validate).

| Phase | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **D – Define** | Project goals aligned with customer & business strategy |
| **M – Measure** | Identify CTQs, measure capabilities and risks |
| **A – Analyze** | Develop design alternatives, create high-level design |
| **D – Design** | Detailed design, optimise, develop prototypes, FMEA |
| **V – Verify** | Pilot, validate production, handover to process owner |

## 2.2 Statistical Foundation of Six Sigma

Six Sigma assumes that any process output follows a **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution** $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

The probability of a defect is the area under the curve **outside the specification limits (USL, LSL)**. A Six Sigma process is engineered so that the nearest specification limit is **6σ** away from the process mean.

| Process Sigma Level | DPMO (with 1.5σ shift) | Yield (%) |
|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.1% |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32% |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977% |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these equations — they are the most-tested numerical concepts in KTU Six Sigma questions.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Number of Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities |
| 2 | $\text{Yield} (\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}}\right) \times 100$ | First Pass Yield |
| 3 | $\text{RTY} = Y_{1} \times Y_{2} \times Y_{3} \times \ldots \times Y_{n}$ | Rolled Throughput Yield (multi-step process) |
| 4 | $Z = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{\sigma} \quad \text{or} \quad Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{\sigma}$ | Z-score (distance from mean to spec limit in σ units) |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{12}$ | For short-term process (using tolerance ÷ 6σ on each side × 2 sides) |
| 6 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index (potential capability) |
| 7 | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability Index (actual, accounts for centering) |
| 8 | $\text{Defect Rate} = 1 - \text{Yield}$ | Conversion between yield and defect rate |
| 9 | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Cost of Poor Quality |
| 10 | $\text{Process Sigma} = Z_{\text{short-term}} + 1.5$ | Long-term sigma accounting for 1.5σ drift |

**Important Note on Table Cells:** All vertical bar symbols (e.g., for absolute value or min functions) have been replaced with `\vert` or the standard mathematical $\min(\cdot)$ notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Why Six Sigma Works — The Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Pillars of Six Sigma:**
> 1. **Customer Focus (External):** Define quality as conformance to customer CTQs. Defects are simply any output that fails a CTQ.
> 2. **Statistical Process Control (Internal):** Use data and statistical tools (SPC, DOE, hypothesis testing) to **drive variation out of the process** rather than inspecting defects out at the end.

This combination converts quality from a **reactive inspection activity** into a **proactive engineering discipline**. It aligns with the KTU 2024 OBE philosophy of *Outcome-Based Education* — every Six Sigma project delivers a **measurable improvement in a process metric** (lead time, defect rate, cost, yield).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing DPMO

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A mobile phone assembly line produces **5,000 units** per day. Each phone has **20 opportunities** for defects (assembly points, solder joints, screen tests, etc.). Inspectors record **48 defects** per day. Calculate DPMO and the corresponding process sigma level.

### Step 1 — Total Number of Opportunities

The total number of opportunities is the number of units multiplied by the number of defect opportunities per unit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= \text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit} \\
&= 5{,}000 \times 20 \\
&= 100{,}000 \text{ opportunities/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the DPMO Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Total Opportunities}} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \dfrac{48}{100{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.00048 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 480 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= \left(1 - \dfrac{48}{100{,}000}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= (1 - 0.00048) \times 100\% \\
&= 99.952\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Determine Process Sigma Level

Using the standard Six Sigma conversion table (Z = 1.5σ shifted), 480 DPMO corresponds approximately to a **4.8σ process** (between 4σ = 6,210 DPMO and 5σ = 233 DPMO). Linear interpolation gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{process}} &\approx 4 + \dfrac{6{,}210 - 480}{6{,}210 - 233} \times 1 \\
&\approx 4 + \dfrac{5{,}730}{5{,}977} \\
&\approx 4 + 0.959 \\
&\approx 4.96\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The process is performing between 4-sigma and 5-sigma — good, but far from the Six Sigma goal of 3.4 DPMO. A DMAIC project is justified.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A printed circuit board (PCB) passes through 4 sequential stages: Soldering → Component Insertion → Inspection → Testing. Their individual First Pass Yields are 0.98, 0.95, 0.99, and 0.97. Calculate the Rolled Throughput Yield and the effective DPMO for a board with 25 defect opportunities.

### Step 1 — RTY Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RTY} &= Y_{1} \times Y_{2} \times Y_{3} \times Y_{4} \\
&= 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 \times 0.97
\end{aligned}
$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.98 \times 0.95 &= 0.931 \\
0.931 \times 0.99 &= 0.92169 \\
0.92169 \times 0.97 &= 0.89404
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{RTY} = 0.89404 \approx 89.40\%
$$

### Step 2 — Overall Defect Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Defect Rate} &= 1 - \text{RTY} \\
&= 1 - 0.89404 \\
&= 0.10596 \text{ (or 10.596\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to DPMO

For 1 unit with 25 opportunities, total opportunities = 25. So the number of defects per million opportunities is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \text{Defect Rate} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.10596 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 105{,}960 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Determine Sigma Level

Using the table, 105,960 DPMO lies between 3σ (66,807) and 2σ (308,537). Linear interpolation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &\approx 2 + \dfrac{308{,}537 - 105{,}960}{308{,}537 - 66{,}807} \times 1 \\
&\approx 2 + \dfrac{202{,}577}{241{,}730} \\
&\approx 2 + 0.838 \\
&\approx 2.84\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight:** Even though each step looks "good" (90%+), the **multiplicative effect** of small losses degrades the end-to-end process to below 3-sigma. This is why Six Sigma targets **every sub-step** of the value stream.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Process Capability ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A CNC lathe produces shafts with specification LSL = 24.90 mm and USL = 25.10 mm. Process mean $\bar{X} = 25.03$ mm, standard deviation $\sigma = 0.015$ mm. Find $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

### Step 1 — Compute Process Capability $C_p$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\
&= \dfrac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.015} \\
&= \dfrac{0.20}{0.090} \\
&= 2.22
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $C_p > 1.33$, the process is **capable** of meeting specifications under ideal conditions.

### Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (Real Capability with Centering)

Upper capability:

$$
C_{pu} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.10 - 25.03}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.045} = 1.56
$$

Lower capability:

$$
C_{pl} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.03 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.13}{0.045} = 2.89
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.56, 2.89) = 1.56
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $C_p$ is high (2.22) but $C_{pk}$ is only 1.56, indicating the process is **off-center** (shifted toward the USL). The process will produce defects at the upper limit. Six Sigma methodology would call for **re-centering the process** to $\bar{X} = 25.00$ mm to achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ (a Six Sigma target of 4.5σ to USL).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Formula Used | Result | Six Sigma Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| DPMO (Example 1) | $\text{Defects} \div \text{Opportunities} \times 10^6$ | 480 | 4.96σ (Improving) |
| RTY (Example 2) | $\prod Y_{i}$ | 89.40% | 2.84σ (Critical) |
| $C_{pk}$ (Example 3) | $\min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | 1.56 | Off-center, needs re-alignment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DMAIC Methodology Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following diagram represents the **DMAIC problem-solving roadmap** as a sequential process with feedback loops from Control back to Define for continuous improvement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DEFINE: Identify Problem, VOC, CTQ, Project Charter, SIPOC] --> B[MEASURE: Baseline Data, MSA, Capability Cp, Cpk, DPMO]
    B --> C[ANALYZE: Pareto, Fishbone Ishikawa, 5-Whys, Hypothesis Tests, Regression]
    C --> D[IMPROVE: DOE, Pilot, FMEA, Poka-Yoke, Solution Selection]
    D --> E[CONTROL: SPC Control Charts, SOP, Mistake Proofing, Documentation]
    E --> F{Improvement Sustained?}
    F -- No --> A
    F -- Yes --> G[Project Closure and Standardization]
```

**Interpretation:** The arrows trace the typical DMAIC journey. The decision diamond "Improvement Sustained?" ensures that gains are locked in via the Control phase before closing the project. If the gains erode, the team re-enters the Define phase — a hallmark of **closed-loop continuous improvement**.

---

## 4.2 Six Sigma Organizational Role Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[CHAMPION: Senior Leader, Sets Strategy, Selects Projects, Removes Obstacles] --> L2[MASTER BLACK BELT: Mentors Black Belts, Trains, Statistical Expert]
    L2 --> L3[BLACK BELT: Full-Time Project Leader, DMAIC Expert, Coaches Green Belts]
    L3 --> L4[GREEN BELT: Part-Time Team Member, Leads Smaller Projects, DMAIC Practitioner]
    L4 --> L5[TEAM MEMBER: Subject Matter Expert, Supports Data Collection]
    L1 --> L6[SPONSOR / PROCESS OWNER: Owns Process, Approves Resources]
```

**Interpretation:** This pyramid reflects the **resource allocation** in a mature Six Sigma deployment. Black Belts are the engine of execution; Champions provide executive alignment; Green Belts diffuse the methodology throughout the organization.

---

## 4.3 SIPOC High-Level Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Suppliers: Vendors, Internal Departments] --> I1[Inputs: Raw Materials, Information, Specifications]
    I1 --> P1[Process: 5 to 7 Key Process Steps, Start to End]
    P1 --> O1[Outputs: Products, Services, Reports]
    O1 --> C1[Customers: Internal or External, End Users]
    C1 -.Feedback.-> S1
```

**Interpretation:** SIPOC is a **high-level scoping tool** used in the Define phase. It clarifies boundaries (where the process starts and ends) and aligns the project team on who supplies what, who receives what, and who is the ultimate customer.

---

## 4.4 Six Sigma vs Traditional Quality Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Traditional Quality] --> T2[Inspection at End, Reactive, Defect Detection]
    T2 --> T3[Quality Department Owned]
    T3 --> T4[Cost of Non-Conformance is Acceptable]
    T4 --> T5[Goal: Meet Specification]

    S1[Six Sigma Quality] --> S2[Process Control, Proactive, Defect Prevention]
    S2 --> S3[Process Owner and Team Owned]
    S3 --> S4[Cost of Poor Quality Reduced via DMAIC]
    S4 --> S5[Goal: 3.4 DPMO, 99.99966 percent Yield]
```

**Interpretation:** This block-level comparison highlights the **paradigm shift** Six Sigma brings. KTU examiners frequently ask students to compare Six Sigma with TQM, ISO 9000, or Kaizen — this diagram provides a quick visual answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Define Six Sigma. Explain the significance of 3.4 DPMO.**

**Model Answer:**
Six Sigma is a disciplined, data-driven quality management methodology that seeks to improve process output by identifying and removing the causes of defects and minimising variability in manufacturing and business processes. It was developed at Motorola in 1986.

The benchmark **3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** represents the allowable defect rate when a process operates at 6 standard deviations from the mean, accounting for a 1.5σ long-term drift. It translates to a yield of 99.99966% — virtually defect-free. This near-zero defect standard is the cornerstone of Six Sigma and the basis for cost savings through prevention rather than inspection.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]

**Differentiate between DMAIC and DMADV methodologies of Six Sigma.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DMAIC | DMADV |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control | Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify |
| Purpose | Improve an existing process | Design a new process or product |
| Application | When process exists but underperforms | When existing process requires redesign or new product launch |
| Output | Improved process with reduced defects | New process / product with built-in quality |
| Also called | — | Design for Six Sigma (DFSS) |
| Typical Tools | Control charts, FMEA, DOE | QFD, FMEA, Robust Design, DOE |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is **mandatory** between **Question A and Question B** in this section.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) Explain in detail the DMAIC methodology of Six Sigma. List the key tools used in each phase. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The DMAIC methodology is a structured, five-phase problem-solving framework used to improve existing processes that underperform against customer specifications.

**1. Define Phase**
The Define phase establishes the problem, project scope, customer requirements, and team charter. The team identifies the Voice of the Customer (VOC), the Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) characteristics, and develops a Project Charter and SIPOC diagram. The cost of poor quality (COPQ) is estimated to justify the project.

**2. Measure Phase**
The Measure phase quantifies the current state of the process. Data is collected, the Measurement System Analysis (MSA) is performed to ensure data integrity, and process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) along with baseline DPMO are calculated. Detailed process maps are created to understand flow.

**3. Analyze Phase**
The Analyze phase identifies the root causes of defects and variation. Statistical tools such as Pareto charts, Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams, the 5-Whys technique, hypothesis testing, and regression analysis are used. The goal is to separate the **vital few** causes from the **trivial many**.

**4. Improve Phase**
In this phase, the team brainstorms, pilots, and implements solutions targeting the validated root causes. Design of Experiments (DOE) helps optimise process parameters. Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) prioritises risks, and Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) devices are introduced.

**5. Control Phase**
The Control phase ensures that the improvements are sustained. Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts are deployed, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) are updated, and a Control Plan is established. The process owner formally takes ownership and the project is documented for replication.

**Key Tools by Phase:**

| Phase | Key Tools |
|---|---|
| Define | Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, COPQ, CTQ Tree |
| Measure | Data Collection Plan, MSA, Process Map, Capability Indices |
| Analyze | Pareto, Fishbone, 5-Whys, Hypothesis Test, Regression |
| Improve | DOE, FMEA, Poka-Yoke, Pilot, Cost-Benefit Analysis |
| Control | Control Charts, Control Plan, SOP, Documentation |

**(b) A manufacturing process produces 12,000 units/day. Each unit has 15 defect opportunities. The number of defects observed is 90 per day. Calculate the DPMO, Yield, and identify the approximate process sigma level. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= 12{,}000 \times 15 = 180{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — DPMO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \dfrac{90}{180{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\
&= 0.0005 \times 10^{6} \\
&= 500 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Yield} &= \left(1 - \dfrac{90}{180{,}000}\right) \times 100\% \\
&= (1 - 0.0005) \times 100\% \\
&= 99.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Process Sigma Level:**

From the standard Six Sigma table, 500 DPMO lies between 4σ (6,210 DPMO) and 5σ (233 DPMO). Using linear interpolation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &\approx 4 + \dfrac{6{,}210 - 500}{6{,}210 - 233} \\
&\approx 4 + \dfrac{5{,}710}{5{,}977} \\
&\approx 4 + 0.955 \\
&\approx 4.96\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The process operates at approximately **4.96σ**, which is between 4-sigma and 5-sigma. A DMAIC project is recommended to push it to the 6-sigma target of 3.4 DPMO.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a) Discuss the roles and responsibilities of various Six Sigma professionals — Champion, Master Black Belt, Black Belt, and Green Belt. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Six Sigma deployment in any organisation follows a structured hierarchy to ensure proper governance, expertise, and project execution.

**1. Champion**
Champions are senior executives (typically Vice President or Director level) who own the Six Sigma vision. They **identify and prioritise projects** aligned with business strategy, secure funding, remove organisational obstacles, and review project progress at steering committee meetings. They are not full-time project workers but **strategic sponsors**.

**2. Master Black Belt (MBB)**
Master Black Belts are the **technical and mentoring backbone** of the deployment. They are full-time Six Sigma experts with deep statistical knowledge. Their responsibilities include: training and coaching Black Belts and Green Belts, leading the most critical and complex projects, developing standard methodologies, and ensuring consistency of statistical practice across the organisation.

**3. Black Belt (BB)**
Black Belts are **full-time project leaders** who drive DMAIC/DMADV projects to completion. They are experts in statistical tools, change management, and team leadership. A typical Black Belt completes 4–6 projects per year delivering measurable financial savings. They coach Green Belts and act as change agents within their functional areas.

**4. Green Belt (GB)**
Green Belts are **part-time practitioners**, often working within their own departments, who lead smaller Six Sigma projects alongside their regular duties. They apply DMAIC tools to local problems, support Black Belt projects, and propagate the Six Sigma culture throughout the workforce. They undergo formal training in basic statistical tools and project methodology.

**5. Yellow Belt / Team Member (Awareness)**
While not always formally certified, team members support data collection, brainstorming, and implementation. They are typically process operators, technicians, or subject matter experts.

**Summary Table of Roles:**

| Role | Time Commitment | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| Champion | Part-time, executive | Strategy, project selection, funding |
| Master Black Belt | Full-time, expert | Training, mentoring, complex projects |
| Black Belt | Full-time, project leader | DMAIC project execution |
| Green Belt | Part-time, practitioner | Local improvements, BB support |
| Team Member | As required | Data collection, subject expertise |

**(b) A process has specification LSL = 9.8 mm and USL = 10.2 mm. The process mean is 10.05 mm with standard deviation 0.025 mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and comment on the process capability. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \\
&= \dfrac{10.2 - 9.8}{6 \times 0.025} \\
&= \dfrac{0.4}{0.15} \\
&= 2.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pu}$ (upper):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pu} &= \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} \\
&= \dfrac{10.2 - 10.05}{3 \times 0.025} \\
&= \dfrac{0.15}{0.075} \\
&= 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pl}$ (lower):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pl} &= \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \\
&= \dfrac{10.05 - 9.8}{3 \times 0.025} \\
&= \dfrac{0.25}{0.075} \\
&= 3.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(2.0, 3.333) = 2.0
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Capability:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $C_p = 2.667$ indicates the **process spread** is well within the specification width (highly capable under ideal centering). However, $C_{pk} = 2.0$ is significantly lower than $C_p$, indicating the process is **off-center** — shifted toward the upper specification limit (since the mean is 10.05 mm, only 0.15 mm below USL but 0.25 mm above LSL). The minimum value of $C_{pk}$ comes from the upper side, confirming this drift. **Six Sigma recommendation:** Re-center the process mean to 10.00 mm to achieve $C_{pk} \approx C_p = 2.667$ and move closer to a true 6-sigma process.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Six Sigma Questions (Lose 2–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Confusing DPMO with PPM (Parts Per Million):** DPMO is normalised by opportunities per unit; PPM is not. Always multiply units × opportunities before dividing.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift in sigma level tables:** KTU examiners explicitly ask "process sigma level" — always quote the **shifted** sigma (e.g., 4.8σ, 5.2σ), not the raw Z-score.
> 3. **Reporting $C_p$ instead of $C_{pk}$:** When the mean is off-center, only $C_{pk}$ reflects reality. KTU answer keys **deduct 2 marks** if you give only $C_p$.
> 4. **Mixing up DMAIC and DMADV:** DMAIC is for *improvement*; DMADV is for *new design*. Examiners frequently set a "compare" question — listing the wrong sequence is a guaranteed zero.
> 5. **Skipping units in formulas:** Always write DPMO in **defects per million opportunities**, $C_{pk}$ is **dimensionless**, and $\sigma$ is in the same units as the specification (e.g., mm).
> 6. **Not including COPQ in Define phase answers:** Cost of Poor Quantification is a **mandatory** deliverable per the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Six Sigma — 60-Second Revision Bullet Points (Print This Before Exam!):**

- Six Sigma = **data-driven quality methodology**; originated at **Motorola (1986)**; popularised by **GE (1995)**.
- Goal: **3.4 DPMO** = **99.99966% yield** = **±6σ from mean** (with 1.5σ shift).
- **Two main methodologies:** **DMAIC** (improve existing) and **DMADV / DFSS** (design new).
- **DMAIC phases:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control (closed-loop).
- **DMADV phases:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Design → Verify.
- **Vocabulary:** CTQ, VOC, SIPOC, COPQ, MSA, FMEA, DOE, SPC, Poka-Yoke, 5-Whys.
- **Roles (pyramid):** Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Team Member.
- **Key formulas (must memorise):**
  - DPMO = (Defects / (Units × Opportunities)) × 10⁶
  - Yield = (1 − Defects/Opportunities) × 100
  - RTY = Y₁ × Y₂ × Y₃ × ... × Yₙ
  - $C_p$ = (USL − LSL) / 6σ ; $C_{pk}$ = min of two one-sided values
  - Z-score = (USL − $\bar{X}$) / σ
- **DMAIC and Lean Six Sigma:** Lean removes **waste (Muda)**; Six Sigma removes **variation (Mura)**; combined **Lean Six Sigma** delivers speed + quality.
- **CTQ Tree:** Translates broad customer needs into measurable, actionable quality metrics.
- **SIPOC:** Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers — used in Define phase.
- **Control phase uses SPC charts (X-bar, R, p, c, u)** to monitor ongoing performance.
- **Six Sigma is a project-based, financial-results-driven methodology** — every project should quantify cost savings or revenue impact.
- **Six Sigma and TQM:** Six Sigma is more **structured, statistical, and project-based**; TQM is more **cultural, organisation-wide, and philosophy-based**.
- **Motorola saved $16 billion** in first 11 years; GE saved **$12 billion** in first 5 years — key facts often quoted in KTU theory questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Quality Function Deployment (QFD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a systematic, structured, customer-driven planning and development methodology that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific engineering, manufacturing, and service requirements at every stage of product/process design and development. It is a matrix-based planning tool that ensures the final product reflects customer needs while optimizing the use of available resources.

QFD was originally developed in **Japan in 1972** by **Yoji Akao** and **Shigeru Mizuno**, with the first company-wide deployment carried out at **Mitsubishi Heavy Industries** in 1972 and later extensively applied at **Toyota Motor Company**. The concept was introduced to the Western world in the early **1980s** through the work of **John Hauser** and **Don Clausing** (MIT), who popularized the **House of Quality (HOQ)** framework.

In the KTU Lean Manufacturing syllabus context, QFD is positioned as a **pre-production planning tool** that complements Lean systems by ensuring **right-first-time** design and reducing downstream **waste (muda)** in the value stream.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Building a Custom House"**

Imagine you are commissioning an architect to build your dream home. You, the customer, do not speak "engineering language" — you speak of *feelings* and *needs*: "I want a peaceful home," "I need natural light in the morning," "I want low electricity bills," "I need safety for my children."

The architect cannot directly build from these wishes. So the architect translates your needs into *technical specifications*: wall thickness, window-to-wall ratio, solar panel wattage, lock-grade certifications, insulation R-value, etc. QFD acts as the **translator** between you (the customer) and the architect (the engineering team).

| Customer Says (Whats) | QFD Translates Into (Hows) |
|---|---|
| "I want peace and quiet" | Sound Transmission Class (STC) ≥ 45 dB |
| "Low electricity bills" | Energy Star rating ≥ 4 stars |
| "Child safety" | Corner radius ≥ 10 mm, slip coefficient ≥ 0.6 |
| "Morning sunlight" | East-facing window area ≥ 20% of floor area |

Without this translation, the architect might deliver a technically excellent house that **misses every emotional need** of the customer. **QFD is the structured translator that prevents this misalignment.**

## 1.3 Standard QFD Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following standard metrics and indices are essential for KTU examinations:

- **Voice of Customer (VOC)**: Qualitative customer statements about a product or service, gathered through surveys, focus groups, interviews, and market research.
- **Customer Requirements (CRs) / "Whats"**: The translated, clarified needs of the customer, prioritized by **Importance Rating (1–5 scale)**.
- **Engineering Characteristics (ECs) / "Hows"**: The measurable technical attributes that fulfill the Whats.
- **Relationship Matrix (9-3-1 Scale)**: Captures strength of correlation between Whats and Hows.
- **Correlation Matrix ("Roof")**: Captures interactions between Hows (positive/negative synergies).
- **Technical Importance Rating (TIR)**: Numerical priority index for each Engineering Characteristic.
- **Absolute Weight**: Unnormalized sum used for cross-team communication.
- **Relative Weight (% Weight)**: Normalized to sum to 100%, used for direct resource allocation.
- **Competitive Benchmark Score**: A value of **1 to 5** comparing the firm's product with competitors.
- **Target Value**: The engineering goal set to meet customer requirements.
- **Unit QFD Matrix**: Standard KTU reference — the **House of Quality (HOQ)** matrix.

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways for KTU Board Exam**
> - QFD = Customer-driven design, NOT a quality inspection tool.
> - Primary deliverable = **House of Quality (HOQ)**.
> - QFD operates in **4 Phases** (Product Planning, Parts Deployment, Process Planning, Production Planning).
> - The 9-3-1 relationship scale is the **most frequently asked** value in calculations.
> - QFD supports Lean by eliminating **overproduction, rework, and design waste** at the conceptual stage itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Phases of QFD (The "4 Houses")

QFD is deployed through a sequence of four cascading matrix houses. Each phase passes outputs to the next, creating a **traceable chain of customer requirements → engineering → parts → process → production**.

| Phase | House Name | Input ("Whats") | Output ("Hows") |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | House of Quality (HOQ) | Customer Requirements | Engineering/Technical Characteristics |
| **Phase 2** | Parts Deployment | Engineering Characteristics | Critical Parts/Components |
| **Phase 3** | Process Planning | Critical Parts | Manufacturing Process Parameters |
| **Phase 4** | Production Planning | Process Parameters | Production/Quality Controls |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In board exams, **Phase 1 (HOQ)** is the most heavily tested. Phases 2, 3, and 4 are typically covered as descriptive/conceptual questions.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Constructing the House of Quality (HOQ)

The House of Quality has **6 main rooms/walls**, constructed in the following sequence:

### Step 1 — List the "Whats" (Customer Requirements)
Capture VOC statements and convert them into measurable, design-relevant requirements. Each is assigned an **Importance Rating (IR)** on a **1–5 scale** (5 = most critical).

### Step 2 — List the "Hows" (Engineering Characteristics)
The technical team's response to the Whats — measurable, controllable design parameters.

### Step 3 — Fill the Relationship Matrix (Central Room)
For every (What × How) pair, assign a strength rating:
- **9** = Strong relationship
- **3** = Moderate relationship
- **1** = Weak relationship
- **Blank / 0** = No relationship

### Step 4 — Fill the Correlation Matrix ("Roof")
Captures the interaction between the *Hows themselves*:
- **++** (Strong Positive Synergy)
- **+** (Positive Synergy)
- **(blank)** (Independent)
- **−** (Negative/Counter-effect, **Trade-off**)
- **− −** (Strong Negative)

> [!WARNING]
> A negative correlation in the roof means improving one How **degrades** another. This is the **most critical** decision-making insight of QFD.

### Step 5 — Calculate Technical Importance Ratings (TIR)
The cornerstone numerical output. For each How (column), compute:

$$TIR_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (IR_i \times R_{ij})$$

Where:
- $TIR_j$ = Technical Importance Rating of How *j*
- $IR_i$ = Importance Rating of What *i*
- $R_{ij}$ = Relationship strength between What *i* and How *j* (values: 0, 1, 3, or 9)
- *n* = Total number of Whats

### Step 6 — Compute Relative Weights (% Weight)
For cross-functional comparison and resource allocation:

$$\%\,Weight_j = \frac{TIR_j}{\sum_{k=1}^{m} TIR_k} \times 100$$

Where *m* = total number of Hows.

### Step 7 — Set Technical Targets / Spec Limits
Based on the Hows' benchmark, market position, and customer expectations, the team sets the **Target Value** and the **Direction of Improvement** (↑ maximize, ↓ minimize, ◇ target).

### Step 8 — Competitive Benchmarking (Optional but Important)
A row-by-row comparison of the firm's product with **two or three key competitors**, scored **1 to 5**. Identifies the **Sales Point** and **Improvement Priority** (a low-scoring "What" with high importance).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Save this table for the KTU 2024 ESE — these are the **only numerical formulas** tested under QFD.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Technical Importance Rating | $TIR_j = \sum_{i} IR_i \cdot R_{ij}$ | Dimensionless (rating × score) |
| 2 | Relative Weight (%) | $\%W_j = \dfrac{TIR_j}{\sum_j TIR_j} \times 100$ | Percentage (sums to 100%) |
| 3 | Absolute Weight | $AW_j = \sum_i IR_i \cdot R_{ij}$ | Same as TIR (raw, unscaled) |
| 4 | Relationship Strength | $R \in \{0, 1, 3, 9\}$ | 9-3-1 Scale (Kao & Ng) |
| 5 | Importance Rating | $IR \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ | 5 = Highest |
| 6 | Benchmark Score | $B \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ | 5 = Best in market |
| 7 | Improvement Priority Index | $IPI_i = IR_i \times \left(5 - B_i^{\text{ours}}\right)$ | Higher = urgent improvement |
| 8 | Sales Point Index | $SPI_i = IR_i \times \left(B_i^{\text{ours}} - B_i^{\text{best}}\right)$ | Positive = competitive edge |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Toyota, Ford, Tata Motors)**: Used to align vehicle features with regional customer preferences.
- **Software/SaaS**: Translates user stories into engineering sprint backlogs (modern Agile analog of QFD).
- **Medical Devices**: Mandatory for FDA 21 CFR 820.30 Design Controls — QFD is the recognized methodology.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus)**: Critical to link passenger comfort specs to fuselage, HVAC, and acoustic engineering.
- **Lean Manufacturing (KTU context)**: QFD is the **"voice of the customer" pillar** that prevents over-engineering and rework waste at the **design level**, complementing **Jidoka**, **Kaizen**, and **Poka-Yoke**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem: Constructing a House of Quality (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked example mirrors the **typical 14-mark problem** in KTU ESE Module 3.

### Problem Statement
A KTU-2024 B.Tech student team is designing a **portable water purifier for rural Kerala households**. The design team has identified:

**Customer Requirements (Whats) and their Importance Ratings (IR):**
| What (CR) | IR |
|---|---|
| $W_1$ — Low cost | 5 |
| $W_2$ — High filtration efficiency | 5 |
| $W_3$ — Long battery life | 4 |
| $W_4$ — Lightweight / Portable | 3 |
| $W_5$ — Easy to use | 4 |

**Engineering Characteristics (Hows):**
| How (EC) | Unit | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| $H_1$ — Filter pore size | µm | ↓ minimize |
| $H_2$ — Battery capacity | mAh | ↑ maximize |
| $H_3$ — Casing weight | g | ↓ minimize |
| $H_4$ — Membrane cost | ₹ | ↓ minimize |
| $H_5$ — Number of operation steps | count | ↓ minimize |

**Relationship Matrix $R_{ij}$ (Whats × Hows):**
| | $H_1$ | $H_2$ | $H_3$ | $H_4$ | $H_5$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ | 0 | 1 | 3 | 9 | 1 |
| $W_2$ | 9 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $W_3$ | 0 | 9 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $W_4$ | 0 | 0 | 9 | 0 | 0 |
| $W_5$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 9 |

**Required:**
1. Compute the **Technical Importance Rating (TIR)** for each How.
2. Compute the **% Weight** for each How.
3. Identify the **top-priority Engineering Characteristic**.
4. Comment on the engineering focus.

---

### Solution 1 — Technical Importance Rating (TIR) for Each How

We apply the formula:

$$TIR_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (IR_i \times R_{ij})$$

**For $H_1$ (Filter pore size):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_1} &= (IR_{W_1} \cdot R_{W_1,H_1}) + (IR_{W_2} \cdot R_{W_2,H_1}) + (IR_{W_3} \cdot R_{W_3,H_1}) + (IR_{W_4} \cdot R_{W_4,H_1}) + (IR_{W_5} \cdot R_{W_5,H_1}) \\
&= (5 \cdot 0) + (5 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) \\
&= 0 + 45 + 0 + 0 + 0 \\
&= \mathbf{45}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_2$ (Battery capacity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_2} &= (5 \cdot 1) + (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 9) + (3 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) \\
&= 5 + 0 + 36 + 0 + 0 \\
&= \mathbf{41}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_3$ (Casing weight):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_3} &= (5 \cdot 3) + (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 0) \\
&= 15 + 0 + 0 + 27 + 0 \\
&= \mathbf{42}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_4$ (Membrane cost):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_4} &= (5 \cdot 9) + (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) \\
&= 45 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 \\
&= \mathbf{45}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_5$ (Number of operation steps):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_5} &= (5 \cdot 1) + (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 9) \\
&= 5 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 36 \\
&= \mathbf{41}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Solution 2 — Relative Weight (% Weight) for Each How

First, compute the total:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{5} TIR_j &= TIR_{H_1} + TIR_{H_2} + TIR_{H_3} + TIR_{H_4} + TIR_{H_5} \\
&= 45 + 41 + 42 + 45 + 41 \\
&= 214
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute each % Weight using:

$$\%\,W_j = \frac{TIR_j}{\sum_j TIR_j} \times 100$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%W_{H_1} &= \frac{45}{214} \times 100 = \mathbf{21.03\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_2} &= \frac{41}{214} \times 100 = \mathbf{19.16\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_3} &= \frac{42}{214} \times 100 = \mathbf{19.63\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_4} &= \frac{45}{214} \times 100 = \mathbf{21.03\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_5} &= \frac{41}{214} \times 100 = \mathbf{19.16\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Solution 3 — Top-Priority Engineering Characteristic

The two top Hows are tied at **TIR = 45**:
- **$H_1$ — Filter pore size** (drives *High filtration efficiency*)
- **$H_4$ — Membrane cost** (drives *Low cost*)

**Top Priority: $H_1$ and $H_4$ (tied at 21.03% weight each).**

These two engineering characteristics jointly account for **42.06%** of the total design effort.

---

### Solution 4 — Engineering Focus Commentary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Cost and filtration efficiency dominate** the design — the team must invest in **membrane R&D** to deliver *both* simultaneously.
> - **Casing weight (19.63%)** is the third priority — supports the *Portability* need.
> - **Battery life (19.16%)** and **Ease of use (19.16%)** are co-equal fourth priorities.
> - **Strategic Insight:** Since $H_1$ and $H_4$ are both equally critical, the roof (correlation matrix) should be checked to confirm that reducing filter pore size does **not increase** membrane cost. If a strong negative correlation exists, the design team must negotiate a **trade-off**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix Summary Table

| Engineering Characteristic ($H_j$) | TIR | % Weight | Rank | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $H_1$ — Filter pore size (µm) | 45 | 21.03% | **1 (Tied)** | Critical — invest in R&D |
| $H_4$ — Membrane cost (₹) | 45 | 21.03% | **1 (Tied)** | Critical — vendor negotiation |
| $H_3$ — Casing weight (g) | 42 | 19.63% | 3 | High — material selection |
| $H_2$ — Battery capacity (mAh) | 41 | 19.16% | **4 (Tied)** | Moderate — cell sourcing |
| $H_5$ — Number of operation steps | 41 | 19.16% | **4 (Tied)** | Moderate — UX design |
| **Total** | **214** | **100%** | — | — |

---

## 3.3 Decision Rules for KTU Exam Interpretation

| Observation | Engineering Decision |
|---|---|
| High TIR on $H_j$ | Allocate disproportionate R&D budget |
| High % weight + high benchmark | **Defend** (sustain) |
| High % weight + low benchmark | **Improve urgently** (Improvement Priority) |
| Low % weight + high benchmark | **Watch** (over-engineered — waste!) |
| Negative roof (-- symbol) | Conduct trade-off study; consider design alternatives |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The House of Quality (HOQ) — Functional Architecture

Below is a Mermaid block-level architecture diagram representing the **House of Quality** matrix and its six functional rooms. This is the most commonly illustrated QFD construct in KTU question papers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Roof["Correlation Matrix (Roof) — Hows x Hows Synergies and Trade-offs"]
    Whats["Customer Requirements (Whats) and Importance Ratings IR 1 to 5"]
    Inter1[" "]
    Relationship["Relationship Matrix — Whats x Hows 9 Strong, 3 Moderate, 1 Weak"]
    Inter2[" "]
    Hows["Engineering Characteristics (Hows) with Direction and Units"]
    Inter3[" "]
    Bench["Competitive Benchmarking Rows 1 to 5 with Sales Points"]
    Inter4[" "]
    Targets["Technical Targets and Spec Limits"]
    Inter5[" "]
    TIR["Technical Importance Ratings and Percent Weights"]

    Roof --> Whats
    Whats --> Relationship
    Relationship --> Hows
    Hows --> TIR
    Bench --> Whats
    Targets --> Hows
    Hows --> Roof
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above maps the **six functional regions** of the HOQ. Reading top-to-bottom: (1) Roof = How-How trade-offs; (2) Whats = Voice of Customer; (3) Central matrix = Relationships; (4) Hows = Engineering responses; (5) TIR/Weights = Numerical priority; (6) Benchmarks and Targets = Strategic outputs.

---

## 4.2 The Four-Phase QFD Cascade (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Phase 1: Product Planning HOQ — Voice of Customer to Engineering Characteristics"]
    P2["Phase 2: Parts Deployment — Engineering Characteristics to Critical Components"]
    P3["Phase 3: Process Planning — Components to Manufacturing Process Parameters"]
    P4["Phase 4: Production Planning — Process Parameters to Production Controls"]

    P1 ==> P2
    P2 ==> P3
    P3 ==> P4

    style P1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706
    style P2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb
    style P3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a
    style P4 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#db2777
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **outputs of Phase 1 become the inputs of Phase 2**, and so on. This cascading structure is the **QFD planning chain** that ensures traceability from customer need to shop-floor operation — a core Lean Manufacturing principle.

---

## 4.3 QFD's Position Within the Lean Manufacturing Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VOC["Voice of Customer VOC Capture and Translation"]
    QFD["Quality Function Deployment QFD Phase 1 to 4"]
    DFM["Design for Manufacturing DFM and Design for Assembly DFA"]
    Lean["Lean Production — Jidoka, Heijunka, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke"]
    Delivery["Customer Satisfaction and Continuous Improvement"]

    VOC ==> QFD
    QFD ==> DFM
    DFM ==> Lean
    Lean ==> Delivery
    Delivery -.Feedback Loop.-> VOC

    style VOC fill:#e0f2fe
    style QFD fill:#fef9c3
    style DFM fill:#dcfce7
    style Lean fill:#fce7f3
    style Delivery fill:#ede9fe
```

> [!NOTE]
> QFD **closes the feedback loop** in Lean by ensuring that production output is continuously validated against customer needs, enabling **Kaizen** (continuous improvement) to be customer-driven rather than internally driven.

---
