# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT413]`
**Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Mention any two of its primary benefits.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a systematic, customer-driven planning methodology that translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into specific engineering, manufacturing, and service requirements at every stage of product design and development. It uses matrix-based tools, primarily the **House of Quality (HOQ)**, to ensure cross-functional alignment and traceability from customer need to shop-floor operation.

**Two primary benefits (out of several):**
1. **Reduction in design rework and engineering change orders** — QFD identifies customer priorities *before* design finalization, eliminating late-stage modifications.
2. **Cross-functional alignment** — Marketing, design, manufacturing, and quality teams converge on a single shared matrix, breaking down silos and improving communication.
3. *(Alternate)* **Documentation of traceability for ISO 9001 and regulatory compliance.**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT413]`
**What is the "House of Quality"? List its six main components.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the principal matrix tool of QFD, used in **Phase 1 (Product Planning)**, that visually captures the relationship between customer requirements and engineering characteristics. It resembles a house plan, with six main components:

| # | Component | Position in HOQ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Requirements (Whats)** | Left wall — listed with importance ratings |
| 2 | **Engineering Characteristics (Hows)** | Floor/ceiling — listed with units and direction |
| 3 | **Relationship Matrix (Whats × Hows)** | Central room — 9-3-1 strength scores |
| 4 | **Correlation Matrix / Roof (How × How)** | Roof — synergy and trade-off symbols (++/+/−/−−) |
| 5 | **Technical Importance Ratings and % Weights** | Right wall — computed priority indices |
| 6 | **Competitive Benchmarking and Technical Targets** | Basement — competitor scores, sales points, target values |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERUT413, 14 Marks]`

**a)** Explain the concept and origin of Quality Function Deployment (QFD) in detail. Discuss its role in the Lean Manufacturing framework. **  (7 Marks)**

**b)** The following customer requirements and engineering characteristics are identified for a **compact electric kettle** to be designed for student hostels:

| What (CR) | IR | | $H_1$ Heating Speed (°C/min) ↑ | $H_2$ Power Rating (W) ↓ | $H_3$ Body Material Cost (₹) ↓ | $H_4$ Capacity (L) ↑ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ Quick boiling | 5 | | 9 | 3 | 0 | 0 |
| $W_2$ Low electricity bill | 4 | | 1 | 9 | 0 | 0 |
| $W_3$ Affordable | 5 | | 0 | 0 | 9 | 1 |
| $W_4$ Adequate capacity | 3 | | 0 | 0 | 1 | 9 |

Compute the **Technical Importance Rating (TIR)** and the **% Weight** for each Engineering Characteristic. Identify the top-priority design parameter. **  (7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution (a) — Concept and Origin (7 Marks)

**Concept of QFD:**
Quality Function Deployment is a **structured, customer-driven planning methodology** that translates customer needs into measurable engineering, manufacturing, and service requirements. It is built on the principle that quality must be **designed in** rather than inspected in. The methodology operates through four cascading matrix houses, beginning with the **House of Quality (HOQ)** in Phase 1.

**Origin and Historical Development:**
1. **1972 — Japan:** QFD was conceptualized by **Yoji Akao** and **Shigeru Mizuno**.
2. **1972 — Mitsubishi Heavy Industries:** First company-wide deployment during the Kobe Shipyard project.
3. **Late 1970s — Toyota Motor Company:** Applied QFD systematically across passenger vehicle platforms.
4. **1983 — USA:** Don Clausing and John Hauser (MIT) introduced the **House of Quality** concept to Western industries through their seminal paper *"The House of Quality"* in the *Harvard Business Review*.
5. **1988 — American Supplier Institute (ASI):** QFD was institutionalized in the USA with the formation of dedicated training and consulting programs.
6. **1990s onwards:** QFD became a global standard, integrated with **Six Sigma, ISO 9001, and DFSS (Design for Six Sigma)**.

**Role in Lean Manufacturing:**
1. **Eliminates design-stage waste (muda)** — QFD prevents over-engineering and rework, both of which are non-value-adding activities from a Lean perspective.
2. **Drives the "Voice of the Customer" pillar** — Lean focuses on customer value; QFD operationalizes the capture and translation of that value.
3. **Supports Jidoka (Autonomation)** — QFD-derived engineering targets with measurable specifications enable built-in quality checks.
4. **Feeds Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** — Benchmarking data in QFD identifies improvement priorities, providing data-driven focus areas for Kaizen events.
5. **Enables Heijunka (Level Scheduling)** — Accurate demand and feature prioritization from QFD supports level production planning.
6. **Reduces Mura (Unevenness)** — QFD-aligned design reduces variability in specifications, smoothing downstream production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> QFD and Lean are **complementary** — QFD is a *front-end* (pre-production) methodology, while Lean is a *back-end* (production) methodology. Together they form an **end-to-end customer-value chain**.

---

#### Solution (b) — Computation of TIR and % Weight (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute TIR for each How using $TIR_j = \sum_i (IR_i \cdot R_{ij})$**

**For $H_1$ (Heating Speed):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_1} &= (5 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 1) + (5 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) \\
&= 45 + 4 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{49}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_2$ (Power Rating):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_2} &= (5 \cdot 3) + (4 \cdot 9) + (5 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) \\
&= 15 + 36 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{51}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_3$ (Body Material Cost):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_3} &= (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (5 \cdot 9) + (3 \cdot 1) \\
&= 0 + 0 + 45 + 3 = \mathbf{48}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_4$ (Capacity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_4} &= (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 0) + (5 \cdot 1) + (3 \cdot 9) \\
&= 0 + 0 + 5 + 27 = \mathbf{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total TIR**

$$
\sum_j TIR_j = 49 + 51 + 48 + 32 = 180
$$

**Step 3: Compute % Weight using $\%W_j = \dfrac{TIR_j}{180} \times 100$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%W_{H_1} &= \frac{49}{180} \times 100 = \mathbf{27.22\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_2} &= \frac{51}{180} \times 100 = \mathbf{28.33\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_3} &= \frac{48}{180} \times 100 = \mathbf{26.67\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_4} &= \frac{32}{180} \times 100 = \mathbf{17.78\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Summary Table and Top Priority**

| How (EC) | TIR | % Weight | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H_2$ — Power Rating (W) | **51** | **28.33%** | 🥇 **1 (Top Priority)** |
| $H_1$ — Heating Speed (°C/min) | 49 | 27.22% | 2 |
| $H_3$ — Body Material Cost (₹) | 48 | 26.67% | 3 |
| $H_4$ — Capacity (L) | 32 | 17.78% | 4 |
| **Total** | **180** | **100.00%** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top-Priority Design Parameter:** $H_2$ — **Power Rating (W)** at **28.33%** relative weight. The design team must focus on **power optimization** to simultaneously satisfy *Quick boiling* (5★) and *Low electricity bill* (4★) — the two highest-importance customer requirements. **Capacity is the lowest priority**, suggesting the design can be optimized for **0.5 L (single-mug) use cases** without losing customer satisfaction.

---

### 📗 Question B: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT413, 14 Marks]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**a)** Explain the **four phases of QFD** with suitable block diagrams. Discuss the significance of the **"Roof" (Correlation Matrix)** in trade-off analysis. **  (7 Marks)**

**b)** A bicycle manufacturer in Kerala has identified the following QFD data:

| What (CR) | IR | | $H_1$ Frame Weight (kg) ↓ | $H_2$ Gearing Levels (count) ↑ | $H_3$ Tire Diameter (in) ↑ | $H_4$ Production Cost (₹) ↓ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ Easy to pedal uphill | 5 | | 9 | 3 | 0 | 1 |
| $W_2$ Suitable for rough roads | 4 | | 0 | 0 | 9 | 3 |
| $W_3$ Affordable | 5 | | 0 | 0 | 0 | 9 |
| $W_4$ Versatile (city + village) | 3 | | 0 | 9 | 0 | 0 |

Determine the **TIR**, **% Weight**, and **rank** of each Engineering Characteristic. What is the **top-priority EC** and what does this imply for the design? **  (7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution (a) — Four Phases of QFD (7 Marks)

**The Four Phases of QFD:**

QFD is executed through four sequential matrix "houses", where the output of one phase becomes the input of the next.

**Phase 1 — Product Planning (House of Quality):**
- **Input (Whats):** Customer Requirements from VOC.
- **Output (Hows):** Engineering Characteristics with target values and priority weights.
- **Purpose:** Translate customer needs into technical specifications.

**Phase 2 — Parts Deployment:**
- **Input (Whats):** Engineering Characteristics from Phase 1.
- **Output (Hows):** Critical parts, components, and sub-assemblies.
- **Purpose:** Identify which components drive customer satisfaction.

**Phase 3 — Process Planning:**
- **Input (Whats):** Critical parts from Phase 2.
- **Output (Hows):** Manufacturing process parameters (cycle time, machine settings, tolerances).
- **Purpose:** Translate component targets into process control points.

**Phase 4 — Production Planning:**
- **Input (Whats):** Process parameters from Phase 3.
- **Output (Hows):** Production control plans, inspection methods, operator instructions.
- **Purpose:** Document shop-floor quality controls linked to customer needs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram of the Four Phases:**
>
> ```
> [VOC] → [Phase 1: HOQ] → [Phase 2: Parts] → [Phase 3: Process] → [Phase 4: Production] → [Customer Satisfaction]
> ```
>
> The arrows form a **closed feedback loop** when the customer's post-purchase feedback is collected and re-fed into the VOC of the next product generation — completing the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle embedded in QFD.

**Significance of the "Roof" (Correlation Matrix):**
- The **Roof** sits atop the House of Quality and captures the **inter-relationships between the Hows themselves** (not between Whats and Hows).
- Symbols used: **++ (strong positive), + (positive), − (negative), −− (strong negative), blank (independent).**
- **Trade-off Identification:** A negative roof symbol between two Hows signals a **design conflict** — improving one engineering characteristic *degrades* another. For example, increasing battery capacity ($H_2$) may increase device weight ($H_3$).
- **Decision Support:** Forces the design team to either (a) accept the trade-off, (b) innovate a solution that breaks the trade-off, or (c) re-scope the design.
- **Cost of Ignoring:** Failure to check the roof leads to **late-stage design changes**, **engineering change orders (ECOs)**, and **schedule slippage** — direct violations of Lean principles.

---

#### Solution (b) — Bicycle QFD Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute TIR using $TIR_j = \sum_i (IR_i \cdot R_{ij})$**

**For $H_1$ (Frame Weight):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_1} &= (5 \cdot 9) + (4 \cdot 0) + (5 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) \\
&= 45 + 0 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{45}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_2$ (Gearing Levels):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_2} &= (5 \cdot 3) + (4 \cdot 0) + (5 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 9) \\
&= 15 + 0 + 0 + 27 = \mathbf{42}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_3$ (Tire Diameter):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_3} &= (5 \cdot 0) + (4 \cdot 9) + (5 \cdot 0) + (3 \cdot 0) \\
&= 0 + 36 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{36}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $H_4$ (Production Cost):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TIR_{H_4} &= (5 \cdot 1) + (4 \cdot 3) + (5 \cdot 9) + (3 \cdot 0) \\
&= 5 + 12 + 45 + 0 = \mathbf{62}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total TIR**

$$
\sum_j TIR_j = 45 + 42 + 36 + 62 = 185
$$

**Step 3: Compute % Weight using $\%W_j = \dfrac{TIR_j}{185} \times 100$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%W_{H_1} &= \frac{45}{185} \times 100 = \mathbf{24.32\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_2} &= \frac{42}{185} \times 100 = \mathbf{22.70\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_3} &= \frac{36}{185} \times 100 = \mathbf{19.46\%} \\[4pt]
\%W_{H_4} &= \frac{62}{185} \times 100 = \mathbf{33.51\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Ranking and Design Implication**

| How (EC) | TIR | % Weight | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H_4$ — Production Cost (₹) | **62** | **33.51%** | 🥇 **1 (Top Priority)** |
| $H_1$ — Frame Weight (kg) | 45 | 24.32% | 2 |
| $H_2$ — Gearing Levels (count) | 42 | 22.70% | 3 |
| $H_3$ — Tire Diameter (in) | 36 | 19.46% | 4 |
| **Total** | **185** | **100.00%** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top-Priority EC:** $H_4$ — **Production Cost (₹)** at **33.51%**.
>
> **Design Implication:** The customer base prioritizes **affordability above all** (combined with low-frame-weight, the focus is on a *lightweight, low-cost* bicycle). The manufacturer should:
> 1. **Sourcing strategy** — Source frames and components from low-cost vendors in Kerala/Tamil Nadu with consistent quality audits.
> 2. **Material selection** — Use **high-tensile steel** or **aluminum alloy 6061** to balance cost, weight, and durability.
> 3. **Process simplification** — Standardize gearing to a **single-speed or 3-speed** system (22.70%) rather than complex 21-speed derailleurs, which inflate cost and require frequent maintenance.
> 4. **Roof Check Required:** Verify that reducing frame weight does **not increase** production cost. If a strong negative correlation exists, a **trade-off study** between *weight* and *cost* must be conducted — possibly leading to a *target weight* slightly above the minimum.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in QFD Problems (Lose 2–3 marks each)**
> 1. **Mixing up the formula:** Using $R_{ij} \cdot IR_i$ in the wrong order. **Rule:** Always multiply **row's IR × column's R**, then sum down the column for TIR.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute % Weight:** TIR alone is **not sufficient** for a complete answer. Examiners expect a normalized % Weight table summing to 100%.
> 3. **Ignoring the "Direction" symbol (↑/↓/◇):** The Hows must specify whether each is to be **maximized, minimized, or targeted**. Marks are reserved for this.
> 4. **Confusing the Roof with the Relationship Matrix:** The **Roof** captures **How × How** correlations (synergies/trade-offs). The **central room** captures **What × How** relationships (9-3-1). They are different.
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation:** After computing TIR/% Weight, the examiner expects a **one-line engineering decision** (e.g., "Top priority is $H_j$, hence the team should invest in…"). **Marks for interpretation are explicit.**
> 6. **Rounding errors:** Use two decimal places minimum for % Weight. Avoid truncating to integers.
> 7. **Writing the 4 phases of QFD as 4 separate methodologies:** QFD is **one continuous methodology** with 4 cascading phases — NOT 4 different tools.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QFD Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Full Form:** Quality Function Deployment.
- **Origin:** Japan, 1972, by **Yoji Akao** and **Shigeru Mizuno**; popularized in the West by **Don Clausing** and **John Hauser** (MIT, 1983).
- **Core Purpose:** Translate **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into **measurable engineering specifications**.
- **Primary Tool:** **House of Quality (HOQ)** — used in **Phase 1 (Product Planning)**.
- **Four Phases:**
  1. **Product Planning** (HOQ — CRs → ECs)
  2. **Parts Deployment** (ECs → Components)
  3. **Process Planning** (Components → Process Parameters)
  4. **Production Planning** (Process Parameters → Quality Controls)
- **Six Components of HOQ:** Whats, Hows, Relationship Matrix, Roof (Correlation), TIR/Weights, Benchmarks/Targets.
- **9-3-1 Scale:** 9 = strong, 3 = moderate, 1 = weak relationship in central matrix.
- **++/+ / − /−− Symbols:** Used in the Roof to capture How-How synergies and trade-offs.
- **Key Formula 1 (TIR):** $TIR_j = \sum_i (IR_i \cdot R_{ij})$
- **Key Formula 2 (% Weight):** $\%W_j = \dfrac{TIR_j}{\sum_j TIR_j} \times 100$
- **Importance Rating (IR):** 1 to 5 scale; 5 = highest priority customer need.
- **Direction Symbols:** ↑ (maximize), ↓ (minimize), ◇ (target value).
- **Position in Lean:** QFD is a **pre-production (front-end)** customer-value tool; complements **Jidoka, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke** at the back-end.
- **Benefits:** Reduces rework, aligns cross-functional teams, ensures customer-driven design, supports ISO/FDA compliance, feeds DFMEA.
- **Limitations:** Time-consuming, requires accurate VOC, subjective 9-3-1 scoring, scaling issues for agile/software.
- **KTU-Exam Golden Rule:** Always **compute % Weight**, always **interpret** the result with an engineering decision sentence, always **mention the Roof trade-off** check.

> **End of Module 3 — Introduction to Quality Function Deployment (QFD) Notes**
> *PERUT413 — Lean Manufacturing | KTU 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** is a systematic, proactive, and structured analytical methodology used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and document potential failure modes in a product, process, or system. It assesses the severity of failure consequences, the likelihood of occurrence, and the ability to detect the failure before it reaches the customer.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PERUT413 — Lean Manufacturing, Module 3), FMEA is taught as a **Quality Engineering** and **Risk Mitigation** tool that integrates seamlessly with the **Lean Six Sigma DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control)** framework, particularly during the Analyze and Improve phases.

The formal definition per **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** and **IEC 60812** standards:

> FMEA is a technique which examines a product, process, or service for possible ways in which **failures** (defects, errors, malfunctions, or non-conformances) can occur, the **effects** of these failures on customers or downstream operations, and the **causes** that drive these failures — all catalogued and ranked numerically for action prioritization.

### Key Terminology Breakdown

| Term | Formal Definition | Industrial Context |
|------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Failure Mode** | The manner in which a component, process, or system could potentially fail to meet its intended function | Bearing seizure, weld porosity, software crash |
| **Effect** | The consequence a failure mode has on the system, product, customer, or downstream operation | Loss of braking force, customer injury, line stoppage |
| **Cause** | The specific root reason (mechanism) that triggers the failure mode | Improper lubrication, contaminated weld pool, memory overflow |
| **Severity (S)** | A numerical rating (1–10) measuring the seriousness of the effect on the end user | 10 = Safety hazard, 1 = Minor inconvenience |
| **Occurrence (O)** | A numerical rating (1–10) estimating the probability that the failure cause will occur | 10 = Very high probability, 1 = Remote/failure eliminated |
| **Detection (D)** | A numerical rating (1–10) measuring the likelihood that the current process control will detect the failure mode before reaching the customer | 10 = No detection method, 1 = Highly reliable detection |
| **RPN** | Risk Priority Number = S × O × D | Used to rank failures for corrective action |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PERUT413 course expects students to understand FMEA as a **Lean-Pull aligned risk reduction tool**. Lean philosophy demands that quality be built into the process — FMEA operationalizes this by predicting failures *before* they occur, eliminating the need for rework, scrap, or recall-driven waste (Muda).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are an **architect designing a 50-storey skyscraper in Kerala's coastal, monsoon-prone climate**. Before pouring concrete, you would not simply start construction and hope for the best. Instead, you would sit down with your team and systematically ask:

1. *"What could go wrong?"* → This is identifying the **Failure Mode** (e.g., foundation corrosion due to saline water).
2. *"If it goes wrong, what happens?"* → This is the **Effect** (e.g., structural tilt, building collapse, loss of life).
3. *"Why might it go wrong?"* → This is the **Cause** (e.g., use of non-IS-grade TMT bars, inadequate RCC cover).
4. *"How serious is it? How likely? Can we catch it early?"* → These are **Severity, Occurrence, Detection**.
5. *"Which problem should we fix FIRST, given limited budget?"* → This is the **RPN-based prioritization**.

**FMEA is exactly this — but applied to a manufacturing process, a product design, or a service workflow.**

### Real-World Industrial Example: Automotive Brake Disc

| Stage | FMEA Question | Brake Disc Application |
|-------|---------------|----------------------|
| Mode | What can fail? | Brake disc cracks during emergency braking |
| Effect | What's the impact? | Vehicle crash, customer fatality |
| Cause | Why might it fail? | Improper alloy composition (excess sulfur content) |
| S | How severe? | 10 (safety hazard) |
| O | How often? | 4 (occasional) |
| D | Can we detect before customer? | 7 (only at end-of-line load test) |
| **RPN** | Priority | 10 × 4 × 7 = **280** |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** FMEA is a *doctor's preventive health check-up* for a product or process. Just as a doctor runs blood tests *before* you feel sick, FMEA hunts for weaknesses *before* failures reach the customer. The **RPN** is like the doctor's "risk score" — higher score = needs urgent attention.

---

## 1.3 Types of FMEA (KTU Board-Favourite Classification)

| FMEA Type | Focus | When Applied | Owner |
|-----------|-------|--------------|-------|
| **System FMEA (SFMEA)** | Inter-system interactions and architecture | Concept development phase | Cross-functional team |
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Product design characteristics and tolerances | Detailed design phase | Design engineer |
| **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** | Manufacturing/assembly process steps | Pre-production / pilot run | Process/Quality engineer |
| **Service FMEA (SVFMEA)** | Post-delivery service operations | After market launch | Service manager |
| **Software FMEA (SWFMEA)** | Algorithm, control logic, cyber-security | Software development | Software engineer |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board examinations in PERUT413, the most commonly tested type is **Process FMEA (PFMEA)**, with the automotive and electronics manufacturing sectors as the standard application domain.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used

The following metrics are standardized by **SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA, and IEC 60812** and are universally accepted in KTU board answers:

- **Severity Scale Range:** 1 to **10** (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic)
- **Occurrence Scale Range:** 1 to **10** (1 = very low, 10 = very high)
- **Detection Scale Range:** 1 to **10** (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = cannot detect)
- **RPN Range (Theoretical Maximum):** 10 × 10 × 10 = **1000**
- **Action Threshold (Industry Standard):** RPN ≥ **100** OR Severity ≥ **9** (regardless of RPN)
- **Recommended Action Priority (AP, per AIAG-VDA 2019):** High, Medium, Low (replaces older threshold approach)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that high **Detection** value = *poor* detection capability (counter-intuitive!). The scale is inverted: a Detection score of 10 means you have **NO** reliable way to catch the failure before it reaches the customer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Sequential Steps of FMEA (KTU Board-Mandated Framework)

FMEA is executed as a **step-gate analytical process**. Skipping a step invalidates the entire analysis.

### Step 1 — Charter the FMEA Project
- Define scope: product, process, system, or sub-system.
- Identify the **boundary diagram** — what is in-scope and out-of-scope.
- Form a **cross-functional team** (CFT): typically Quality, Design, Manufacturing, Supplier Quality, Service, and Reliability engineers.
- Set the **Analysis Level** (component, sub-assembly, full system).

### Step 2 — Construct the Block Diagram / Process Flow
- For **DFMEA:** Create a **Block Diagram** showing how sub-systems interact.
- For **PFMEA:** Create a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** showing step-by-step manufacturing operations.
- This is the **visual foundation** on which failure analysis is overlaid.

### Step 3 — Identify Failure Modes, Effects, and Causes
For *each* element/operation:
- **Failure Mode:** *"How could this part/process fail to deliver its intended function?"*
- **Effect:** *"What does the customer (or next operation) observe when this fails?"*
- **Cause:** *"What specific mechanism triggers this failure?"*

> [!TIP]
> The **5-Why Technique** is heavily used to drill from Effect → Cause in FMEA worksheets. KTU students must mention this integration as it links FMEA to Lean root-cause analysis.

### Step 4 — Assign Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D) Ratings
- **S** is rated first (effect-driven).
- **O** is rated next (cause-driven).
- **D** is rated last (control-mechanism-driven).

### Step 5 — Calculate RPN and Determine Action Priority
- Compute $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$.
- Apply **Action Threshold:** RPN > 100 OR Severity ≥ 9.
- Assign **Action Priority (AP)** per AIAG-VDA 2019: **H (High), M (Medium), L (Low)**.

### Step 6 — Recommend Actions, Re-rate, and Document
- For high-priority items, define **countermeasures**.
- After implementation, **re-rate** S, O, D to compute a *new* RPN.
- Document results in the standard FMEA worksheet.

---

## 2.2 The Standard 10-Point Severity Scale (KTU Must-Know)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners award **3 marks** for correctly stating the Severity table. Memorize this!

| S | Description | Criterion |
|---|-------------|-----------|
| **10** | Hazardous without warning | Safety failure, non-compliant with regulations |
| **9** | Hazardous with warning | Safety failure, with warning |
| **8** | Very High | Major loss of primary function |
| **7** | High | Significant degradation of primary function |
| **6** | Moderate | Loss of secondary function |
| **5** | Low | Significant degradation of secondary function |
| **4** | Very Low | Minor degradation of secondary function |
| **3** | Minor | Cosmetic, noticed by most customers |
| **2** | Very Minor | Cosmetic, noticed by discerning customers |
| **1** | None | No effect |

---

## 2.3 The Standard 10-Point Occurrence Scale

| O | Description | Failure Rate (per 1000 items / per million ops) |
|---|-------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| **10** | Very High: Failure almost inevitable | ≥ 1 in 2 |
| **9** | Very High: Failure persistent | 1 in 3 |
| **8** | High: Frequent failure | 1 in 8 |
| **7** | High: Frequent failure | 1 in 20 |
| **6** | Moderate: Occasional failure | 1 in 80 |
| **5** | Moderate: Occasional failure | 1 in 400 |
| **4** | Low: Relatively few failures | 1 in 2000 |
| **3** | Low: Relatively few failures | 1 in 15000 |
| **2** | Low: Failure unlikely | 1 in 150000 |
| **1** | Remote: Failure unlikely | ≤ 1 in 1500000 |

---

## 2.4 The Standard 10-Point Detection Scale

> [!WARNING]
> **Detection is INVERTED.** A score of **10 = No detection capability** (worst case). A score of **1 = Highly reliable detection** (best case). KTU students frequently lose marks by mistakenly assuming higher Detection = better.

| D | Description | Detection Probability |
|---|-------------|----------------------|
| **10** | Absolute Uncertainty | No detection method exists |
| **9** | Very Remote | Detection method exists, very low capability |
| **8** | Remote | Low detection capability |
| **7** | Very Low | Low to moderate detection capability |
| **6** | Low | Moderate detection capability |
| **5** | Moderate | Medium detection capability |
| **4** | Moderately High | Reasonably high detection |
| **3** | High | High detection capability |
| **2** | Very High | Very high detection capability |
| **1** | Almost Certain | Detection is virtually certain; preventive control in place |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Standard Reference |
|---|----------------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number | SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA |
| 2 | $S_{\max} = 10, \ O_{\max} = 10, \ D_{\max} = 10$ | Scale upper bound | Universal standard |
| 3 | $\text{RPN}_{\max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$ | Theoretical maximum RPN | Universal standard |
| 4 | $\text{Action Threshold} = \text{RPN} \geq 100$ | Common industry threshold | Classical FMEA |
| 5 | $\text{Severity Threshold} = S \geq 9$ | Mandatory action trigger | AIAG-VDA 2019 |
| 6 | $\text{Reduction Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \times 100\%$ | Measures improvement post-action | Internal KPI |
| 7 | $\text{AP} = H, M, L$ | Action Priority per AIAG-VDA 2019 | AIAG-VDA Handbook |
| 8 | $\text{Criticality} = S \times O$ | Used in AP determination | AIAG-VDA 2019 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE SYNTAX RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `\mid` or `\vert` in all mathematical expressions within tables above to prevent markdown parser conflicts. This is a non-negotiable protocol for KTU-premium-engine notes.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | FMEA Application |
|--------------------|------------------|
| **Automotive (IATF 16949)** | Mandatory for PPAP submission before serial production |
| **Aerospace (AS9100D)** | Required for flight-critical systems and engine components |
| **Medical Devices (ISO 13485, FDA)** | Mandatory for risk management per ISO 14971 |
| **Semiconductor Fabrication** | Used in cleanroom process control for yield management |
| **Lean Six Sigma Green Belt/Black Belt** | Core tool in DMAIC Analyze phase |
| **Pharmaceutical (cGMP)** | Used in process validation and batch consistency |
| **Construction & Civil** | Used in structural safety audits (bridges, high-rises) |
| **Software Engineering** | Used in DevSecOps, FMEA on code modules and APIs |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry 4.0 Connection:** Modern FMEA is increasingly integrated with **AI-driven predictive maintenance** and **digital twin simulations**. In KTU's Industry 4.0–aligned curriculum, FMEA outputs (RPN rankings) feed directly into IoT sensor-based condition monitoring and machine learning predictive models.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Priority Number (RPN) Mathematical Framework

The RPN is a **multiplicative risk score** derived from three independent 1–10 risk ratings. Let us derive the theoretical bounds and key properties.

### Derivation 1: Maximum Theoretical RPN

The maximum RPN occurs when all three independent ratings reach their upper bound of 10.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\max} &= S_{\max} \times O_{\max} \times D_{\max} \\
&= 10 \times 10 \times 10 \\
&= 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Step:** Since each rating is bounded above by 10, the product of three independent upper bounds is $10^3 = 1000$. This is the **upper theoretical limit**.

### Derivation 2: Minimum Theoretical RPN

The minimum RPN occurs when all three ratings are 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\min} &= S_{\min} \times O_{\min} \times D_{\min} \\
&= 1 \times 1 \times 1 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Step:** Lower bound is 1 (not 0), since even the most trivial failure has *some* measurable effect. RPN = 0 is impossible in classical FMEA.

### Derivation 3: Action Threshold Justification

The standard industry threshold is RPN ≥ 100. We derive this from a **uniform distribution assumption**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean RPN under uniform distribution} &= \frac{(S \times O \times D)_{\text{expected}}}{(10 \times 10 \times 10)} \\
&\approx \frac{(5.5)^3}{1000} \\
&\approx \frac{166.375}{1000} \\
&\approx 16.6\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Step:** The empirical median of RPN values in a mature FMEA is around 100. Failures above this median are statistically in the *top decile* of risk and require immediate action.

### Derivation 4: RPN Reduction Ratio (Post-Action Metric)

To measure the effectiveness of corrective action, the **percentage reduction** in RPN is computed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction Ratio (\%)} &= \left( \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Step:** This metric quantifies *how much risk was eliminated* by the corrective measure. Industry target: **≥ 30% reduction** post-action.

### Derivation 5: Action Priority (AP) — AIAG-VDA 2019 Method

The AIAG-VDA 2019 handbook replaces the old RPN-only method with an **Action Priority table** that depends on Severity and Criticality.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Criticality} &= S \times O \\
\text{Detection Factor} &= D
\end{aligned}
$$

The AP is determined by cross-referencing $(S, \text{Criticality})$ and then factoring in Detection:

| Severity | Criticality Range | Detection Range | AP Result |
|----------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 8–10 | High | Any | **H (High)** |
| 5–7 | High (≥50) | D ≥ 4 | **H (High)** |
| 5–7 | Medium (15–49) | D ≥ 6 | **M (Medium)** |
| 4 | High | D ≥ 6 | **M (Medium)** |
| 1–3 | Low | Any | **L (Low)** |

> **Logic Step:** AP = H, M, or L — this is the **modern KTU-recommended** way to prioritize (instead of raw RPN).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A sheet metal stamping process in an automobile plant is being analyzed using FMEA. The following failure modes have been identified with their respective ratings:

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|--------------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| F1: Crack in stamped panel | 8 | 5 | 4 |
| F2: Dimensional out-of-tolerance | 6 | 7 | 3 |
| F3: Surface scratch | 3 | 8 | 2 |
| F4: Burr at edge | 4 | 6 | 5 |

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{F1} &= S \times O \times D = 8 \times 5 \times 4 = 160 \\
\text{RPN}_{F2} &= 6 \times 7 \times 3 = 126 \\
\text{RPN}_{F3} &= 3 \times 8 \times 2 = 48 \\
\text{RPN}_{F4} &= 4 \times 6 \times 5 = 120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking (highest to lowest RPN):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{F1 (160)} &> \text{F2 (126)} > \text{F4 (120)} > \text{F3 (48)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Action Decision:**

- F1: RPN = 160 > 100 AND S = 8 → **Action Required (High Priority)**
- F2: RPN = 126 > 100 → **Action Required (Medium Priority)**
- F4: RPN = 120 > 100 → **Action Required (Medium Priority)**
- F3: RPN = 48 < 100 AND S = 3 → **Monitor Only (Low Priority)**

> [!TIP]
> This is the **canonical FMEA numerical problem** in KTU exams. Students must show all four RPN calculations, rank them, and justify the action decision.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: FMEA Integration with Lean Tools

| Lean Tool | Integration with FMEA | Output Synergy |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------|
| **5S (Workplace Organization)** | Cleans work area, reduces occurrence of contamination failures | Reduces **O** rating |
| **Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing)** | Adds fail-safe devices that *prevent* failure or *detect* it 100% | Reduces **O** and **D** ratings |
| **Kaizen (Continuous Improvement)** | FMEA findings become Kaizen project topics | Closes PDCA loop |
| **Kanban (Pull System)** | Prevents overproduction, reduces handling damage | Reduces **O** rating |
| **Jidoka (Autonomation)** | Built-in quality checks stop line on detection | Drastically reduces **D** rating |
| **Value Stream Mapping (VSM)** | Identifies process steps to be included in PFMEA scope | Defines FMEA boundaries |
| **5-Why Analysis** | Drills from Failure Mode to root Cause | Strengthens Cause analysis |
| **Gemba Walks** | Verifies real-world failure mechanisms | Validates S, O, D ratings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Connection:** The examiner loves asking: *"How does FMEA integrate with the Lean Production System?"* The answer should explicitly reference at least **three** of the above tools.

---

## 3.4 Common Errors in RPN Interpretation (Anti-Pitfall Analysis)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Common Mistake:** Students often assume two RPNs of equal value imply equal risk. This is FALSE. For example, RPN = 100 could be:
> - $(S, O, D) = (10, 10, 1)$ — Severity 10 = safety hazard, must be acted on
> - $(S, O, D) = (5, 5, 4)$ — Moderate on all axes, deferrable
>
> **Rule:** Always look at the *components* of RPN, not just the product.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start FMEA Project] --> stepB[Charter Scope and Form Cross Functional Team]
    stepB --> stepC[Build Process Flow or Block Diagram]
    stepC --> stepD[Identify Failure Modes for Each Step]
    stepD --> stepE[Determine Effects on Customer or Next Operation]
    stepE --> stepF[Identify Root Causes using 5 Why Technique]
    stepF --> stepG[Assign Severity S Rating 1 to 10]
    stepG --> stepH[Assign Occurrence O Rating 1 to 10]
    stepH --> stepI[Assign Detection D Rating 1 to 10]
    stepI --> stepJ[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    stepJ --> stepK{RPN greater than 100 or S greater than 9}
    stepK -->|Yes| stepL[Mark as Action Priority HIGH]
    stepK -->|No| stepM[Monitor and Review Periodically]
    stepL --> stepN[Define Corrective Action and Countermeasure]
    stepN --> stepO[Re-rate S O D after Action Implemented]
    stepO --> stepP[Recompute New RPN]
    stepP --> stepQ{New RPN Acceptable}
    stepQ -->|No| stepN
    stepQ -->|Yes| stepR[Document Closure in FMEA Worksheet]
    stepM --> stepR
    stepR --> endS[End FMEA Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart visualizes the **complete FMEA methodology** in a single image. KTU students can reproduce a simplified version in exam answers to secure **methodology marks**.

---

## 4.2 PFMEA Worksheet Structure (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    colA[Process Step] --> colB[Potential Failure Mode]
    colB --> colC[Potential Effect on Customer]
    colC --> colD[Potential Cause]
    colD --> colE[Severity S]
    colE --> colF[Occurrence O]
    colF --> colG[Detection D]
    colG --> colH[RPN equals S times O times D]
    colH --> colI[Recommended Action]
    colI --> colJ[Responsibility and Target Date]
    colJ --> colK[Action Taken]
    colK --> colL[New S New O New D New RPN]
```

> [!TIP]
> This block diagram maps the **10 standard columns** of a PFMEA worksheet (per AIAG-VDA). In KTU board answers, drawing this structure earns full methodology credit.

---

## 4.3 RPN Decision Matrix Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Failure Mode Identified] --> nodeB[Assign S rating]
    nodeB --> nodeC{S greater than equal 9}
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Immediate Action Mandatory Safety Issue]
    nodeC -->|No| nodeE[Assign O and D ratings]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute RPN]
    nodeF --> nodeG{RPN greater than 100}
    nodeG -->|Yes| nodeH[Action Priority High]
    nodeG -->|No| nodeI{RPN between 50 and 100}
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ[Action Priority Medium Monitor]
    nodeI -->|No| nodeK[Action Priority Low Acceptable]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Define Countermeasure and Re-rate]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Verify New RPN Reduced and Close]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision tree explicitly handles the **two independent action triggers**: (1) Severity ≥ 9 (safety override), and (2) RPN > 100 (statistical risk override). KTU examiners award marks for distinguishing these.

---

## 4.4 FMEA–Lean Tools Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LeanCore[Lean Production System Core]
        direction TB
        leanA[5S Workplace Organization]
        leanB[Poka Yoke Mistake Proofing]
        leanC[Jidoka Autonomation]
        leanD[Kaizen Continuous Improvement]
        leanE[Kanban Pull System]
    end
    
    subgraph FMEACore[FMEA Risk Engine]
        direction TB
        fmeaA[Failure Mode Identification]
        fmeaB[Effect and Cause Analysis]
        fmeaC[RPN Computation]
    end
    
    leanA --> fmeaA
    leanB --> fmeaC
    leanC --> fmeaC
    leanD --> fmeaB
    leanE --> fmeaA
    
    FMEACore --> outputX[Reduced Defects Lower Waste Higher Quality]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram shows FMEA as the **central nervous system** of a Lean Production System — every Lean tool feeds risk data into FMEA, and FMEA outputs drive Lean improvements. KTU answers incorporating this integrated view score higher.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define FMEA. List its three core ratings. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> **FMEA (Failure Modes and Effects Analysis)** is a systematic, proactive analytical method used to identify potential failure modes in a product or process, evaluate their effects, and prioritize corrective actions.
>
> The three core ratings are:
> 1. **Severity (S)** — measures the seriousness of the failure's effect on the customer (1 = no effect, 10 = hazardous).
> 2. **Occurrence (O)** — estimates the probability of the failure cause happening (1 = remote, 10 = very high).
> 3. **Detection (D)** — measures the ability of current controls to detect the failure before reaching the customer (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = no detection method).

---

### Question 2: What is RPN? Write its formula and the standard action threshold. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

> **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** is a numerical score that quantifies the overall risk of a failure mode by multiplying the three FMEA ratings.
>
> $$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$
>
> **Standard action threshold:** When RPN ≥ **100** OR Severity ≥ **9**, the failure mode must be flagged for mandatory corrective action. The maximum theoretical RPN is $10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### 📘 Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive FMEA on a Manufacturing Process

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3, 14 Marks]**

> Consider a **CNC turning process** producing a critical automotive shaft (material: EN8 steel, tolerance: ±0.02 mm). Apply Process FMEA (PFMEA) to the operation and answer the following:
>
> **(a)** Construct a PFMEA worksheet with at least 5 process steps, identifying the Failure Mode, Effect, and Cause for each. Assign S, O, D ratings and compute RPN. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Identify the failure mode with the highest RPN, recommend a corrective action, and compute the post-action RPN reduction percentage. Discuss how FMEA integrates with Poka-Yoke and Jidoka. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — PFMEA Worksheet Construction [7 Marks]

**Process Steps Identified:**
1. Raw material loading on CNC chuck
2. Facing operation (rough)
3. Turning operation (finish cut)
4. Part dimension measurement (CMM)
5. Unloading and binning

**PFMEA Worksheet:**

| # | Process Step | Failure Mode | Effect on Customer | Cause | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|--------------|--------------|---------------------|-------|---|---|---|-----|
| 1 | Raw material loading | Incorrect material grade loaded | Shaft fails in service (material mismatch) | Operator reads wrong barcode | 9 | 4 | 6 | 216 |
| 2 | Facing operation | Excessive tool wear | Surface roughness exceeds Ra 0.8 µm | Coolant flow blocked | 7 | 5 | 5 | 175 |
| 3 | Turning finish cut | Dimensional out of tolerance (±0.02 mm exceeded) | Part rejected at assembly fitment | Tool deflection due to long overhang | 8 | 6 | 4 | 192 |
| 4 | CMM measurement | Operator skips measurement | Defective part ships to customer | No automated interlock | 10 | 3 | 8 | 240 |
| 5 | Unloading and binning | Part dropped, edge damage | Cosmetic defect, customer complaint | Improper bin design | 4 | 5 | 3 | 60 |

> **Highest RPN:** Row 4 — CMM measurement skipped, **RPN = 240**, Severity = 10 (safety/customer hazard).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Corrective Action and RPN Reduction [7 Marks]

**Recommended Corrective Action for Row 4 (RPN = 240):**

Install a **Poka-Yoke interlock** that physically prevents the CNC machine from completing the cycle unless the CMM measurement is logged into the MES (Manufacturing Execution System).

**New Ratings After Action:**

| Parameter | Before | After | Justification |
|-----------|--------|-------|----------------|
| S | 10 | 10 | Severity cannot be reduced (safety-related) |
| O | 3 | 2 | Interlock eliminates skip behavior |
| D | 8 | 1 | 100% detection — interlock enforces measurement |

**New RPN:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{\text{new}} &= 10 \times 2 \times 1 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction \%} &= \left( \frac{240 - 20}{240} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \frac{220}{240} \right) \times 100 \\
&= 91.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Integration with Poka-Yoke and Jidoka:**

- **Poka-Yoke Integration:** The interlock *itself* is a Poka-Yoke device — it makes the error physically impossible to commit, eliminating the "human forgetfulness" failure mode.
- **Jidoka Integration:** The interlock transforms the CNC cell into an **autonomous quality-checked station** — the machine halts (andon logic) whenever measurement is skipped. This embodies Jidoka's principle of "stop-the-line" authority.
- **Lean Outcome:** With RPN reduced from 240 to 20, the process now operates with built-in quality (Muda of rework, scrap, and recall are eliminated).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks in Part B by (1) forgetting to compute the **reduction percentage** explicitly, (2) not justifying the *new* S, O, D ratings after the action, and (3) writing generic "FMEA is good for quality" statements instead of tool-specific integration with Poka-Yoke/Jidoka. Always show: **numbers → calculations → tool integration**.

---

### 📙 Question B (Choice 2): Theoretical & Comparative Analysis

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3, 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Explain the **6 sequential steps** of conducting an FMEA with a suitable block diagram. Differentiate between **DFMEA** and **PFMEA** with examples. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A gearbox assembly line has 4 critical failure modes with the following ratings: **(i)** (S,O,D) = (9, 5, 4), **(ii)** (7, 8, 3), **(iii)** (6, 6, 6), **(iv)** (4, 3, 2). Calculate the RPN for each, rank them in descending order, identify which require immediate action, and justify using the AIAG-VDA Action Priority method. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 6 Steps + DFMEA vs PFMEA [7 Marks]

**The 6 Steps of FMEA (with block diagram reference):**

1. **Project Chartering:** Define scope, form cross-functional team.
2. **Construct Flow/Block Diagram:** Build a Process Flow (PFMEA) or Block Diagram (DFMEA).
3. **Identify Failure Modes, Effects, Causes:** For each element, list FM → Effect → Cause (using 5-Why).
4. **Assign S, O, D Ratings:** Severity first, then Occurrence, then Detection.
5. **Compute RPN & Prioritize:** RPN = S × O × D; rank; identify AP = H/M/L.
6. **Recommend Actions & Re-rate:** Define countermeasure, re-assess new S/O/D/RPN, document.

> *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for the visual flow.)*

**DFMEA vs PFMEA — Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | DFMEA (Design FMEA) | PFMEA (Process FMEA) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Focus** | Product design characteristics | Manufacturing process steps |
| **Phase** | During design / pre-PPAP | Pre-production / during pilot run |
| **Failure Mode Source** | Design flaw (e.g., wrong GD&T, material selection) | Process flaw (e.g., wrong parameter, operator error) |
| **Customer** | End user / vehicle owner | Next operation / assembly line |
| **Example FM** | Cracking in cast iron bracket due to inadequate fillet radius | Welding spatter due to incorrect current setting |
| **Owner** | Design engineer | Process / Manufacturing engineer |
| **Standard** | SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA 2019 | SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA 2019 |

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical RPN Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**

| Mode | S | O | D |
|------|---|---|---|
| (i) | 9 | 5 | 4 |
| (ii) | 7 | 8 | 3 |
| (iii) | 6 | 6 | 6 |
| (iv) | 4 | 3 | 2 |

**RPN Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{(i)} &= 9 \times 5 \times 4 = 180 \\
\text{RPN}_{(ii)} &= 7 \times 8 \times 3 = 168 \\
\text{RPN}_{(iii)} &= 6 \times 6 \times 6 = 216 \\
\text{RPN}_{(iv)} &= 4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking (Descending):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(iii): 216 \; > \; (i): 180 \; > \; (ii): 168 \; > \; (iv): 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Action Decision (RPN threshold + Severity override):**

| Mode | RPN | S | Decision | Justification |
|------|-----|---|----------|---------------|
| (iii) | 216 | 6 | **Action Required — AP = High** | RPN > 100; S, O, D all ≥ 6 indicate systemic process gap |
| (i) | 180 | 9 | **Action Required — AP = High** | S = 9 (safety override, regardless of RPN) |
| (ii) | 168 | 7 | **Action Required — AP = Medium** | RPN > 100; moderate on all axes |
| (iv) | 24 | 4 | **Monitor / No Action** | RPN < 100, S < 9; acceptable risk |

**Justification (AIAG-VDA 2019 AP Method):**

- **Mode (i):** Severity = 9 mandates **High Action Priority** even if RPN were lower — safety cannot be traded off.
- **Mode (iii):** Although S = 6 (not safety-critical), Criticality (S × O = 36) is medium and D = 6 indicates poor detection control → **High AP**.
- **Mode (ii):** RPN = 168 is high, but Severity = 7 and Criticality = 56 → **Medium AP** under AIAG-VDA 2019.
- **Mode (iv):** All ratings low → **Low AP**; no immediate action needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A frequent student error is treating (i) and (ii) as equivalent priorities because their RPNs (180 vs 168) are numerically close. The correct answer must highlight that **S = 9 in mode (i) is a safety override** and demands top priority — this nuance is the **differentiator** between a 10-mark and 14-mark answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize These 10 Points:**

- ✅ **FMEA** = Failure Modes and Effects Analysis — a proactive, structured risk-assessment methodology.
- ✅ **Two Domains:** *DFMEA* (design) and *PFMEA* (process). Also: System, Service, Software FMEA.
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$, where each rating is on a 1–10 scale.
- ✅ **Theoretical RPN Range:** 1 to **1000** (max = 10 × 10 × 10).
- ✅ **Detection is INVERTED:** High D = poor detection (counter-intuitive!).
- ✅ **Action Threshold:** RPN ≥ 100 **OR** Severity ≥ 9 → mandatory corrective action.
- ✅ **AIAG-VDA 2019 Update:** Action Priority (AP) = H / M / L (replaces pure RPN-only method).
- ✅ **Reduction Ratio:** $\left( \frac{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{after}}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{before}}} \right) \times 100\%$
- ✅ **Lean Integration:** FMEA links with Poka-Yoke (reduce D), Jidoka (auto-stop on defect), 5-Why (root cause), Kaizen (continuous improvement).
- ✅ **Six Steps:** Charter → Flow Diagram → Identify FM/Effect/Cause → Rate S/O/D → Compute RPN & AP → Recommend Action & Re-rate.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** When asked to "explain FMEA" in an exam, always structure the answer as: **Definition → Types → Steps → Formula → Severity/Occurrence/Detection scales → RPN calculation → Lean integration → Industrial example**. This structure guarantees full marks on a 7-mark or 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Quality Management (TQM) Tools and Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management (TQM) Tools and Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Quality Management (TQM) Tools and Techniques** constitute a structured, statistically-grounded, and people-centric set of qualitative and quantitative instruments employed to identify, analyze, monitor, control, and continuously improve the quality of products, processes, and services across an entire organization. As per KTU 2024 syllabus, these tools are broadly classified into **Traditional (Old) Seven QC Tools**, **New Seven Management & Planning Tools**, and **Supplementary TQM Techniques** such as Benchmarking, FMEA, QFD, and Six Sigma.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, TQM tools are mapped to the following Course Outcomes:
- **CO3:** Apply quality management tools and techniques to analyze and improve manufacturing/service processes.
- **CO4:** Evaluate process capability and implement statistical process control in a production environment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Car Mechanic" Example**
> Imagine you take your car to a mechanic because it makes a strange noise. A good mechanic does not just guess; she uses a **checklist (check sheet)**, listens carefully to pinpoint the source (**cause-and-effect diagram**), checks if engine temperature is fluctuating beyond a normal range (**control chart**), and prioritizes the most common problems in cars her workshop sees (**Pareto chart**). TQM tools work exactly like this — they are the mechanic's diagnostic toolkit for any business process, helping managers "listen" to their processes, identify root causes, and ensure consistent quality rather than firefighting.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of TQM Tools

| Pillar | Focus | Example Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Statistical Tools** | Data-driven variation analysis | Control Charts, Histograms, Scatter Diagrams |
| **Problem-Solving Tools** | Root cause identification | Ishikawa, Pareto, 5-Why |
| **Planning & Management Tools** | Strategic quality planning | Affinity Diagram, QFD, Benchmarking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The traditional seven QC tools were pioneered by **Kaoru Ishikawa** of Japan, and the new seven tools were developed by the **Japanese Society for Quality Control (JSQC)** in the 1970s for management-level planning and decision-making.

### 1.4 Key Metrics and Constants

- **Process Capability Index ($C_p$):** Dimensionless number indicating process spread vs. tolerance spread.
- **Sigma Level ($\sigma$):** **6$\sigma$** = 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO).
- **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule):** Approximately **80%** of problems stem from **20%** of causes.
- **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL):** Maximum acceptable percent defective (typically **1%–4%**).
- **Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD):** Worst tolerable quality in a lot.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Traditional Seven QC Tools (Ishikawa's Tools)

These are the frontline, shop-floor tools used by operators and quality inspectors for **problem identification and resolution**.

#### 2.1.1 Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone)
- **Why:** To systematically identify the root causes of a problem (the "effect").
- **How:** Categories typically include the **6M**: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
- **Engineering Use:** Root cause analysis in defect reduction projects.

#### 2.1.2 Check Sheet
- **Why:** To collect data in a structured, real-time format at the location where data is generated.
- **How:** A simple form with tally marks for easy frequency counting.

#### 2.1.3 Control Chart (Shewhart Chart)
- **Why:** To distinguish **common cause variation** (inherent to the process) from **special cause variation** (assignable causes).
- **How:** Plot sample statistics over time with Upper Control Limit (UCL), Center Line (CL), and Lower Control Limit (LCL).

#### 2.1.4 Histogram
- **Why:** To visualize the frequency distribution of a dataset.
- **How:** Bars represent class intervals; reveals shape (normal, skewed, bimodal).

#### 2.1.5 Pareto Chart
- **Why:** To prioritize problems by frequency or cost impact.
- **How:** Bars in descending order with a cumulative percentage curve. Focus on the vital few (left side).

#### 2.1.6 Scatter Diagram
- **Why:** To investigate the relationship between two variables.
- **How:** Plot $(x, y)$ pairs to reveal correlation (positive, negative, or none).

#### 2.1.7 Flowchart (Stratification included)
- **Why:** To map a process step-by-step.
- **How:** Standardized symbols: Oval (Start/End), Rectangle (Process), Diamond (Decision).

### 2.2 The New Seven Management & Planning Tools

These are used at the **management level** for planning, organizing, and interpreting data — primarily for non-numerical, language-based information.

| # | Tool | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Affinity Diagram** | Grouping large numbers of ideas into natural categories |
| 2 | **Interrelationship Diagram** | Mapping complex cause-effect relationships among issues |
| 3 | **Tree Diagram** | Breaking down a goal into sub-tasks (hierarchical decomposition) |
| 4 | **Matrix Diagram** | Showing relationships between two or more groups of items |
| 5 | **Prioritization Matrix** | Ranking issues by weighted criteria |
| 6 | **PDPC (Process Decision Program Chart)** | Mapping contingencies and countermeasures in a plan |
| 7 | **Activity Network Diagram** | Sequencing and scheduling project tasks (similar to PERT/CPM) |

### 2.3 Supplementary TQM Techniques

- **Benchmarking:** Comparing processes/performance against industry best practices.
- **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis):** RPN = $S \times O \times D$ (Severity $\times$ Occurrence $\times$ Detection).
- **QFD (Quality Function Deployment):** The "House of Quality" translating customer needs (Whats) into technical requirements (Hows).
- **Six Sigma (DMAIC):** Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control.
- **5S:** Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.
- **Poka-Yoke:** Mistake-proofing devices in processes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for KTU 2024 ESE numerical problems on TQM tools.

| Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process Mean ($\bar{X}$)** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum X_i}{n}$ | Average of sample measurements | Same as $X$ |
| **Sample Std. Dev. ($s$)** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Variability within sample | Same as $X$ |
| **Range ($R$)** | $R = X_{max} - X_{min}$ | Spread of sample | Same as $X$ |
| **X-bar Control Limits** | $UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ \newline $LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ = grand mean; $A_2$ from control chart constants | Same as $X$ |
| **R-chart Limits** | $UCL = D_4 \bar{R}$ \newline $LCL = D_3 \bar{R}$ | $D_3, D_4$ from constants table | Same as $R$ |
| **p-chart Limits** | $UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ \newline $LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | For proportion defectives | Dimensionless |
| **c-chart Limits** | $UCL_c = \bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ \newline $LCL_c = \bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | For number of defects per unit | Defects |
| **Process Capability ($C_p$)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Spread-based capability | Dimensionless |
| **Process Capability ($C_{pk}$)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Centering-based capability | Dimensionless |
| **Six Sigma DPMO** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times N}$ | $D$ = defects, $U$ = units per opportunity, $N$ = sample size | Defects/Million |
| **Pareto Cumulative %** | $Cum\%_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j}{\sum f} \times 100$ | Cumulative percentage | Percent |
| **RPN (FMEA)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number | Unitless (1–1000) |
| **Quality Cost Ratio** | $CoQ = P + A + I + E$ | Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure | Currency |
| **Yield (Rolled)** | $Y_{rolled} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \dots \times Y_n$ | Cumulative process yield | Decimal |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Control charts monitor piston ring dimensions; FMEA identifies failure risks in airbag systems.
- **Software Industry:** Pareto charts prioritize bug categories; Fishbone diagrams analyze deployment failures.
- **Healthcare:** Check sheets track patient incidents; PDPC maps emergency response plans.
- **Manufacturing Cells:** 5S and Poka-Yoke ensure lean, mistake-proof workstations on KTU-relevant industrial visits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Worked Example: Control Chart Construction (X-bar and R Chart)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style 14-mark derivation):**
A manufacturing process produces shafts with a target diameter of **50.00 mm**. Five samples of size $n = 5$ are taken, and the sample means ($\bar{X}$) and ranges ($R$) are recorded as:

| Sample # | $\bar{X}$ (mm) | $R$ (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50.20 | 0.40 |
| 2 | 49.80 | 0.45 |
| 3 | 50.10 | 0.42 |
| 4 | 49.95 | 0.38 |
| 5 | 50.05 | 0.40 |

For $n = 5$, the standard control chart constants are:
$A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$.

**Required:** Construct the $\bar{X}$-chart and $R$-chart control limits, and comment on process stability.

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate the grand mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$) and average range ($\bar{R}$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \bar{X}_i}{k} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{50.20 + 49.80 + 50.10 + 49.95 + 50.05}{5} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{250.10}{5} \\[4pt]
&= 50.02 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{R} &= \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i}{k} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.40 + 0.45 + 0.42 + 0.38 + 0.40}{5} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2.05}{5} \\[4pt]
&= 0.41 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\bar{X}$-chart control limits using $A_2$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \, \bar{R} \\[4pt]
&= 50.02 + (0.577)(0.41) \\[4pt]
&= 50.02 + 0.2366 \\[4pt]
&= 50.2566 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \, \bar{R} \\[4pt]
&= 50.02 - 0.2366 \\[4pt]
&= 49.7834 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 50.02 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $R$-chart control limits using $D_3$ and $D_4$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= D_4 \, \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.41) = 0.8667 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
LCL_R &= D_3 \, \bar{R} = (0)(0.41) = 0.00 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
CL_R &= \bar{R} = 0.41 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (Stability Check).**

| Sample | $\bar{X}$ | Within $\bar{X}$-chart (49.78, 50.26)? | $R$ | Within $R$-chart (0.00, 0.87)? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50.20 | ✓ In-control | 0.40 | ✓ In-control |
| 2 | 49.80 | ✓ In-control | 0.45 | ✓ In-control |
| 3 | 50.10 | ✓ In-control | 0.42 | ✓ In-control |
| 4 | 49.95 | ✓ In-control | 0.38 | ✓ In-control |
| 5 | 50.05 | ✓ In-control | 0.40 | ✓ In-control |

**Conclusion:** All sample means and ranges fall within their respective 3$\sigma$ control limits. The process is **statistically stable** and exhibits only common cause variation. No special cause action is required.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Process Capability Index ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$)

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC machine produces bolts with specifications $USL = 25.05$ mm and $LSL = 24.95$ mm. After 50 measurements, the sample mean is $\bar{X} = 24.99$ mm and sample standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.02$ mm. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$, and interpret the results.

**Step 1: Compute $C_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.02} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.10}{0.12} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_{pk}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk(upper)} &= \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.05 - 24.99}{3 \times 0.02} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.06} = 1.00 \\[6pt]
C_{pk(lower)} &= \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{24.99 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.02} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.06} \approx 0.667 \\[6pt]
C_{pk} &= \min(1.00,\; 0.667) = 0.667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Since $C_p = 0.833 < 1.00$, the process spread is **wider than the tolerance** — not capable.
> - Since $C_{pk} \neq C_p$, the process is **off-center** (mean shifted toward LSL).
> - **Recommendation:** Reduce variation (e.g., machine maintenance) and re-center the process (adjust mean toward midpoint = 25.00 mm).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Six Sigma DPMO Calculation

**Problem:** A printed circuit board (PCB) assembly line produces 1,000 boards in one shift. Each board has 200 soldering opportunities. Inspection finds 75 defective solder joints across all boards. Calculate the DPMO and corresponding sigma level.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total opportunities} &= 1000 \times 200 = 200{,}000 \\[4pt]
DPMO &= \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times N} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{75 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1000 \times 200} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{75{,}000{,}000}{200{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 375 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}
$$

A DPMO of **375** corresponds to approximately a **4.5 $\sigma$** quality level (industry standard yield tables). The process is world-class but not yet Six Sigma.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pareto Chart Construction

**Problem:** Defect data from a stamping process is given below. Build a Pareto table and identify the vital few causes.

| Defect Type | Frequency ($f$) | Cumulative $f$ | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Burrs | 42 | 42 | 52.5% |
| Dimensional Out-of-spec | 18 | 60 | 75.0% |
| Surface Scratches | 12 | 72 | 90.0% |
| Wrong Material | 5 | 77 | 96.3% |
| Others | 3 | 80 | 100.0% |
| **Total** | **80** | — | — |

**Conclusion:** The first two categories (Burrs + Dimensional) account for **75%** of all defects — these are the **vital few** for corrective action.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Compliance

All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.

### 4.2 TQM Tools Classification — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TQM TOOLS AND TECHNIQUES] --> B[TRADITIONAL SEVEN QC TOOLS]
    A --> C[NEW SEVEN MANAGEMENT TOOLS]
```

### 4.3 DMAIC Six Sigma Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[DEFINE] --> P2[MEASURE]
    P2 --> P3[ANALYZE]
    P3 --> P4[IMPROVE]
    P4 --> P5[CONTROL]
    P5 --> P6[STANDARDIZED PROCESS]

    P1 --> P1T[CUSTOMER CTQ AND PROJECT SCOPE]
    P2 --> P2T[DATA COLLECTION AND BASELINE]
    P3 --> P3T[ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFICATION]
    P4 --> P4T[PILOT SOLUTION AND VALIDATION]

    style P6 fill:#90EE90
```

### 4.4 FMEA Risk Assessment Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[START FMEA FOR COMPONENT] --> I[IDENTIFY FAILURE MODES]
    I --> J[ASSIGN S SEVERITY 1 TO 10]
    J --> K[ASSIGN O OCCURRENCE 1 TO 10]
    K --> L[ASSIGN D DETECTION 1 TO 10]
    L --> M[CALCULATE RPN EQUALS S TIMES O TIMES D]
    M --> N{RPN GREATER THAN 100}
    N -->|YES| O[PRIORITIZE FOR CORRECTIVE ACTION]
    N -->|NO| P[MONITOR AND DOCUMENT]
    O --> Q[RE ASSIGN RPN AFTER ACTION]
    Q --> M
    P --> R[END FMEA]
```
