<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. List and briefly explain the Traditional Seven QC Tools introduced by Ishikawa.**
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

The Traditional Seven QC Tools (also called Ishikawa's Seven Tools) are:
1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** — identifies root causes of a problem grouped under 6M categories (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature).
2. **Check Sheet** — a simple structured form for real-time data collection using tally marks.
3. **Control Chart** — graphical tool distinguishing common from special cause variation using UCL, CL, and LCL.
4. **Histogram** — bar chart showing frequency distribution of a dataset.
5. **Pareto Chart** — bar chart with cumulative curve to prioritize the vital few problems (80/20 rule).
6. **Scatter Diagram** — $(x, y)$ plot to study correlation between two variables.
7. **Flowchart** — process map using standard symbols (oval, rectangle, diamond).

---

**Q2. Differentiate between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ in process capability analysis.**
*(Model Answer — 3 marks)*

| Parameter | $C_p$ (Process Capability) | $C_{pk}$ (Process Capability Index) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Measures process spread relative to specification width | Measures how well-centered the process is within specifications |
| **Formula** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\bar{X}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{X}-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ |
| **Considers centering?** | No | Yes |
| **Benchmark** | $C_p \geq 1.33$ acceptable | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ acceptable |
| **Use** | Spread assessment only | Combined spread + centering assessment |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question Paper Pattern]**

---

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q(A). (a)** Explain the FMEA methodology in detail with a suitable industrial example. Compute the Risk Priority Number (RPN) for the given failure modes and recommend corrective action. **(7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

| Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bearing seizure | 9 | 4 | 5 |
| Oil leakage | 7 | 6 | 4 |
| Shaft wear | 8 | 3 | 7 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition.** FMEA is a systematic, proactive method for evaluating a process or product to identify where and how it might fail, and to assess the relative impact of different failures.

**Step 2: Calculate RPN for each failure mode.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN_1 &= 9 \times 4 \times 5 = 180 \quad \text{(Bearing seizure)} \\[4pt]
RPN_2 &= 7 \times 6 \times 4 = 168 \quad \text{(Oil leakage)} \\[4pt]
RPN_3 &= 8 \times 3 \times 7 = 168 \quad \text{(Shaft wear)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Prioritize using RPN ranking.**

- All three RPN values are above the typical threshold of 100.
- The highest RPN is **Bearing seizure (180)** — highest priority for corrective action.
- A reduction in Occurrence (O) for bearing seizure through preventive maintenance scheduling is recommended.

**Step 4: Industrial Example.** In an automotive brake assembly plant, FMEA is applied to the disc brake sub-system. The failure mode "brake fade under high temperature" might have S=10, O=5, D=6 → RPN=300, triggering immediate material upgrade and thermal testing.

**Step 5: Recommend Action for highest RPN (Bearing seizure, RPN=180):**
- Install condition monitoring sensors (reduces D).
- Implement preventive lubrication schedule (reduces O).
- Redesign housing for better heat dissipation (reduces S over the long term).

---

**Q(A). (b)** A process produces components with USL = 12.00 mm and LSL = 11.90 mm. After data collection, the process mean is 11.94 mm and $\sigma = 0.015$ mm. Calculate $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Is the process capable? If not, suggest improvements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $C_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{12.00 - 11.90}{6 \times 0.015} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{0.10}{0.090} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $C_{pk}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk(upper)} &= \dfrac{12.00 - 11.94}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.045} = 1.333 \\[6pt]
C_{pk(lower)} &= \dfrac{11.94 - 11.90}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.045} = 0.889 \\[6pt]
C_{pk} &= \min(1.333,\; 0.889) = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

- $C_p = 1.111 < 1.33$ — process spread is borderline acceptable.
- $C_{pk} = 0.889 < C_p$ — process is **off-center**, shifted toward the LSL.
- **Not capable** under typical KTU/industry benchmark of $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.

**Step 4: Recommendations.**
- **Re-center the process:** Adjust mean from 11.94 mm toward 11.95 mm (midpoint).
- **Reduce variation:** Machine tool maintenance, tighter raw material specifications.
- **Statistical monitoring:** Implement $\bar{X}$ and $R$ control charts for ongoing surveillance.

---

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q(B). (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and interpretation of a Pareto Chart. The following data refers to types of defects in 200 batches produced. Identify the "vital few" categories. **(7 marks)**

| Defect Type | Frequency |
|---|---|
| Surface finish | 85 |
| Dimensional error | 60 |
| Hardness | 25 |
| Color mismatch | 18 |
| Others | 12 |
| **Total** | **200** |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition.** A Pareto Chart is a dual-axis graph combining a descending bar chart (frequency) with a cumulative percentage line. It is based on Vilfredo Pareto's 80/20 principle, which states that roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of causes.

**Step 2: Build the Pareto Table.**

| Defect | $f$ | Cumulative $f$ | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface finish | 85 | 85 | 42.5% |
| Dimensional error | 60 | 145 | 72.5% |
| Hardness | 25 | 170 | 85.0% |
| Color mismatch | 18 | 188 | 94.0% |
| Others | 12 | 200 | 100.0% |

**Step 3: Pareto Chart Sketch (described).**
- **X-axis:** Defect types (in descending order of frequency).
- **Left Y-axis:** Frequency (0 to 90).
- **Right Y-axis:** Cumulative percentage (0% to 100%).
- **Bars:** Decreasing in height from left to right.
- **Curve:** Monotonically increasing, passing through (Surface finish, 42.5%), (Dimensional error, 72.5%), (Hardness, 85.0%).

**Step 4: Identify Vital Few.**
The first two categories — **Surface finish (42.5%)** and **Dimensional error (72.5%)** — together account for **72.5%** of defects. Adding **Hardness (85.0%)** brings the cumulative to **85%**, which is the conventional vital few cut-off. Hence, the vital few are:
- Surface finish → 85
- Dimensional error → 60
- Hardness → 25

**Step 5: Conclusion.** Corrective action should focus first on **Surface finish** defects (highest frequency, highest cumulative impact), followed by Dimensional error, to achieve maximum quality improvement with minimum resource expenditure.

---

**Q(B). (b)** Explain the DMAIC methodology of Six Sigma in detail. State the role of each phase and list the statistical tools typically used in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition.** Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology focused on near-elimination of defects (3.4 DPMO at 6$\sigma$ level). DMAIC is the standard improvement roadmap: **D**efine, **M**easure, **A**nalyze, **I**mprove, **C**ontrol.

**Step 2: Phase-wise Role and Tools.**

| Phase | Purpose | Tools Used |
|---|---|---|
| **DEFINE** | Identify the problem, customer CTQs, project scope, and team charter | Voice of Customer (VOC), SIPOC diagram, Project Charter, Kano Model |
| **MEASURE** | Collect baseline data, validate measurement system, establish process capability | Control Charts, MSA (Gage R&R), Histogram, $\bar{X}$-R charts, Capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) |
| **ANALYZE** | Identify root causes of variation and defects | Pareto Chart, Fishbone Diagram, 5-Why Analysis, Scatter Diagram, Hypothesis Testing, Regression |
| **IMPROVE** | Develop, pilot, and implement solutions to address root causes | DOE (Design of Experiments), Poka-Yoke, Kaizen events, FMEA, Mistake Proofing |
| **CONTROL** | Standardize improvements, monitor sustainability, transfer ownership | SPC Control Charts, Control Plans, SOPs, Training, Visual Management |

**Step 3: Conclusion.** DMAIC is a closed-loop framework ensuring that improvements are **sustained** through statistical monitoring, not just one-time fixes. It directly links customer needs (Define) with engineering actions (Improve) and long-term control (Control).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 TQM Papers:**
> 1. **Control Chart Constant Confusion:** Students often apply $A_2$ to R-chart limits or $D_4$ to X-bar limits. **Always match the constant to the correct chart type** ($A_2$ for $\bar{X}$, $D_3/D_4$ for $R$, $d_2$ for $\sigma$-estimation).
> 2. **$C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$ Mix-up:** Many students compute only $C_p$ and conclude "capable." KTU examiners specifically test for off-center processes by comparing $C_{pk}$ to $C_p$. Always compute **both** and comment.
> 3. **Forgetting Units in UCL/LCL:** UCL and LCL must carry the same units as the variable being plotted (e.g., mm, kg, °C). Omitting units costs marks.
> 4. **Pareto Mis-sorting:** Categories must be arranged in **descending order of frequency** — alphabetical or arbitrary order loses full marks.
> 5. **FMEA RPN Threshold:** Always state a threshold (typically 100) and justify prioritization. Just listing RPN values without action plan loses the application marks.
> 6. **DPMO Formula Mistake:** Use $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times N}$ with $U$ = units per opportunity and $N$ = number of units, **not** total opportunities in the denominator.
> 7. **Six Sigma vs Six Sigma Quality Level:** Clearly distinguish the **methodology** (DMAIC) from the **quality level** (3.4 DPMO at 6$\sigma$).
> 8. **Fishbone Categories:** The 6M is a guideline, not a rule. Some industries use **4M** (Man, Machine, Material, Method). Mention the chosen categories explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — TQM Tools and Techniques (KTU 2024 Module 3)**

- **Traditional 7 QC Tools** = Ishikawa's frontline tools: Cause-and-Effect, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart.
- **New 7 Management Tools** = JSQC planning tools: Affinity, Interrelationship, Tree, Matrix, Prioritization Matrix, PDPC, Activity Network.
- **Control Chart Constants** depend only on sample size $n$. Memorize: $n=5 \Rightarrow A_2=0.577, D_3=0, D_4=2.114, d_2=2.326$.
- **Control Limits (3$\sigma$):** $\bar{X}$-chart: $UCL/LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} \pm A_2 \bar{R}$; $R$-chart: $UCL = D_4 \bar{R}$, $LCL = D_3 \bar{R}$.
- **p-chart** for variable sample sizes; **np-chart** for fixed $n$ and proportion defectives; **c-chart** for defects per unit; **u-chart** for defects per unit with variable area.
- **$C_p \geq 1.33$** = capable; $C_p = 1.00$ = process spread equals tolerance; $C_p < 1.00$ = not capable.
- **$C_{pk} < C_p$** indicates off-centering; difference quantifies the shift.
- **Pareto Principle (80/20):** Focus on the vital few (left bars) for maximum impact; trivial many on the right.
- **FMEA RPN** = $S \times O \times D$; each rating is 1–10; threshold for action is typically **RPN > 100**.
- **QFD House of Quality** translates customer "Whats" into engineering "Hows" using a relationship matrix; roof of the house shows correlation among technical requirements.
- **Six Sigma DPMO** = $\dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times N}$; **6$\sigma$ = 3.4 DPMO**.
- **DMAIC** = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control. Closed-loop methodology.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. Investment in **P** reduces **I+E** (the 1-10-100 rule).
- **5S** = Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain — foundation of Lean + TQM integration.
- **Poka-Yoke** = Mistake-proofing; uses contact, fixed-value, and motion-step methods.
- **Benchmarking** types: Internal, Competitive, Functional, Generic.
- **For KTU 2024:** Expect at least one **14-mark numerical** on control charts or process capability, and at least one **descriptive 14-mark** on FMEA/QFD/DMAIC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Worker Involvement Techniques (Quality Circles)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Worker Involvement Techniques (Quality Circles)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Worker Involvement Techniques (Quality Circles)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Quality Circle (QC)** is a small, voluntary group of workers, typically **6 to 12 employees**, who perform similar or related tasks within the same work area, and who meet regularly (usually once a week) to identify, analyze, and solve work-related quality, productivity, safety, and cost problems using elementary statistical and problem-solving tools. Quality Circles were formally institutionalized in Japan by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa** in **1962** and constitute a foundational pillar of the Japanese "people-centered" approach to Total Quality Management (TQM) and Lean Manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examination Standard):**
> "A Quality Circle is a group of workers doing the same or similar work, meeting regularly to identify, investigate, analyze, and solve quality, productivity, and other problems in their own work area, with the objectives of improving quality, productivity, and the work environment, while promoting the self-development of the workers."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Neighborhood Watch Council**
>
> Imagine a residential apartment complex where residents form a small, voluntary council. The council members (the Quality Circle members) all live in the same building (the same work area). Once a week, they gather in the community hall (the meeting room) to discuss everyday issues — a flickering streetlight, a leaking pipe, safety concerns in the playground. They don't outsource these problems to a distant city office; they study the cause (using simple checklists and charts), discuss solutions, and often implement fixes themselves. The city corporation (top management) only provides small budgets and recognizes their efforts. Over time, residents become more confident, skilled, and emotionally invested in their community.
>
> **This is exactly how a Quality Circle functions inside a factory.** Workers — who know the shop floor better than anyone — voluntarily gather to fix the problems they face every day, using simple analytical tools, with management acting as a sponsor rather than a dictator.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for KTU Exam Recall)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Quality Circle (QC)** | Small group of 6–12 workers from the same work area |
| **Facilitator** | A middle-management coordinator who guides the circle |
| **Leader (Chiku-Jyo)** | The worker elected to lead circle meetings |
| **Steering Committee** | Senior management body that approves and funds QC projects |
| **Self-Development (Jishu-Kaihatsu)** | Continuous personal growth of members |
| **Mutual Development (Sogo-Kaihatsu)** | Collective growth through group activity |
| **Total Participation Control (Kan-Gata-Seigyo)** | All employees engaged in quality control |
| **7 QC Tools** | The seven standard analytical tools used by QCs |
| **PDCA Cycle** | Plan-Do-Check-Act problem-solving framework |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | Money lost due to defects, rework, and waste |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU 14-mark answers, always begin with the formal Ishikawa definition (1962, 6–12 members, voluntary, same work area, regular meetings, quality tools). This is the most common opening sentence expected by board examiners.

### 1.4 Position in Lean Manufacturing (Module 4 Context)

Within the Lean Manufacturing framework, the **"People Pillar"** or **"Respect for Humanity"** (one of the **Two Pillars of the Toyota Production System**, the other being Just-in-Time) is operationalized primarily through structured worker involvement mechanisms. Quality Circles are the most historically significant and globally diffused technique under this pillar. They directly support the Lean principle of **"Empowering the workforce to solve problems"** and complement other techniques such as Kaizen Teams, Suggestion Systems, and Autonomous Work Groups.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin and Historical Evolution

| Year | Milestone |
|---|---|
| **1950** | W. Edwards Deming and J.M. Juran lecture Japanese engineers on quality |
| **1962** | **Kaoru Ishikawa** formally introduces the Quality Circle concept at the First QC Conference in Tokyo |
| **1962** | Japan's first registered Quality Circle is launched at the **Nippon Telegraph and Telephone (NTT)** plant |
| **1968** | National QC Circle Convention is held in Japan, attended by thousands of workers |
| **1974** | Lockeed Missiles & Space Company introduces QCs in the **USA** |
| **1978** | The **World's QC Circle Convention** is inaugurated |
| **1980s** | Spread to **India, UK, Mexico, Brazil, Korea, China** through JUSE and Asian Productivity Organization |
| **1990s–Present** | QCs are integrated with ISO 9000, Six Sigma, and Lean programs worldwide |

## 2.2 Structural Anatomy of a Quality Circle

A Quality Circle organization has **four distinct layers**:

### Layer 1: The Core Circle (Members)
- **6 to 12 frontline workers** from the same work area.
- **Voluntary** membership (not assigned by supervisor).
- Elect their own **Leader (Circle Leader)**.
- Members are **trained** in the **7 QC Tools**, basic statistics, group dynamics, and presentation skills.
- Meet for **~1 hour per week** during working hours (paid time).

### Layer 2: The Facilitator
- A **middle-level manager** (e.g., a Section Head or Junior Engineer).
- Acts as a **coach, mentor, and bridge** between the circle and senior management.
- Helps schedule meetings, arranges resources, and removes obstacles.
- Does **not** direct the circle; his/her role is supportive.

### Layer 3: The Coordinator
- A **senior manager** (often from HR or Quality Department).
- Coordinates **all circles** in the plant.
- Organizes training programs, presentations, and inter-circle competitions.
- Reports progress to the Steering Committee.

### Layer 4: The Steering Committee
- Comprises **top management** (Plant Head, Functional Heads).
- Reviews and **approves** QC projects.
- Provides **funding, resources, and authority** to implement solutions.
- Ensures alignment of QC activities with organizational goals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Layers:** **"M-F-C-S"** = **M**embers → **F**acilitator → **C**oordinator → **S**teering Committee. (Read from bottom of the org chart upward.)

## 2.3 Core Operating Principles (The QC Philosophy)

The Quality Circle philosophy rests on **Five Guiding Principles** rooted in the Japanese management thought:

1. **Respect for Humanity (Ningen Soncho)** — Every worker has dignity, intelligence, and the potential to contribute.
2. **Happiness and Prosperity of Workers** — A satisfied worker is a productive worker.
3. **Self-Management (Jishu Kanri)** — Workers manage their own improvement.
4. **Self-Development (Jishu Kaihatsu)** — Workers grow through circle activity.
5. **Total Participation (Sanka Kanri)** — Quality is everyone's responsibility, not just the Quality Department's.

## 2.4 The Problem-Solving Methodology (QC Standard Procedure)

A Quality Circle follows a structured **8-Step Problem-Solving Model** (often mapped to the **PDCA / Deming Wheel**):

| Step | Activity | Lean/PDCA Link |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Identify the Problem** (use Check Sheet, Brainstorming) | Plan |
| 2 | **Define the Problem** (clearly state in measurable terms) | Plan |
| 3 | **Analyze the Cause** (use 5-Why, Fishbone, Pareto) | Plan |
| 4 | **Develop Countermeasures** (brainstorm solutions, evaluate) | Plan |
| 5 | **Implement the Countermeasure** (small-scale trial first) | Do |
| 6 | **Check the Results** (compare before/after, verify effectiveness) | Check |
| 7 | **Standardize the Solution** (update SOP, train others) | Act |
| 8 | **Document and Present** (prepare QC Story for review) | Act |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The "QC Story" is a standardized **A3 / 8-step written narrative** that every circle must prepare when presenting its case study. Examiners frequently ask students to list these 8 steps in order.

## 2.5 The Seven QC Tools (7QC Tools)

These are the analytical instruments every Quality Circle member is trained to use. They were codified by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in his 1968 book *"What is Total Quality Control?"* and are the **7 New Tools** he added in 1976.

| # | Tool | Purpose | Visual Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa / Fishbone) | Identify root causes of a problem | Fishbone |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | Collect data in a structured format | Tabular |
| 3 | **Control Chart** (Shewhart) | Monitor process stability over time | Line graph with UCL/LCL |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Show frequency distribution of data | Bar chart |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Prioritize problems by 80/20 frequency | Sorted bar + cumulative line |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Examine correlation between two variables | XY plot |
| 7 | **Stratification / Flowchart** | Separate data by category / show process flow | Boxed flowchart |

> [!TIP]
> **7 New Tools (for advanced circles):** Affinity Diagram, Tree Diagram, Matrix Diagram, Matrix Data Analysis, Arrow Diagram (PERT/CPM), PDPC, and Relations Diagram. These are tested occasionally in KTU questions on QCs.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metrics Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While Quality Circles are not heavily quantitative, the following **measurable metrics** are often used in KTU questions to evaluate QC effectiveness. They are extremely high-yield for 14-mark numerical parts.

$$
\text{QC Participation Rate} \; (\%) = \frac{\text{Number of Employees in QCs}}{\text{Total Number of Employees in the Unit}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Problem Resolution Rate} \; (\%) = \frac{\text{Number of Problems Resolved Successfully}}{\text{Total Number of Problems Identified}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}
$$

$$
\text{QC Return on Investment (ROI)} = \frac{\text{Monetary Savings Generated by QC Projects} - \text{Cost of Operating QCs}}{\text{Cost of Operating QCs}} \times 100 \; \%
$$

$$
\text{Mean of Defects per Unit} \;\; (\bar{u}) = \frac{\text{Total Number of Defects in a Sample}}{\text{Number of Units Inspected}}
$$

$$
\text{Control Chart Limits (X-bar chart)} \quad
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R} \\
CL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Metric | Typical Target | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **QC Team Size** | 6 to 12 members | Optimal group size for participation |
| **Meeting Frequency** | 1 hour per week | Sustained engagement |
| **Project Duration** | 3 to 6 months | One project per cycle |
| **Training Hours per Member** | 30 to 40 hours/year | Skill building |
| **Participation Rate** | $\geq$ 50 % of workforce | Coverage indicator |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | 15–40 % of sales (typical pre-QC) | Quality cost reduction |
| **Savings-to-Cost Ratio** | 5 : 1 to 30 : 1 (typical in Japan) | ROI benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on LaTeX formatting:** All vertical bar symbols inside the table row (e.g., $\vert \text{Number} \vert$) have been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax, as per the KTU engine protocol.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Quality Circles in Engineering

| Industry | Typical QC Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive (Toyota, Maruti Suzuki)** | Reducing defect rates, improving assembly ergonomics |
| **Electronics (Samsung, Foxconn)** | Solder quality, line stoppage reduction |
| **Healthcare (Apollo, AIIMS)** | Reducing patient wait times, infection control |
| **Banking (ICICI, HDFC)** | Reducing transaction errors, customer service time |
| **Software (TCS, Infosys)** | Defect density reduction, faster sprint delivery |
| **Indian Manufacturing (BHEL, L&T)** | Productivity, safety, and inventory reduction |

## 2.8 Advantages and Limitations (High-Yield for KTU)

### Advantages
1. **Improves Quality** — Workers know defects best.
2. **Boosts Morale** — Recognition and participation increase job satisfaction.
3. **Cost Reduction** — Eliminating rework saves money.
4. **Communication Bridge** — Reduces the gap between workers and management.
5. **Skill Development** — Statistical thinking, leadership, and presentation skills.
6. **Taps Tacit Knowledge** — Surface undocumented know-how of the shop floor.
7. **Foundation for Kaizen** — Builds the continuous-improvement mindset.

### Limitations
1. **Time Overhead** — Weekly meetings may slow production if poorly managed.
2. **Slow for Radical Change** — Best for incremental, not breakthrough, improvements.
3. **Dependence on Management Support** — Without top management buy-in, circles die.
4. **Quality of Facilitator** — A weak facilitator can demotivate the entire circle.
5. **Cultural Resistance** — Difficult to implement in hierarchical or blame-based cultures.
6. **Scope Creep** — Circles may attempt problems outside their authority.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** When asked to "list advantages and limitations of QCs," do **not** mix up Quality Circles with Suggestion Systems. Suggestion systems are **individual**, monetary-incentive based, and unilateral; QCs are **group-based**, voluntary, and use analytical tools. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: QC ROI Calculation (Step-by-Step Derivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (KTU 14-mark style numerical problem):**
> A manufacturing unit employs **400 workers**. **160 workers** are active members of 20 Quality Circles. The total annual cost of operating all QCs (training, materials, facilitator time) is **₹ 2,00,000**. The QC projects generated documented savings of **₹ 12,00,000** in scrap reduction, **₹ 8,00,000** in downtime reduction, and **₹ 4,00,000** in productivity gains. Additionally, **80** problems were identified and **64** were successfully resolved during the year. Calculate the **Participation Rate**, **Total Savings**, **QC ROI**, and **Problem Resolution Rate**.

### Step 1: Compute the QC Participation Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Participation Rate} &= \frac{\text{Employees in QCs}}{\text{Total Employees}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{160}{400} \times 100 \\
&= 0.40 \times 100 \\
&= 40 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 40 % participation rate is **good** for a first-year QC program (benchmark: 30–50 % is considered healthy in Indian manufacturing).

### Step 2: Compute the Total Monetary Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Savings} &= \text{Scrap Savings} + \text{Downtime Savings} + \text{Productivity Gains} \\
&= 12{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 24{,}00{,}000 \;\; \text{INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the QC ROI

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{QC ROI} &= \frac{\text{Total Savings} - \text{Cost of Operating QCs}}{\text{Cost of Operating QCs}} \times 100 \; \% \\
&= \frac{24{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \; \% \\
&= \frac{22{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \; \% \\
&= 11 \times 100 \; \% \\
&= 1100 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The QC program is yielding **₹11 in net savings for every ₹1 spent** — a typical Japanese benchmark is 5:1 to 30:1, so this plant is performing very well.

### Step 4: Compute the Problem Resolution Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Problem Resolution Rate} &= \frac{\text{Problems Resolved}}{\text{Problems Identified}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{64}{80} \times 100 \\
&= 0.80 \times 100 \\
&= 80 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** An 80 % resolution rate is **excellent**; world-class benchmarks target $\geq$ 75 %.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Memory Aid:** Always compute Savings → Cost Ratio → ROI in that order. Examiners give partial credit for each step.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Control Chart Limit Derivation (for QC Monitoring)

A circle wants to monitor the **mean diameter of a turned shaft**. The grand mean of 25 samples is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.05$ mm, and the mean range is $\bar{R} = 0.20$ mm. For sample size $n = 5$, the standard **A₂ constant from the control chart table is 0.577**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R} \\
&= 50.05 + (0.577)(0.20) \\
&= 50.05 + 0.1154 \\
&= 50.1654 \;\; \text{mm} \\
\\
CL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} = 50.05 \;\; \text{mm} \\
\\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R} \\
&= 50.05 - (0.577)(0.20) \\
&= 50.05 - 0.1154 \\
&= 49.9346 \;\; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** If any sample mean falls outside $\vert 49.9346, \ 50.1654 \vert$, the process is **out of control** and the circle should investigate.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: QCs vs. Other Worker Involvement Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **extremely high-yield** for KTU Part B 14-mark "compare and contrast" questions.

| Dimension | **Quality Circle** | **Suggestion System** | **Kaizen Team** | **Autonomous Work Group (AWG)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Group Size** | 6–12 | Individual (1 person) | 3–10 | 8–30 |
| **Focus** | Quality + Productivity + Safety | Cost savings, ergonomics | Process improvement | Self-management of entire unit |
| **Frequency** | Weekly meetings | One-off submission | Project-based | Continuous |
| **Tools Used** | 7 QC Tools | Often none | Lean + QC tools | Full management authority |
| **Authority Level** | Recommend to management | Management decides | Implement within scope | Decide and execute |
| **Voluntariness** | Voluntary | Voluntary | Often assigned | Assigned |
| **Origin** | Japan, 1962 (Ishikawa) | USA, late 1800s | Japan, 1970s (Toyota) | Sweden (Volvo, Saab) |
| **Time Horizon** | Medium-term (3–6 months) | Short-term | Short to medium | Long-term |
| **Incentive** | Recognition, certificates | Monetary reward | Recognition, awards | Job enrichment |
| **Primary Benefit** | Skills + morale | Ideas flow | Quick wins | Ownership of process |

## 3.4 Failure-Mode Analysis: Why Quality Circles Fail (Root Cause Fishbone)

A **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa) Diagram** is the most commonly used tool by QCs. The **6M categories** are the standard branches:

| **6M Branch** | **Typical Failure Mode** |
|---|---|
| **Man (People)** | Lack of training, poor attitude, low literacy |
| **Machine** | Old equipment, frequent breakdowns, no maintenance |
| **Material** | Poor incoming quality, inconsistent raw materials |
| **Method** | No SOPs, complex procedures, unclear instructions |
| **Measurement** | Faulty gauges, no calibration, ambiguous specs |
| **Mother Nature (Environment)** | Poor lighting, noise, heat, dust |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip:** The 6M mnemonic is **"4M + Environment"** in older texts, and **"6M"** in modern Lean texts.

## 3.5 Implementation Roadmap (Step-by-Step Rollout Plan)

A typical Indian manufacturing plant follows this **8-step implementation ladder** when launching QCs:

1. **Top Management Commitment** — Plant Head signs the QC charter.
2. **Awareness Campaign** — Seminars on Ishikawa's philosophy for all employees.
3. **Train the Trainers** — Send senior staff to JUSE/QCFI (Quality Circle Forum of India) programs.
4. **Form the Steering Committee** — Include HR, Quality, and Production Heads.
5. **Pilot Launch** — Start with 2–3 circles in one department.
6. **Train Members in 7 QC Tools** — 30 to 40 hours of structured training.
7. **Identify First Projects** — Pick visible, winnable problems for early motivation.
8. **Scale-Up & Recognize** — Annual QC Conventions, trophies, and CEO awards.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Phrasing:** Examiners often award a 2-mark bonus if the answer mentions **"QCFI (Quality Circle Forum of India)"** as the national body that promotes QCs in India, founded in **1982**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define a Quality Circle. List any four characteristics of a Quality Circle. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Quality Circle (QC) is a small, voluntary group of **6 to 12 workers** from the same work area who meet regularly (typically weekly for one hour) to identify, analyze, and solve quality, productivity, safety, and cost-related problems in their own work area using simple statistical and analytical tools such as the **7 QC Tools**, under the guidance of a trained facilitator.

**Four Key Characteristics:**

1. **Voluntary membership** — Workers join by choice, not by order.
2. **Same work area** — Members perform similar or related tasks.
3. **Regular meetings** — Typically one hour per week during paid work time.
4. **Use of 7 QC Tools** — Brainstorming, Pareto, Fishbone, Check Sheet, Histogram, Scatter Diagram, and Control Charts.

*(Additional acceptable characteristics: Small group size, self-development focus, recommendation-only authority, recognition through presentations.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Differentiate between a Quality Circle and a Suggestion System. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | **Quality Circle** | **Suggestion System** |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Group activity (6–12 members) | Individual activity (single person) |
| **Voluntariness** | Voluntary; members self-nominate | Voluntary; anyone can submit |
| **Methodology** | Uses the 7 QC Tools and PDCA cycle | Usually no formal analytical tools |
| **Incentive** | Recognition, certificates, awards | Often monetary rewards |
| **Frequency** | Continuous (weekly meetings) | One-time submission per idea |
| **Decision Authority** | Recommends to management | Management alone decides |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 3-Mark Memory Aid:** Quality Circle = **Group + Tools + Recognition**; Suggestion System = **Individual + No Tools + Money**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **structure and organization** of a Quality Circle. Draw the **hierarchical diagram** and describe the roles of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **8-step problem-solving methodology** followed by Quality Circles. Explain how it maps to the **PDCA cycle**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — Structure of a Quality Circle (7 Marks)

A Quality Circle organization in a typical manufacturing unit has a **four-layer hierarchical structure**, designed to balance **frontline ownership** with **management support**.

**Layer 1 — The Core Circle (Members) [2 Marks]**
- **6 to 12 frontline workers** from the **same work area** (e.g., the welding section of an assembly line).
- Membership is **voluntary**; workers self-nominate.
- They elect their own **Circle Leader** (called the *Chiku-Jyo*).
- Members are trained in the **7 QC Tools**, basic statistics, brainstorming, and group dynamics.
- They meet for **1 hour per week** during paid work time, usually in a designated meeting room near the shop floor.

**Layer 2 — The Facilitator [2 Marks]**
- A **middle-level manager** (e.g., Junior Engineer or Section Supervisor).
- Acts as a **coach, mentor, and resource person** for one or more circles.
- Helps the circle in scheduling, arranging tools, securing data, and removing obstacles.
- **Does not command or direct** the circle — this is a fundamental rule.

**Layer 3 — The Coordinator [1 Mark]**
- A **senior manager** (often from HR or the Quality Department).
- **Coordinates all circles** in the plant.
- Organizes training programs, internal QC competitions, and inter-circle presentations.

**Layer 4 — The Steering Committee [2 Marks]**
- Comprises **top management** (Plant Head, Functional Heads, QC Coordinator).
- **Approves projects** that require capital expenditure or cross-functional cooperation.
- Provides **funding, recognition, and policy direction**.
- Conducts the **annual QC Convention** to recognize outstanding circles.

**Communication Flow [Bonus Point]:**
- **Upward:** Circle → Facilitator → Coordinator → Steering Committee.
- **Downward:** Steering Committee → Coordinator → Facilitator → Circle.
- **Lateral:** Circles share best practices through monthly inter-circle meetings.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 8-Step Methodology and PDCA Mapping (7 Marks)

The Quality Circle problem-solving process is a structured **8-step methodology** that aligns with the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle** introduced by Walter Shewhart and popularized by W. Edwards Deming.

| Step | Activity | Detailed Description | PDCA Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Identify the Problem** | Use a **Check Sheet** or brainstorming to find a specific, visible problem (e.g., high rejection rate in the morning shift). | **PLAN** |
| **2** | **Define the Problem** | Write a clear, measurable problem statement (e.g., "Rejection rate is 8% in the morning shift, vs. 3% in the evening shift, for the last 3 months"). | **PLAN** |
| **3** | **Analyze the Cause** | Use the **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** and **5-Why Analysis** to find root causes. Verify with a **Pareto Chart** to identify the vital few causes. | **PLAN** |
| **4** | **Develop Countermeasures** | Brainstorm possible solutions. Evaluate them on cost, feasibility, and impact. Select the best one. | **PLAN** |
| **5** | **Implement the Countermeasure** | Pilot the solution on a small scale (e.g., one machine for one week). Document the implementation process carefully. | **DO** |
| **6** | **Check the Results** | Collect **before** and **after** data. Plot a **Control Chart** or **Histogram** to verify the improvement. Compute savings in COPQ. | **CHECK** |
| **7** | **Standardize the Solution** | If results are positive, update the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)**, train all operators, and lock the improvement through **poka-yoke** (mistake-proofing) where possible. | **ACT** |
| **8** | **Document and Present** | Prepare the **QC Story** (A3 report) and present it to the Steering Committee. Submit for QC competitions and recognize the team. | **ACT** |

**Key Insight [1 Mark]:** The first four steps fall under **PLAN**, Step 5 is **DO**, Step 6 is **CHECK**, and Steps 7–8 are **ACT**. This tight mapping ensures the circle's work is never a one-off event but a continuous, **closed-loop improvement cycle**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Draw a small PDCA wheel in the margin of your answer sheet and annotate each step. This visual cue often fetches a **bonus mark** in KTU valuation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** What are the **Seven QC Tools**? Explain the **Pareto Chart** and the **Fishbone Diagram** in detail with suitable illustrations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and critical success factors** for implementing Quality Circles in an Indian manufacturing organization. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — The 7 QC Tools (7 Marks)

The **7 QC Tools** (also called the **"Old Seven Tools"**) were codified by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in 1968. They are the primary analytical instruments used by every Quality Circle.

**List of the 7 Tools [2 Marks]:**

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa Fishbone)
2. **Check Sheet**
3. **Control Chart** (Shewhart Chart)
4. **Histogram**
5. **Pareto Chart**
6. **Scatter Diagram**
7. **Flowchart** (or Stratification in some texts)

**Detailed Explanation of Pareto Chart [2.5 Marks]:**

The **Pareto Chart** is a bar chart with a superimposed cumulative percentage line, used to **prioritize problems** based on the **80/20 rule** (Pareto Principle), which states that roughly **80% of effects come from 20% of causes**.

- **Construction:** Defect categories are listed on the X-axis in **descending order of frequency**. The Y-axis (left) shows the frequency, and the Y-axis (right) shows the **cumulative percentage**.
- **Use in QCs:** Helps the circle decide **which problem to tackle first**. The tallest bar (left) is the "vital few" and the small bars on the right are the "trivial many."
- **Example:** A circle finds 50% of defects come from "welding porosity", 20% from "dimensional error", 15% from "paint peel-off", and 15% from "scratches". The circle targets welding porosity first.

**Detailed Explanation of Fishbone Diagram [2.5 Marks]:**

The **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone / Ishikawa) Diagram** is a structured brainstorming tool used to **identify the root causes** of a problem.

- **Structure:** The problem statement is written at the **head** (right end) of the fish. **6M branches** (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature) form the **ribs**.
- **Use in QCs:** After a problem is identified and prioritized (via Pareto), the circle uses the Fishbone to brainstorm **all possible causes** under each 6M category.
- **Refinement:** The 5-Why technique is then applied to the most likely causes to drill down to the **root cause**.
- **Example:** For "high rejection in morning shift", the circle lists "operator fatigue" under Man, "machine warm-up time" under Machine, "ambient humidity" under Mother Nature, and so on.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and CSFs (7 Marks)

**Advantages of Quality Circles [2 Marks]:**

1. **Improved Quality and Productivity** — Workers closest to the process fix problems fastest.
2. **Boost in Morale and Job Satisfaction** — Recognition and participation motivate workers.
3. **Cost Reduction** — Reduces COPQ (scrap, rework, warranty, downtime).
4. **Skill Development** — Members learn statistical tools, leadership, and presentation.
5. **Better Communication** — Bridges the gap between workers and management.
6. **Taps Tacit Knowledge** — Surfaces undocumented know-how of the shop floor.
7. **Foundation for Continuous Improvement** — Builds the Kaizen culture.

**Limitations of Quality Circles [2 Marks]:**

1. **Time Overhead** — Weekly meetings can interfere with production.
2. **Slow for Radical Innovation** — Suited to incremental, not breakthrough, improvements.
3. **Dependence on Management Support** — Withdraws, circles collapse.
4. **Cultural Resistance** — Hierarchical or blame-based organizations resist open discussion.
5. **Scope Creep** — Circles may attempt problems beyond their authority.
6. **Quality of Facilitator** — A weak facilitator demotivates the entire circle.

**Critical Success Factors (CSFs) for Indian Industry [3 Marks]:**

1. **Unwavering Top Management Commitment** — Plant Head must chair the Steering Committee.
2. **Strong Facilitator Training** — Send facilitators to **QCFI (Quality Circle Forum of India)** courses; membership in QCFI is a strong enabler.
3. **Voluntary Participation** — Never force membership; it kills intrinsic motivation.
4. **Real, Winnable First Projects** — Early success stories are essential to build momentum.
5. **Recognition Culture** — Annual QC Conventions, trophies, certificates, and "Best Circle" awards.
6. **Integration with Performance Appraisal** — QC participation should be valued, not penalized.
7. **Alignment with Lean Six Sigma / TQM** — QCs should not be an isolated program; they must feed into the larger improvement system.
8. **Adequate Resources** — Budget for data collection, training, and small capital projects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Quality Circles with Suggestion Systems** — They differ in *group vs. individual*, *tools vs. no tools*, and *recognition vs. money*.
> 2. **Omitting the name "Kaoru Ishikawa"** — Examiners explicitly test this. Always mention **Ishikawa (1962)** and **Japan** in the definition.
> 3. **Forgetting the size limit** — Always write **6 to 12 members**. Saying "small group" without a number loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Not listing the 6M categories in Fishbone** — Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
> 5. **Skipping the "voluntary" attribute** — Voluntary membership is the soul of QCs and is a high-frequency test point.
> 6. **Writing 7 QC Tools but missing one** — The most commonly forgotten tool is **Stratification/Flowchart**. Use a single line to list all 7 to avoid this.
> 7. **Confusing "Facilitator" with "Leader"** — The Facilitator is a manager; the Leader is a worker. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 8. **Writing the 8 steps out of order** — Memorize them in the **Identify → Define → Analyze → Develop → Implement → Check → Standardize → Document** sequence.
> 9. **Ignoring QCFI** — For Indian-context questions, mention **Quality Circle Forum of India (1982)** as the national body.
> 10. **Writing the PDCA mapping wrong** — Steps 1–4 = Plan, Step 5 = Do, Step 6 = Check, Steps 7–8 = Act. Many students incorrectly put Step 5 under Plan.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Read this 30 minutes before the KTU exam.**

### 1. Definition Snapshot
- **Quality Circle (QC):** A small, **voluntary** group of **6 to 12 workers** from the **same work area**, meeting **weekly for 1 hour**, to identify and solve **quality, productivity, safety, and cost** problems using **7 QC Tools**, under the **guidance of a facilitator**, with the **support of a steering committee**.

### 2. The One-Liner Origin
- **Kaoru Ishikawa, Japan, 1962** (formally launched at the **First QC Conference**, Tokyo).
- **JUSE** (Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers) is the parent body that promoted QCs.
- **QCFI (Quality Circle Forum of India)** was founded in **1982** to promote QCs in India.

### 3. The Four Layers (Mnemonic: M-F-C-S)
- **M**embers (6–12) → **F**acilitator (Middle Manager) → **C**oordinator (Senior Manager) → **S**teering Committee (Top Management).

### 4. The 7 QC Tools (Mnemonic: "**C**harts **C**an **H**elp **P**roblem **S**olving **F**inely")
- **C**heck Sheet, **C**ause-and-Effect (Fishbone), **C**ontrol Chart, **H**istogram, **P**areto, **S**catter Diagram, **F**lowchart.

### 5. The 8 QC Steps (Mnemonic: "**I** **D**o **A** **D**rill **I** **C**an **S**olve **D**efects")
- **I**dentify → **D**efine → **A**nalyze → **D**evelop → **I**mplement → **C**heck → **S**tandardize → **D**ocument.

### 6. The 6M Fishbone Branches
- **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature** (Environment).

### 7. The 5 Guiding Principles
- Respect for Humanity, Happiness of Workers, Self-Management, Self-Development, Total Participation.

### 8. Key Metrics
- **Participation Rate**, **Problem Resolution Rate**, **COPQ**, **QC ROI**, **Savings-to-Cost Ratio** (5:1 to 30:1 benchmark).
- **Control Chart Limits:** $UCL = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$, $LCL = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$.

### 9. Critical Success Factors
- Top management commitment → Trained facilitator → Voluntary membership → Winnable first projects → Recognition culture → QCFI affiliation → Lean/TQM integration.

### 10. Position in Lean Manufacturing
- QCs are the **"People Pillar"** of the Toyota Production System.
- They operationalize the Lean principle of **"Empowering workers to solve problems."**
- On the **empowerment spectrum**: Suggestion System (low) → Quality Circle (medium) → Kaizen Team (medium-high) → Autonomous Work Group (high).

### 11. One Killer Exam Line
> "Quality Circles are not a cost — they are an investment. Japanese plants report savings-to-cost ratios of 5:1 to 30:1, demonstrating that the wisdom of the frontline worker is the most under-utilized resource in any manufacturing organization."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suggestions Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suggestions Programs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Suggestion Program (SP)**: A formally structured, organization-wide management system designed to systematically solicit, collect, evaluate, recognize, and implement improvement ideas contributed voluntarily by employees across all hierarchical levels, in alignment with the organization's continuous improvement (Kaizen) philosophy.

In the context of **Lean Manufacturing** and the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**, the Suggestion Program is referred to as the **"Teian System"** (提案制度) — literally meaning "proposal system" in Japanese. It is one of the foundational pillars supporting **Kaizen (改善)**, which means "continuous, incremental improvement."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Suggestion Programs are studied under Japanese Management Philosophy. The KTU board examiner expects students to differentiate between Japanese Suggestion Schemes and Western Suggestion Systems, and to understand the role of SPs in sustaining a Lean culture.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large office building** where the company has installed a "Suggestion Box" at every floor. However, unlike a passive suggestion box where ideas are forgotten, the Suggestion Program is a **well-oiled machine** — like a **water treatment plant**:

| Stage | Real-World Analogy | Suggestion Program Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Water Source** | Rivers, lakes, rainfall | Employee ideas and observations |
| **Collection** | Reservoirs, pipelines | Submission forms, digital portals |
| **Filtration** | Treatment plants | Evaluation committee, screening |
| **Distribution** | Clean water to homes | Implementation in operations |
| **Recycling** | Water cycle | Recognition, learning, more ideas |

Just as a **single drop of water is valuable in a treatment plant**, **every idea — no matter how small** — is treated as a potential source of value in a Lean Suggestion Program.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** A Suggestion Program is the **"voice amplifier"** of frontline workers who actually *do* the work and *see* the waste (Muda) every day.

---

## 1.3 Historical Origin and Evolution

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1890s** | **Charles P. Steinmetz** at General Electric | First formal employee suggestion program |
| **1948** | **Quality Control Circles (QCCs)** in Japan | Japanese adaptation of suggestion schemes |
| **1950s** | **Toyota Adopts Teian System** | Embedded in TPS culture |
| **1970s–80s** | Expansion in Japanese Industry | Average 30–60 suggestions/employee/year |
| **1990s** | Global Adoption (Six Sigma, TQM) | Integration with Western quality systems |
| **2000s** | Digital Suggestion Platforms | Cloud-based, real-time submission |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: In Japan, the average is **30+ suggestions per employee per year**, with acceptance rates of **70–80%**, whereas Western programs historically average only **0.5–1 suggestion per employee per year**. This cultural difference is a common KTU exam question.

---

## 1.4 Objectives of Suggestion Programs

The principal objectives are:

1. **Harness Collective Intelligence** — Tap into the tacit knowledge of every worker.
2. **Drive Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Generate small, frequent improvements.
3. **Engage and Empower Employees** — Build ownership and morale.
4. **Eliminate Waste (Muda)** — Identify and remove the 7 (+1) wastes.
5. **Reduce Costs and Improve Quality** — Bottom-line impact.
6. **Foster Innovation Culture** — Build a learning organization.
7. **Improve Safety and Ergonomics** — Identify hazards proactively.
8. **Strengthen Communication** — Bridge gaps between management and shop floor.
9. **Standardize Best Practices** — Spread improvements horizontally.
10. **Build a Lean Culture** — Sustain Lean transformation long-term.

---

## 1.5 Categories / Types of Suggestions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Type**: "Classify the different types of suggestions in a Lean Suggestion Program with examples." *(Expected length: 5–6 marks)*

Suggestions are typically classified into the following categories:

### A. Based on Scope

| Type | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Individual Suggestion** | Submitted by a single employee | "Add a mirror at a blind corner" |
| **Group / Team Suggestion** | Submitted by a team or QCC | "Redesign the cell layout for 15% flow improvement" |
| **Cross-functional Suggestion** | Involves multiple departments | "Integrate supplier delivery schedule with production calendar" |

### B. Based on Subject Area

| Category | Focus | Typical % in Japanese firms |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Productivity / Efficiency** | Cycle time, throughput, motion | 25–30% |
| **Cost Reduction** | Material, labor, energy | 15–20% |
| **Quality Improvement** | Defects, rework, inspection | 15–20% |
| **Safety / Ergonomics** | Hazards, posture, PPE | 10–15% |
| **5S / Workplace Organization** | Cleanliness, order, visual | 10–15% |
| **Tooling / Equipment** | Jigs, fixtures, machines | 5–10% |
| **Environmental** | Waste, emissions, recycling | 5–10% |
| **Morale / Communication** | Suggestion system itself | <5% |

### C. Based on Impact

| Tier | Description | Reward Range (Typical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Minor** | Small, easily implemented | Certificate, small gift |
| **Moderate** | Departmental impact | Cash bonus (₹500–₹5,000) |
| **Major** | Plant-wide, measurable savings | Significant bonus, formal recognition |
| **Breakthrough** | Strategic, transformational | Trophy, award ceremony, promotion track |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Suggestion Program — Conceptual Framework

The Suggestion Program operates on **four pillars**, often called the **"Four Pillars of Teian"**:

1. **Participation (参加)** — Every employee participates, not just engineers.
2. **Equity (公平)** — Fair and transparent evaluation, regardless of seniority.
3. **Recognition (表彰)** — Prompt, sincere appreciation for every idea.
4. **Implementation (実施)** — The idea must be implemented, not just filed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these four pillars?** Because in a Lean culture, the **"respect for people"** (人を尊重する) principle of TPS demands that every voice is heard, fairly judged, appreciated, and acted upon.

---

## 2.2 Operational Process Flow — Step by Step

The Suggestion Program follows a structured **8-stage lifecycle**:

### Stage 1: Idea Generation
- Workers observe **Muda (waste)**, **Mura (unevenness)**, **Muri (overburden)** in their daily work.
- Triggers: Gemba walks, problem-solving sessions, 5S audits, A3 thinking.

### Stage 2: Submission
- The employee writes the idea on a **Suggestion Form** (paper or digital).
- Typical fields: *Problem, Current Condition, Proposed Solution, Expected Benefit, Category*.

### Stage 3: Initial Screening
- A **Suggestion Coordinator** (often an HR or CI officer) checks for completeness.
- Incomplete forms are returned with friendly guidance (NOT rejection).

### Stage 4: Technical Evaluation
- Routed to the relevant **department head or subject matter expert**.
- Evaluated on: *Feasibility, Cost, Benefit, Risk, Effort*.

### Stage 5: Approval / Rejection
- Decision communicated within a **defined SLA (e.g., 7–14 days)**.
- Reasons for rejection are documented and shared.

### Stage 6: Implementation
- Approved suggestions are piloted, standardized, and rolled out.
- Often handled via **PDCA** (Plan–Do–Check–Act).

### Stage 7: Recognition and Reward
- **Speed of recognition** is critical — KTU board favorite line: *"Delayed recognition is no recognition."*

### Stage 8: Feedback and Learning
- Results measured, savings tracked, lessons shared across the organization.

---

## 2.3 Reward Structures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Question**: "Discuss the reward system in Japanese Suggestion Programs. How does it differ from Western systems?"

| Reward Type | Japanese Approach | Western Approach |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Monetary** | Modest, symbolic (¥500–¥10,000) | Large cash bonuses (often profit-linked) |
| **Frequency** | Per suggestion | Per implemented idea only |
| **Non-Monetary** | Public praise, ceremony, badge | Certificates, newsletter mention |
| **Group Rewards** | Strong emphasis | Rare |
| **Intrinsic** | High — belonging, ownership | Often missing |
| **Focus** | Participation and effort | Outcome and ROI |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the exam**: **"PIG-ME"** — **P**articipation, **I**mplementation, **G**rowth, **M**orale, **E**ngagement are the *true* rewards in Japanese SPs.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Note**: When writing these formulas in the answer sheet, use **clear subscripts** (e.g., $S_R$, $A_R$, $I_R$) and **state units explicitly**. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

| # | Metric | Formula | Description | Unit / Norm |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Suggestion Rate** $\left(S_R\right)$ | $S_R = \dfrac{N_s}{N_e \cdot T}$ | Suggestions per employee per period | $\text{suggestions / employee / year}$ |
| 2 | **Acceptance Rate** $\left(A_R\right)$ | $A_R = \dfrac{N_a}{N_s} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of accepted suggestions | $\%$; Target: 70–90% |
| 3 | **Implementation Rate** $\left(I_R\right)$ | $I_R = \dfrac{N_i}{N_a} \times 100\%$ | % of accepted that are actually implemented | $\%$; Target: 80–100% |
| 4 | **Participation Rate** $\left(P_R\right)$ | $P_R = \dfrac{N_p}{N_e} \times 100\%$ | % of employees who submitted ≥1 idea | $\%$; Target: ≥60% |
| 5 | **Cost Savings per Suggestion** $\left(C_s\right)$ | $C_s = \dfrac{\sum \Delta C_i}{N_i}$ | Average ₹ saved per implemented idea | ₹ / suggestion |
| 6 | **Program ROI** | $ROI = \dfrac{\sum B_i - C_{prog}}{C_{prog}} \times 100\%$ | Return on program investment | $\%$; Target: >300% |
| 7 | **Net Benefit** | $NB = \sum B_i - C_{prog}$ | Total savings minus program cost | ₹ |
| 8 | **Average Reward per Suggestion** | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{N_s}$ | Mean reward paid per submission | ₹ / suggestion |
| 9 | **Idea Density** | $D = \dfrac{N_s}{A_{floor}}$ | Suggestions per unit floor area | $\text{suggestions / m}^2$ |
| 10 | **Response Time** | $T_r = t_{close} - t_{submit}$ | Time from submission to decision | days; Target: ≤14 |

**Where the variables mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \text{Total number of suggestions submitted} \\
N_e &= \text{Number of eligible employees} \\
T &= \text{Time period (usually 1 year)} \\
N_a &= \text{Number of accepted suggestions} \\
N_i &= \text{Number of implemented suggestions} \\
N_p &= \text{Number of participating employees} \\
\Delta C_i &= \text{Cost saving from idea } i \\
C_{prog} &= \text{Total program operating cost} \\
B_i &= \text{Benefit (monetary) from idea } i \\
A_{floor} &= \text{Total floor area of the plant in m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application

| Industry Sector | Application of Suggestion Programs |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Automotive** | Toyota averages 30+ ideas/employee/year; saved millions in Jidoka line redesigns |
| **Healthcare** | Nurses submit ergonomic suggestions reducing patient-handling injuries |
| **Software/IT** | DevOps teams submit process improvements (CI/CD pipeline optimization) |
| **Manufacturing SMEs** | Indian MSMEs use digital suggestion portals (Kaizen App) to crowdsource ideas |
| **Banking** | Branch staff suggest process simplifications reducing customer wait time |
| **Aviation** | Ground crew suggest turnaround-time reductions, improving on-time performance |

---

## 2.6 Critical Success Factors (CSF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"MICE-Q" Mnemonic** for CSF — easy to remember in the exam hall:
> - **M**anagement commitment
> - **I**mmediate feedback
> - **C**lear procedures
> - **E**quitability
> - **Q**uality of evaluation

If any of these fails, the entire program collapses.

---

## 2.7 Common Failure Modes (KTU Board Favorite)

| # | Failure Mode | Symptom | Root Cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Suggestion Fatigue** | Drop in submissions after initial spike | Lack of recognition / poor feedback |
| 2 | **Bureaucratic Bottleneck** | Long response times | Understaffed evaluation committee |
| 3 | **Cherry-picking** | Only "safe" ideas accepted | Risk-averse culture |
| 4 | **One-person Dominance** | Same employee submits all ideas | Group not engaged |
| 5 | **"Suggestion Box" Trap** | Box exists, nothing happens | No follow-through |
| 6 | **Punitive Reaction** | Employees fear speaking up | Blame culture |
| 7 | **No Linkage to KPIs** | Program seen as "extra" | No strategic integration |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculation of Suggestion Rate and Its Variants

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context**: A manufacturing plant has **500 employees**. In the calendar year 2024, the Suggestion Program received **3,200 suggestions**, of which **2,560 were accepted** and **2,304 were implemented**. The total cost savings reported from these implementations was **₹1,15,20,000** (One Crore Fifteen Lakhs Twenty Thousand). The program operating cost was **₹12,00,000**. Calculate all key Suggestion Program metrics.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Data

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_e &= 500 \text{ employees} \\
N_s &= 3{,}200 \text{ suggestions submitted} \\
N_a &= 2{,}560 \text{ suggestions accepted} \\
N_i &= 2{,}304 \text{ suggestions implemented} \\
\sum B_i &= \text{₹}1{,}15{,}20{,}000 \\
C_{prog} &= \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000 \\
T &= 1 \text{ year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Suggestion Rate $\left(S_R\right)$

We use the formula:

$$
S_R = \frac{N_s}{N_e \cdot T}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_R &= \frac{3{,}200}{500 \times 1} \\
S_R &= \frac{3{,}200}{500} \\
S_R &= 6.4 \text{ suggestions / employee / year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: Each employee submitted, on average, **6.4 ideas** in the year. While respectable, this is still **below the Japanese benchmark of 30+**.

---

### Step 3 — Calculate the Acceptance Rate $\left(A_R\right)$

Using the formula:

$$
A_R = \frac{N_a}{N_s} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_R &= \frac{2{,}560}{3{,}200} \times 100\% \\
A_R &= 0.80 \times 100\% \\
A_R &= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: **80% of all submitted ideas were accepted.** This is within the healthy Japanese benchmark range of 70–90%.

---

### Step 4 — Calculate the Implementation Rate $\left(I_R\right)$

Using the formula:

$$
I_R = \frac{N_i}{N_a} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_R &= \frac{2{,}304}{2{,}560} \times 100\% \\
I_R &= 0.90 \times 100\% \\
I_R &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: **90% of accepted ideas were actually implemented.** This is excellent and indicates strong follow-through.

---

### Step 5 — Calculate the Cost Savings per Implemented Suggestion $\left(C_s\right)$

$$
C_s = \frac{\sum B_i}{N_i}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_s &= \frac{1{,}15{,}20{,}000}{2{,}304} \\
C_s &= \text{₹}5{,}000 \text{ per implemented suggestion}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: On average, every implemented idea saved the company **₹5,000**.

---

### Step 6 — Calculate the Net Benefit $\left(NB\right)$

$$
NB = \sum B_i - C_{prog}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
NB &= 1{,}15{,}20{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 \\
NB &= \text{₹}1{,}03{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 7 — Calculate the Program ROI

$$
ROI = \frac{NB}{C_{prog}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI &= \frac{1{,}03{,}20{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
ROI &= 8.6 \times 100\% \\
ROI &= 860\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: For every **₹1 spent** on the program, the company gained **₹8.60 in net savings**, an **860% ROI** — a highly successful program.

---

### Step 8 — Analytical Summary Table

| Metric | Symbol | Calculated Value | Benchmark | Status |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Suggestion Rate | $S_R$ | 6.4 | 30+ (JP) | Needs improvement |
| Acceptance Rate | $A_R$ | 80% | 70–90% | Healthy |
| Implementation Rate | $I_R$ | 90% | 80–100% | Excellent |
| Net Benefit | $NB$ | ₹1,03,20,000 | — | Strong |
| Program ROI | $ROI$ | 860% | >300% | Excellent |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight**: When asked "Is the program successful?", answer using **all five metrics together**, not just one. A high ROI with low Suggestion Rate indicates the program is financially strong but **culturally weak**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bonus Calculation Model (Individual Reward)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context**: A company uses a **tiered bonus model** for suggestions. The tiers are: **₹500 for minor, ₹2,000 for moderate, ₹10,000 for major, and ₹50,000 for breakthrough**. In a given year: 1,200 minor, 800 moderate, 400 major, and 50 breakthrough suggestions were accepted. Calculate the total bonus payout and the **average reward per accepted suggestion**.

### Step 1 — List Tier Data

| Tier | Count $\left(N_k\right)$ | Reward $\left(R_k\right)$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Minor | 1,200 | ₹500 |
| Moderate | 800 | ₹2,000 |
| Major | 400 | ₹10,000 |
| Breakthrough | 50 | ₹50,000 |

### Step 2 — Total Bonus Payout Formula

$$
R_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{4} N_k \cdot R_k
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Each Tier Contribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minor:} \quad & 1{,}200 \times 500 = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Moderate:} \quad & 800 \times 2{,}000 = \text{₹}16{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Major:} \quad & 400 \times 10{,}000 = \text{₹}40{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Breakthrough:} \quad & 50 \times 50{,}000 = \text{₹}25{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum Total

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= 6{,}00{,}000 + 16{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 \\
R_{total} &= \text{₹}87{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Average Reward per Accepted Suggestion

Total accepted suggestions: $N_a = 1{,}200 + 800 + 400 + 50 = 2{,}450$.

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{R_{total}}{N_a} = \frac{87{,}00{,}000}{2{,}450} = \text{₹}3{,}551.02
$$

**Interpretation**: On average, **₹3,551** is paid per accepted suggestion — well within the typical Japanese range of modest, symbolic monetary rewards.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Implementation Cost Recovery Period

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context**: A suggestion requires a one-time implementation cost of **₹2,00,000** and is expected to generate **₹50,000 in monthly savings**. Find the **payback period** and the **net benefit over 12 months**.

### Step 1 — Payback Period Formula

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{C_{impl}}{S_{monthly}}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pb} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} \\
T_{pb} &= 4 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Net Benefit over 12 Months

$$
\begin{aligned}
NB_{12} &= (S_{monthly} \times 12) - C_{impl} \\
NB_{12} &= (50{,}000 \times 12) - 2{,}00{,}000 \\
NB_{12} &= 6{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 \\
NB_{12} &= \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: The suggestion pays back in **4 months** and yields a net benefit of **₹4,00,000** in the first year.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study (Japan vs West)

| Dimension | Japanese Approach (Toyota-style) | Western Approach (Traditional) | Lean Hybrid (Best of Both) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Focus** | Participation of all | Productivity gains | Balanced |
| **Reward Size** | Small, symbolic | Large, financial | Tiered |
| **Evaluation Speed** | 1–7 days | 1–3 months | 7–14 days |
| **Group vs Individual** | Group encouraged | Individual focused | Both |
| **Implementation %** | 80–100% | 10–30% | 70–90% |
| **Cultural Anchor** | Kaizen, respect for people | ROI, KPIs | Lean + KPIs |
| **Failure Tolerance** | High (learn from rejection) | Low (penalize miss) | Constructive |
| **Documentation** | A3 / simple forms | Detailed business cases | Tiered based on impact |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Suggestion Rate over Multiple Years (Trend Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context**: A plant tracks suggestion data for 5 years. Compute the year-on-year **growth rate** of suggestions and identify the trend.

| Year | Suggestions $\left(N_s\right)$ | Employees $\left(N_e\right)$ | $S_R$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2020 | 500 | 500 | 1.0 |
| 2021 | 900 | 500 | 1.8 |
| 2022 | 1,500 | 500 | 3.0 |
| 2023 | 2,400 | 500 | 4.8 |
| 2024 | 3,200 | 500 | 6.4 |

### Growth Rate Formula

$$
g_{n} = \frac{N_{s,n} - N_{s,n-1}}{N_{s,n-1}} \times 100\%
$$

### Year-wise Growth

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_{2021} &= \frac{900 - 500}{500} \times 100\% = 80\% \\
g_{2022} &= \frac{1500 - 900}{900} \times 100\% = 66.67\% \\
g_{2023} &= \frac{2400 - 1500}{1500} \times 100\% = 60\% \\
g_{2024} &= \frac{3200 - 2400}{2400} \times 100\% = 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Insight**: The growth rate is **decelerating** (80% → 33%) — an early sign of **suggestion fatigue**. Management must intervene with new campaigns, recognition events, or category expansion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
