Concurrent Engineering's "intervention" works at *every* stage by **pushing decisions leftward** — i.e., making them at the earliest possible stage to keep cost multipliers low. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1.** Define Concurrent Engineering. List any four benefits of adopting it. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** > *Concurrent Engineering (CE) is a systematic, multidisciplinary product development philosophy in which all phases of the product life cycle — design, manufacturing, assembly, quality, service, and disposal — are executed in parallel by a cross-functional Integrated Product Team, starting from the conceptual stage itself.* **Four benefits of CE:** 1. **Reduced Time-to-Market (TTM):** Parallel execution compresses the development cycle by 30–70%. 2. **Lower Development Cost:** Early consideration of downstream constraints minimizes rework, scrap, and engineering change orders. 3. **Improved Product Quality:** Issues are identified and resolved at the design stage (upstream) rather than during production or in the field. 4. **Enhanced Customer Satisfaction:** Continuous Voice-of-Customer integration via QFD ensures the product truly meets market needs. 5. *Bonus:* Better supplier integration and improved design reuse. --- #### **Question 2.** What is an Integrated Product Team (IPT)? Why is it called the "backbone" of Concurrent Engineering? **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > An **Integrated Product Team (IPT)** is a **cross-functional, co-located (or virtually co-located) group of experts** drawn from all disciplines involved in the product's life cycle — design, manufacturing, quality, service, supply chain, finance, and marketing — who work together *as a single empowered unit* on a product development project from concept to launch. It is called the *backbone* of CE because: 1. **It physically embodies parallelism** — every life-cycle function is represented simultaneously, not sequentially. 2. **It enables early downstream involvement**, ensuring that manufacturing, assembly, and service constraints are considered from Day 1. 3. **It replaces fragmented, over-the-wall handoffs** with continuous, multi-directional communication, breaking the silo mentality. 4. **It is empowered to make binding trade-off decisions** (e.g., cost vs. performance), accelerating the decision-making cycle. Without the IPT, the philosophy of CE cannot be operationally realized. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Examiner will offer an **internal choice** — you answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]` #### (a) Explain the traditional Sequential Engineering approach. Why is it considered inefficient in modern product development? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition of Sequential Engineering (2 marks):** Sequential Engineering, also called *Over-the-Wall Engineering* or *Traditional Waterfall Product Development*, is a linear approach in which each phase of the product life cycle is completed *fully* before the next phase begins. The output of one department is "thrown over the wall" to the next, with **no parallel activity and no structured feedback loop**. **Typical Sequence (2 marks):** $$\text{Marketing} \rightarrow \text{Concept Design} \rightarrow \text{Detailed Design} \rightarrow \text{Process Planning} \rightarrow \text{Manufacturing} \rightarrow \text{Assembly} \rightarrow \text{Quality} \rightarrow \text{Service} \rightarrow \text{Customer}$$ **Why it is inefficient (3 marks):** 1. **Late discovery of design flaws** — Manufacturing, service, and quality experts join too late; a defect not caught in design propagates and explodes in cost (cost-of-change multiplier 10×–1000×). 2. **Long time-to-market** — Each phase is gated; feedback is delayed and slow. 3. **Silo mentality and poor communication** — Departments optimize locally, not for the whole product. 4. **High rework and engineering change orders (ECOs)** — late changes are expensive and risk schedule slip. 5. **Customer voice is filtered** through the marketing department, often distorted. 6. **Knowledge is lost** at every handoff. #### (b) Describe the principles and key tools of Concurrent Engineering. How does CE eliminate the inefficiencies of sequential engineering? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Principles of CE (3 marks):** 1. Parallel execution of all life-cycle activities. 2. Early involvement of downstream (manufacturing, quality, service, supply chain) functions. 3. Cross-functional empowered Integrated Product Team. 4. Information integration via shared PLM/CAD/CAE database. 5. Customer focus throughout (QFD). 6. Robust design via DFM/DFA/DFT/DFSS. 7. Knowledge reuse and continuous learning. **Key Tools of CE (2 marks):** - **QFD (Quality Function Deployment):** Translates VoC into engineering specifications (House of Quality). - **DFM (Design for Manufacture):** Simplifies manufacturing. - **DFA (Design for Assembly — Boothroyd–Dewhurst):** Minimizes part count. - **DFMEA / PFMEA:** Identifies failure modes early. - **CAD/CAE/CAM + PLM:** Shared digital backbone. - **Stage-Gate Process:** Structured phase reviews. - **Digital Twin & Rapid Prototyping:** Validates design virtually and physically. **How CE eliminates sequential inefficiencies (2 marks):** - By **collapsing the time axis** through parallelism, it slashes TTM. - By **shifting decisions left** to the concept stage, it captures the cost-of-change curve at its lowest point. - By **integrating information** through PLM, it removes communication gaps. - By **empowering the IPT**, it makes trade-off decisions in real time, not after the fact. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]` #### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a Concurrent Engineering team. Discuss the role of at least six members in detail. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Team Structure (Diagram) — 2 marks:** ``` ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ TEAM LEADER / IPT │ │ PROJECT MANAGER │ └────────────┬────────────┘ │ ┌────────────┬───────────┼────────────┬────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ MARKETING DESIGN ENG. MFG. ENG. QUALITY ENG. SERVICE ENG. (VoC) (CAD/CAE) (DFM) (SPC, FMEA) (DFR) │ │ │ │ │ └────────────┴─────┬─────┴────────────┴────────────┘ │ ┌────────────┼────────────┐ │ │ │ SUPPLY CHAIN COST ENG. COMPLIANCE / REGULATORY (Sourcing) (Target Cost) (ISO, BIS, CE) ``` **Role of Six Members (5 marks — 1 mark each, ½ mark for crisp role + ½ mark for tool/method used):** 1. **Marketing / VoC Specialist** — Captures customer needs, runs surveys, builds the QFD House of Quality. 2. **Design Engineer** — Owns the CAD model, performance specs, and CAE simulations (FEA, CFD). 3. **Manufacturing Engineer** — Conducts **DFM** analysis, selects processes (casting, machining, sheet metal), defines tooling. 4. **Quality Engineer** — Performs **DFMEA/PFMEA**, plans inspection methods, and ensures Six-Sigma-level capability (Cpk ≥ 1.33). 5. **Service / After-Sales Engineer** — Applies **Design for Service (DFS)** and **Design for Disassembly (DFD)**, ensuring maintainability and recyclability. 6. **Supply Chain / Sourcing Engineer** — Co-designs components with suppliers, negotiates should-cost, and ensures supplier capability. 7. *(Bonus member)* **Cost Engineer** — Drives target costing and value engineering. The team is **empowered**, **co-located** (or virtually co-located), and reports through the IPT leader, not through functional silos. #### (b) Explain the Cost-of-Change Curve in Concurrent Engineering. A design change introduced at the concept stage costs ₹10,000. Estimate its cost at the design, prototype, production, and field stages using the exponential model with $k = 0.55$. Comment on the engineering significance. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Explanation of Cost-of-Change Curve (3 marks):** The Cost-of-Change Curve is a fundamental economic principle in product development. It states that **the cost of correcting or modifying a design rises exponentially with the project stage at which the change is introduced**. The intuition is simple: - At the **concept stage**, a change is a mere edit to a sketch or specification. - At the **design stage**, it requires re-running CAE simulations and re-releasing drawings. - At the **prototype stage**, tooling may have to be modified, prototypes re-built. - At the **production stage**, tooling changes, line stoppages, and re-qualification are needed. - In the **field**, recall costs, warranty claims, and brand damage dominate. The mathematical model is: $$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$$ **Numerical Computation (3 marks):** Given $C_0 = 10{,}000$ and $k = 0.55$, taking $t = 0$ (Concept), $t = 1$ (Design), $t = 2$ (Prototype), $t = 3$ (Production), $t = 4$ (Field): $$\begin{aligned} C_{\text{concept}} (t=0) &= 10{,}000 \cdot e^{0} = 10{,}000 \cdot 1.000 = \text{₹}10{,}000 \\ C_{\text{design}} (t=1) &= 10{,}000 \cdot e^{0.55} = 10{,}000 \cdot 1.733 = \text{₹}17{,}330 \\ C_{\text{prototype}} (t=2) &= 10{,}000 \cdot e^{1.10} = 10{,}000 \cdot 3.004 = \text{₹}30{,}040 \\ C_{\text{production}} (t=3) &= 10{,}000 \cdot e^{1.65} = 10{,}000 \cdot 5.207 = \text{₹}52{,}070 \\ C_{\text{field}} (t=4) &= 10{,}000 \cdot e^{2.20} = 10{,}000 \cdot 9.025 = \text{₹}90{,}250 \end{aligned}$$ **Tabular Summary:** | Stage | $t$ | Cost $C(t)$ | Multiplier vs. Concept | |-------|-----|--------------|--------------------------| | Concept | 0 | ₹10,000 | 1.0× | | Design | 1 | ₹17,330 | 1.73× | | Prototype | 2 | ₹30,040 | 3.00× | | Production | 3 | ₹52,070 | 5.21× | | Field Use | 4 | ₹90,250 | 9.03× | **Engineering Significance (1 mark):** A change costing ₹10,000 at the concept stage will cost over **₹90,000 in the field — nearly 9× more**. This single insight justifies Concurrent Engineering: by **pushing decisions leftward** (earlier in the project), CE captures design changes when they are cheapest, saving lakhs of rupees across a product portfolio. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 ways students LOSE marks on Concurrent Engineering questions:** > > 1. **Confusing CE with "Lean Production"** — CE is *front-end* (design phase), not the shop-floor 5S/Kanban. Many students write Kanban cards in a CE answer. ❌ > 2. **Writing "Concurrent Engineering = doing things fast"** — Wrong. It is about *parallel* execution with *cross-functional integration*. Speed is a *consequence*, not the definition. ❌ > 3. **Forgetting to mention cross-functional team / IPT** — A CE answer without "IPT" is incomplete. Examiners explicitly look for this keyword. ❌ > 4. **Listing tools without explaining their role** — Writing "QFD, DFM, DFA" is half-credit. You must say *what each tool does* and *how it supports CE*. ❌ > 5. **Skipping the cost-of-change calculation in numericals** — A common 14-mark question asks for a numerical estimate. If you only describe the curve qualitatively, expect 4–5 marks out of 7. Always show the exponential formula and plug in numbers. ❌ > > **Pro Tip:** Always end your CE answer with a crisp line: *"Concurrent Engineering prevents waste by design, while Lean removes waste in operations — together they form a complete excellence system."* Examiners love this integrative line. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Concurrent Engineering (CE) = Simultaneous Engineering = Integrated Product Development (IPD).** All three terms mean the same thing in KTU exams. - CE is a **product development philosophy**, not a production-floor tool (unlike 5S, Kanban, TPM, SMED). - The **single most important concept** in CE is the **Cost-of-Change Curve**: $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}$ — cost rises *exponentially* with project stage. - The **backbone of CE is the Integrated Product Team (IPT)** — cross-functional, co-located, empowered. - The **7 Principles of CE**: Parallelism, Early Downstream Involvement, Information Integration, CFT Empowerment, Customer Focus, Robust Design, Continuous Learning. - **Key tools of CE**: QFD (House of Quality), DFM, DFA (Boothroyd–Dewhurst), DFMEA/PFMEA, CAD/CAE/CAM/PLM, Digital Twin, Stage-Gate Process, Rapid Prototyping. - **DFA Boothroyd Rule**: A part is justified *only if* it (a) moves, (b) needs separation, or (c) requires a separate process. - **Sequential = Over-the-Wall, linear, late feedback.** **Concurrent = Parallel, integrated, early feedback.** Always contrast in tabular form. - **DLR (Development Lead Time Reduction)** is the key time-metric: $DLR = \frac{T_s - T_c}{T_s} \times 100\%$. - **CE prevents waste by design; Lean removes waste in operations** — they are *complementary*, not substitutes. - **Real-world adopters**: Boeing 787, Airbus A350, Tesla Model 3, Toyota, Apple, Ford. - **Exam keywords examiners scan for**: *cross-functional, parallel, integrated product team, upstream, cost-of-change, life-cycle, simultaneous, DFM, DFA, QFD, voice of customer*. If your answer contains at least 5 of these, expect full marks. - **Always include a diagram** (IPT structure or over-the-wall vs. CE comparison) — visually anchored answers score higher. - **Numerical favorite**: Given $C_0$ and $k$, compute $C(t)$ at multiple stages. Memorize $e^{0.5} \approx 1.65$, $e^{1.0} \approx 2.72$, $e^{1.5} \approx 4.48$, $e^{2.0} \approx 7.39$. - **KTU 2024 CO Mapping**: This topic directly serves **CO4** of PERUT413 (Apply lean concepts in product/process design) and **CO5** (Evaluate integration of lean with modern product development).

topics.title: Basics of Design of Experiments (DoE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT413 - LEAN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Design of Experiments (DoE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Design of Experiments (DoE)** is a systematic, statistically rigorous methodology used to plan, conduct, analyze, and interpret controlled tests in order to evaluate the causal relationships between multiple input variables (called **factors** or **independent variables**) and one or more measured output variables (called **responses** or **dependent variables**). In the context of Lean Manufacturing, DoE is a critical tool under the "Six Sigma" and "DMAIC" (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) frameworks that enables engineers and managers to identify the optimal set of process parameters that minimize variability, reduce defects, and maximize process capability.

Formally, DoE can be expressed as the mathematical mapping:

$$Y = f(X_1, X_2, X_3, \ldots, X_k) + \varepsilon$$

Where:
- $Y$ = the **response** (output/dependent variable)
- $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k$ = the **factors** (input/controllable variables)
- $f(\cdot)$ = the unknown functional relationship
- $\varepsilon$ = the **experimental error** (residual noise)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** DoE is a Module 4 topic bridging Lean Manufacturing with statistical process control. Students must understand that DoE is NOT the same as "trial and error" — it is a *planned* experiment that extracts maximum information from minimum runs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a **baker** trying to perfect a chocolate cake recipe. You suspect that three things matter: (1) oven temperature, (2) baking time, and (3) amount of sugar. If you change one thing at a time and bake 30 cakes over a month, you might find the "best" recipe — but it will take forever, and you won't know if two ingredients **interact** (e.g., maybe high sugar only works at low temperature).

**DoE is like a smart, parallel testing strategy**: instead of testing one variable at a time, you bake a *carefully selected* set of 8 or 9 cakes in a single afternoon, where each cake is made with a unique combination of all three variables chosen by a statistical pattern. From those 8–9 cakes, you can deduce the effect of each variable AND how they interact — a process called **interaction analysis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU-Industry Connection:** In Kerala's rubber processing, cashew, and electronics manufacturing industries, DoE is used to optimize curing times, roasting temperatures, and soldering profiles respectively — directly reducing the 7 classical wastes of Lean.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary in DoE

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Factor** | An independent variable controlled by the experimenter | Temperature, pressure, time |
| **Level** | The specific value a factor takes | 150°C, 180°C, 200°C |
| **Response** | The measured outcome (dependent variable) | Tensile strength, defect rate |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels | (180°C, 30 min, 1.5 kg) |
| **Replication** | Repeating the same treatment to estimate error | 3 runs of (180°C, 30 min) |
| **Block** | A grouping variable to control nuisance factors | Shift A, Shift B |
| **Noise Factor** | An uncontrollable variable affecting response | Humidity, raw material lot |
| **Main Effect** | The average effect of one factor across all levels | "Temperature changes yield by 5%" |
| **Interaction Effect** | The effect of one factor depends on another | "Sugar only matters at high temp" |
| **Experimental Unit** | The smallest entity receiving a treatment | A single product, a batch |
| **Confounding** | When two effects cannot be separated | Block effect masks factor effect |
| **Orthogonality** | Factors vary independently — no correlation | Standard feature of factorial designs |

### 1.4 Historical Context
- **Sir Ronald A. Fisher (1920s, Rothamsted Agricultural Station, UK):** Pioneered the statistical foundations of DoE while studying crop yields. He introduced **randomization, replication, and blocking** — the three foundational pillars.
- **Genichi Taguchi (1950s–1980s, Japan):** Developed **Taguchi Methods** emphasizing *quality loss function* and *robust design* — minimizing variation due to noise factors. Taguchi introduced **orthogonal arrays** for efficient fractional factorial experimentation.
- **George Box & K. Wilson (1951):** Introduced **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** for optimizing processes after the main effects are known.
> [!NOTE]
> Taguchi's work directly influenced Japanese manufacturing excellence (Toyota, Sony) and later, Six Sigma at Motorola, making DoE inseparable from modern Lean Six Sigma practice.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Principles of DoE

1. **Randomization**
   - The order of experimental runs is randomly determined.
   - **Why?** Prevents the effect of uncontrolled, time-related nuisance variables (e.g., machine warm-up, operator fatigue) from biasing the results.
   - **How?** Use random number tables or software-generated random sequences.

2. **Replication**
   - Each treatment combination is repeated multiple times (typically 2–5 times).
   - **Why?** Allows estimation of *experimental error* (pure error) and provides statistical power.
   - **How?** $n$ replications of $k$ factor levels in a $2^k$ factorial design yield $n \cdot 2^k$ total runs.

3. **Blocking**
   - Grouping homogeneous experimental units together to control known sources of variability.
   - **Why?** Removes the effect of a nuisance factor (e.g., raw material batch, day of the week) from the error term, increasing precision.
   - **How?** Each block contains a complete replicate of the design.

### 2.2 Comparison: OFAT vs. Factorial Approach

| Aspect | One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT) | Full Factorial DoE |
|--------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Variables changed per run | **1** | **All (k)** |
| Detects interactions? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Number of runs | High | $L^k$ (L = levels, k = factors) |
| Optimal for | Quick screening | Comprehensive modeling |
| Risk of false optimum | **High** | **Low** |
| Information gained per run | Low | High |
| Statistical efficiency | Poor | Excellent |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common exam question asks: *"Why is OFAT inefficient?"* The answer: it cannot detect **interaction effects** between factors, often leading to incorrect conclusions about the true optimum.

### 2.3 Classification of Experimental Designs

#### A. **Full Factorial Designs**
Every possible combination of factor levels is tested. For $k$ factors, each at $L$ levels:
$$\text{Total Runs} = L^k$$
- $2^k$ design: 2 levels per factor (most common, linear model).
- $3^k$ design: 3 levels per factor (can estimate quadratic curvature).

#### B. **Fractional Factorial Designs** ($2^{k-p}$)
A carefully selected subset (1/2, 1/4, etc.) of the full factorial:
$$\text{Runs} = 2^{k-p}$$
- The $2^{k-p}$ notation means a $1/2^p$ fraction of the full factorial.
- **Trade-off:** Fewer runs, but some effects are **confounded** (aliased) with others.

#### C. **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**
- **Central Composite Design (CCD)** and **Box-Behnken Design (BBD)** are the two most popular.
- Used to find the *optimal* operating point after important factors are identified.
- Models a second-order polynomial:
$$Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i X_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} X_i^2 + \sum_{i<j} \beta_{ij} X_i X_j + \varepsilon$$

#### D. **Taguchi Methods (Orthogonal Arrays)**
- Pre-designed matrices (e.g., L4, L8, L9, L16, L18, L27) that are robust, balanced, and orthogonal.
- Example: An **L9 orthogonal array** can study 4 factors at 3 levels each in just **9 runs**.

#### E. **Screening Designs**
- **Plackett-Burman designs**: very efficient for identifying the most influential factors among many (typically 7–31 factors in 12–32 runs).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Total runs in full factorial | $N = L^k$ | L = levels, k = factors |
| Total runs in fractional factorial | $N = 2^{k-p}$ | p = fraction index |
| Resolution of design | $R = \text{length of shortest word in defining relation}$ | Higher = better (R-III, R-IV, R-V) |
| Total Sum of Squares (SST) | $SST = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Total variability in data |
| Sum of Squares for Factor A (SSA) | $SSA = n \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{a} (\bar{Y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{Y})^2$ | Variability due to factor A |
| Sum of Squares for Error (SSE) | $SSE = SST - SSA - \text{other factors}$ | Unexplained variability |
| Mean Square (MS) | $MS = \frac{SS}{\text{Degrees of Freedom}}$ | Variance estimate |
| Degrees of Freedom (factor) | $df_A = a - 1$ | a = number of levels |
| F-statistic | $F = \frac{MS_{\text{Factor}}}{MS_{\text{Error}}}$ | Tests factor significance |
| Effect of Factor A | $E_A = \bar{Y}_{A+} - \bar{Y}_{A-}$ | Difference between level means |
| Sum of Squares for Interaction (AB) | $SS_{AB} = \sum (\bar{Y}_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y})^2$ | Measures interaction |
| Confidence Interval for mean | $\bar{Y} \pm t_{\alpha/2, df} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | s = std dev, t = t-distribution value |
| Replicates needed for power | $n = \frac{2 \cdot (z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}$ | Δ = detectable difference |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Taguchi) | $SN = -10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum Y_i^2 \right)$ | Smaller-is-better (default form) |
| | $SN = -10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\bar{Y}^2}{s^2} \right)$ | Larger-is-better (alternative) |
| Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \frac{s}{\bar{Y}} \times 100\%$ | Relative variability (in %) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Exam:** Never use the absolute value symbol `|x|` directly inside markdown table cells. Always write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking the table formatting.

### 2.5 Step-by-Step Methodology of a DoE Study (DMAIC Alignment)

1. **Define** the problem and response variable (e.g., "reduce defect rate").
2. **Identify** potential factors via brainstorming, fishbone diagrams, and FMEA.
3. **Plan** the experiment: choose design type, levels, ranges, replicates.
4. **Conduct** runs in randomized order; record data carefully.
5. **Analyze** using ANOVA, regression, and graphical tools (main effects plots, interaction plots, normal probability plots).
6. **Conclude** which factors significantly affect the response.
7. **Confirm** by running verification experiments at the optimal settings.
8. **Implement** the optimal settings and monitor (Control phase).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering
- **Manufacturing:** Optimize CNC machining parameters (cutting speed, feed, depth of cut) to minimize surface roughness.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Maximize yield of a chemical reaction by tuning temperature, pressure, pH, and catalyst concentration.
- **Electronics:** Optimize solder reflow profile (preheat, soak, peak temp, time) for defect-free joints.
- **Biomedical:** Determine drug formulation factors affecting tablet dissolution rate.
- **Lean Six Sigma:** Core tool in the "Improve" phase of DMAIC projects.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Two-Factor Full Factorial (2² Design)

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024-Style):**
An engineer at a rubber-moulding plant wants to study the effect of two factors on the tensile strength (in MPa) of a rubber product:
- Factor **A** = Curing Temperature (levels: $A_1 = 140°C$, $A_2 = 160°C$)
- Factor **B** = Curing Time (levels: $B_1 = 20$ min, $B_2 = 30$ min)

Each treatment is replicated **twice** (n = 2). The data table is:

| Run | Temperature (A) | Time (B) | Rep 1 | Rep 2 |
|-----|-----------------|----------|-------|-------|
| 1   | $A_1$ (140)     | $B_1$ (20) | 32    | 30    |
| 2   | $A_1$ (140)     | $B_2$ (30) | 38    | 36    |
| 3   | $A_2$ (160)     | $B_1$ (20) | 36    | 34    |
| 4   | $A_2$ (160)     | $B_2$ (30) | 44    | 42    |

**Find:** (a) The main effects of A and B, (b) The interaction effect AB, (c) The ANOVA table.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1: Calculate the Treatment Totals and Grand Total

| Treatment | A | B | Rep 1 | Rep 2 | Total $\bar{T}_{ij}$ | Mean $\bar{Y}_{ij}$ |
|-----------|---|---|-------|-------|----------------------|---------------------|
| (1)       | $A_1$ | $B_1$ | 32 | 30 | $T_{11} = 62$ | $\bar{Y}_{11} = 31$ |
| a         | $A_1$ | $B_2$ | 38 | 36 | $T_{12} = 74$ | $\bar{Y}_{12} = 37$ |
| b         | $A_2$ | $B_1$ | 36 | 34 | $T_{21} = 70$ | $\bar{Y}_{21} = 35$ |
| ab        | $A_2$ | $B_2$ | 44 | 42 | $T_{22} = 86$ | $\bar{Y}_{22} = 43$ |

Grand total:
$$\sum Y = 62 + 74 + 70 + 86 = 292$$

Grand mean (with $N = 8$ runs):
$$\bar{Y}_{\ldots} = \frac{292}{8} = 36.5$$

---

#### Step 2: Compute Main Effects

The main effect of a factor is the difference between the average response at its high level and at its low level.

**Main Effect of A (Temperature):**
$$A = \bar{Y}_{A_2} - \bar{Y}_{A_1}$$

Marginal mean of A at level $A_1$ (low):
$$\bar{Y}_{A_1} = \frac{T_{11} + T_{12}}{2n} = \frac{62 + 74}{4} = \frac{136}{4} = 34$$

Marginal mean of A at level $A_2$ (high):
$$\bar{Y}_{A_2} = \frac{T_{21} + T_{22}}{2n} = \frac{70 + 86}{4} = \frac{156}{4} = 39$$

Therefore:
$$A = 39 - 34 = 5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Interpretation:** Increasing temperature from 140°C to 160°C increases tensile strength by **5 MPa** (averaged over both times).

**Main Effect of B (Time):**
$$B = \bar{Y}_{B_2} - \bar{Y}_{B_1}$$

Marginal mean of B at level $B_1$ (low):
$$\bar{Y}_{B_1} = \frac{T_{11} + T_{21}}{2n} = \frac{62 + 70}{4} = \frac{132}{4} = 33$$

Marginal mean of B at level $B_2$ (high):
$$\bar{Y}_{B_2} = \frac{T_{12} + T_{22}}{2n} = \frac{74 + 86}{4} = \frac{160}{4} = 40$$

Therefore:
$$B = 40 - 33 = 7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Interpretation:** Increasing time from 20 min to 30 min increases tensile strength by **7 MPa** (averaged over both temperatures).

---

#### Step 3: Compute the Interaction Effect AB

The interaction effect is half the difference between the effect of A at $B_2$ and the effect of A at $B_1$:

$$\text{Effect of A at } B_1 = \bar{Y}_{21} - \bar{Y}_{11} = 35 - 31 = 4$$

$$\text{Effect of A at } B_2 = \bar{Y}_{22} - \bar{Y}_{12} = 43 - 37 = 6$$

Interaction effect:
$$AB = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (6 - 4) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Alternative direct formula:**
$$AB = \frac{ab + (1) - a - b}{2n}$$

Where (using standard Yates notation with totals $T$):
- $ab = T_{22} = 86$
- $(1) = T_{11} = 62$
- $a = T_{12} = 74$
- $b = T_{21} = 70$
- $n = 2$

Substituting:
$$AB = \frac{86 + 62 - 74 - 70}{2 \cdot 2} = \frac{148 - 144}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Interpretation:** The interaction is small (1 MPa), suggesting that the effect of temperature is **roughly similar** at both curing times. In other words, the lines of the interaction plot would be approximately parallel.

---

#### Step 4: Compute the Sum of Squares

**Total Sum of Squares (SST):**
$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - \bar{Y}_{\ldots})^2$$

Using the algebraic identity:
$$SST = \sum Y_i^2 - \frac{(\sum Y_i)^2}{N}$$

Calculate $\sum Y_i^2$:
$$\sum Y_i^2 = 32^2 + 30^2 + 38^2 + 36^2 + 36^2 + 34^2 + 44^2 + 42^2$$

Computing each square:
$$= 1024 + 900 + 1444 + 1296 + 1296 + 1156 + 1936 + 1764$$

$$\sum Y_i^2 = 10816$$

Correction factor:
$$CF = \frac{(292)^2}{8} = \frac{85264}{8} = 10658$$

Therefore:
$$SST = 10816 - 10658 = 158$$

---

**Sum of Squares for Factor A (SSA):**
$$SSA = \frac{(A_1 \text{ total} - A_2 \text{ total})^2}{2 \cdot 2 \cdot n}$$

Using the standard formula for a 2-level design:
$$SSA = \frac{(\text{Contrast}_A)^2}{N \cdot 2^{k-1}}$$

Wait — for a $2^2$ with 2 replicates, the simpler form is:
$$SSA = \frac{[\sum(\text{totals at } A_2) - \sum(\text{totals at } A_1)]^2}{N}$$

$A_2$ totals: $T_{21} + T_{22} = 70 + 86 = 156$
$A_1$ totals: $T_{11} + T_{12} = 62 + 74 = 136$

$$SSA = \frac{(156 - 136)^2}{8} = \frac{400}{8} = 50$$

**Sum of Squares for Factor B (SSB):**
$B_2$ totals: $T_{12} + T_{22} = 74 + 86 = 160$
$B_1$ totals: $T_{11} + T_{21} = 62 + 70 = 132$

$$SSB = \frac{(160 - 132)^2}{8} = \frac{784}{8} = 98$$

**Sum of Squares for Interaction AB (SSAB):**
$$SSAB = \frac{(ab + (1) - a - b)^2}{N}$$

$$SSAB = \frac{(86 + 62 - 74 - 70)^2}{8} = \frac{(4)^2}{8} = \frac{16}{8} = 2$$

**Sum of Squares for Error (SSE):**
$$SSE = SST - SSA - SSB - SSAB$$

$$SSE = 158 - 50 - 98 - 2 = 8$$

---

#### Step 5: Degrees of Freedom

| Source | $df$ |
|--------|------|
| A | $2 - 1 = 1$ |
| B | $2 - 1 = 1$ |
| AB | $(2-1)(2-1) = 1$ |
| Error | $N - ab = 8 - 4 = 4$ |
| **Total** | $N - 1 = 7$ |

---

#### Step 6: Complete the ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | F-crit (α=0.05) |
|--------|------|----|------|------|------|
| A (Temp) | 50 | 1 | 50 | 25.00 | $F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$ |
| B (Time) | 98 | 1 | 98 | 49.00 | $F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$ |
| AB | 2 | 1 | 2 | 1.00 | $F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$ |
| Error | 8 | 4 | 2 | — | — |
| **Total** | 158 | 7 | — | — | — |

---

#### Step 7: Conclusions

Since $F_A = 25.00 > 7.71$ and $F_B = 49.00 > 7.71$, both **Temperature and Time are highly significant** at $\alpha = 0.05$.

Since $F_{AB} = 1.00 < 7.71$, the **interaction AB is NOT significant** — the two factors act independently.

**Practical recommendation:** Operate at $A_2$ (160°C) and $B_2$ (30 min) to maximize tensile strength.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The DoE Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Define Problem and Response Y] --> A1[Identify Factors X1 to Xk]
    A1 --> A2[Choose Design Type]
    A2 --> A3[Determine Number of Runs N]
    A3 --> A4[Randomize Run Order]
    A4 --> A5[Conduct Experimental Runs]
    A5 --> A6[Collect Response Data]
    A6 --> A7[Compute Main and Interaction Effects]
    A7 --> A8[Perform ANOVA Analysis]
    A8 --> A9{All Factors Significant?}
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[Identify Optimal Settings]
    A9 -- No --> A11[Refine Model or Remove Insignificant Factors]
    A11 --> A10
    A10 --> A12[Run Confirmation Experiment]
    A12 --> A13[Implement and Control in Production]
```

### 4.2 Classification of DoE Designs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DOE[Design of Experiments] --> FF[Full Factorial]
    DOE --> FracF[Fractional Factorial]
    DOE --> RSM[Response Surface Methodology]
    DOE --> Tag[Taguchi Orthogonal Arrays]
    DOE --> Scr[Screening Designs]

    FF --> FF2[2 to the k Design]
    FF --> FF3[3 to the k Design]

    FracF --> FracF1[2 raised to k minus p]
    FracF1 --> FracF2[Resolution III IV V]

    RSM --> RSM1[Central Composite]
    RSM --> RSM2[Box Behnken]

    Tag --> Tag1[L4 L8 L9 L16 L18 L27]

    Scr --> Scr1[Plackett Burman]
```

### 4.3 The Three Pillars of DoE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Three Principles of DoE] --> P1[Randomization]
    P --> P2[Replication]
    P --> P3[Blocking]

    P1 --> P1A[Eliminates Time Based Bias]
    P1A --> P1B[Order of Runs is Randomized]

    P2 --> P2A[Provides Estimate of Error]
    P2A --> P2B[Increases Statistical Power]

    P3 --> P3A[Controls Nuisance Variables]
    P3A --> P3B[Homogeneous Groups of Experimental Units]
```

### 4.4 OFAT vs. Factorial Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OFAT[One Factor at a Time Approach]
        direction LR
        O1[Fix B and C at baseline]
        O1 --> O2[Vary A across all levels]
        O2 --> O3[Record Response Y]
        O3 --> O4[Reset A to baseline]
        O4 --> O5[Vary B across all levels]
        O5 --> O6[Vary C across all levels]
    end

    subgraph FAC[Factorial Approach]
        direction LR
        F1[Plan all combinations in one matrix]
        F1 --> F2[Run all combinations in randomized order]
        F2 --> F3[Analyze main and interaction effects together]
    end

    OFAT -. Misses Interactions .-> X1[False Optimum Risk]
    FAC -. Captures All Effects .-> X2[True Optimum with Statistical Confidence]
```

### 4.5 ANOVA Logic Flow (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Compute F statistic] --> Q2{F greater than F critical}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Factor is Significant]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Factor is NOT Significant]
    Q3 --> Q5[Compute Effect Size]
    Q5 --> Q6[Check Practical Significance]
    Q4 --> Q7[Consider Pooling into Error]
    Q7 --> Q8[Refit Reduced Model]
    Q6 --> Q9[Identify Optimal Setting]
    Q8 --> Q9
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define Design of Experiments (DoE). List any FOUR principles of DoE.*

**Model Answer:**
Design of Experiments (DoE) is a structured, statistically grounded methodology for planning experiments so that valid and objective conclusions can be drawn efficiently. It involves selecting the levels of input factors, running experiments in a randomized manner, and analyzing the results to determine the effects of factors and their interactions on one or more response variables.

**Four Principles of DoE:**
1. **Randomization** – Running experimental trials in random order to neutralize time-related bias.
2. **Replication** – Repeating each treatment multiple times to obtain an estimate of experimental error.
3. **Blocking** – Grouping experimental units into homogeneous blocks to remove the effect of nuisance variables.
4. **Orthogonality** – Designing factor combinations such that the factors vary independently of one another, enabling clean estimation of main effects.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Differentiate between One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT) and Full Factorial experimental approaches.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | OFAT | Full Factorial |
|--------|------|----------------|
| Variables varied per run | One factor at a time | All factors simultaneously |
| Number of runs | High | $L^k$ (efficient) |
| Detects interactions | No | Yes |
| Risk of false optimum | High | Low |
| Statistical rigor | Low | High |
| Information per run | Less | More |

OFAT cannot detect interaction effects between factors, often leading to incorrect conclusions about the process optimum. Full factorial designs are more efficient because every run contributes to estimating multiple effects.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions for a 14-mark module question typically carry **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** sub-parts. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the THREE fundamental principles of DoE with suitable examples. Why is randomization particularly important in industrial experiments?

**(b) [7 Marks)** A $2^2$ full factorial experiment is conducted to study the effect of Feed Rate (A: 0.2 mm/rev low, 0.4 mm/rev high) and Cutting Speed (B: 100 m/min low, 200 m/min high) on Surface Roughness (Ra, in µm). Each treatment is replicated **3 times**. The data are:

| Run | A | B | R1 | R2 | R3 |
|-----|---|---|----|----|----|
| 1 | $A_1$ | $B_1$ | 1.2 | 1.4 | 1.3 |
| 2 | $A_1$ | $B_2$ | 1.0 | 0.9 | 1.1 |
| 3 | $A_2$ | $B_1$ | 1.8 | 1.7 | 1.9 |
| 4 | $A_2$ | $B_2$ | 0.7 | 0.8 | 0.6 |

**Calculate:** (i) The main effects of A and B, (ii) The interaction effect AB, (iii) The complete ANOVA table and state your conclusions at $\alpha = 0.05$. [Given $F_{0.05, 1, 8} = 5.32$]

---

**MODEL SOLUTION for Question A:**

**(a) [7 Marks] The Three Principles of DoE:**

**1. Randomization:**
- Definition: All experimental runs are performed in a *randomly determined* order, not the order in which they are written down.
- Example: In a CNC machining experiment, the four treatments (A1B1, A1B2, A2B1, A2B2) should be run in a randomized sequence such as A2B2 → A1B1 → A2B1 → A1B2, decided by a random number table.
- Importance in industry: Randomization neutralizes the influence of *time-related nuisance variables* such as tool wear progression, ambient temperature drift, coolant depletion, and operator fatigue. If we run all "low" settings first and then "high" settings, any drift in tool condition would be wrongly attributed to the factor.

**2. Replication:**
- Definition: Each treatment combination is repeated independently multiple times (n times).
- Example: Running each of the 4 treatments of the $2^2$ design 3 times = 12 total runs.
- Importance: Replication provides:
  - An *estimate of experimental error* (pure error variance).
  - Increased *precision* of factor effect estimates (standard error decreases as $\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$).
  - Increased *statistical power* to detect small but practically important effects.
  - The degrees of freedom needed to perform ANOVA.

**3. Blocking:**
- Definition: A technique for dealing with *known* nuisance factors (e.g., raw material lot, machine, day, shift) by grouping homogeneous experimental units into blocks.
- Example: Suppose a 16-run experiment must be done across 2 days. We could block the 16 runs into two blocks of 8 each. The block effect is then estimated and removed from the error term.
- Importance: Blocking improves precision by removing the variability due to the blocking factor from the error, making the experiment more sensitive to the factors of interest.

> **Why is randomization particularly important in industrial experiments?**
> In a factory environment, uncontrolled variables (humidity, machine warm-up, batch variation in raw material, operator skill) change with time. Without randomization, these *systematic* changes get *aliased* with the treatment effects, leading to biased estimates. Randomization ensures that the noise is *averaged out* across all treatments, so the *only* systematic difference between treatments is the deliberate change in factor levels.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Treatment Totals and Grand Total**

| Run | A | B | R1 | R2 | R3 | Total $T_{ij}$ | Mean $\bar{Y}_{ij}$ |
|-----|---|---|----|----|----|----------------|---------------------|
| 1   | $A_1$ | $B_1$ | 1.2 | 1.4 | 1.3 | 3.9 | 1.300 |
| 2   | $A_1$ | $B_2$ | 1.0 | 0.9 | 1.1 | 3.0 | 1.000 |
| 3   | $A_2$ | $B_1$ | 1.8 | 1.7 | 1.9 | 5.4 | 1.800 |
| 4   | $A_2$ | $B_2$ | 0.7 | 0.8 | 0.6 | 2.1 | 0.700 |

Grand total:
$$\sum Y = 3.9 + 3.0 + 5.4 + 2.1 = 14.4$$

Grand mean (N = 12):
$$\bar{Y} = \frac{14.4}{12} = 1.2$$

**Step 2: Compute Main Effects**

Marginal mean of A at $A_1$:
$$\bar{Y}_{A_1} = \frac{3.9 + 3.0}{6} = \frac{6.9}{6} = 1.15$$

Marginal mean of A at $A_2$:
$$\bar{Y}_{A_2} = \frac{5.4 + 2.1}{6} = \frac{7.5}{6} = 1.25$$

**Main effect of A:**
$$A = \bar{Y}_{A_2} - \bar{Y}_{A_1} = 1.25 - 1.15 = +0.10 \text{ µm}$$

Marginal mean of B at $B_1$:
$$\bar{Y}_{B_1} = \frac{3.9 + 5.4}{6} = \frac{9.3}{6} = 1.55$$

Marginal mean of B at $B_2$:
$$\bar{Y}_{B_2} = \frac{3.0 + 2.1}{6} = \frac{5.1}{6} = 0.85$$

**Main effect of B:**
$$B = \bar{Y}_{B_2} - \bar{Y}_{B_1} = 0.85 - 1.55 = -0.70 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 3: Compute Interaction Effect AB**

Using:
$$AB = \frac{(ab + (1)) - (a) - (b)}{2n}$$

Where (using totals): $ab = 2.1$, $(1) = 3.9$, $a = 3.0$, $b = 5.4$, $n = 3$.

$$AB = \frac{(2.1 + 3.9) - 3.0 - 5.4}{2 \cdot 3} = \frac{6.0 - 8.4}{6} = \frac{-2.4}{6} = -0.40 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 4: Compute Sum of Squares**

$$\sum Y_i^2 = 1.2^2 + 1.4^2 + 1.3^2 + 1.0^2 + 0.9^2 + 1.1^2 + 1.8^2 + 1.7^2 + 1.9^2 + 0.7^2 + 0.8^2 + 0.6^2$$

$$= 1.44 + 1.96 + 1.69 + 1.00 + 0.81 + 1.21 + 3.24 + 2.89 + 3.61 + 0.49 + 0.64 + 0.36 = 19.34$$

Correction factor:
$$CF = \frac{(14.4)^2}{12} = \frac{207.36}{12} = 17.28$$

Total SS:
$$SST = 19.34 - 17.28 = 2.06$$

SS for A:
$$SSA = \frac{(7.5 - 6.9)^2}{12} = \frac{0.36}{12} = 0.030$$

SS for B:
$$SSB = \frac{(5.1 - 9.3)^2}{12} = \frac{(-4.2)^2}{12} = \frac{17.64}{12} = 1.470$$

SS for AB:
$$SSAB = \frac{(-2.4)^2}{12} = \frac{5.76}{12} = 0.480$$

SS Error:
$$SSE = SST - SSA - SSB - SSAB = 2.06 - 0.030 - 1.470 - 0.480 = 0.080$$

**Step 5: ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | F-crit (α=0.05) | Conclusion |
|--------|------|----|----|------|------|------------|
| A (Feed) | 0.030 | 1 | 0.030 | $\frac{0.030}{0.010} = 3.00$ | 5.32 | Not significant |
| B (Speed) | 1.470 | 1 | 1.470 | $\frac{1.470}{0.010} = 147.00$ | 5.32 | Highly significant |
| AB | 0.480 | 1 | 0.480 | $\frac{0.480}{0.010} = 48.00$ | 5.32 | Highly significant |
| Error | 0.080 | 8 | 0.010 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | 2.060 | 11 | — | — | — | — |

**Step 6: Conclusions**
- Factor **A (Feed Rate) alone is NOT significant** ($F_A = 3.00 < 5.32$).
- Factor **B (Cutting Speed) is highly significant** ($F_B = 147.00 \gg 5.32$).
- The **AB interaction is highly significant** ($F_{AB} = 48.00 \gg 5.32$).

The presence of a significant interaction means we cannot interpret the main effects in isolation. Specifically:
- At **low feed** ($A_1$), increasing speed from 100 to 200 **reduces** roughness slightly (1.30 → 1.00).
- At **high feed** ($A_2$), increasing speed from 100 to 200 **reduces** roughness dramatically (1.80 → 0.70).

**Optimal setting:** $A_2 B_2$ (high feed rate + high cutting speed) gives the lowest roughness (0.70 µm), which is the recommended operating point for production.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative — KTU 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Full Factorial, Fractional Factorial, and Taguchi designs. Under what conditions would you prefer a Taguchi L9 orthogonal array over a full $3^3$ factorial design?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the procedure of conducting a Design of Experiments study in a Lean Six Sigma DMAIC project. How does DoE contribute to the "Improve" phase specifically?

---

**MODEL SOLUTION for Question B:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Comparison Table**

| Feature | Full Factorial | Fractional Factorial | Taguchi Design |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Runs | $L^k$ | $2^{k-p}$ | Fixed (L4, L9, L18, etc.) |
| Number of factors handled | Few (≤ 5) | Many | Many |
| Levels per factor | Any | Usually 2 | 2, 3, or 4 |
| Interaction estimation | All | Some (high resolution required) | Confounded by design |
| Robustness to noise | Manual setup | Manual setup | Explicitly designed-in |
| Best for | Detailed modeling | Screening many factors | Industrial robust design |
| Disadvantage | Run size explodes | Confounding risks | Cannot estimate all interactions |

**When to prefer Taguchi L9 over $3^3$ full factorial:**

1. **Resource constraints:** $3^3$ full factorial = **27 runs**; Taguchi L9 = only **9 runs** — a 67% reduction in experimental effort, time, and cost.
2. **Screening large numbers of factors:** If we have 4 factors at 3 levels each, the full factorial needs $3^4 = 81$ runs, but an L9 array can still handle it (4 factors in 9 runs) using the *linear graphs* and *column assignment* technique.
3. **Industrial robust design:** Taguchi's explicit goal is to find factor settings that are *insensitive* to noise factors, accomplished through inner/outer array signal-to-noise analysis.
4. **Practical compromise:** When interactions among factors are *a priori* assumed to be weak or negligible.
5. **L9 is preferred when** $k = 4$ factors at $L = 3$ levels, and we can tolerate that some 2-factor interactions will be confounded with each other.

> **Decision Rule:** Choose Taguchi L9 when run economy is critical, noise factors are important, and a few interactions can be sacrificed for efficiency.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] DoE Procedure in DMAIC:**

The DoE procedure is integrated into the DMAIC roadmap as follows:

| DMAIC Phase | DoE Activity | Output |
|-------------|--------------|--------|
| **Define** | Identify the problem, customer CTQ (Critical-to-Quality), and the response variable $Y$ | Project charter, $Y$ definition |
| **Measure** | Validate measurement system (Gage R&R), establish baseline process capability | Baseline $\sigma$, Cp, Cpk |
| **Analyze** | Brainstorm potential factors $X_i$; create fishbone, FMEA, screening experiment | List of vital few $X$'s |
| **Improve** | Design and run the DoE (factorial or RSM); analyze via ANOVA; determine optimal $X$ settings | Optimum $(X_1^*, X_2^*, \ldots, X_k^*)$ |
| **Control** | Verify with confirmation run; install SPC charts; document standard operating procedure | Sustained improvement |

**Specific Contribution to the "Improve" Phase:**

1. **Replaces guesswork with data:** The Lean tool *5-Why* gives qualitative root causes; DoE quantitatively verifies them.
2. **Identifies interaction effects:** 5-Why cannot detect that "Speed × Feed" interact; only DoE can. This prevents over-spending on factors that don't matter.
3. **Finds the global optimum:** Unlike OFAT, DoE maps the entire response surface and pinpoints the true optimal setting, reducing *waste of over-processing* (Lean 7th waste).
4. **Quantifies robustness:** DoE allows studying noise factors, leading to settings that are *insensitive* to variation — directly supporting the Lean goal of *built-in quality* (Jidoka).
5. **Reduces process variation:** By identifying the factors most affecting variability, DoE lowers $\sigma$, which improves Cp/Cpk — a direct Lean Six Sigma benefit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Most Common Mistake):**
> Many students **do not randomize the experimental run order** in their solutions. The KTU examiner will deduct **1 full mark** if randomization is omitted. Also, when an interaction effect is significant, students often still interpret main effects in isolation — this is incorrect and will cost **1.5 marks**. Always remember the hierarchy: significant interaction → interpret interaction first; if interaction is not significant, then interpret main effects.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] DoE = **planned** experimentation, NOT trial and error.
- [ ] The **three pillars** are Randomization, Replication, and Blocking.
- [ ] **OFAT is statistically inefficient** and **misses interactions** — full factorial designs are preferred.
- [ ] The total number of runs in a full factorial is $\mathbf{L^k}$; in a fractional factorial, it is $\mathbf{2^{k-p}}$.
- [ ] A **main effect** is the average change in response when one factor moves from low to high level.
- [ ] An **interaction effect** exists when the effect of one factor depends on the level of another.
- [ ] **ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)** partitions the total variability (SST) into components due to factors (SSA, SSB), interactions (SSAB), and error (SSE).
- [ ] The **F-statistic** is computed as $F = \frac{MS_{Factor}}{MS_{Error}}$ and compared with the critical F-value $F_{\alpha, df_1, df_2}$ from the F-table.
- [ ] In a $2^2$ design with $n$ replicates, $df_{Error} = 4(n-1)$.
- [ ] **Taguchi's signal-to-noise ratio (SN ratio)** is used to minimize variability in robust design.
- [ ] **Resolution** of a fractional factorial: **R-III** (confounds main effects), **R-IV** (confounds main with 2-factor interactions), **R-V** (confounds 2-factor with 2-factor). Always prefer R-V for serious industrial work.
- [ ] DoE is the **statistical engine** of the **Improve phase** of DMAIC, and aligns directly with Lean's principle of *built-in quality* (Jidoka).
- [ ] When reporting results, always include: experimental conditions, run order randomization, ANOVA table, and a confirmation run.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** When solving a 14-mark numerical, always show: (1) data table with totals, (2) main effect calculations, (3) interaction calculations, (4) full ANOVA table with $F$ comparison, and (5) a clear written conclusion with practical recommendation. This structure is what the KTU Board examiner expects and will fetch you the full 14 marks.
